topics.title: Basic Structure of Computers: Functional Units, Basic Operational Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Structure of Computers: Functional Units, Basic Operational Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **computer system** is an integrated assembly of electronic and electromechanical components designed to accept data (input), process it according to a stored set of instructions (program), produce meaningful results (output), and store both the data and the instructions for future use. The **basic structure** of a computer is described by its **functional units**, which are the major logical building blocks that cooperate to execute instructions.

The **functional units** of a computer system are categorized into five major logical blocks:

1. **Input Unit** – Accepts data and instructions from the external environment and converts them into a binary form the computer can understand.
2. **Memory (Storage) Unit** – Stores data, instructions, and intermediate results. It is split into Primary (fast, volatile) and Secondary (slow, non-volatile) memory.
3. **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)** – Performs all arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, comparisons).
4. **Control Unit (CU)** – Directs and coordinates all other units. It fetches, decodes, and sequences the execution of instructions.
5. **Output Unit** – Converts the internally stored binary results back into a human-readable form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The CPU (Central Processing Unit) is a *chip-level combination* of the **ALU**, the **Control Unit**, and **Registers** (high-speed internal memory). The CPU is the *brain* of the computer. Memory and I/O devices lie *outside* the CPU.

### 1.2 The Stored Program Concept

The modern computer is built on the **Stored Program Concept** proposed by **John von Neumann (1945)**. In this architecture:

- Both **instructions** and **data** are stored in the **same memory unit**.
- Instructions are fetched sequentially (or with branches) and executed by the CPU.
- The architecture in which program and data share a common bus and memory is called the **Von Neumann Architecture**.

> [!NOTE]
> A **Von Neumann bottleneck** occurs because the CPU and memory share a single bus for fetching both instructions and data, limiting the throughput. This is the reason modern processors use *caches* and *Harvard-style* instruction/data separation internally.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a computer as a **highly efficient, fully automated restaurant kitchen**:

| Kitchen Component | Computer Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Customer placing an order | **Input Unit** (Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner) | Brings in raw "data" (the order). |
| Recipe book on the counter | **Memory Unit** (RAM + Hard Disk) | Stores recipes (programs) and ingredients (data). |
| Master Chef (head of kitchen) | **Control Unit** (CU) | Reads the recipe, tells everyone what to do, and in what order. |
| Sous-chef with knife and stove | **Arithmetic Logic Unit** (ALU) | Actually chops, boils, and mixes — performs the real "processing." |
| Waiter delivering the dish | **Output Unit** (Monitor, Printer) | Sends the finished product to the user. |
| The Chef's small notepad | **Registers** (inside CPU) | Super-fast scratchpad memory for immediate calculations. |

Just as a kitchen cannot function if the chef stops coordinating, a computer cannot work if the **Control Unit** fails. The "order" (program) is essentially a step-by-step recipe the chef follows precisely.

### 1.4 Standard System Metrics and Constants

- **Word Length** = **Number of bits** a CPU processes in a single operation. Modern desktops use **64 bits**.
- **Clock Speed** is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, typically **GHz** ($1\,\text{GHz} = 10^9$ cycles per second).
- **Bus Width** is measured in **bits** (commonly 8, 16, 32, or 64).
- **Memory Capacity** is measured in **Bytes (B)** with prefixes: **KB** ($2^{10}$), **MB** ($2^{20}$), **GB** ($2^{30}$), **TB** ($2^{40}$).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU follows the **binary** (power-of-2) convention for memory sizing, not the decimal (SI) convention. **1 KB = 1024 Bytes**, not 1000 Bytes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Functional Unit Breakdown

A computer's behavior is the orchestrated cooperation of its functional units. Below is a structured, in-depth analysis of each unit, its internal sub-components, and its operational role.

#### 2.1.1 Input Unit

- **Function:** Translates human-readable information into binary signals.
- **Components:** Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner, Microphone, Touchscreen, Barcode reader, Network Interface Card (NIC).
- **Internal mechanism:** An **Interface** converts analog signals to digital, and a **Buffer** temporarily holds the data before the CPU is ready.
- **Speed mismatch problem:** I/O devices are far slower than the CPU. This is resolved using **interrupts** and **DMA (Direct Memory Access)**.

#### 2.1.2 Memory Unit

The memory hierarchy is structured as follows (from fastest/smallest to slowest/largest):

- **Registers:** Inside the CPU, access time ~**1 nanosecond**. Capacity: a few hundred bytes.
- **Cache (L1, L2, L3):** Very fast SRAM, access time ~**5–20 ns**.
- **Main Memory (RAM):** DRAM-based, access time ~**50–100 ns**. Volatile.
- **Secondary Storage (HDD/SSD):** Access time in **milliseconds** (HDD) or **microseconds** (SSD). Non-volatile, high capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Memory** = RAM + ROM. **Secondary Memory** = Hard Disk, SSD, Optical Discs, USB drives. The CPU can directly access only **primary memory**. All secondary data must first be loaded into RAM.

#### 2.1.3 ALU (Arithmetic and Logic Unit)

The ALU has two sub-sections:

- **Arithmetic Section:** Performs addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, increment, decrement. Built using **binary adders** (e.g., ripple-carry adder, carry-lookahead adder).
- **Logic Section:** Performs AND, OR, XOR, NOT, shift (logical/arithmetic), rotate, and comparison operations.

The ALU operates on operands fetched from **registers**, not directly from RAM, to minimize access time.

#### 2.1.4 Control Unit (CU)

The CU fetches each instruction using a **Program Counter (PC)**, decodes it using the **Instruction Register (IR)**, and generates the appropriate **control signals** (read/write/ALU-op/branch) through its **Control Logic**. The micro-operations are timed by a master **Clock**.

CU design approaches:
- **Hardwired CU:** Uses fixed combinational logic circuits. Faster, but inflexible.
- **Microprogrammed CU:** Uses a *microprogram* stored in a *Control Memory*. Slower but flexible and easy to modify.

#### 2.1.5 Output Unit

- **Function:** Translates internal binary results into human-perceivable form.
- **Components:** Monitor, Printer, Speakers, LEDs, Actuators, Network cards.
- **Common output device interfaces:** VGA/HDMI (monitor), USB (universal), Bluetooth, Wi-Fi.

### 2.2 System Interconnection — The Bus Structure

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common lines through which data, addresses, and control signals are transmitted.

Three main bus types:

- **Data Bus:** Carries the actual data. **Bidirectional**. Width = word length (e.g., 64 bits).
- **Address Bus:** Carries the memory/I/O address. **Unidirectional** (CPU → Memory/I/O). Width determines maximum addressable memory.
- **Control Bus:** Carries control signals (Read, Write, Clock, Interrupt Acknowledge, Bus Request). Used for timing and coordination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Maximum addressable memory with an *n*-bit address bus = $2^n$ bytes. With a **32-bit** address bus, we can address $2^{32}$ bytes = **4 GB** of memory. This is why 32-bit OSes cap at 4 GB RAM.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addressable Memory | $M = 2^n$ | Memory addressable with $n$-bit address bus | Bytes |
| Clock Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Inverse of clock frequency | Seconds |
| CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f}$ | Total time for $N$ instructions | Seconds |
| CPU Clock Cycles | $C = N \times CPI$ | Total clock cycles required | Cycles |
| CPI (avg) | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times IC_i)$ | Weighted average cycles per instruction | Cycles/Instr |
| MIPS Rating | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Millions of Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| MFLOPS Rating | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{FP operations}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^6}$ | Floating-point operations per second | MFLOPS |
| Amdahl's Law (Speedup) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | Overall speedup with improvement factor $k$ for fraction $f$ | Dimensionless |
| Throughput | $Th = \dfrac{1}{T_{CPU}}$ | Inverse of execution time | Instructions/s |
| Performance Ratio | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | Performance is *inversely* proportional to time | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, **Performance ∝ 1/Execution Time** is the most frequently tested relationship. Memorize: a machine with execution time **2 seconds** is *twice as slow* as one with **1 second**, even though many students mistakenly reverse the ratio.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Von Neumann model is the foundation for **all general-purpose processors** — from the **Intel Core i9** in your laptop to the **ARM Cortex-M4** in your washing machine. Understanding functional units is essential for:

- **Embedded systems design** (selecting the right microcontroller for a drone or pacemaker).
- **Compiler optimization** (knowing the CPU has only a few registers guides register allocation).
- **Performance engineering** (reducing memory accesses via caching dramatically speeds up programs).
- **Cybersecurity** (buffer overflow attacks exploit the linear memory model and bus structure).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CPU Performance Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Computer Organization papers. It links instruction count, clock cycles, and clock frequency.

**Given definitions:**

Let $N$ = total number of instructions executed in a program (also called the *instruction count* or *IC*).
Let $CPI$ = average cycles per instruction for the program.
Let $f$ = clock frequency in cycles per second (Hz).
Let $T$ = clock period = $\dfrac{1}{f}$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Compute total clock cycles required.**

The CPU must execute $N$ instructions. Each instruction, on average, consumes $CPI$ clock cycles. Therefore, the **total clock cycles** $C$ is:

$$C = N \times CPI$$

**Step 2 — Convert total clock cycles to elapsed time.**

Each clock cycle takes $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ seconds. So the total time is:

$$T_{CPU} = C \times T = C \times \dfrac{1}{f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $C$ from Step 1 into Step 2.**

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f}$$

This is the **fundamental CPU performance equation** in KTU 2024 Scheme.

**Step 4 — Derive the MIPS rating.**

MIPS = Millions of Instructions Per Second.

$$\text{IPS} = \dfrac{N}{T_{CPU}} = \dfrac{N \times f}{N \times CPI} = \dfrac{f}{CPI}$$

Converting to millions:

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$$

**Step 5 — Derive weighted average CPI.**

Suppose a program has $n$ distinct instruction classes. Class $i$ has $IC_i$ instructions and requires $CPI_i$ cycles each. Then:

$$CPI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i}$$

This is the **weighted average CPI** formula. The denominator is simply the total instruction count $N$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law

Amdahl's Law quantifies the *theoretical maximum* speedup achievable by improving one part of a system.

**Setup:**

- Let $f$ = fraction of execution time that the *enhanced* portion can speed up.
- Let $(1-f)$ = fraction of execution time unaffected.
- Let $k$ = speedup factor of the enhanced portion (i.e., it runs $k$ times faster).

**Step 1 — Original execution time.** Normalize to $T_{\text{old}} = 1$.

**Step 2 — New execution time.**

The enhanced portion now takes $\dfrac{f}{k}$ time. The un-enhanced portion still takes $(1-f)$ time.

$$T_{\text{new}} = (1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}$$

**Step 3 — Speedup ratio.**

$$S = \dfrac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting case (ideal enhancement).**

If $k \to \infty$ (the enhanced portion becomes instantaneous), then $\dfrac{f}{k} \to 0$ and:

$$S_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Even if you make *90%* of a program infinitely fast ($f = 0.9$), the overall speedup is capped at $S_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1}{0.1} = 10\times$. This is why optimizing the *un-common* 10% of code matters enormously.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Memory Types

This matrix is a high-yield KTU comparison question. Memorize it.

| Parameter | Register | Cache (L1) | Main Memory (RAM) | SSD | Hard Disk (HDD) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Technology** | Flip-flops | SRAM | DRAM | NAND Flash | Magnetic Platter |
| **Access Time** | ~1 ns | ~2–5 ns | ~50–100 ns | ~50–150 µs | ~5–15 ms |
| **Capacity** | ~Bytes–KB | ~32–256 KB | ~4–64 GB | ~256 GB–4 TB | ~500 GB–10 TB |
| **Volatile?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Cost per GB** | Very High | High | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| **Managed by** | Compiler / Hardware | Hardware | OS | OS / Firmware | OS / Firmware |
| **Location** | Inside CPU | Inside/Outside CPU | Motherboard | SATA/NVMe bus | SATA bus |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A processor runs at **2.5 GHz**. A program consists of **100 million instructions**, distributed as follows:

- **40%** are ALU instructions with **CPI = 1**.
- **30%** are Load/Store instructions with **CPI = 5**.
- **20%** are Branch instructions with **CPI = 2**.
- **10%** are Floating-point instructions with **CPI = 4**.

**Find:** (a) Effective CPI. (b) Total execution time. (c) MIPS rating.

**Solution (a) — Effective CPI:**

$$CPI = \sum IC_i \times CPI_i = (0.40 \times 1) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 2) + (0.10 \times 4)$$

$$CPI = 0.40 + 1.50 + 0.40 + 0.40 = 2.70 \text{ cycles/instruction}$$

**Solution (b) — Total execution time:**

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^6 \times 2.70}{2.5 \times 10^9}$$

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{2.7 \times 10^8}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.108 \text{ seconds} = 108 \text{ ms}$$

**Solution (c) — MIPS rating:**

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^9}{2.70 \times 10^6} \approx 925.93 \text{ MIPS}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Von Neumann Computer System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture, showing all interconnections between the **CPU, Memory, Input, and Output units** via the **System Bus**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT UNITS"]
        mon["Monitor"]
        prn["Printer"]
        spk["Speakers"]
    end

    subgraph IN["INPUT UNITS"]
        kbd["Keyboard"]
        mse["Mouse"]
        scn["Scanner"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["MEMORY UNIT"]
        rom["ROM Firmware"]
        ram["RAM Primary Memory"]
        cache["Cache L1 L2 L3"]
    end

    subgraph CPU["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT"]
        cu["Control Unit CU"]
        alu["Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU"]
        reg["Registers PC IR AC"]
    end

    BUS{{"SYSTEM BUS Data Address Control"}}

    IN --> BUS
    OUT <-- BUS
    MEM <-- BUS
    CPU <-- BUS

    CU -- Micro Signals --> ALU
    ALU -- Status Flags --> CU
    CPU <--> MEM
```

### 4.2 Detailed CPU Internal Architecture

The diagram below zooms into the **CPU** to show the **Control Unit**, **ALU**, and **Register File**, and how instructions are fetched, decoded, and executed.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EXT_MEM["External Memory"] -->|Instruction Fetch| PC["Program Counter PC"]
    PC -->|Address| MAR["Memory Address Register MAR"]
    MAR --> EXT_MEM
    EXT_MEM -->|Instruction Word| IR["Instruction Register IR"]
    IR -->|Opcode Operand| DEC["Instruction Decoder"]
    DEC -->|Control Signals| CU["Control Logic Hardwired or Microprogrammed"]
    DEC -->|Operation| ALU["Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU"]
    CU -->|Control Lines| ALU
    ALU -->|Result| AC["Accumulator AC"]
    AC -->|Stores Result| EXT_MEM
    CU -->|Clock Timing| CLK["Master Clock Signal"]
    CLK --> CU
    CU -->|Sequence| PC
```

### 4.3 Bus System Topology

This diagram clarifies how **Data**, **Address**, and **Control** buses are routed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU_M["CPU Module"] -- Data Bus 64 bit --> BUSM["Main Bus Controller"]
    CPU_M -- Address Bus 32 bit --> BUSM
    CPU_M -- Control Bus --> BUSM
    BUSM --> RAMM["Main Memory RAM"]
    BUSM --> IOMM["I O Controller"]
    IOMM --> KBDM["Keyboard"]
    IOMM --> MONM["Monitor"]
    IOMM --> PRNM["Printer"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** Node IDs in the above diagrams (e.g., `CPU_M`, `BUSM`, `RAMM`) are alphanumeric strings prefixed with letters to avoid conflicts with Mermaid reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph`. All labels are written in plain uppercase text without any markdown bold or italics syntax, ensuring clean rendering across Mermaid versions.

### 4.4 Memory Hierarchy Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["REGISTERS Few Bytes 1 ns Fastest"] --> B["L1 CACHE 32 to 64 KB 2 ns"]
    B --> C["L2 CACHE 256 KB to 1 MB 5 ns"]
    C --> D["L3 CACHE 4 to 32 MB 20 ns"]
    D --> E["MAIN MEMORY RAM 4 to 64 GB 100 ns"]
    E --> F["SSD 256 GB to 4 TB 100 microseconds"]
    F --> G["HDD 500 GB to 10 TB 10 ms Slowest"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As we move **down** the hierarchy, **capacity increases** but **speed decreases**. This trade-off is the *fundamental principle* of computer memory design and is a favorite KTU 2-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(3 Marks)**
List and briefly explain the **five functional units of a computer system** with one example device for each unit.

**Model Answer:**

A computer system is composed of the following five functional units:

1. **Input Unit** – Accepts data and instructions from the user in human-readable form and converts them to binary. *Example:* Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner.
2. **Memory (Storage) Unit** – Stores data, instructions, and results. It includes primary memory (RAM, ROM) and secondary memory (HDD, SSD). The CPU can directly access only primary memory.
3. **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)** – Performs all arithmetic operations (add, subtract, multiply, divide) and logical operations (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, comparison). It operates on data stored in registers.
4. **Control Unit (CU)** – Acts as the central coordinator. It fetches instructions via the Program Counter, decodes them via the Instruction Register, and generates control signals at the correct clock timing to direct the ALU, memory, and I/O units.
5. **Output Unit** – Receives binary results from the computer and converts them into human-perceivable form. *Example:* Monitor, Printer, Speakers.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(3 Marks)**
Explain the **stored program concept** in computer architecture. Mention one limitation of this concept.

**Model Answer:**

The **stored program concept** was proposed by **John von Neumann (1945)**. It states that:

- Both **instructions** and **data** are stored in the **same memory unit**.
- Programs can manipulate themselves (self-modifying code is possible in principle).
- Instructions are fetched from memory, decoded, and executed sequentially by the CPU.

**Limitation — Von Neumann Bottleneck:**

Since instructions and data share a **single bus** between the CPU and memory, the CPU spends significant time waiting for data transfers. This creates a **bottleneck** that limits the overall processing speed. It is mitigated by using *instruction pipelining*, *caches*, and the *Harvard architecture* (separate instruction and data memories) internally in modern processors.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students write the formula $T = N \times CPI \times f$ instead of $T = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f}$. The frequency is in the **denominator**. Marks are deducted for this.
> - In Amdahl's Law problems, do **not** forget that the un-enhanced fraction is $(1 - f)$, not $1$. A common error is writing $S = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{(1-f)}{k}}$, which is the *inverse* of the correct formula.
> - When asked to list "functional units," students often forget **Memory** and only list four. The **CPU is a chip-level combination** of ALU + CU + Registers; it is **not** itself a functional unit at the architectural level.
> - Always specify **units** in performance problems (ns, µs, ms, MIPS). Answers without units lose ½ to 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **basic structure of a computer system**. Describe the roles of the System Bus, ALU, and Control Unit.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A program contains **200 million instructions**. The processor clock frequency is **3 GHz**. The instruction mix is:
- **Class A:** 25% of instructions, CPI = 2
- **Class B:** 40% of instructions, CPI = 3
- **Class C:** 20% of instructions, CPI = 4
- **Class D:** 15% of instructions, CPI = 5

Calculate: (i) Effective CPI, (ii) CPU execution time, (iii) MIPS rating.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Basic Structure of a Computer (7 Marks)**

A computer system consists of **five functional units** interconnected via the **system bus**:

- **Input Unit:** Translates user input into binary. *Example:* Keyboard.
- **Memory Unit:** Stores data and instructions (RAM for primary, HDD/SSD for secondary).
- **ALU:** Performs arithmetic and logic operations on operands fetched from registers.
- **Control Unit:** Fetches, decodes, and sequences instruction execution using a clock.
- **Output Unit:** Converts binary results into human-readable form. *Example:* Monitor.

**System Bus:** A set of three sub-buses:
- **Data Bus** (bidirectional, 64-bit typical) – carries actual data.
- **Address Bus** (unidirectional, 32 or 64 bit) – carries the memory/I/O address.
- **Control Bus** – carries read/write/clock/interrupt signals.

**ALU Role:** Performs all computational work. It receives operands from registers, performs the operation specified by the CU, and returns the result to the accumulator or a register. Status flags (zero, carry, overflow) are set.

**Control Unit Role:** Acts as the "conductor" of the system. It uses the **Program Counter (PC)** to fetch the next instruction from memory into the **Instruction Register (IR)**, decodes the opcode, and emits control signals on the control bus to orchestrate ALU, memory, and I/O activity at the correct clock edge. *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4 for a visual reference.)*

---

**Part (b) — Performance Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data:**

$N = 200 \times 10^6$ instructions; $f = 3 \times 10^9$ Hz; mix as given.

**Step 2 — Compute Effective CPI (i):**

$$CPI = (0.25 \times 2) + (0.40 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 5)$$

$$CPI = 0.50 + 1.20 + 0.80 + 0.75 = 3.25 \text{ cycles/instruction}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Execution Time (ii):**

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^6 \times 3.25}{3 \times 10^9}$$

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{6.5 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^9} \approx 0.2167 \text{ seconds} = 216.7 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compute MIPS Rating (iii):**

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^9}{3.25 \times 10^6} \approx 923.08 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $CPI = 3.25$
- (ii) $T_{CPU} \approx 216.67$ ms
- (iii) MIPS $\approx 923.08$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Von Neumann architecture** in detail. Differentiate between **Hardwired Control Unit** and **Microprogrammed Control Unit**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A computer system uses **30%** of its time in floating-point operations. A proposed enhancement makes floating-point operations **8 times faster**.

Using **Amdahl's Law**, calculate:
(i) The overall speedup achieved.
(ii) The maximum possible speedup if floating-point operations could be made infinitely fast.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Von Neumann Architecture & Control Unit Design (7 Marks)**

**Von Neumann Architecture (1945):**

The architecture has the following key characteristics:
1. A **single memory** stores both instructions and data.
2. A **single bus** connects CPU and memory (data + instructions share it).
3. The **CPU** has a Control Unit, ALU, and Registers.
4. Instructions are executed **sequentially** (unless branch instructions alter flow).
5. The system is *general-purpose* — the program can change at runtime.

**Block Diagram:** *(Draw the Mermaid-style block diagram from Section 4.1 — CPU, Memory, I/O all connected via System Bus.)*

**Hardwired vs. Microprogrammed Control Unit:**

| Parameter | Hardwired CU | Microprogrammed CU |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | Combinational logic gates, flip-flops | Control memory (ROM) with microinstructions |
| **Speed** | Faster (no memory access) | Slower (microinstruction fetch) |
| **Flexibility** | Inflexible — must rewire to change | Flexible — only update the microprogram |
| **Complexity for CISC** | Very high | Manageable |
| **Used in** | RISC processors (ARM, MIPS) | CISC processors (older x86) |
| **Cost** | Higher design cost | Lower design cost |

---

**Part (b) — Amdahl's Law Application (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables:**

$f = 0.30$ (fraction of execution time that can be sped up — floating-point).
$k = 8$ (speedup factor of the enhanced portion).

**Step 2 — Compute Overall Speedup (i):**

$$S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \dfrac{0.30}{8}}$$

$$S = \dfrac{1}{0.70 + 0.0375} = \dfrac{1}{0.7375} \approx 1.356$$

**Step 3 — Compute Maximum Speedup (ii):**

Let $k \to \infty$:

$$S_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.30} = \dfrac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.4286$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $S \approx 1.356\times$ (about 35.6% faster)
- (ii) $S_{\text{max}} \approx 1.43\times$ (theoretical upper limit)

**Engineering Insight:** Even making the floating-point unit *infinitely fast* yields only ~43% overall speedup because the remaining 70% of the program (non-FP code) is unaffected. This is a classic Amdahl's Law pitfall — *the common code dominates the runtime*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this as your **last-night revision checklist** before the KTU exam:

- **Five Functional Units:** Input, Output, Memory, ALU, Control Unit. (CPU is the *combination* of ALU + CU + Registers.)
- **CPU Performance Equation:** $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f}$ — frequency in the **denominator**.
- **MIPS Equation:** $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$.
- **Weighted CPI:** Always multiply $IC_i \times CPI_i$ and divide by total $IC$.
- **Addressable Memory:** $M = 2^n$ bytes for an $n$-bit address bus.
- **Von Neumann Bottleneck:** Single shared bus between CPU and memory is the main limitation.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$; maximum speedup = $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$.
- **Memory Hierarchy:** Registers > Cache > RAM > SSD > HDD (faster but smaller, descending).
- **Primary vs. Secondary Memory:** Primary is volatile, fast, CPU-accessible. Secondary is non-volatile, slow, used for storage.
- **Bus Types:** Data (bidirectional, wide), Address (unidirectional, determines max memory), Control (timing/coord signals).
- **Hardwired vs. Microprogrammed CU:** Hardwired is fast but rigid; Microprogrammed is slow but flexible.
- **Clock Period vs. Frequency:** $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$. Higher $f$ → shorter $T$ → faster CPU.
- **Performance Inverse Law:** *Faster machine = less execution time*. Performance ratio = inverse of time ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Day Mantra for KTU 2024:** "**Functional units = 5. CPU ≠ unit, it's a chip. Frequency in denominator. Weighted CPI uses fractions. Amdahl's bottleneck is the un-enhanced part. Memory hierarchy: small and fast OR large and slow.**"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Map
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Map

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Memory Map** is a structured tabular or graphical representation of the entire addressable memory space of a computer system (or microprocessor-based system), showing how the available address range is partitioned and assigned to different physical entities — such as system RAM, ROM/EPROM, memory-mapped I/O devices, and reserved regions.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCST404)**, a memory map defines the complete address space layout that the CPU can access. Since the CPU interacts with memory only through binary addresses issued on the address bus, the memory map is essentially the **contract** that translates these addresses into specific physical hardware components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A *Memory Map* is a diagram/table that shows the allocation of the addressable memory space (from address `0000...0` to `FFFF...F`) to various memory chips and I/O devices in a microcomputer system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **apartment building** where every apartment has a unique number (the address). The building manager keeps a **directory** (the memory map) that tells:
- Which floor and apartment number belongs to which family (system RAM, ROM, I/O device).
- How many apartments are reserved for guests (reserved area).
- How many apartments are vacant (unused memory space).

Similarly, the CPU uses the address on the address bus to "knock on the door" of a specific memory location. The **memory map** is the directory that tells which door belongs to which device.

A simpler real-world analogy: Consider a **postman delivering letters** in a city. The city is divided into zones (memory regions), and each zone has a specific range of house numbers (address range). The postman's delivery chart (memory map) tells him which zone corresponds to which type of recipient — residential (RAM), post office box (ROM), or business (I/O device).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea for KTU Students:** The memory map does NOT describe the physical layout of memory chips on the motherboard. It describes the **logical address space** as seen by the programmer/CPU.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics apply to memory maps in a 16-bit microprocessor-based system (the most common KTU context):

- **Address Bus Width:** **16 bits** (for systems like 8086 — wait, KTU 2024 typically uses 8086 in this module)
- **Total Addressable Locations:** $2^n$, where $n$ is the number of address lines.
- **Total Memory Capacity:** $2^n \times \text{word size}$ (typically $2^n$ bytes for byte-addressable memory).
- **Standard Hexadecimal Address Range (for 16-bit):** `00000H` to `FFFFFH` (for 20-bit physical address in 8086) — but logical/segmented view is `0000H–FFFFH`.
- **Memory Size Units:** **1 KB = $2^{10}$ = 1024 bytes**, **1 MB = $2^{20}$ = 1,048,576 bytes**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of a Memory Map

A typical memory map is built upon the following logical regions, each serving a distinct function:

1. **System ROM / EPROM Region (Lower or Upper End)**
   - Contains firmware, BIOS, boot loader, monitor programs.
   - Usually mapped to the **upper end** of the address space (e.g., `F000H–FFFFH`) to ensure the CPU starts execution here after reset.
   - In some designs (like the 8086 minimum mode), it is mapped to the **top** of memory.

2. **System RAM Region (User Area)**
   - Used for user programs, stack, and data storage.
   - Occupies the **middle** or **lower** portion of the address space.
   - Typically volatile; loses data on power-off.

3. **Memory-Mapped I/O Region**
   - I/O devices treated as memory locations.
   - Each I/O device is assigned a unique address range.
   - No special `IN`/`OUT` instructions required (unlike isolated I/O).

4. **Reserved / Unused Region**
   - Address ranges left empty for future expansion.
   - Decoding logic must ensure no device responds here (to avoid bus contention).

## 2.2 Why Memory Maps Are Essential — The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|------|----------------|
| **Address Decoding** | Multiple devices share the address bus; only one must respond to a given address. The memory map defines which device responds to which address. |
| **System Design Planning** | Designers must partition a fixed address space (e.g., 64 KB for 8085) among RAM, ROM, and I/O before hardware is built. |
| **Software Compatibility** | Compilers/assemblers generate code assuming a known memory map; changing the map changes where code/data resides. |
| **No Bus Contention** | A well-designed memory map guarantees that **exactly one** device is selected for any valid address, preventing hardware damage or data corruption. |
| **Expandability** | Reserved regions allow adding new devices without redesigning the entire decoding logic. |

## 2.3 Types of Memory Maps (I/O Organization)

There are **two fundamental I/O addressing schemes** that influence how the memory map is structured:

### A. Memory-Mapped I/O
- I/O devices are treated **as if they are memory locations**.
- They occupy a portion of the **same address space** as memory.
- The CPU uses the **same instructions** (e.g., `MOV`, `LDA`, `STA`) to access both memory and I/O.
- **Advantage:** No special I/O instructions needed; more addressing modes available.
- **Disadvantage:** I/O devices consume part of the valuable memory address space.

### B. I/O-Mapped I/O (Isolated I/O / Port-Mapped I/O)
- I/O devices have a **separate, dedicated address space**.
- A special control line (e.g., `IO/M` in 8086, `RD` & `WR` combined with `IO/M` in 8085) distinguishes between memory and I/O access.
- Special instructions (`IN`, `OUT` in 8086) are required.
- **Advantage:** Full memory address space is available for memory; faster I/O.
- **Disadvantage:** Limited addressing modes for I/O; extra hardware for the control line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** In **memory-mapped I/O**, the memory map contains I/O regions; in **isolated I/O**, the memory map contains only memory chips, and I/O has a separate port map.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Total Addressable Locations | $N = 2^n$ | where $n$ = number of address lines |
| Total Memory Capacity (byte-addressable) | $C = 2^n$ bytes | $1\text{ KB} = 1024$ bytes |
| Memory Size from Address Range | $\text{Size} = (\text{End Addr} - \text{Start Addr} + 1) \times \text{word size}$ | Result in bytes (or words) |
| Number of Address Lines Required | $n = \lceil \log_2 (\text{Bytes}) \rceil$ | Round up to next integer |
| Hex → Decimal Conversion | $(\text{Hex digit value}) \times 16^{\text{position}}$ | Position 0 = rightmost |
| Number of Memory Chips Required | $\text{Chips} = \frac{\text{Total Memory}}{\text{Size of one chip}}$ | Round up if not exact |
| Memory-Mapped I/O Address Space | Same as memory (overlapping) | No special `IO/M` line required |
| Isolated I/O Address Space | Separate from memory | Requires `IO/M` control signal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Check (KTU 2024):** A 16-bit address bus can access $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ locations = **64 KB**. A 20-bit address bus can access $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ locations = **1 MB**.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Embedded System Design (ARM, AVR, PIC):** Every microcontroller has a published memory map; firmware developers must respect it to place code in Flash, variables in RAM, and configure peripheral registers.
- **Operating Systems (Linux, Windows):** The kernel's virtual memory map defines user space vs kernel space regions.
- **IoT Devices:** Memory maps allocate precise regions for bootloaders, application code, and wireless protocol stacks.
- **FPGA Design:** Hardware description languages (VHDL/Verilog) use memory maps to define register interfaces for IP cores.
- **Automotive ECUs:** ISO 26262 compliance requires a documented memory map for safety-critical software partitioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating Total Memory Size from Address Range

**Problem Statement:** A memory chip is mapped to the address range `2000H` to `7FFFH` in an 8085 system (byte-addressable, 8-bit data bus). Calculate the size of the memory chip.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the start and end addresses.**

$$\text{Start Address} = 2000\text{H}$$
$$\text{End Address} = 7FFF\text{H}$$

**Step 2 — Convert hexadecimal addresses to decimal for clarity.**

$$2000\text{H} = (2 \times 16^3) + (0 \times 16^2) + (0 \times 16^1) + (0 \times 16^0) = 8192$$

$$7FFF\text{H} = (7 \times 16^3) + (15 \times 16^2) + (15 \times 16^1) + (15 \times 16^0) = 32767$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the number of addressable locations.**

$$\text{Number of Locations} = \text{End Address} - \text{Start Address} + 1$$

$$\text{Number of Locations} = 32767 - 8192 + 1 = 24576$$

**Step 4 — Convert to standard memory units.**

$$\text{Size in bytes} = 24576 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{Size in KB} = \frac{24576}{1024} = 24 \text{ KB}$$

**Alternative Hex-Only Method (faster for exams):**

$$\text{Size} = (7FFF - 2000 + 1)\text{H} = 6000\text{H}$$

$$6000\text{H} = (6 \times 16^3) = 6 \times 4096 = 24576 \text{ bytes} = 24 \text{ KB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Shortcut:** $1\text{H} = 1$ memory location, $10\text{H} = 16$ locations, $100\text{H} = 256$ locations, $1000\text{H} = 4096$ locations (4 KB).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Address Lines Required for a Given Memory

**Problem Statement:** A system designer needs 32 KB of RAM and 16 KB of ROM in a microprocessor system with a 16-bit address bus. Verify if this fits and determine the number of address lines actually needed for chip selection.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate total memory required.**

$$\text{Total Memory} = 32 \text{ KB (RAM)} + 16 \text{ KB (ROM)} = 48 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the address bus can accommodate it.**

A 16-bit address bus supports $2^{16} = 65536 = 64 \text{ KB}$.

$$64 \text{ KB} \geq 48 \text{ KB} \quad \checkmark \text{ (fits with 16 KB unused)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate address lines needed per chip.**

For **32 KB RAM**:
$$n_{\text{RAM}} = \log_2(32 \times 1024) = \log_2(2^{15}) = 15 \text{ address lines}$$

For **16 KB ROM**:
$$n_{\text{ROM}} = \log_2(16 \times 1024) = \log_2(2^{14}) = 14 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the high-order address lines used for chip selection (decoding).**

The 32 KB RAM requires 15 address lines (`A0–A14`) internally, leaving `A15` for chip selection.

The 16 KB ROM requires 14 address lines (`A0–A13`) internally, leaving `A14` and `A15` for chip selection.

**Step 5 — Design the memory map.**

$$\text{RAM: } \quad 0000\text{H} - 7FFF\text{H} \quad (32\text{ KB, }A_{15}=0)$$
$$\text{ROM: } \quad 8000\text{H} - BFFF\text{H} \quad (16\text{ KB, }A_{15}=1, A_{14}=0)$$
$$\text{Unused: } C000\text{H} - FFFF\text{H} \quad (16\text{ KB, }A_{15}=1, A_{14}=1)$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU 2024 Memory Map Scenarios

| Scenario | Address Range (Hex) | Size (Decimal) | Size (KB) | Address Lines Used |
|----------|---------------------|----------------|-----------|--------------------|
| 2 KB ROM at the top | `F800H – FFFFH` | 2048 | 2 KB | 11 (`A0–A10`) for chip, `A11–A15` for decoding |
| 4 KB RAM at the bottom | `0000H – 0FFFH` | 4096 | 4 KB | 12 (`A0–A11`) for chip, `A12–A15` for decoding |
| 8 KB Memory block | `4000H – 5FFFH` | 8192 | 8 KB | 13 (`A0–A12`) for chip, `A13–A15` for decoding |
| 32 KB Block | `0000H – 7FFFH` | 32768 | 32 KB | 15 (`A0–A14`) for chip, `A15` for decoding |
| 64 KB Full Space | `0000H – FFFFH` | 65536 | 64 KB | 16 (all lines) — no decoding needed |

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Memory-Mapped I/O vs Isolated I/O Address Calculation

**Problem Statement:** An 8086-based system uses memory-mapped I/O. There are 8 I/O devices, each requiring 16 addresses. Calculate the total I/O address space consumed, and the remaining memory available if total memory is 1 MB.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate total I/O addresses consumed.**

$$\text{I/O addresses} = 8 \text{ devices} \times 16 \text{ addresses/device} = 128 \text{ addresses}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bytes (for memory-mapped I/O).**

Since each address in memory-mapped I/O consumes 1 byte of memory space:

$$\text{Memory consumed by I/O} = 128 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate remaining memory for RAM/ROM.**

$$\text{Remaining Memory} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} - 128 \text{ bytes} = 1{,}048{,}448 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{Remaining Memory} = \frac{1{,}048{,}448}{1024} = 1024 - 0.125 = 1023.875 \text{ KB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Isolated I/O:** The I/O addresses do NOT consume memory space; the full 1 MB remains available. The I/O devices have a separate 16-bit I/O port address space (`0000H–FFFFH` = 64K I/O ports).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Typical Memory Map of an 8085/8086-Based Microcomputer

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates a standard memory map structure with the address space partitioned into functional regions:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ADDR["16-BIT ADDRESS SPACE : 0000H to FFFFH (64 KB Total)"]
        direction TB
        nodeROM["ROM REGION : F000H to FFFFH (4 KB) : Monitor Firmware and Boot Code"]
        nodeROM2["ROM RESERVED : E000H to EFFFH (4 KB) : Available for Expansion"]
        nodeIO["MEMORY MAPPED I/O : D000H to DFFFH (4 KB) : I O Device Registers"]
        nodeRAM2["USER RAM 2 : 8000H to CFFFH (20 KB) : User Programs and Data"]
        nodeSTACK["STACK AREA : 4000H to 7FFFH (16 KB) : System Stack"]
        nodeRAM1["USER RAM 1 : 1000H to 3FFFH (12 KB) : Application Data"]
        nodeVAR["VARIABLE AREA : 0000H to 0FFFH (4 KB) : System Variables and Vectors"]
    end
    ADDR --> nodeVAR
    nodeVAR --> nodeRAM1
    nodeRAM1 --> nodeSTACK
    nodeSTACK --> nodeRAM2
    nodeRAM2 --> nodeIO
    nodeIO --> nodeROM2
    nodeROM2 --> nodeROM
```

## 4.2 Address Decoding Logic Flow

The diagram below shows how a CPU address is decoded to select the correct physical device, which is the underlying mechanism that makes a memory map functional:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Issues Address on Address Bus A15 to A0"] --> DECODER["Address Decoder : 74LS138 or 74LS139"]
    DECODER --> CHECKROM{"Is A15 to A12 = 1111 ?"}
    CHECKROM -->|YES| ROMSEL["Activate ROM Chip Select : ROM Responds"]
    CHECKROM -->|NO| CHECKIO{"Is A15 to A12 = 1101 ?"}
    CHECKIO -->|YES| IOSEL["Activate I O Device : I O Device Responds"]
    CHECKIO -->|NO| CHECKRAM{"Is A15 = 0 ?"}
    CHECKRAM -->|YES| RAMSEL["Activate RAM Chip Select : RAM Responds"]
    CHECKRAM -->|NO| NOSEL["No Device Selected : Reserved or Error"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Memory-Mapped I/O vs Isolated I/O

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MMAP["MEMORY MAPPED I/O SCHEME"]
        direction TB
        CPU1["CPU"] --> ADDR1["Address Bus A0 to A15"]
        ADDR1 --> MEM1["RAM and ROM : 0000H to CFFFH"]
        ADDR1 --> IOSHARED["I O Devices : D000H to FFFFH : Treated as Memory"]
        MEM1 --> DATABUS1["Common Data Bus"]
        IOSHARED --> DATABUS1
        CPU1 --> DATABUS1
    end
    
    subgraph IMAP["ISOLATED I/O SCHEME"]
        direction TB
        CPU2["CPU"] --> ADDR2["Address Bus A0 to A15"]
        CPU2 --> CTRL["Control Line IO/M"]
        ADDR2 --> MEM2["RAM and ROM : Full 64 KB"]
        ADDR2 --> IODED["Separate I O Port Bus : 8 or 16 bit"]
        IODED --> IOPORT["I O Devices : 00H to FFH : 256 Ports"]
        MEM2 --> DATABUS2["Common Data Bus"]
        IOPORT --> IOPORTBUS["Dedicated I O Data Bus"]
        CPU2 --> DATABUS2
        CPU2 --> IOPORTBUS
        CPU2 --> CTRL
    end
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Memory Map Table Representation

| Address Range (Hex) | Size | Region Type | Function | Decoding Signal |
|---------------------|------|-------------|----------|-----------------|
| `F800H – FFFFH` | 2 KB | ROM | System Monitor / BIOS | `A15–A11 = 11111` |
| `F000H – F7FFH` | 2 KB | ROM (Reserved) | Future Firmware | `A15–A11 = 11110` |
| `E000H – EFFFH` | 4 KB | Memory-Mapped I/O | I/O Device Registers | `A15–A12 = 1110` |
| `C000H – DFFFH` | 8 KB | RAM | Extended User Memory | `A15–A13 = 110` |
| `4000H – BFFFH` | 32 KB | RAM | Main User RAM | `A15 = 0` |
| `0000H – 3FFFH` | 16 KB | RAM | System Variables & Vectors | `A15–A14 = 00` |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a memory map. What is its significance in a microprocessor-based system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **memory map** is a tabular/pictorial representation that shows how the entire addressable memory space of a computer system is allocated to various memory devices (RAM, ROM) and I/O devices.

**Significance (any 2 for full marks):**
1. It defines the **logical address space layout** as seen by the programmer and CPU.
2. It guides the design of **address decoding logic** so that only one device responds to a given address (preventing bus contention).
3. It enables **systematic partitioning** of memory between system firmware (ROM), user programs (RAM), and peripheral devices.
4. It facilitates **future expandability** by reserving unused address regions.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between memory-mapped I/O and I/O-mapped I/O (isolated I/O).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Memory-Mapped I/O | I/O-Mapped I/O (Isolated) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Address Space | I/O devices share the memory address space | I/O devices have a separate address space |
| Instructions Used | Normal memory instructions (`MOV`, `LDA`, `STA`) | Special instructions (`IN`, `OUT`) |
| Control Signal | Not required | Separate `IO/M` (or `RD/WR` + `IO/M`) signal needed |
| Memory Available | Reduced (I/O consumes memory space) | Full memory address space available |
| Flexibility | More addressing modes for I/O | Limited addressing modes |
| Speed | Slightly slower (longer instructions possible) | Faster (shorter 1-byte I/O instructions) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**An 8085 microprocessor system has the following memory devices connected:**
- 8 KB EPROM using address range starting from `0000H`
- 8 KB RAM using the next available address
- 4 KB memory-mapped I/O device at the top of the 64 KB address space

**(a)** Draw the complete memory map showing address ranges for each device.  
**(b)** Determine the number of address lines required for each device and identify the address lines used for chip selection. Also calculate any unused memory space.

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Memory Map (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Allocate 8 KB EPROM starting at `0000H`.**

8 KB = $8 \times 1024 = 8192$ bytes = $2000\text{H}$ bytes.

$$\text{EPROM Range: } 0000\text{H} \text{ to } (0000 + 2000 - 1)\text{H} = 0000\text{H} \text{ to } 1FFF\text{H}$$

**Step 2 — Allocate 8 KB RAM at the next address (`2000H`).**

$$\text{RAM Range: } 2000\text{H} \text{ to } (2000 + 2000 - 1)\text{H} = 2000\text{H} \text{ to } 3FFF\text{H}$$

**Step 3 — Allocate 4 KB memory-mapped I/O at the top of 64 KB (`FFFFH`).**

4 KB = $1000\text{H}$ bytes.

$$\text{I/O Range: } (FFFF - 1000 + 1)\text{H} \text{ to } FFFF\text{H} = F000\text{H} \text{ to } FFFF\text{H}$$

**Step 4 — Identify unused memory.**

$$\text{Used: } 2000\text{H} + 2000\text{H} + 1000\text{H} = 5000\text{H} = 20480 \text{ bytes} = 20 \text{ KB}$$

$$\text{Unused: } 10000\text{H} - 5000\text{H} = B000\text{H} = 45056 \text{ bytes} = 44 \text{ KB}$$

**Final Memory Map:**

| Address Range (Hex) | Size | Device |
|---------------------|------|--------|
| `0000H – 1FFFH` | 8 KB | EPROM |
| `2000H – 3FFFH` | 8 KB | RAM |
| `4000H – EFFFH` | 44 KB | **Unused (Reserved)** |
| `F000H – FFFFH` | 4 KB | Memory-Mapped I/O |

---

### Part (b) — Address Line Analysis (7 marks)

**For 8 KB EPROM (`0000H – 1FFFH`):**

Internal address lines needed: $n = \log_2(8 \times 1024) = \log_2(8192) = 13$ lines.

$$\text{Internal: } A_0 \text{ to } A_{12} \quad (13 \text{ lines})$$

$$\text{Chip Selection: } A_{13}, A_{14}, A_{15} \quad (3 \text{ lines for decoding})$$

**For 8 KB RAM (`2000H – 3FFFH`):**

Internal address lines: $A_0$ to $A_{12}$ (13 lines).

Observation: $A_{13} = 1$ for the entire range. So $A_{13}$ alone can select this RAM.

$$\text{Chip Selection: } A_{13} = 1, \quad A_{14} = 0, \quad A_{15} = 0$$

**For 4 KB Memory-Mapped I/O (`F000H – FFFFH`):**

Internal address lines: $n = \log_2(4 \times 1024) = 12$ lines.

$$\text{Internal: } A_0 \text{ to } A_{11} \quad (12 \text{ lines})$$

$$\text{Chip Selection: } A_{12} = 0, \quad A_{13} = 0, \quad A_{14} = 0, \quad A_{15} = 1$$

**Unused Address Range (`4000H – EFFFH`):**

For this range, no device's chip select signal is active — the decoder outputs `1` (or the gate combination is unmatched) for all these addresses.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to add **+1** when calculating size from address range, leading to an off-by-one error (e.g., 8191 instead of 8192 bytes).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **internal address lines** (used inside the chip) with **chip select lines** (used for decoding). The sum must equal the total address bus width.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not stating **which combination** of high-order lines activates each chip's select input — vague answers lose 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A 16-bit microprocessor has a 64 KB address space. Design a memory map with the following requirements:**
- 16 KB ROM
- 32 KB RAM  
- 8 KB memory-mapped I/O
- The remaining address space is unused

**(a)** Draw the memory map with proper address ranges.  
**(b)** Explain how address decoding is achieved using a 3-to-8 decoder (74LS138), specifying the input and enable conditions for each device.

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Memory Map Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate each region size in hexadecimal.**

- 16 KB ROM = $4000\text{H}$ bytes
- 32 KB RAM = $8000\text{H}$ bytes
- 8 KB I/O = $2000\text{H}$ bytes
- Total used = $4000 + 8000 + 2000 = E000\text{H}$ = 56 KB
- Unused = $10000 - E000 = 2000\text{H}$ = 8 KB

**Step 2 — Allocate regions from the bottom up (as a standard convention).**

| Address Range (Hex) | Size | Device |
|---------------------|------|--------|
| `0000H – 3FFFH` | 16 KB | ROM |
| `4000H – BFFFH` | 32 KB | RAM |
| `C000H – DFFFH` | 8 KB | Memory-Mapped I/O |
| `E000H – FFFFH` | 8 KB | **Unused (Reserved)** |

---

### Part (b) — Address Decoding with 74LS138 (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify high-order address lines used for decoding.**

A 74LS138 is a 3-to-8 line decoder with **3 select inputs** (`A`, `B`, `C`) and **3 enable inputs** (`G1`, `$\overline{G2A}$`, `$\overline{G2B}$`).

The 3 high-order address lines `A15`, `A14`, `A13` are used as select inputs to the decoder.

**Step 2 — Determine the active output for each device.**

| Address Range | $A_{15} A_{14} A_{13}$ | Active 74LS138 Output | Device Selected |
|---------------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| `0000H – 3FFFH` | `0 0 0` | $\overline{Y_0}$ | ROM |
| `4000H – 7FFFH` | `0 0 1` | $\overline{Y_1}$ | RAM (Part 1) |
| `8000H – BFFFH` | `1 0 0` | $\overline{Y_4}$ | RAM (Part 2) |
| `C000H – DFFFH` | `1 1 0` | $\overline{Y_6}$ | Memory-Mapped I/O |
| `E000H – FFFFH` | `1 1 1` | $\overline{Y_7}$ | Unused (no device) |

**Step 3 — Connect the enable pins.**

To ensure the decoder is enabled only during a memory or I/O access (not during invalid bus cycles):
- `G1` is connected to `$+5\text{V}$` (always enabled).
- `$\overline{G2A}$` and `$\overline{G2B}$` are connected to control signals like `RD` and `WR` (active low), so the decoder activates only during a valid read/write cycle.

**Step 4 — Verify the decoding logic.**

For example, to access ROM at address `2000H`:
- Binary: `0010 0000 0000 0000`
- $A_{15}A_{14}A_{13} = 000$ → Output $\overline{Y_0}$ goes LOW → ROM's $\overline{CS}$ activated.
- Internal address lines $A_0$ to $A_{13}$ select the exact byte inside the 16 KB ROM.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget that the 74LS138 outputs are **active LOW** ($\overline{Y_0}$ to $\overline{Y_7}$). The chip select of the memory chip must therefore also be **active low** — failing to mention this loses 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not specifying how the **enable pins** (`G1`, `$\overline{G2A}$`, `$\overline{G2B}$`) are connected. Without proper enabling, the decoder may produce false selects during DMA or interrupt acknowledge cycles.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Leaving the unused output ($\overline{Y_7}$) floating — it should be either left unconnected or pulled up to $V_{CC}$ through a resistor to prevent noise-induced false activation.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing the **binary pattern of $A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}$** with the actual hex value of the address. The pattern `1 1 0` corresponds to `C000H–DFFFH` (where $A_{13} = 0$, $A_{14} = 1$, $A_{15} = 1$), NOT `6000H`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Definition:** A memory map is the **logical address space allocation** of a system — it is **not** the physical memory layout on the board.
- **Key Formula:** $\text{Size} = (\text{End Address} - \text{Start Address} + 1) \times \text{word size}$.
- **Hex-Byte Conversion Quick Reference:**  
  - $1\text{H} = 1$ byte  
  - $10\text{H} = 16$ bytes  
  - $100\text{H} = 256$ bytes  
  - $1000\text{H} = 4\text{ KB}$  
  - $4000\text{H} = 16\text{ KB}$  
  - $8000\text{H} = 32\text{ KB}$  
  - $10000\text{H} = 64\text{ KB}$ (full 16-bit space)
- **Address Lines vs Chip Select:** Internal address lines access bytes within a chip; high-order lines perform **chip selection** via decoders.
- **Memory-Mapped I/O** shares the address space; uses memory instructions; needs **no special control signal**.
- **Isolated I/O** uses a separate address space; requires **`IN`/`OUT` instructions** and a control line like `IO/M`.
- **ROM is usually placed at the top** (`FFFFH` end) so the CPU starts execution from a known reset vector location.
- **RAM is usually placed at the bottom** (`0000H` start) for efficient stack growth downward and interrupt vector storage.
- **Unused regions must have no device responding** — the decoder must produce a "no select" output for these addresses.
- **Bus Contention Rule:** Never let two devices respond to the same address — the memory map and decoder must guarantee **uniqueness**.
- **Examiner Loves:** Address range calculations, decoder truth tables, distinguishing memory-mapped vs isolated I/O, and neat memory map diagrams with hex labels.
- **Examiner Hates:** Off-by-one errors, forgetting active-low notation on $\overline{CS}$ pins, vague decoding explanations, and missing the +1 in size calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
