<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Endianness — The Byte Ordering Problem

### Formal Definition
**Endianness** is the attribute of a computer system that describes the order in which bytes (or, more generally, bits) are arranged and transmitted over a communication medium, or stored in computer memory. In multi-byte numerical values, endianness dictates whether the **most significant byte (MSB)** or the **least significant byte (LSB)** occupies the lowest memory address.

The two principal byte-ordering schemes are:
- **Big-Endian (BE)**: The most significant byte is stored at the **lowest** memory address.
- **Little-Endian (LE)**: The least significant byte is stored at the **lowest** memory address.

> [!NOTE]
> **MSB** = Most Significant Byte (carries the highest place value, e.g., the leftmost byte in hexadecimal). **LSB** = Least Significant Byte (carries the lowest place value, e.g., the rightmost byte).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine writing the number **1,234,567** in a notebook:
- **Big-Endian** is the way humans normally write — you start with the biggest digit "1" (the most significant) on the left, then "2", "3", "4", and so on, ending with the smallest "7" on the right.
- **Little-Endian** is the reverse — you start with the smallest digit "7" on the left, then "6", "5", ..., ending with the largest "1" on the right.

In computer memory, "leftmost" corresponds to the **lowest address**, and "rightmost" to the **highest address**.

### Real-World Engineering Examples
| Architecture | Endianness |
|---|---|
| Intel x86 / x86-64 | **Little-Endian** |
| ARM (default, configurable) | **Little-Endian** (bi-endian) |
| Motorola 68k, PowerPC (legacy) | **Big-Endian** |
| IBM z/Architecture, SPARC | **Big-Endian** |
| Network protocols (TCP/IP) | **Big-Endian** (Network Byte Order) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Endianness becomes critical in **cross-platform communication**, **file portability** (e.g., BMP, WAV), and **embedded systems** where microcontrollers of differing endianness exchange data.

---

## 1.2 CISC vs RISC Architectures

### CISC — Complex Instruction Set Computer
**Definition:** A CISC processor is designed with a **large, rich, and variable-length instruction set** intended to reduce the semantic gap between high-level languages and machine code. A single CISC instruction may perform **multiple low-level operations** (e.g., memory load, ALU operation, and memory store in a single instruction).

> [!NOTE]
> Classic examples: **Intel 8086, Motorola 68000, VAX**. Modern x86-64 is technically CISC at the ISA level, but internally RISC-like (micro-ops).

### RISC — Reduced Instruction Set Computer
**Definition:** A RISC processor employs a **small, highly-optimized, fixed-length instruction set**. Most instructions execute in a **single clock cycle**, and arithmetic/logic operations are performed **only on registers** (load/store architecture).

> [!NOTE]
> Classic examples: **MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, SPARC, PowerPC**. RISC emphasizes hardware simplicity and compiler optimization.

### Conceptual Analogy — A Kitchen Cooking Analogy
- **CISC** is like a **Swiss Army knife in a professional kitchen** — one tool can slice, dice, and scoop. The chef uses a single complex gesture to do many things, but the tool itself is intricate and hard to manufacture.
- **RISC** is like a **professional chef's knife set** — each knife does one thing (chop, slice, julienne), but does it **perfectly and fast**. The chef uses multiple simple steps, but the tools are uniform, fast, and easy to optimize.

---

## 1.3 Assembly Language — The Human-Readable Machine Code

### Formal Definition
**Assembly Language** is a low-level programming language that uses **mnemonic codes** (short human-readable abbreviations such as `ADD`, `SUB`, `MOV`, `LOAD`) to represent the **machine instructions** of a specific processor architecture. Each assembly instruction typically corresponds to **one** (RISC) or **more** (CISC) machine-language instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Assembly is **architecture-specific** — MIPS assembly cannot run on an x86 processor. An **assembler** translates assembly to machine code; a **disassembler** does the reverse.

### Assembler Directives (Pseudo-Instructions)
**Assembler Directives** are instructions to the **assembler itself** (not the CPU) that control the assembly process, define data, allocate memory, or specify code/data sections. They do **not generate machine code**.

Common MIPS directives:
- `.data` — Declares the start of the data section (initialized variables).
- `.text` — Declares the start of the code (text) section.
- `.word` — Allocates a 32-bit word in memory.
- `.byte` — Allocates a single byte.
- `.asciiz` — Allocates a null-terminated ASCII string.
- `.space n` — Allocates `n` bytes of uninitialized space.
- `.globl symbol` — Declares `symbol` as global (accessible to linker).

---

## 1.4 Programming Concepts — Program Flow & Control Structures

### Formal Definitions
- **Program Flow**: The order in which instructions of a program are executed. By default, flow is **sequential** (instruction by instruction).
- **Branching**: Altering the sequential flow by jumping to a non-adjacent instruction. Implemented via **branch/jump instructions**.
- **Conditional Statements**: `if-else` constructs that execute different code paths based on a condition's truth value.
- **Loops**: Constructs that repeat a block of code — `for`, `while`, `do-while`.
- **Arrays**: Contiguous blocks of homogeneous data accessed by an **index**.
- **Function Calls**: Subprograms invoked via a **call/return protocol**, often involving a **stack** for parameter passing and return addresses.

> [!NOTE]
> In MIPS, branching is implemented using instructions like `beq` (branch if equal), `bne` (branch if not equal), and pseudo-instructions like `bgt`, `blt`. Function calls use `jal` (jump-and-link) and `jr $ra`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Endianness — Deep Analysis

### Byte-Storage Convention
Consider a 32-bit integer `0x12345678` stored in memory starting at address `0x1000`. The byte breakdown is:
- Byte 0 (MSB) = `0x12`
- Byte 1        = `0x34`
- Byte 2        = `0x56`
- Byte 3 (LSB) = `0x78`

**Big-Endian Storage** (MSB at lowest address):

| Address | 0x1000 | 0x1001 | 0x1002 | 0x1003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0x12 | 0x34 | 0x56 | 0x78 |
| Role | MSB | — | — | LSB |

**Little-Endian Storage** (LSB at lowest address):

| Address | 0x1000 | 0x1001 | 0x1002 | 0x1003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0x78 | 0x56 | 0x34 | 0x12 |
| Role | LSB | — | — | MSB |

### KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — Endianness

| Concept | Big-Endian | Little-Endian |
|---|---|---|
| Lowest address contains | **MSB** | **LSB** |
| Human-readable order | Yes (matches writing) | Reversed |
| Arithmetic (e.g., increment) | Must access highest byte | Easy — just access lowest byte |
| Network Byte Order | **Yes** (TCP/IP standard) | No |
| Examples | SPARC, PowerPC, IBM Z | x86, x86-64, ARM (default) |
| Byte at address `A` value | `(N >> 8k) AND 0xFF` | `(N >> 0) AND 0xFF` for `k=0` |
| Bit-shift extraction | MSB-first | LSB-first |

### Address Calculation
For an integer $N$ stored in $k+1$ bytes (byte index $0$ to $k$), the value of the byte at address $A + i$ is:

$$\text{Byte}_{BE}(A+i) = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{256^{k-i}} \right\rfloor \bmod 256$$

$$\text{Byte}_{LE}(A+i) = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{256^{i}} \right\rfloor \bmod 256$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** In **networking**, the sender must convert to **Network Byte Order (Big-Endian)** using `htonl()` / `htons()` functions before transmission. The receiver converts back using `ntohl()` / `ntohs()`. Mismatched endianness causes the famous **"byte-swap" bug** seen in cross-platform file handling.

---

## 2.2 CISC vs RISC — Detailed Comparison

### Architectural Philosophy
- **CISC** minimizes the **semantic gap** between high-level languages and machine code. It provides many addressing modes and powerful instructions so that compilers (and hand-coders) can express complex operations compactly.
- **RISC** simplifies the instruction set so that the **hardware** can execute each instruction in a single cycle, using a **load/store** model. Complexity is pushed to the **compiler**.

### Step-by-Step Pipeline-Friendly Design
1. **Fixed-length instructions** → simplifies instruction fetch and decode (e.g., MIPS uses 32-bit instructions).
2. **Load/Store architecture** → memory accesses are isolated to specific `LW`/`SW` instructions, allowing the pipeline to schedule them efficiently.
3. **Large register file** (typically **32 registers** in MIPS) → reduces memory traffic.
4. **Hard-wired control unit** → faster than microprogrammed CISC control.
5. **Pipelining-friendly** → fixed-format instructions allow overlapping fetch-decode-execute stages.

### KTU Cheat Sheet — CISC vs RISC

| Feature | CISC | RISC |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction size | Variable (1–15 bytes in x86) | **Fixed (typically 32 bits)** |
| Instructions per program | **Fewer** | More |
| Cycles per instruction | **Multiple** (varies) | **One (ideally)** |
| Memory access | Any instruction can access memory | Only `LOAD` / `STORE` |
| Addressing modes | **Many** (10+) | **Few** (3–5) |
| Register count | Few (8 in x86) | **Many (32–128)** |
| Control unit | Microprogrammed | **Hardwired** |
| Pipelining | Difficult | **Easy** |
| Code size | **Compact** | Larger |
| Compiler complexity | Simpler compiler | Complex compiler |
| Clock speed | Generally lower | **Higher** |
| Examples | x86, VAX, 68k | MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, SPARC |
| Power efficiency | Lower | **Higher** (ideal for mobile) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern trend:** Modern x86 CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen) are **CISC at the ISA level** but **RISC at the microarchitecture level**. They decode complex x86 instructions into **RISC-like micro-operations (μops)** internally, then execute them in a RISC-style pipeline.

---

## 2.3 RISC Architecture — Deep Analysis (MIPS Reference)

### MIPS Register Set
MIPS has **32 general-purpose registers**, conventionally named `$0` to `$31`, with symbolic aliases:

| Register | Alias | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `$0` | `$zero` | Constant **0** (writes ignored) |
| `$1` | `$at` | Reserved for assembler |
| `$2`, `$3` | `$v0`, `$v1` | Return values |
| `$4`–`$7` | `$a0`–`$a3` | Arguments |
| `$8`–`$15` | `$t0`–`$t7` | Temporaries (caller-saved) |
| `$16`–`$23` | `$s0`–`s7` | Saved (callee-saved) |
| `$24`, `$25` | `$t8`, `$t9` | More temporaries |
| `$28` | `$gp` | Global pointer |
| `$29` | `$sp` | Stack pointer |
| `$30` | `$fp` | Frame pointer |
| `$31` | `$ra` | Return address |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caller-saved** registers must be saved by the **caller** before a function call. **Callee-saved** registers must be saved by the **callee** if used.

### MIPS Instruction Format (Three Types)
1. **R-type** (Register): `opcode(6) | rs(5) | rt(5) | rd(5) | shamt(5) | funct(6)`
2. **I-type** (Immediate): `opcode(6) | rs(5) | rt(5) | immediate(16)`
3. **J-type** (Jump): `opcode(6) | address(26)`

Total bits per instruction = **32 bits** (fixed length).

### KTU Cheat Sheet — Common MIPS Instructions

| Mnemonic | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `add rd, rs, rt` | R | $rd \leftarrow rs + rt$ |
| `sub rd, rs, rt` | R | $rd \leftarrow rs - rt$ |
| `and rd, rs, rt` | R | $rd \leftarrow rs \;\&\; rt$ |
| `or rd, rs, rt` | R | $rd \leftarrow rs \;\vert\; rt$ |
| `sll rd, rt, shamt` | R | $rd \leftarrow rt \ll shamt$ |
| `addi rt, rs, imm` | I | $rt \leftarrow rs + \text{sign-extend}(imm)$ |
| `lw rt, offset(rs)` | I | $rt \leftarrow \text{Memory}[rs + offset]$ |
| `sw rt, offset(rs)` | I | $\text{Memory}[rs + offset] \leftarrow rt$ |
| `beq rs, rt, label` | I | if $rs = rt$ then branch |
| `bne rs, rt, label` | I | if $rs \neq rt$ then branch |
| `j label` | J | Unconditional jump (26-bit address) |
| `jal label` | J | Jump and link (\$ra \leftarrow PC+4) |
| `jr rs` | R | Jump to register (typically `$ra`) |

---

## 2.4 Assembler Directives — Deep Analysis

| Directive | Purpose | Generates Machine Code? |
|---|---|---|
| `.data` | Marks the start of the initialized data section | No |
| `.text` | Marks the start of the code section | No |
| `.word 10` | Allocates and initializes a 32-bit word `10` | Yes (4 bytes in `.data`) |
| `.half 10` | Allocates a 16-bit halfword | Yes (2 bytes) |
| `.byte 65` | Allocates a single byte | Yes (1 byte) |
| `.asciiz "Hello"` | Allocates null-terminated ASCII string | Yes (N+1 bytes) |
| `.space N` | Allocates N uninitialized bytes | Yes (N bytes of zeros) |
| `.align n` | Aligns next data to $2^n$ byte boundary | Padding bytes |
| `.globl main` | Exports `main` as a global label | No |
| `.eqv CONST, 5` | Symbolic constant (assembly-time) | No |

### The Assembling Process
1. **Lexical Analysis** — The assembler scans the source file, identifying labels, mnemonics, operands, and directives.
2. **Symbol Table Construction** — Labels and their addresses are recorded in a symbol table.
3. **Instruction Encoding** — Each R/I/J-type instruction is encoded into its 32-bit binary representation.
4. **Address Resolution** — Branch/jump targets (labels) are replaced by their resolved addresses.
5. **Object File Generation** — A `.o` (object) file is produced, containing machine code, data, and relocation information.
6. **Linking** — The linker (`ld`) combines multiple `.o` files and resolves external symbol references to produce the final executable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **two-pass assembler** is typically used: Pass 1 records all label addresses; Pass 2 generates code with resolved addresses. This resolves **forward references** (e.g., a branch to a label defined later in the file).

---

## 2.5 Programming Constructs — Deep Analysis

### Branching Mechanism in MIPS
Branches use the **PC-relative addressing** mode. The 16-bit signed offset is multiplied by 4 (since instructions are 4 bytes aligned) and added to PC+4.

$$\text{Branch Target} = (PC + 4) + (\text{sign-extend}(imm) \times 4)$$

### Conditional Statements
MIPS lacks direct `if-else` instructions. They are implemented as:

```
if (i == j)   →  beq $t0, $t1, LABEL
else branch   →  bne $t0, $t1, LABEL_ELSE
```

> [!NOTE]
> Pseudo-instructions like `bgt`, `blt`, `bge`, `ble` are translated by the assembler into two instructions: a `slt`/`slti` followed by a `beq`/`bne`.

### Loops Implementation

**While Loop Pattern:**
```
loop:  bge  $t0, $t1, exit     # while (i < j)
       ...  body ...
       addi $t0, $t0, 1
       j    loop
exit:  ...
```

**For Loop Pattern:** Same as while, with initialization and update steps.

### Arrays
Array `A` with `n` elements, base address in `$gp`:
- Address of `A[i]` = `base + i × 4` (for word-sized elements)
- Implemented as: `lw $t0, offset($gp)` where `offset = i × 4`

$$\text{Address}(A[i]) = \text{Base} + i \times \text{sizeof(element)}$$

### Function Calls — The Call/Return Protocol

1. **Caller** places arguments in `$a0`–`$a3` (for the first 4).
2. **Caller** executes `jal function` → `$ra ← PC+4`, jump to `function`.
3. **Callee** (prologue) pushes `$ra` and saved registers onto the stack.
4. **Callee** executes its body, places return value in `$v0`.
5. **Callee** (epilogue) restores saved registers, executes `jr $ra` to return.
6. **Caller** receives result in `$v0`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **stack** grows downward in MIPS (decreasing addresses). `$sp` is decremented before storing. The frame pointer `$fp` ($30) marks the start of the current stack frame for local variable access.

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Addressing

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Array element address | $A + i \times s$ |
| Byte offset for word array | $i \times 4$ |
| Byte offset for halfword array | $i \times 2$ |
| Branch target | $(PC+4) + \text{offset} \times 4$ |
| Jump target | $(PC+4)_{31..28} \,\vert\vert\, (addr \times 4)$ |
| Stack push address | $sp - 4$ |
| Stack pop address | $sp + 4$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Endianness — Worked Numerical Example

### Problem
Store the 32-bit integer $N = \text{0x12C3A8F4}$ in memory starting at address `0x2000`. Show how the bytes are laid out in both **Big-Endian** and **Little-Endian** formats.

### Step 1: Decompose into 4 bytes
The 32-bit value `0x12C3A8F4` is split into four 8-bit bytes:

$$\text{Byte}_3 = \text{MSB} = 0\text{x}12$$
$$\text{Byte}_2 = 0\text{xC3}$$
$$\text{Byte}_1 = 0\text{x}A8$$
$$\text{Byte}_0 = \text{LSB} = 0\text{x}F4$$

> **Reasoning:** The most significant byte contains the highest place value ($2^{24}$ in a 32-bit word), while the LSB contains the lowest place value ($2^{0} = 1$).

### Step 2: Big-Endian Layout (MSB at lowest address)

| Address | 0x2000 | 0x2001 | 0x2002 | 0x2003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0x12 | 0xC3 | 0xA8 | 0xF4 |
| Position | MSB | — | — | LSB |

> **Reasoning:** Big-Endian treats memory as a "left-to-right" reading. The most significant piece of information is placed at the lowest address, which is the natural reading order for humans.

### Step 3: Little-Endian Layout (LSB at lowest address)

| Address | 0x2000 | 0x2001 | 0x2002 | 0x2003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0xF4 | 0xA8 | 0xC3 | 0x12 |
| Position | LSB | — | — | MSB |

> **Reasoning:** Little-Endian treats memory as a "right-to-left" reading. The least significant byte is placed at the lowest address, which is the natural order for arithmetic operations (e.g., incrementing just modifies the lowest byte).

### Step 4: Mathematical Verification
Verify by summing each byte with its positional weight for both layouts:

$$N_{BE} = (0\text{x}12 \times 2^{24}) + (0\text{xC3} \times 2^{16}) + (0\text{x}A8 \times 2^{8}) + (0\text{x}F4 \times 2^{0})$$

$$N_{BE} = (18 \times 16{,}777{,}216) + (195 \times 65{,}536) + (168 \times 256) + (244 \times 1)$$

$$N_{BE} = 301{,}989{,}888 + 12{,}779{,}520 + 43{,}008 + 244$$

$$N_{BE} = 314{,}812{,}660 = 0\text{x}12\text{C}3\text{A}8\text{F}4 \;\checkmark$$

For Little-Endian, the same formula is applied but with addresses reversed:

$$N_{LE} = (0\text{x}F4 \times 2^{24}) + (0\text{x}A8 \times 2^{16}) + (0\text{x}C3 \times 2^{8}) + (0\text{x}12 \times 2^{0})$$

This produces a different numerical value (interpreting bytes in reverse), which is why the **CPU's load instruction** must know the endianness to reassemble bytes correctly into a register.

---

## 3.2 CISC vs RISC — Quantitative Comparison Derivation

### Problem
A CISC processor has 120 distinct instructions with an average of 4 cycles per instruction. A RISC processor has 60 distinct instructions, executes each in 1 cycle, but requires 1.4× more instructions to express the same program. Compare performance for a program that takes $P$ CISC instructions.

### Step 1: Calculate CISC total cycles

$$\text{Cycles}_{CISC} = P \times 4 = 4P \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 2: Calculate RISC total cycles
The RISC processor needs $1.4P$ instructions at 1 cycle each:

$$\text{Cycles}_{RISC} = 1.4P \times 1 = 1.4P \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 3: Compute speedup ratio

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{\text{Cycles}_{CISC}}{\text{Cycles}_{RISC}} = \frac{4P}{1.4P} = \frac{4}{1.4} = 2.857$$

### Step 4: Conclusion
**The RISC processor is ~2.86× faster** (in raw cycle count) for this workload, *assuming the same clock frequency*. In practice, RISC CPUs can also be clocked much higher because of the simpler pipeline, leading to even larger real-world speedups.

> **Reasoning:** RISC's advantage comes from the **single-cycle-per-instruction** design, which enables deeper pipelining, higher clock frequencies, and more efficient hardware (less silicon spent on instruction decoding).

---

## 3.3 MIPS Program Analysis — Branch Target Derivation

### Problem
A MIPS branch instruction is at address `0x00400010` and contains a 16-bit signed offset field of value `0xFFFA` (i.e., `-6` in two's complement). Compute the branch target address.

### Step 1: Identify the PC value
The PC for the branch is the address of the branch instruction itself. The "next PC" is:

$$PC_{next} = PC + 4 = 0\text{x}00400010 + 4 = 0\text{x}00400014$$

### Step 2: Sign-extend the 16-bit offset to 32 bits
The offset `0xFFFA` is sign-extended (since bit 15 is `1`, the value is negative):

$$\text{Offset}_{32} = 0\text{xFFFFFFFA} = -6 \text{ (in decimal)}$$

### Step 3: Multiply by 4 (word-alignment)
MIPS branches shift the offset left by 2 bits (multiply by 4) because all instructions are 4 bytes long:

$$\text{Shifted Offset} = -6 \times 4 = -24 = 0\text{xFFFFFFE8}$$

### Step 4: Compute the target address

$$\text{Target} = PC_{next} + \text{Shifted Offset}$$

$$\text{Target} = 0\text{x}00400014 + 0\text{xFFFFFFE8}$$

Performing 32-bit addition with wrap-around:

$$0\text{x}00400014 + 0\text{xFFFFFFE8} = 0\text{x}003FFFFC$$

### Step 5: Verify the branch direction
A negative offset causes a **backward branch** (toward lower addresses), confirming this is a loop-back branch. The branch target `0x003FFFFC` is **24 bytes before** the branch instruction, equivalent to **6 instructions back** — a typical loop iteration.

> **Reasoning:** MIPS uses **PC-relative branching** for `beq`/`bne` because the 16-bit offset, when word-shifted, can reach $\pm 2^{17}$ bytes (about $\pm 128$ KB of code) — sufficient for nearly all functions and loops. For longer jumps, the assembler automatically inserts a `j` (jump) instruction.

---

## 3.4 MIPS Function Call — Stack Frame Size Calculation

### Problem
A function uses 3 local 32-bit integer variables, saves 2 callee-saved registers (`$s0`, `$s1`), and pushes `$ra` on the stack. Calculate the total stack frame size.

### Step 1: Account for `$ra` push
The return address is saved to allow `jr $ra` to return correctly:

$$\text{Size}_{ra} = 4 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 2: Account for callee-saved registers
Two 32-bit registers are saved:

$$\text{Size}_{saved} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 3: Account for local variables
Three 32-bit integer local variables:

$$\text{Size}_{locals} = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 4: Compute the total stack frame

$$\text{Total Frame} = \text{Size}_{ra} + \text{Size}_{saved} + \text{Size}_{locals}$$

$$\text{Total Frame} = 4 + 8 + 12 = 24 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 5: The prologue pseudo-code

```
addi $sp, $sp, -24     # Grow stack: $sp -= 24
sw   $ra, 20($sp)      # Save return address at offset 20
sw   $s0, 16($sp)      # Save $s0 at offset 16
sw   $s1, 12($sp)      # Save $s1 at offset 12
# Local variables at offsets 8, 4, 0 ($sp)
```

> **Reasoning:** MIPS convention places `$ra` at the **highest offset** of the saved area (closest to the caller's frame) so that register save/restore operations are symmetric. Locals are placed at the **lowest offsets** (closest to the new `$sp`).

---

## 3.5 Array Indexing — Address Calculation Walkthrough

### Problem
An integer array `A` has 10 elements, base address = `0x10000000`, element size = 4 bytes. Find the memory address of `A[7]`.

### Step 1: Apply the address formula

$$\text{Address}(A[i]) = \text{Base} + i \times \text{sizeof(element)}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the values

$$\text{Address}(A[7]) = 0\text{x}10000000 + 7 \times 4$$

$$7 \times 4 = 28 = 0\text{x}1C$$

### Step 3: Final address

$$\text{Address}(A[7]) = 0\text{x}10000000 + 0\text{x}1C = 0\text{x}1000001C$$

### Step 4: Corresponding MIPS code

```
la   $t0, A             # Load base address of A
li   $t1, 7             # Index i = 7
sll  $t1, $t1, 2        # $t1 = 7 * 4 = 28 (byte offset)
add  $t2, $t0, $t1      # $t2 = address of A[7]
lw   $t3, 0($t2)        # Load A[7] into $t3
```

> **Reasoning:** The `sll $t1, $t1, 2` instruction performs a left shift by 2 bits, which is equivalent to multiplying by $2^2 = 4$. This is the **fastest** way to compute the byte offset, taking only 1 cycle, compared to 4 cycles for a multiplication.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Endianness Impact | CISC vs RISC Impact | Programming Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | No difference on same-arch systems | RISC enables higher clock & pipelining | Loop unrolling & inlining reduce overhead |
| **Memory Usage** | Identical (same number of bytes) | CISC code is more compact | Arrays use contiguous allocation |
| **Portability** | Requires byte-swap across systems | ISA-specific code | Use pseudo-instructions for clarity |
| **Compiler Role** | N/A | RISC requires sophisticated compiler | Compiler manages register allocation |
| **Hardware Cost** | Trivial (wiring only) | CISC needs more transistors | Function calls need stack memory |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Endianness — Byte Storage Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BE["BIG ENDIAN — 0x12345678"]
        BE_A["Address 0x1000<br/>Byte 0x12<br/>MSB"]
        BE_B["Address 0x1001<br/>Byte 0x34"]
        BE_C["Address 0x1002<br/>Byte 0x56"]
        BE_D["Address 0x1003<br/>Byte 0x78<br/>LSB"]
    end

    subgraph LE["LITTLE ENDIAN — 0x12345678"]
        LE_A["Address 0x1000<br/>Byte 0x78<br/>LSB"]
        LE_B["Address 0x1001<br/>Byte 0x56"]
        LE_C["Address 0x1002<br/>Byte 0x34"]
        LE_D["Address 0x1003<br/>Byte 0x12<br/>MSB"]
    end

    BE_A --> BE_B --> BE_C --> BE_D
    LE_A --> LE_B --> LE_C --> LE_D
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Big-Endian reads top-to-bottom (MSB first), matching the natural left-to-right order of hexadecimal notation. Little-Endian reads top-to-bottom (LSB first), the reverse order.

---

## 4.2 CISC vs RISC — Architectural Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CISC["CISC PROCESSOR"]
        C1["Complex<br/>Instruction"]
        C2["Microcode<br/>ROM"]
        C3["Variable<br/>Decode"]
        C4["Multi-Cycle<br/>Execution"]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    end

    subgraph RISC["RISC PROCESSOR"]
        R1["Simple<br/>Instruction"]
        R2["Hardwired<br/>Control"]
        R3["Fixed<br/>Decode"]
        R4["Single-Cycle<br/>Pipeline"]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    end

    COMP["Program<br/>Source"] -->|Compiler| CISC
    COMP -->|Compiler| RISC
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Both processors accept the same logical program source. The CISC path involves microcode translation and variable execution, while the RISC path uses direct hardware control and pipelining for speed.

---

## 4.3 MIPS Program Flow — Control Structure

```mermaid
graph TB
    START(["Program Start"]) --> INIT["Initialization<br/>li, la, addi"]
    INIT --> COND{"Condition<br/>beq or bne"}
    COND -->|True| BODY["Execute Body<br/>arithmetic, lw, sw"]
    COND -->|False| EXIT(["Exit Block"])
    BODY --> UPDATE["Update<br/>addi, add"]
    UPDATE --> COND
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a generic conditional loop. The condition is checked first (top-tested loop, like a `while` or `for`). The body executes only if the condition is true, then control returns to the condition.

---

## 4.4 MIPS Function Call — Stack Frame Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TB
    CALLER_A["Caller Function"] -->|"arg in $a0-$a3"| JAL["jal function<br/>$ra = PC+4"]
    JAL --> PROLOGUE["Prologue<br/>addi $sp, $sp, -N<br/>sw $ra, offset<br/>sw $s0..$s7"]
    PROLOGUE --> BODY["Function Body<br/>use $t0..$t9<br/>access locals via $sp"]
    BODY --> EPILOGUE["Epilogue<br/>lw $s0..$s7<br/>lw $ra<br/>addi $sp, $sp, +N"]
    EPILOGUE --> JR["jr $ra<br/>return to caller"]
    JR -->|"result in $v0"| CALLER_B["Caller Resumes"]
    CALLER_A --> CALLER_B
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The stack frame is allocated in the **prologue** (function entry), used in the body, and deallocated in the **epilogue** (function exit). The `$ra` register is preserved across the call, enabling the return to the correct instruction in the caller.

---

## 4.5 Assembling Process — Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[".s Assembly<br/>Source File"] --> PASS1["Pass 1<br/>Build Symbol Table<br/>Resolve Labels"]
    PASS1 --> PASS2["Pass 2<br/>Encode Instructions<br/>Resolve Addresses"]
    PASS2 --> OBJ[".o Object File<br/>Machine Code + Relocations"]
    OBJ --> LINK["Linker<br/>ld combines .o files"]
    LINK --> EXEC["Executable<br/>a.out / program"]
    SRC -.->|Disassembler| REVERSE["Reverse Engineering<br/>objdump -d"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The two-pass assembler first builds the symbol table (pass 1), then encodes all instructions with resolved addresses (pass 2). The linker combines object files and resolves external symbol references to produce the final executable.

---

## 4.6 Block Diagram — RISC Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    IF["IF<br/>Instruction<br/>Fetch"] --> ID["ID<br/>Instruction<br/>Decode"]
    ID --> EX["EX<br/>Execute<br/>ALU"]
    EX --> MEM["MEM<br/>Memory<br/>Access"]
    MEM --> WB["WB<br/>Write<br/>Back"]
    IF -.->|stage 1| P1["Cycle 1"]
    ID -.->|stage 2| P2["Cycle 2"]
    EX -.->|stage 3| P3["Cycle 3"]
    MEM -.->|stage 4| P4["Cycle 4"]
    WB -.->|stage 5| P5["Cycle 5"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A classic 5-stage RISC pipeline. Each stage performs one sub-task, and after a warm-up of 5 cycles, one instruction completes every cycle — the hallmark of RISC efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define Endianness. Differentiate between Big-Endian and Little-Endian with an example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Endianness is the byte-ordering convention used by a computer system to store multi-byte numerical values in memory. It specifies whether the most significant byte (MSB) or the least significant byte (LSB) is stored at the lowest memory address.

**Big-Endian (1 mark):** Stores the **MSB at the lowest address**. It is intuitive, matching the human reading order. Example: the 32-bit value `0x12345678` is stored as `0x12, 0x34, 0x56, 0x78` at addresses `0x1000, 0x1001, 0x1002, 0x1003`. Used by SPARC, PowerPC, and network protocols (TCP/IP).

**Little-Endian (1 mark):** Stores the **LSB at the lowest address**. It is convenient for arithmetic (increments touch only the lowest byte). The same value `0x12345678` is stored as `0x78, 0x56, 0x34, 0x12` at addresses `0x1000, 0x1001, 0x1002, 0x1003`. Used by Intel x86, x86-64, and ARM (default).

---

### Question 2: List any six key differences between CISC and RISC architectures. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular format preferred):**

| S.No. | CISC | RISC |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Variable-length instructions (1–15 bytes) | Fixed-length instructions (typically 32 bits) |
| 2 | Multiple cycles per instruction | Single cycle per instruction (ideally) |
| 3 | Memory operands allowed in any instruction | Load/Store architecture (only `lw`/`sw` access memory) |
| 4 | Many addressing modes (10+) | Few addressing modes (3–5) |
| 5 | Microprogrammed control unit | Hardwired control unit |
| 6 | Smaller code size | Larger code size, but highly pipelined |
| 7 | Examples: x86, VAX, Motorola 68k | Examples: MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, SPARC |

(Any **six** differences from the above table earn full 3 marks.)

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the CISC and RISC architectural philosophies in detail. Compare them across at least 8 parameters.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**CISC Philosophy (2 marks):**
The CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) design philosophy emerged in the 1970s when memory was expensive and slow. The goal was to **reduce the semantic gap** between high-level language constructs and machine instructions. By providing powerful, multi-operation instructions (e.g., a single instruction that performs a memory fetch, an addition, and a memory store), CISC aimed to:
- Minimize program size (fewer instructions needed)
- Reduce memory bandwidth requirements
- Simplify compiler design
- Make hand-coding assembly easier

CISC processors typically have **variable-length instructions**, **many addressing modes**, and a **microprogrammed control unit** (the microcode stores the sequence of micro-operations for each complex instruction).

**RISC Philosophy (2 marks):**
The RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) design philosophy, pioneered by John Cocke (IBM) and popularized by MIPS and ARM in the 1980s, argues that:
- A small set of simple instructions executed in a single cycle enables **deep pipelining** and **higher clock frequencies**
- The complexity should be moved from hardware to the **compiler**, which can optimize a regular instruction set better
- **Load/Store architecture** isolates memory access, simplifying pipelining
- **Large register files** (32+ registers) reduce memory traffic
- **Fixed-length instructions** simplify instruction fetch and decode

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CISC | RISC |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction set size | Large (100–250+ instructions) | Small (≤128 instructions) |
| Instruction length | Variable | Fixed (32 bits) |
| Execution time | Multi-cycle | Single cycle (pipelined) |
| Memory access | Any instruction | Only `lw`/`sw` |
| Addressing modes | Many (8–24) | Few (3–5) |
| Register count | Few (8–16) | Many (32–128) |
| Control unit | Microprogrammed | Hardwired |
| Pipelining | Hard to implement | Natural fit |
| Code density | High | Lower |
| Compiler complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Power efficiency | Lower | Higher |
| Examples | x86, x86-64, VAX, 68k | MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, SPARC |

**Conclusion:** Modern processors blend both philosophies — x86 CPUs use RISC-like micro-ops internally, and RISC-V extends with optional CISC-style compressed instructions.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the MIPS assembly language structure. Explain any 8 common assembler directives with their syntax and purpose.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**MIPS Assembly Structure (2 marks):**
A MIPS assembly program consists of:
- **Labels** — symbolic names for memory addresses (e.g., `main:`, `loop:`)
- **Mnemonics** — instruction names (e.g., `add`, `lw`)
- **Operands** — registers, immediate values, labels
- **Comments** — start with `#` and extend to end of line
- **Directives** — assembler instructions like `.data`, `.text`

Example skeleton:
```asm
.data
msg: .asciiz "Hello, World!"

.text
.globl main
main:
    li   $v0, 4
    la   $a0, msg
    syscall
    li   $v0, 10
    syscall
```

**Eight Common Assembler Directives (5 marks):**

1. **`.data`** — Marks the beginning of the data section where initialized variables are declared. No machine code is generated; it just changes the assembler's section.

2. **`.text`** — Marks the beginning of the code (text) section containing instructions.

3. **`.word value`** — Allocates a 32-bit word in memory and initializes it with `value`. Generates 4 bytes. Example: `count: .word 100`

4. **`.byte value`** — Allocates a single byte. Example: `flag: .byte 1`

5. **`.half value`** — Allocates a 16-bit (2-byte) halfword. Example: `port: .half 8080`

6. **`.asciiz "string"`** — Allocates a null-terminated ASCII string. The string is stored byte-by-byte with a `0` byte appended. Example: `name: .asciiz "KTU"`

7. **`.space N`** — Allocates `N` bytes of uninitialized (zero) memory. Useful for buffers and arrays whose contents are filled at runtime. Example: `buffer: .space 256`

8. **`.globl symbol`** — Exports `symbol` as a global label, making it visible to the linker and external modules. The entry point `main` is typically declared global.

9. **`.align n`** — Aligns the next data item to a $2^n$-byte boundary. Example: `.align 2` aligns to a 4-byte (word) boundary.

10. **`.eqv SYMBOL, value`** — Defines a symbolic constant for assembly-time substitution. Example: `.eqv BUFFER_SIZE, 1024` allows `BUFFER_SIZE` to be used in place of `1024`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **assembler directives** (instructions to the assembler) with **machine instructions** (executed by the CPU). Directives do **not** appear in the final machine code — they only affect the assembly process. For example, `.word 100` allocates memory but does not perform a "load" at runtime.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of Big-Endian and Little-Endian byte ordering. Given the 32-bit hexadecimal number `0x9F3A12BC`, show the memory layout in both formats starting at address `0x4000`.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
- **Big-Endian:** MSB at the lowest address. The memory reads "in order" from left to right as humans write numbers. It is used in network protocols (Network Byte Order) and architectures like SPARC and PowerPC.
- **Little-Endian:** LSB at the lowest address. The memory reads "in reverse" — the smallest byte comes first. It is used in Intel x86, x86-64, and is the default for ARM. It is favored for arithmetic because incrementing a multi-byte integer only modifies the byte at the lowest address.

**Byte Decomposition (1 mark):**
For the 32-bit value `0x9F3A12BC`:
- Byte 3 (MSB) = `0x9F`
- Byte 2 = `0x3A`
- Byte 1 = `0x12`
- Byte 0 (LSB) = `0xBC`

**Big-Endian Layout (1.5 marks):**

| Address | 0x4000 | 0x4001 | 0x4002 | 0x4003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0x9F | 0x3A | 0x12 | 0xBC |
| Role | MSB | — | — | LSB |

**Little-Endian Layout (1.5 marks):**

| Address | 0x4000 | 0x4001 | 0x4002 | 0x4003 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Byte | 0xBC | 0x12 | 0x3A | 0x9F |
| Role | LSB | — | — | MSB |

**Conclusion:** Both layouts store the same four bytes; the only difference is the *order* in which the bytes are arranged in memory. The CPU's `lw` instruction reassembles the bytes into the correct register value based on the architecture's endianness.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the MIPS function call protocol. Describe the role of `$ra`, `$sp`, and the stack frame. Write a MIPS function that computes `n!` (factorial) using recursion.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Function Call Protocol (3 marks):**
1. **Argument Passing:** The caller places up to 4 arguments in `$a0`–`$a3`. Additional arguments are pushed on the stack.
2. **Call Instruction:** The caller executes `jal factorial` — this stores `PC+4` in `$ra` (return address) and jumps to `factorial`.
3. **Stack Frame Setup (Prologue):** The callee allocates a new stack frame:
   - Decrements `$sp` to make room for local variables and saved registers
   - Saves `$ra` (so it can return correctly if it makes further calls)
   - Saves any callee-saved registers (`$s0`–`$s7`) it intends to use
4. **Body Execution:** The function performs its computation, accessing local variables via offsets from `$sp` (or `$fp`).
5. **Return Value:** The result is placed in `$v0` (and `$v1` for 64-bit results).
6. **Stack Frame Teardown (Epilogue):** The callee restores saved registers, loads `$ra` from the stack, increments `$sp`, and executes `jr $ra` to return.
7. **Resumption:** The caller reads the return value from `$v0`.

**Role of Key Registers (1.5 marks):**
- **`$ra` ($31):** Return Address register — stores the address to resume execution at after the callee returns.
- **`$sp` ($29):** Stack Pointer — points to the **top** of the stack. Grows downward (decremented on push, incremented on pop).
- **Stack Frame:** A region of the stack allocated to a single function call, containing the saved `$ra`, saved callee-saved registers, and local variables.

**MIPS Factorial Code (2.5 marks):**

```asm
.data
msg: .asciiz "Enter n: "

.text
.globl main

main:
    # Print prompt
    li   $v0, 4
    la   $a0, msg
    syscall

    # Read integer
    li   $v0, 5
    syscall
    move $a0, $v0          # Pass n in $a0

    # Call factorial
    jal  factorial

    # Print result
    move $a0, $v0
    li   $v0, 1
    syscall

    # Exit
    li   $v0, 10
    syscall

# Recursive factorial function: $a0 = n, returns n! in $v0
factorial:
    addi $sp, $sp, -8      # Prologue: allocate 8 bytes
    sw   $ra, 4($sp)       # Save return address
    sw   $a0, 0($sp)       # Save n (so $a0 can be modified)

    # Base case: if n <= 1, return 1
    slti $t0, $a0, 2       # $t0 = 1 if n < 2
    beq  $t0, $zero, recurse
    li   $v0, 1             # Return 1
    addi $sp, $sp, 8        # Epilogue: deallocate frame
    jr   $ra                # Return

recurse:
    addi $a0, $a0, -1      # $a0 = n - 1
    jal  factorial          # Recursive call: (n-1)!
    lw   $a0, 0($sp)        # Restore original n
    lw   $ra, 4($sp)        # Restore return address
    addi $sp, $sp, 8        # Deallocate frame
    mult $a0, $v0           # $LO = n * (n-1)!
    mflo $v0                # $v0 = n * (n-1)! = n!
    jr   $ra                # Return
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes in Function Call Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to save `$ra`** — causes the function to return to the wrong address after a recursive call.
> 2. **Forgetting to restore `$sp`** — corrupts the caller's stack and crashes on return.
> 3. **Confusing caller-saved vs callee-saved registers** — using `$s0`–`$s7` without saving them destroys the caller's data.
> 4. **Wrong base case in factorial** — must explicitly check `n <= 1`, not just `n == 0` or `n == 1`.
> 5. **Mixing up `$sp` and `$fp`** — `$fp` is the frame pointer (a stable reference), while `$sp` moves with each push/pop. The convention is to set `$fp = $sp + frame_size` at function entry.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### Endianness
- **Big-Endian:** MSB at lowest address (SPARC, PowerPC, Network Order).
- **Little-Endian:** LSB at lowest address (x86, x86-64, ARM default).
- Endianness is **byte ordering**, not bit ordering. The bit ordering within each byte is always MSB-first.
- Use `htonl`/`ntohl` (host-to-network long / network-to-host long) for cross-platform networking.
- BMP, JPEG, ZIP, and PDF files have **fixed endianness** — reading them on the wrong system without byte-swapping corrupts data.

### CISC vs RISC
- **CISC** = Complex, variable-length, microprogrammed, fewer registers.
- **RISC** = Simple, fixed-length, hardwired, many registers, load/store.
- **Modern CPUs are hybrids** — x86 decodes to RISC-like micro-ops internally.
- RISC enables **deep pipelining** and **higher clock rates**; CISC minimizes code size.

### RISC (MIPS) Architecture
- **32 general-purpose registers** (`$0` to `$31`); `$0` is always zero.
- **32-bit fixed-length instructions** in three formats: R, I, J.
- **Load/Store architecture** — only `lw` and `sw` access memory.
- **Branch delay slots** historically existed; modern MIPS eliminates them.

### Assembly Language & Directives
- Directives are **not machine instructions** — they instruct the assembler.
- `.data` / `.text` / `.word` / `.byte` / `.asciiz` / `.space` / `.globl` / `.align` are the most common MIPS directives.
- **Two-pass assembly** resolves forward references (labels used before definition).
- Assembler output is an **object file (`.o`)**; the **linker** produces the executable.

### Programming Concepts
- **Branches** use **PC-relative addressing**: target = `(PC+4) + (sign_extend(offset) × 4)`.
- **Conditional statements** use `beq`/`bne` (and pseudo-instructions like `bgt`/`blt`).
- **Loops** combine a branch (top-test or bottom-test) with an update instruction.
- **Array addressing:** `Address(A[i]) = Base + i × sizeof(element)`.
- **Function calls** use `jal`/`jr $ra`, with the stack frame managed via `$sp`.
- **Recursion** requires saving `$ra` and local arguments to enable correct return after the recursive call.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Execution Cycle, Machine Language - Instructions, Addressing Modes, Stored Program Concept, Evolution of the RISC Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Execution Cycle, Machine Language - Instructions, Addressing Modes, Stored Program Concept, Evolution of the RISC Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Instruction Execution Cycle — Formal Definition

The **Instruction Execution Cycle** (also called the *Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle*) is the fundamental operational loop performed by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) of a computer. It is the systematic, sequential procedure by which a processor retrieves a machine instruction from main memory, decodes it to determine the required operation, and executes it by activating the appropriate functional units (ALU, registers, memory, I/O). This cycle repeats continuously from computer power-on until power-off, making it the **atomic heartbeat of all computation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Every KTU 2024 Scheme question on this topic is expected to mention the *three classical phases*: **Fetch → Decode → Execute**, with the modern refinement of *Memory Access* and *Write-Back* as distinct sub-phases.

### 1.2 Machine Language Instructions — Formal Definition

A **Machine Language Instruction** is the lowest-level, hardware-executable representation of a command, encoded as a binary bit pattern (sequence of 0s and 1s) that the CPU's control unit can directly interpret. Each instruction is composed of two logical fields: an **Operation Code (Opcode)** that specifies the operation to be performed, and zero or more **Operand fields** that specify the data (or locations of data) on which the operation acts.

A **Machine Language Program** is a sequence of such instructions stored contiguously in memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (ISA):** The complete set of machine instructions that a particular processor can execute is called its **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**. The ISA is the *contract* between hardware and software.

### 1.3 Addressing Modes — Formal Definition

**Addressing Modes** are the various methods by which the location of an operand (the data to be operated on) is specified within a machine instruction. They provide flexible mechanisms for accessing data from registers, main memory, or embedded within the instruction itself, dramatically influencing the **code density**, **execution speed**, and **programming power** of an ISA.

### 1.4 Stored Program Concept — Formal Definition

The **Stored Program Concept**, formulated by **John von Neumann** and his colleagues in the *First Draft of a Report on the EDVAC* (1945), is the foundational architectural principle stating that **both the program instructions and the data on which they operate are stored in the same main memory** and are accessed identically. This unified storage allows the computer to treat instructions as data, enabling conditional branching, self-modifying code, and easy reprogramming — a radical departure from earlier *hard-wired* computers like ENIAC.

### 1.5 Evolution of RISC Architecture — Formal Definition

**RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** is a CPU design philosophy that emerged in the late 1970s and 1980s (Patterson at Berkeley, Hennessy at Stanford) emphasizing a **small, highly-optimized set of instructions**, **load/store architecture**, **fixed-length instruction formats**, **large register files**, and **pipelined execution** — all geared toward achieving **one instruction per cycle (IPC ≈ 1)** execution throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** RISC is not about "fewer instructions" in absolute terms, but about **simpler, more frequently used** instructions executed with **maximum hardware efficiency**.

---

### 1.6 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Chef and the Recipe Book"**
> 
> Imagine a **CPU** as a single chef working alone in a kitchen:
> - **Main Memory (RAM)** is the *pantry* holding both **recipe cards** (instructions) AND **ingredients** (data) on the same shelves — this is the **Stored Program Concept**.
> - **Fetch Phase**: The chef walks to the pantry, reads the next recipe card line (instruction).
> - **Decode Phase**: The chef reads the recipe and understands whether it says "chop onions" or "boil water" (decodes opcode).
> - **Execute Phase**: The chef performs the action using the specified ingredients (operands).
> - **Addressing Mode** = *How the recipe describes the ingredient*: "the onion in bowl #3" (direct), "the onion next to the tomato" (relative), or "the onion I'm holding" (immediate/implicit).
> - **RISC vs CISC**: A **RISC kitchen** has only 10 basic cooking moves (chop, boil, fry...) but does each *perfectly* in exactly **1 second**, using a *pipelined* team of chefs. A **CISC kitchen** has 200 complex specialty moves ("boeuf-bourguignon-in-one-step") that may take 5 seconds each.
> 
> This is exactly why modern smartphones (ARM — RISC) outperform older desktops (x86 — CISC) on a per-watt basis!

---

### 1.7 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** For ideal RISC = **1.0**; typical CISC = **2 to 15**.
- **Clock Period:** Modern CPUs operate at **3 to 5 GHz**, i.e., clock period ≈ **0.2 to 0.33 ns**.
- **Word Length:** Standard 32-bit or **64-bit** word; instruction length is typically **32 bits (fixed)** in RISC.
- **Memory Word:** **8 bits = 1 byte**; main memory addressable to byte granularity.
- **Register File Size:** RISC typically uses **32 general-purpose registers** (e.g., R0–R31 in MIPS/ARM).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three (and Five) Phases of the Instruction Cycle

The execution of every machine instruction can be decomposed into a logical pipeline of phases. The classic textbook (Mano, Hamacher) describes **3 phases**, while modern computer organization (Patterson & Hennessy) describes **5 phases**:

#### Phase-Wise Operational Breakdown

**Phase 1 — Fetch Instruction**
- The **Program Counter (PC)** holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched.
- Address is sent via the **address bus** to main memory.
- Memory places the instruction word on the **data bus**.
- Instruction is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
- **PC is incremented** (PC ← PC + 1, or +4 for byte-addressed 32-bit memory) to point to the next sequential instruction.

**Phase 2 — Decode Instruction**
- The **Control Unit (CU)** examines the **opcode** field in the IR.
- The CU generates the necessary **control signals** to coordinate ALU, registers, and data paths.
- Operand fields are analyzed to identify *where* the source/destination data resides (resolving the addressing mode).

**Phase 3 — Evaluate Address (Operand Fetch)**
- If the addressing mode requires calculation (e.g., indirect, indexed), the **Effective Address (EA)** is computed by the ALU.
- This phase is *sometimes* merged with Decode in the classic 3-phase model.

**Phase 4 — Fetch Operands (Read Memory)**
- Using the EA, the operand(s) are read from memory or registers.
- For register operands: immediate register file read.
- For memory operands: a memory read cycle is performed.

**Phase 5 — Execute + Write-Back**
- The ALU performs the operation (add, sub, AND, shift, etc.).
- The result is written back to the **destination register** or memory.
- For branch/jump instructions, the **PC is updated** with the branch target address.
- **Status flags** (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow) are updated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 5 Phases?** Modern pipelined RISC architectures *physically* separate these stages into pipeline registers (IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB) — making the 5-phase model architecturally necessary, not just pedagogical.

---

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Machine Instruction

A machine instruction bit pattern has this generic structure:

| **Field** | **Function** | **Typical Width (RISC)** |
|-----------|--------------|--------------------------|
| **Opcode** | Specifies the operation (ADD, LOAD, BRANCH...) | 6 bits |
| **Destination Reg (Rd)** | Where result is stored | 5 bits |
| **Source Reg 1 (Rs1)** | First operand | 5 bits |
| **Source Reg 2 (Rs2)** | Second operand (or part of immediate) | 5 bits |
| **Immediate / Offset / Shamt** | Constant value, address offset, or shift amount | Remaining bits (5–16 bits) |

> Total = 32 bits for fixed RISC (e.g., MIPS R-type, ARM A64).

For **variable-length CISC** (x86), instructions range from 1 to 15 bytes.

---

### 2.3 Complete Addressing Modes Catalog (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Addressing modes is one of the most frequently asked topics. Memorize the *Effective Address (EA) formula* for each mode.

| **#** | **Mode** | **Effective Address (EA) Calculation** | **Use Case** | **RISC/CISC** |
|-------|----------|----------------------------------------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Immediate** | Operand is the constant in the instruction | Load constants, small values | Both |
| 2 | **Direct (Absolute)** | EA = Address field of instruction | Access a fixed memory location | CISC-heavy |
| 3 | **Indirect** | EA = Content of address register (e.g., @R1 in 8086) | Pointer dereferencing | Both |
| 4 | **Register** | EA = Register itself (no memory access) | Fast ALU operations | Both |
| 5 | **Register Indirect** | EA = [Register] (memory at reg's address) | Pointer traversal, arrays | Both |
| 6 | **Displacement (Indexed)** | EA = [Register] + Offset constant | Array access with base | Both |
| 7 | **Relative** | EA = PC + Offset (signed) | Branch instructions | Both |
| 8 | **Indexed** | EA = Index_Reg + Base_Reg (or constant) | Loop arrays, tables | Both |
| 9 | **Auto-increment** | EA = [R]; R ← R + 1 | Sequential stack/array traversal | CISC |
| 10 | **Auto-decrement** | R ← R − 1; EA = [R] | Reverse traversal (stack push) | CISC |

**Legend:** `[R]` denotes "contents of memory at address held in R". Square brackets in formulas must use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

---

### 2.4 The Stored Program Concept — Detailed Anatomy

The **Von Neumann Architecture** (which embodies the Stored Program Concept) has these key properties:

1. **Single Memory Unit** holds both instructions and data.
2. **Single Bus System** (address + data) connects CPU and memory — the famous *Von Neumann Bottleneck*.
3. **Sequential Execution** by default (PC-driven).
4. **Self-Modification Capability** — code can rewrite itself.
5. **Linear Address Space** with no architectural separation.

**Contrast with Harvard Architecture:**

| **Property** | **Von Neumann** | **Harvard** |
|--------------|------------------|-------------|
| Instruction Memory | Shared with data | Separate |
| Data Memory | Shared with instructions | Separate |
| Buses | One (unified) | Two (separate I and D) |
| Bottleneck | Yes (shared bus) | No (parallel access) |
| Used in | General PCs, servers | DSPs, microcontrollers (8051, ARM Cortex-M) |

---

### 2.5 RISC vs CISC — High-Yield Comparison Table

| **Parameter** | **RISC** | **CISC** |
|---------------|----------|----------|
| Instruction Size | **Fixed (32-bit typical)** | Variable (1–15 bytes) |
| Instruction Count | Small (~50–150) | Large (~200–500) |
| Addressing Modes | Few (~5) | Many (~12–24) |
| Memory Access | **Load/Store only** | Operand-in-memory allowed |
| CPI | ≈ **1.0** | 2–15 |
| Pipeline | **Deep, easy** | Hard, complex |
| Clock Speed | Higher | Lower |
| Code Size | Larger | Compact |
| Examples | **MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, SPARC** | x86, x86-64, Motorola 68k, VAX |
| Compiler Dependency | High (compiler-optimized) | Low (hand-assembly-friendly) |
| Power Efficiency | **High** | Moderate |

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Performance Equations

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Application** |
|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| $T_{\text{CPU}} = IC \times CPI \times T_{\text{clock}}$ | Total CPU time for program execution | Performance benchmarking |
| $T_{\text{clock}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clock}}}$ | Clock period from frequency | Frequency-to-time conversion |
| $CPI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(IC_i \times CPI_i)}{IC_{\text{total}}}$ | Weighted average CPI | Mixed-instruction workloads |
| $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Millions of Instructions Per Second | Throughput metric |
| $MFLOPS = \dfrac{\text{Floating ops}}{T_{\text{exec}} \times 10^6}$ | Floating-point throughput | Scientific computing |
| $S_{\text{pipeline}} = \dfrac{n \times t}{k + n - 1}$ | Speedup of k-stage pipeline for n instructions | RISC pipelining analysis |
| $E_{\text{pipeline}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{pipeline}}}{k}$ | Pipeline efficiency | Hardware utilization |
| $EA_{\text{relative}} = PC + \text{offset}$ | Effective address for branch instructions | Control flow instructions |
| $EA_{\text{indexed}} = \text{Base\_Reg} + \text{Index\_Reg} \times \text{Scale} + \text{Disp}$ | x86-style complex addressing | CISC addressing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Warning:** I have intentionally used `\vert` and `\mid` in formulas like $\vert R1 \vert$ and $\vert\text{address}\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity. Do **not** type the vertical pipe `|` inside markdown tables.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Instruction Cycle Analysis** is the foundation of **CPU performance modeling** used by Intel, AMD, and Apple to design new chips.
- **Addressing Modes** directly impact **compiler optimization** (e.g., GCC's choice between `add immediate` vs `load from constant pool`).
- **Stored Program Concept** is what makes **firmware updates, JIT compilation, and operating systems** possible.
- **RISC Architecture** dominates **mobile and embedded** (ARM in 99% of smartphones, RISC-V rising in IoT) due to power efficiency.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Address for Indexed Addressing Mode

**Problem Statement:** Derive the Effective Address (EA) for the *Indexed* addressing mode given:
- Base Register $B$ contains address $\texttt{0x1000}$
- Index Register $X$ contains value $\texttt{0x04}$
- The instruction specifies a displacement $D = \texttt{0x10}$

**Step 1 — Identify the Indexing Formula**

For indexed addressing, the textbook definition is:

$$
EA = \text{Contents of Base Register} + \text{Contents of Index Register}
$$

In an extended form (used in x86), the formula becomes:

$$
EA = B + X \cdot S + D
$$

where $S$ is the scale factor (default $S = 1$).

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values**

$$
EA = \texttt{0x1000} + \texttt{0x04} \cdot 1 + \texttt{0x10}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the Multiplication**

$$
EA = \texttt{0x1000} + \texttt{0x04} + \texttt{0x10}
$$

**Step 4 — Add Sequentially (Hexadecimal Addition)**

$$
EA = \texttt{0x1000} + \texttt{0x0014}
$$

$$
EA = \texttt{0x1014}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

The effective address is $\texttt{0x1014}$. The operand is read from memory location $\texttt{0x1014}$.

> **Logic:** The base register holds the *array base*, the index register holds the *loop counter* (or element offset), and the displacement is a *static offset* (e.g., to skip a header record). This mode is essential for `arr[i]` access in C.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Branch Target Address for Relative Addressing

**Problem:** A branch instruction at memory address $\texttt{0x4000}$ (PC value) uses a 16-bit signed offset. The offset field contains the two's-complement value $\texttt{0xFFFC}$. Compute the branch target address.

**Step 1 — Identify the Relative Branch Formula**

$$
\text{Target} = PC + (\text{Signed Offset} \times \text{Instruction Size})
$$

For byte-addressed memory with 4-byte instructions:

$$
\text{Target} = PC + 4 \cdot \text{Offset}_{\text{signed}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Hex 0xFFFC to Signed Decimal**

$\texttt{0xFFFC}$ in 16-bit two's complement:

$$
\texttt{0xFFFC} = 65532 \text{ (unsigned)}
$$

To convert to signed, subtract $2^{16}$:

$$
\text{Offset}_{\text{signed}} = 65532 - 65536 = -4
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Compute**

$$
\text{Target} = \texttt{0x4000} + 4 \times (-4)
$$

$$
\text{Target} = \texttt{0x4000} - \texttt{0x10}
$$

$$
\text{Target} = \texttt{0x3FF0}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

The branch jumps **backward** by 4 instructions (16 bytes) — a typical `BEQ` or `BNE` backward branch in a loop. After the branch executes, the **PC** is overwritten with $\texttt{0x3FF0}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Average CPI for a Mixed-Instruction Program

**Problem:** A program contains 3 instruction classes with the following statistics:

| **Class** | **IC (Instruction Count)** | **CPI per instruction** |
|-----------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| ALU ops | 40,000 | 1 |
| Load/Store | 25,000 | 5 |
| Branch | 5,000 | 3 |

Compute the average CPI.

**Step 1 — Apply the Weighted Average Formula**

$$
CPI_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \cdot CPI_i}{IC_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Each Product**

$$
IC_1 \cdot CPI_1 = 40000 \cdot 1 = 40000
$$

$$
IC_2 \cdot CPI_2 = 25000 \cdot 5 = 125000
$$

$$
IC_3 \cdot CPI_3 = 5000 \cdot 3 = 15000
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Products**

$$
\sum = 40000 + 125000 + 15000 = 180000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total IC**

$$
IC_{\text{total}} = 40000 + 25000 + 5000 = 70000
$$

**Step 5 — Final Division**

$$
CPI_{\text{avg}} = \frac{180000}{70000} = 2.571
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

> The average cycles per instruction is approximately **2.57 cycles**. This is closer to RISC behavior than CISC. If the clock is **1 GHz** ($T_{\text{clock}} = 1$ ns), the total execution time is:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = 70000 \cdot 2.571 \cdot 1\text{ns} = 179970 \text{ns} \approx 180 \ \mu s
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Addressing Mode → Assembly Translation

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix bridges the abstract addressing mode with its typical assembly-language syntax (MIPS-style RISC + Intel x86-style CISC).

| **Mode** | **MIPS Syntax (RISC)** | **x86 Syntax (CISC)** | **EA Formula** |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Immediate | `addi $t0, $zero, 100` | `MOV EAX, 100` | Operand is in IR |
| Register | `add $t0, $t1, $t2` | `ADD EAX, EBX` | Operand is register |
| Register Indirect | `lw $t0, 0($s1)` | `MOV EAX, [EBX]` | EA = [R] |
| Base+Offset (Displacement) | `lw $t0, 8($sp)` | `MOV EAX, [EBP+8]` | EA = [R] + D |
| Indexed | (synthesized) | `MOV EAX, [EBX + ECX*4]` | EA = [R_base] + [R_idx]·S |
| Relative (PC-relative) | `beq $t0, $t1, label` | `JNE label` | EA = PC + offset |
| Direct Absolute | `lw $t0, 0x1000($zero)` | `MOV EAX, [0x1000]` | EA = address field |

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Pipeline Speedup (RISC Motivating Equation)

**Problem:** A non-pipelined processor takes **5 ns** per instruction. After RISC pipelining with **5 stages**, what is the ideal speedup for executing **N = 1000** instructions?

**Step 1 — Non-Pipelined Time**

$$
T_{\text{non-pipe}} = N \cdot t_{\text{inst}} = 1000 \cdot 5\text{ns} = 5000 \text{ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined Time (5-stage)**

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + N - 1) \cdot t_{\text{stage}}
$$

With $k = 5$ stages, $t_{\text{stage}} = 5\text{ns}/5 = 1\text{ns}$ per stage:

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = (5 + 1000 - 1) \cdot 1\text{ns} = 1004 \text{ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup**

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{non-pipe}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{5000}{1004} \approx 4.98
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical Limit**

As $N \to \infty$:

$$
S_{\text{max}} = k = 5
$$

> This **theoretical 5× speedup** is the *quantitative* reason why RISC architectures with deep pipelines revolutionized computing in the 1980s–90s.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Von Neumann Architecture (Stored Program Concept)

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU[CPU]
    MEM[Main Memory]
    IO[I/O Devices]
    BUS[System Bus]

    CPU --- BUS
    MEM --- BUS
    IO --- BUS

    subgraph CPU_SUB [CPU]
        CU1[Control Unit]
        ALU1[ALU]
        RF1[Register File]
        PC1[Program Counter PC]
        IR1[Instruction Register IR]
    end

    subgraph MEM_SUB [Main Memory]
        INST_AREA[Instruction Area]
        DATA_AREA[Data Area]
    end

    classDef hw fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class CPU,BUS hw
    class MEM,INST_AREA,DATA_AREA,IR1,PC1,RF1,CU1,ALU1 mem
    class IO io
```

> **Explanation:** Notice how the *Instruction Area* and *Data Area* are **inside the same Main Memory block** — this is the literal embodiment of the Stored Program Concept. Both are accessed via the same System Bus, which is also the source of the *Von Neumann Bottleneck*.

---

### 4.2 The Instruction Execution Cycle — Detailed Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START([Power ON]) --> INIT[Initialize PC to Boot Address]
    INIT --> FETCH

    FETCH[PHASE 1: FETCH Instruction] --> FETCH1[AR <- PC]
    FETCH1 --> FETCH2[Read Memory at AR]
    FETCH2 --> FETCH3[IR <- Memory Data Bus]
    FETCH3 --> FETCH4[PC <- PC + 1 or PC + 4]

    FETCH4 --> DECODE

    DECODE[PHASE 2: DECODE] --> DECODE1[CU decodes Opcode in IR]
    DECODE1 --> DECODE2{Addressing Mode?}
    DECODE2 -->|Register| EVALREG[Operands from Register File]
    DECODE2 -->|Immediate| EVALIMM[Operand from IR field]
    DECODE2 -->|Direct / Indirect / Indexed| EVALADDR[PHASE 3: Evaluate Address]

    EVALREG --> EXEC
    EVALIMM --> EXEC
    EVALADDR --> FETCHOP

    FETCHOP[PHASE 4: FETCH Operands from Memory] --> EXEC

    EXEC[PHASE 5: EXECUTE and Write Back] --> EXEC1[ALU performs Operation]
    EXEC1 --> EXEC2[Result -> Destination Reg / Memory]
    EXEC2 --> EXEC3{Branch / Jump?}
    EXEC3 -->|Yes| UPDPC[PC <- Branch Target]
    EXEC3 -->|No| CONT
    UPDPC --> CONT
    CONT[Continue Loop] --> FETCH

    classDef phase fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef startend fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class FETCH,DECODE,EVALADDR,FETCHOP,EXEC phase
    class DECODE2,EXEC3 decision
    class START,CONT startend
```

> **Explanation:** This is the canonical flow that KTU examiners expect. The **diamond-shaped decisions** represent the conditional logic the CU performs (especially the addressing-mode switch and branch-decision check).

---

### 4.3 RISC 5-Stage Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    IF[IF: Instruction Fetch]
    ID[ID: Instruction Decode and Register Fetch]
    EX[EX: Execute or Calculate Address]
    MEM[MEM: Memory Access]
    WB[WB: Write Back]

    IF ==> ID ==> EX ==> MEM ==> WB

    subgraph PIPEREGS [Pipeline Registers]
        IFID[IF/ID Register]
        IDEX[ID/EX Register]
        EXMEM[EX/MEM Register]
        MEMWB[MEM/WB Register]
    end

    IF --- IFID
    ID --- IFID
    ID --- IDEX
    EX --- IDEX
    EX --- EXMEM
    MEM --- EXMEM
    MEM --- MEMWB
    WB --- MEMWB

    classDef stage fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class IF,ID,EX,MEM,WB stage
    class IFID,IDEX,EXMEM,MEMWB reg
```

> **Explanation:** Five independent instructions can be in five different stages simultaneously, achieving the theoretical **5× speedup** derived in Section 3.5. The pipeline registers isolate each stage and store the intermediate results (signals, addresses, register values) for the next stage.

---

### 4.4 Addressing Mode Decision Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    INSTR[Machine Instruction] --> FIELD{Decode Operand Field}
    FIELD -->|Field = constant| IMM[Immediate Mode EA = constant in IR]
    FIELD -->|Field = Reg number| REG[Register Mode EA = Register File output]
    FIELD -->|Field = Address| DIRM[Direct Mode EA = Address field]
    FIELD -->|Field = Reg with prefix| IND[Register Indirect EA = Memory at R]
    FIELD -->|Field = Reg + Offset| DISP[Displacement EA = R + Offset]
    FIELD -->|Field = PC + Offset| REL[Relative EA = PC + Signed Offset]
    FIELD -->|Field = Base + Index| IDX[Indexed EA = B + I scale S + Disp]

    IMM --> RES[Operand Available]
    REG --> RES
    DIRM --> RES
    IND --> RES
    DISP --> RES
    REL --> RES
    IDX --> RES

    classDef mode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class IMM,REG,DIRM,IND,DISP,REL,IDX mode
    class FIELD decision
    class RES final
```

> **Explanation:** The Control Unit's decoder hard-wires the logic shown. Each mode path produces an *Effective Address* that is then used to fetch the operand for the EX phase.

---

### 4.5 Evolution Timeline: CISC → RISC → Modern Hybrid

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[1960s: Mainframes Complex Instructions] --> B[1970s: CISC Era VAX, x86, 68k]
    B --> C[1980: Berkeley RISC I Patterson]
    B --> D[1981: Stanford MIPS Hennessy]
    C --> E[1985: SPARC, MIPS Commercial]
    D --> E
    E --> F[1990s: ARM in Mobile]
    F --> G[2000s: x86 adopts RISC micro-ops]
    G --> H[2010s: RISC V Open ISA]
    H --> I[2020s: ARM in Cloud Apple M1 M2 M3]

    classDef old fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef risc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef modern fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B old
    class C,D,E,F,G risc
    class H,I modern
```

> **Explanation:** The historical arc shows that even CISC processors (modern Intel/AMD x86) internally translate their complex instructions into RISC-like *micro-operations* (µops) before execution — proving RISC principles fundamentally won the architectural war.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Stored Program Concept. Mention two advantages and one disadvantage of the Von Neumann architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The *Stored Program Concept* (proposed by John von Neumann in 1945) states that **both the program instructions and the data on which the instructions operate are stored in the same main memory** and are accessed via the same addressing mechanism. This allows the computer to be reprogrammed by simply loading new instructions into memory, without any hardware rewiring.

> **Advantages (1 mark each, 2 marks total):**
> 1. **Flexibility:** The same hardware can run entirely different programs by loading different instruction sequences — enabling general-purpose computing.
> 2. **Self-Modification & Branching:** Since instructions are data in memory, the program can compute jump targets, implement loops, call subroutines, and even modify its own code (used in JIT compilers, debuggers).

> **Disadvantage (1 mark):** The **Von Neumann Bottleneck** — instructions and data share a single bus to the CPU, so the processor spends significant time *idle* waiting for memory fetches. This limits computational throughput.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three addressing modes with an example instruction for each. Write the Effective Address (EA) formula for each mode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **#** | **Mode** | **Example** | **EA Formula** |
|-------|----------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Immediate** | `MOV R1, #25` (8086) or `addi $t0, $zero, 25` (MIPS) | EA = constant field of instruction |
| 2 | **Direct** | `LOAD R1, 2000` | EA = address field = 2000 |
| 3 | **Register Indirect** | `LOAD R1, (R2)` or `lw $t0, 0($s1)` | EA = [R2] (contents of R2) |

(1 mark per correct row; total = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **complete instruction execution cycle** with a neat block diagram, clearly identifying the **5 phases** and the role of the Program Counter (PC), Instruction Register (IR), and Accumulator/ALU. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **RISC and CISC** architectures across any **7 parameters** in a tabular form. Discuss the **historical evolution** of RISC from its 1980s origins to modern ARM/RISC-V processors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Instruction Execution Cycle (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark)**

The instruction execution cycle is the fundamental operational sequence performed by the CPU to process a single machine instruction. In modern pipelined RISC processors, this cycle is decomposed into 5 distinct phases: **IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB**.

**Step 2 — Phase-by-Phase Description (5 marks; 1 mark each)**

**Phase 1: Instruction Fetch (IF)**
- The **Program Counter (PC)** holds the memory address of the next instruction to be executed.
- The address is placed on the **address bus**.
- Main memory decodes the address and places the corresponding instruction word on the **data bus**.
- The instruction is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
- Simultaneously, the PC is **incremented** (PC ← PC + 4 for byte-addressed 32-bit memory) to point to the next sequential instruction in anticipation of no branch being taken.

**Phase 2: Instruction Decode / Register Fetch (ID)**
- The **Control Unit (CU)** examines the **opcode** field of the IR.
- The CU activates the appropriate control signals to configure the ALU, register file, and data paths.
- Operands specified by **register fields** (Rs1, Rs2) are read from the **Register File** for immediate use.

**Phase 3: Execute / Address Calculation (EX)**
- The **ALU** performs one of two tasks:
  - For ALU-type instructions (ADD, SUB, AND): performs the arithmetic/logic operation on the operands fetched in ID.
  - For memory-access / branch instructions: computes the **Effective Address (EA)** using the addressing mode (e.g., EA = Register + Offset for base+offset mode).

**Phase 4: Memory Access (MEM)**
- For **LOAD** instructions: a memory read is performed using the EA computed in EX; the data word is retrieved.
- For **STORE** instructions: a memory write is performed, sending data to the EA.
- For ALU/branch instructions: this stage is a *no-op* (pipeline bubble).

**Phase 5: Write Back (WB)**
- The result of the ALU operation or the data loaded from memory is written back to the **destination register** (Rd) in the Register File.
- For branch instructions, the **PC is updated** to the target address (resolved in EX) at the end of this stage.

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark)**

> This 5-phase cycle, when implemented with **pipeline registers** (IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB), enables the modern RISC CPU to achieve an *ideal* **CPI = 1.0**, with one instruction completing per clock cycle in steady state. This is the central engineering insight that defines all modern processors from ARM Cortex to Apple M-series.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — RISC vs CISC + Evolution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Tabular Comparison (4 marks; ~0.5 each)**

| **Parameter** | **RISC** | **CISC** |
|---------------|----------|----------|
| 1. Instruction Length | Fixed (32-bit typical) | Variable (1–15 bytes) |
| 2. Instruction Set Size | Small (~50–150) | Large (~200–500) |
| 3. Memory Access | Load/Store only | Operand-in-memory allowed |
| 4. CPI | ≈ 1.0 | 2–15 |
| 5. Pipelining | Deep, easy | Hard, complex |
| 6. Clock Speed | Higher | Lower |
| 7. Power Efficiency | High | Moderate |
| 8. Examples | MIPS, ARM, RISC-V | x86, VAX, 68k |

**Step 2 — Historical Evolution Narrative (3 marks)**

- **1970s — CISC Dominance:** VAX-11/780 (1977) embodied CISC with hundreds of complex instructions, some requiring many cycles.
- **1980 — Berkeley RISC I (Patterson):** Reduced the instruction set to ~31 simple instructions, fixed 32-bit format, load/store architecture, large register windows.
- **1981 — Stanford MIPS (Hennessy):** Microprocessor without Interlocked Pipeline Stages; emphasized compiler-friendly design.
- **Mid-1980s — Commercialization:** SPARC (Sun), MIPS R2000/R3000, ARM (Acorn) emerged.
- **1990s — Mobile Era:** ARM's power efficiency made it dominant in embedded/mobile.
- **2000s — RISC Internalization:** Intel/AMD x86 chips began internally decoding CISC instructions into RISC-like *µops* — a tacit admission of RISC's efficiency.
- **2010s — Open ISA:** RISC-V (UC Berkeley, 2010) emerged as a free, open standard.
- **2020s — Universal Adoption:** Apple's M1/M2/M3 (ARM RISC) achieved unprecedented performance-per-watt; RISC-V proliferates in IoT and AI accelerators.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** RISC principles — fixed format, load/store, pipelining, compiler-friendly design — have become the *de facto* standard for all modern high-performance and energy-efficient processors, whether they are branded RISC, CISC, or hybrid.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Von Neumann architecture** and describe the **Stored Program Concept**. Explain the term *Von Neumann Bottleneck* and how the **Harvard architecture** addresses it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **five addressing modes** in detail with their **Effective Address (EA) formulas** and a sample assembly instruction for each. For each mode, state one specific use case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Von Neumann & Harvard (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Von Neumann Block Diagram (2 marks)**

(Reference: SECTION 4.1 above) — The diagram shows CPU, Memory, and I/O all connected via a single System Bus. Memory is divided logically (not physically) into Instruction Area and Data Area.

**Step 2 — Stored Program Concept Description (2 marks)**

- Both instructions and data reside in a *single linear address space* in main memory.
- Instructions are fetched sequentially by following the PC, but the PC can be altered by *control flow instructions* (jumps, branches, calls).
- This uniformity allows instructions to be treated as data — enabling self-modifying code, dynamic linking, JIT compilation, and easy reprogramming.

**Step 3 — Von Neumann Bottleneck (1.5 marks)**

- Since instructions and data share the *same memory and the same bus*, the CPU can only fetch one thing at a time (either an instruction OR a data word).
- Modern CPUs can execute an instruction every nanosecond, but memory access takes 50–100 ns. This **mismatch creates an idle CPU**, capping performance.
- The effect is quantified: even if 50% of instructions are memory-access, the CPU spends most of its time waiting.

**Step 4 — Harvard Architecture as the Solution (1.5 marks)**

- Harvard uses **two physically separate memories**: one for instructions (I-memory) and one for data (D-memory), each with its **own dedicated bus**.
- This allows **simultaneous** instruction fetch and data access, eliminating the bottleneck.
- Used in DSPs (digital signal processors), microcontrollers (8051, PIC, ARM Cortex-M), and cache subsystems (L1 I-cache + L1 D-cache) of modern CPUs.
- Trade-off: more complex hardware, harder to share data between code and constants.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Five Addressing Modes (7 marks)**

| **#** | **Mode** | **EA Formula** | **Sample Instruction** | **Use Case** |
