|-------|----------|----------------|------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Immediate** | EA = constant embedded in instruction | `MOV R1, #50` (ARM) or `ADDI $t0, $zero, 50` (MIPS) | Loading constants, small initial values |
| 2 | **Direct (Absolute)** | EA = address field of instruction | `LOAD R1, 2000` (hypothetical) | Accessing a fixed global/static variable |
| 3 | **Register Indirect** | EA = [Rn] (contents of register) | `LDR R1, [R2]` (ARM) or `lw $t0, 0($s1)` (MIPS) | Pointer dereferencing, array traversal |
| 4 | **Displacement (Base+Offset)** | EA = [Rn] ± offset | `LDR R1, [R2, #8]` (ARM) or `lw $t0, 8($sp)` (MIPS) | Accessing struct fields, local variables, function parameters |
| 5 | **PC-Relative** | EA = PC + signed offset | `BEQ $t0, $t1, label` (MIPS) | Branch instructions, position-independent code |

(1.4 marks per row; total = 7 marks)

**Supplementary Note:** Each mode reduces a different class of programming problem — Immediate avoids memory access entirely (fastest), Direct is simple but inflexible, Register Indirect enables dynamic data structures (linked lists, trees), Displacement handles structured data (arrays, records), and PC-Relative makes code *position-independent* (essential for shared libraries).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Memory Address with Memory Content** (–2 marks typical): In Register Indirect, students often write "EA = R1" instead of "EA = [R1] = contents of memory at R1". The square-bracket distinction is mandatory.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting PC Increment in Fetch Phase** (–1 mark): Students describe fetching but forget to mention that the PC is incremented to point to the next instruction. Without this, the loop cannot continue.
> 
> 3. **Mixing Up the 3-Phase and 5-Phase Models** (–1 mark): Use the 5-phase model (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) for any pipelined or modern processor question; use the 3-phase model (Fetch, Decode, Execute) only if the question *specifically* asks for the basic/classical cycle.
> 
> 4. **Vague Definitions of Addressing Modes** (–1.5 marks): KTU examiners want the **EA formula** explicitly written for each mode, not just a verbal description. A description without a formula gets *partial credit only*.
> 
> 5. **Conflating Von Neumann and Harvard** (–2 marks): Many students write "Harvard has separate memories for instructions and data" but forget to mention the **separate buses** — which is the actual mechanism that eliminates the bottleneck.
> 
> 6. **Missing the "+4" in PC increment** (–0.5 mark): For 32-bit byte-addressed memory, PC increments by **4**, not 1. A common silly mistake.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

**🔹 Instruction Execution Cycle**
- ☐ 3 classical phases: **Fetch → Decode → Execute**
- ☐ 5 modern phases: **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB**
- ☐ PC increments by **+4** in 32-bit byte-addressed systems
- ☐ IR holds the current instruction; PC holds the address of the *next* instruction
- ☐ Status flags (Z, C, S, V) updated during EX phase

**🔹 Machine Language Instructions**
- ☐ Two parts: **Opcode** (operation) + **Operands** (data/locations)
- ☐ Fixed-length (32 bits) in RISC; Variable-length in CISC
- ☐ ISA = the complete contract of all instructions + addressing modes + registers

**🔹 Addressing Modes**
- ☐ **Immediate** — fastest, no memory access
- ☐ **Direct** — single memory access, fixed address
- ☐ **Register Indirect** — pointer dereferencing
- ☐ **Displacement** — base+offset, most common in compilers
- ☐ **PC-Relative** — branches, position-independent code
- ☐ **Indexed** — array access with scale
- ☐ **Auto-inc/dec** — stack operations (mostly CISC)
- ☐ **EA formula is mandatory** in exam answers

**🔹 Stored Program Concept**
- ☐ Same memory holds instructions and data
- ☐ Proposed by **John von Neumann (1945)** in EDVAC report
- ☐ Enables general-purpose computing, branching, subroutines
- ☐ **Von Neumann Bottleneck** = shared bus limits throughput
- ☐ **Harvard** = separate I/D memories and buses (solves bottleneck)

**🔹 RISC Evolution**
- ☐ Origin: **Berkeley RISC (Patterson, 1980)**, **Stanford MIPS (Hennessy, 1981)**
- ☐ Principles: **Load/Store, Fixed format, Pipelined, Large register file, CPI ≈ 1**
- ☐ Examples: **MIPS, SPARC, ARM, RISC-V, Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3)**
- ☐ Modern CISC (x86) internally translates to RISC µops
- ☐ RISC dominates **mobile (ARM), embedded (RISC-V), and now cloud (Apple Silicon)**

**🔹 Key Formulas to Memorize**
- ☐ $T_{\text{CPU}} = IC \times CPI \times T_{\text{clock}}$
- ☐ $S_{\text{pipeline}} = \dfrac{n \cdot t}{(k + n - 1) \cdot t}$
- ☐ $EA_{\text{rel}} = PC + 4 \cdot \text{offset}$
- ☐ $EA_{\text{disp}} = [R_b] + \text{offset}$
- ☐ $CPI_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum IC_i \cdot CPI_i}{IC_{\text{total}}}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazards, Solving Data/Control Hazards, Performance Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hazards, Solving Data/Control Hazards, Performance Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pipeline Hazards — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PIPELINE HAZARD (KTU Definition):**
> A **pipeline hazard** is any condition or situation in a pipelined processor that prevents the next instruction in the instruction stream from executing during its designated clock cycle, thereby causing **pipeline stalls (bubbles)** or **pipeline flushes** that degrade the ideal throughput of the pipeline.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, hazards are formally classified into three primary categories:

| # | Hazard Type | Core Cause | KTU Term |
|---|-------------|------------|----------|
| 1 | **Structural Hazard** | Hardware resource conflict | Resource Conflict |
| 2 | **Data Hazard** | Data dependency between instructions | Data Dependency |
| 3 | **Control Hazard** | Branch / Jump instruction flow change | Control Flow Change |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Triage Pipeline

Imagine a hospital triage assembly line with three stages: **Registration → Diagnosis → Treatment**.

- **Structural Hazard Analogy**: Only one X-ray machine exists, but two patients need X-rays at the same time. The second patient must **wait** (stall).
- **Data Hazard Analogy**: Doctor B needs the blood test report of Patient A before prescribing, but the lab hasn't returned it yet. Doctor B must **wait or guess** the result.
- **Control Hazard Analogy**: The receptionist is unsure whether a patient should go to the Orthopedic or Cardiology ward. The line **pauses** until the decision is clarified.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** Every hazard in a pipelined CPU directly translates to **stall cycles inserted into the pipeline**, which increases the **effective CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** and reduces the **speedup** of the pipeline.

## 1.3 Sub-Categorization of Data Hazards (RAW, WAR, WAW)

Data hazards arise due to **Read-After-Write (RAW)**, **Write-After-Read (WAR)**, and **Write-After-Write (WAW)** dependencies.

- **RAW (True Data Dependency)**: Instruction $i$ produces a register that instruction $j$ (where $j$ is later in the pipeline) needs to read. Most common hazard in MIPS-style pipelines.
- **WAR (Anti-Dependency)**: Instruction $j$ writes to a register that instruction $i$ (earlier) needs to read. Caused by **out-of-order execution** or **register renaming limitations**.
- **WAW (Output Dependency)**: Two instructions write to the same register. Caused by **instruction reordering** or **pipelined multi-stage writes**.

> [!NOTE]
> In a classic **5-stage in-order MIPS pipeline**, only RAW hazards occur naturally. WAR and WAW appear when we use **out-of-order execution**, **superscalar**, or **VLIW** architectures.

## 1.4 Control Hazards — The Branch Penalty

A **control hazard** occurs when the pipeline cannot determine the outcome of a branch instruction (BEQ, BNE, J, JAL, JR) until late in the execution stage, leaving the fetch stage unsure which instruction to fetch next.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Term — Branch Penalty:** The number of stall cycles introduced per branch instruction in the absence of any branch prediction or delay slot technique.

The standard branch penalty for a **5-stage pipeline** without any mitigation is typically **2 to 3 clock cycles**, depending on when the branch condition is resolved.

## 1.5 Performance Metrics in Hazard Analysis

The following metrics are **boldly highlighted** as KTU's standard performance metrics:

- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)**
- **Speedup Factor (S)**
- **Pipeline Efficiency (η)**
- **Throughput (T)**
- **Pipeline Cycle Time (τ)**
- **Total Execution Time (T_exec)**
- **Amdahl's Law constant (fraction enhanced)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect students to know that the **ideal pipeline CPI = 1**, but the **actual CPI = Base CPI + Memory Stall Cycles + Branch Stall Cycles + Data Hazard Stall Cycles**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Hazard — Deep Analysis

A **structural hazard** occurs when two instructions in different stages of the pipeline simultaneously demand the same hardware resource.

### Common Causes (KTU 2024):

- A single **memory port** serving both Instruction Fetch (IF) and Memory Access (MEM) stages.
- A single **write-back bus** when two instructions complete WB simultaneously.
- Limited **ALU ports** in superscalar pipelines.

### Resolution Strategies:

1. **Resource Duplication** (Harvard architecture → separate I-cache and D-cache).
2. **Pipeline Stall Insertion** (insert NOPs/bubbles).
3. **Multi-port Memory Architecture**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 explicitly mentions: *"A unified memory leads to structural hazard in single-cycle memory pipelined designs."*

## 2.2 Data Hazard — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 The RAW Hazard Timeline (5-Stage Pipeline)

For a sequence `I1: ADD R1, R2, R3` followed by `I2: SUB R4, R1, R5`, the RAW hazard unfolds as:

| Cycle | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I1 | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — | — |
| I2 | — | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — |

The data is written to the register file in the **first half** of cycle 5 (WB stage) and can be read in the **second half** of cycle 5. So I2's ID stage in cycle 3 reads the **stale** value.

### 2.2.2 Three Solutions for Data Hazards (KTU 2024 Mandatory Topics)

#### Solution A: Pipeline Stalling (Hardware Interlock)
- Detect hazard in **ID stage** of I2.
- Insert **bubbles (NOPs)** by freezing the PC and IF/ID pipeline register.
- Cost: **1, 2, or 3 stall cycles** depending on forwarding availability.

#### Solution B: Data Forwarding (Bypassing) — *Most Important*
- Forward the result from EX, MEM, or WB stage of I1 directly to the ALU input of I2.
- Requires **multiplexers** at the ALU input sourced from EX/MEM, MEM/WB, and WB pipeline latches.
- Forwarding paths: **EX-to-EX**, **MEM-to-EX**, **WB-to-EX**.
- Reduces RAW stalls to **zero** for register-register ALU operations.
- **Load-use hazard still requires 1 stall** even with full forwarding.

#### Solution C: Compiler Scheduling (Software Interlock)
- Compiler rearranges instructions to **insert independent instructions** between producer and consumer.
- Eliminates many hazards without hardware changes.

## 2.3 Control Hazard — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Five Resolution Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Technique | Stall Cycles | Hardware/Software |
|---|-----------|--------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Stall until resolved** | 2–3 | Hardware |
| 2 | **Predict Branch Not Taken** | 0 (correct) / flush (wrong) | Hardware |
| 3 | **Predict Branch Taken** | 0 (correct) / flush (wrong) | Hardware |
| 4 | **Delayed Branch (Delay Slot)** | 0 | Software (Compiler) |
| 5 | **Branch Target Buffer (BTB)** | 0 (hit) | Hardware |

### 2.3.2 Delayed Branch Semantics
$$I_{branch} \rightarrow I_{delay\_slot} \rightarrow I_{target}$$
The instruction in the **delay slot** is **always executed**, regardless of whether the branch is taken. The compiler fills this slot with:
- An instruction from before the branch (if safe), or
- A NOP (if no safe instruction exists).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $S_n = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$ | Speedup of k-stage pipeline for n tasks | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{S_n}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$ | Pipeline Efficiency | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 3 | $T_{exec} = \tau \cdot (k + n - 1)$ | Total execution time of pipelined task set | seconds |
| 4 | $CPI_{actual} = CPI_{base} + \sum_i (\text{Stalls}_i \times \text{Freq}_i)$ | Effective CPI with hazards | cycles/instruction |
| 5 | $\text{Total Stalls} = \sum_i (StallCycles_i \times Frequency_i)$ | Sum of all stall cycles | cycles |
| 6 | $T_{CPU} = I \cdot CPI \cdot \tau$ | CPU execution time (fundamental) | seconds |
| 7 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{n}{T_{exec}} = \dfrac{1}{\tau \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{k-1}{n}\right)}$ | Tasks completed per unit time | tasks/second |
| 8 | $Speedup_{hazards} = \dfrac{CPI_{no\_hazard} \cdot \tau_{no\_hazard}}{CPI_{hazards} \cdot \tau_{hazards}}$ | Realistic speedup with hazards | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Branch Penalty} = \text{Predict\_Miss\_Rate} \times \text{Flush\_Cycles}$ | Effective penalty per branch | cycles/branch |
| 10 | $\text{Amdahl's Law: } Speedup_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + F/S}$ | Overall speedup with fraction F enhanced | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** In Formula 1, $k$ is the **number of pipeline stages** and $n$ is the **number of instructions/tasks**. As $n \to \infty$, $S_n \to k$ (theoretical maximum speedup).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Intel Hyper-Threading** uses branch prediction + BTB to mitigate control hazards.
- **ARM Cortex-A series** uses **speculative execution** with hardware rollback for branch mispredictions.
- **GPU pipelines (NVIDIA CUDA cores)** rely on massive **structural resource duplication** to avoid structural hazards.
- **Forwarding logic** is the bedrock of every modern RISC processor (MIPS, RISC-V, ARM, SPARC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ideal Pipeline Speedup (Without Hazards)

### Setup:
- $k$ = number of pipeline stages
- $n$ = number of instructions (assume $n \gg k$)
- $\tau$ = clock cycle time (pipelined)

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Non-pipelined execution time:**
Each instruction takes $k$ clock cycles. For $n$ instructions:
$$T_{non-pipe} = n \cdot k \cdot \tau$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined execution time:**
In a pipelined processor, the first instruction takes $k$ cycles to fill the pipeline; the remaining $(n - 1)$ instructions complete in 1 cycle each.
$$T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot \tau$$

**Step 3 — Speedup definition:**
$$S = \dfrac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions:**
$$S = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot \tau}{(k + n - 1) \cdot \tau}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\tau$ and simplify:**
$$S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency definition:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit analysis:** As $n \to \infty$, $S \to k$. Hence, the theoretical maximum speedup of a $k$-stage pipeline is $k$ — a key KTU result.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CPI With Hazards (Comprehensive)

### Setup:
- $CPI_{ideal} = 1$ (no hazards, perfect pipelining)
- $f_{branch}$ = fraction of branch instructions
- $f_{LW}$ = fraction of load instructions
- $\text{Stall}_{branch}$ = stalls per branch
- $\text{Stall}_{load-use}$ = stalls per load-use hazard

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Ideal execution time:**
$$T_{ideal} = I \cdot CPI_{ideal} \cdot \tau = I \cdot 1 \cdot \tau = I\tau$$

**Step 2 — Real CPI formulation:**
Each hazard type contributes stalls proportional to its frequency.
$$CPI_{actual} = CPI_{base} + \sum (\text{Stalls} \times \text{Frequency})$$

**Step 3 — Apply to data and control hazards:**
$$CPI_{actual} = 1 + (f_{branch} \times \text{Stall}_{branch}) + (f_{LW} \times f_{ALU\_after\_LW} \times \text{Stall}_{load-use})$$

**Step 4 — Real execution time:**
$$T_{actual} = I \cdot CPI_{actual} \cdot \tau$$

**Step 5 — Realistic speedup:**
$$S_{real} = \dfrac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{actual}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot \tau}{n \cdot CPI_{actual} \cdot \tau} = \dfrac{k}{CPI_{actual}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU standard):**
Assume $k = 5$, $f_{branch} = 0.20$, $\text{Stall}_{branch} = 2$, $f_{LW} = 0.25$, $f_{ALU\_after\_LW} = 0.5$, $\text{Stall}_{load-use} = 1$.

$$CPI_{actual} = 1 + (0.20 \times 2) + (0.25 \times 0.5 \times 1) = 1 + 0.4 + 0.125 = 1.525$$

$$S_{real} = \dfrac{5}{1.525} \approx 3.28$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The ideal speedup of 5 is reduced to ~3.28 due to hazards. This 34% loss is the realistic impact of hazards in a typical 5-stage pipeline.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pipeline Throughput and Cycle Time Trade-Off

### Setup:
- Unpipelined critical path delay $= T$
- A $k$-stage pipeline divides the delay into roughly equal segments.
- $S_{latch}$ = latch (pipeline register) setup + propagation delay.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Pipelined clock cycle time:**
$$\tau_{pipe} = \dfrac{T}{k} + S_{latch}$$

**Step 2 — Ideal cycle time (no latch overhead):**
$$\tau_{ideal} = \dfrac{T}{k}$$

**Step 3 — Throughput of pipelined processor:**
$$TP = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{pipe}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{T}{k} + S_{latch}}$$

**Step 4 — Throughput penalty due to latches:**
$$TP_{penalty} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k \cdot \dfrac{S_{latch}}{T}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Warning:** As $k$ increases, $TP_{penalty}$ decreases (more latches → more overhead). This is why real-world pipelines rarely exceed **15–20 stages** (e.g., Intel Pentium 4 had 20 stages, later reduced to 14 in Core 2).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Amdahl's Law Applied to Hazard Reduction

### Setup:
- Let $F$ = fraction of execution time that can be improved (e.g., portion spent on ALU operations we can optimize with forwarding).
- $S$ = speedup of that improved portion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Total execution time decomposition:**
$$T_{new} = (1 - F) \cdot T_{old} + \dfrac{F \cdot T_{old}}{S}$$

**Step 2 — Total speedup:**
$$S_{overall} = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{T_{old}}{(1-F)T_{old} + \dfrac{F T_{old}}{S}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**
$$S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + \dfrac{F}{S}}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting case as $S \to \infty$:**
$$S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-F}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Example:** If 30% of instructions suffer data hazards and we eliminate them with forwarding ($F = 0.30$, $S = \infty$):
> $$S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.30} = \dfrac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.43$$
> This means even perfect hazard resolution caps our speedup at 1.43x for that 30% portion.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When Does Each Hazard Cost Occur?

| Pipeline Stage | I1 (Producer) | I2 (Consumer) | Hazard Active? | Resolution |
|----------------|---------------|---------------|----------------|------------|
| Cycle 1 | IF | — | No | — |
| Cycle 2 | ID | IF | No | — |
| Cycle 3 | EX | ID | **RAW (if no forwarding)** | Stall 1 cycle |
| Cycle 4 | MEM | EX | **RAW (if no MEM-to-EX forwarding)** | Forward from MEM |
| Cycle 5 | WB | MEM | No (data already available) | — |
| Cycle 6 | — | WB | No | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's 5-stage pipeline expects students to identify that **EX-to-EX forwarding** resolves ALU-to-ALU dependencies, but **MEM-to-EX forwarding** is needed when the producer is a **load instruction** (because the data is not available until after MEM).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Pipeline Hazard Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Pipeline Hazards in KTU 2024] --> B[Structural Hazard]
    A --> C[Data Hazard]
    A --> D[Control Hazard]
    
    B --> B1[Resource Conflict]
    B --> B2[Solution: Duplicate Hardware]
    B --> B3[Solution: Stall Pipeline]
    
    C --> C1[RAW - Read After Write]
    C --> C2[WAR - Write After Read]
    C --> C3[WAW - Write After Write]
    
    C1 --> C1A[Solution: Forwarding]
    C1 --> C1B[Solution: Stall Interlock]
    C1 --> C1C[Solution: Compiler Schedule]
    
    D --> D1[Unconditional Branch]
    D --> D2[Conditional Branch]
    D2 --> D2A[Solution: Branch Prediction]
    D2 --> D2B[Solution: Delay Slot]
    D2 --> D2C[Solution: BTB]
    
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#c5504b,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: 5-Stage MIPS Pipeline With Data Forwarding Paths

```mermaid
graph LR
    IF[IF: Instruction Fetch] --> ID[ID: Instruction Decode]
    ID --> EX[EX: Execute ALU]
    EX --> MEM[MEM: Memory Access]
    MEM --> WB[WB: Write Back]
    
    WB -. Forwarding Path 3 .-> EX
    MEM -. Forwarding Path 2 .-> EX
    EX -. Forwarding Path 1 .-> EX
    
    ID --> HZD{Hazard Detection Unit}
    HZD -->|Load Use| STALL[Insert Stall Bubble]
    HZD -->|No Hazard| EX
    
    style IF fill:#bdd7ee
    style ID fill:#9bc2e6
    style EX fill:#5b9bd5
    style MEM fill:#2e75b6
    style WB fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style HZD fill:#c5504b,color:#ffffff
    style STALL fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Branch Resolution Flow (Control Hazard)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Fetch Instruction] --> S2{Is it a Branch?}
    S2 -->|No| S3[Continue Sequential Fetch]
    S2 -->|Yes| S4[Predict Outcome]
    S4 -->|Predict Taken| S5[Fetch from Target Address]
    S4 -->|Predict Not Taken| S6[Fetch Next Sequential]
    S5 --> S7{Resolve in EX Stage}
    S6 --> S7
    S7 -->|Correct Prediction| S8[Continue Pipeline]
    S7 -->|Misprediction| S9[Flush Pipeline]
    S9 --> S10[Fetch Correct Target]
    S10 --> S8
    
    style S1 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style S2 fill:#c5504b,color:#ffffff
    style S4 fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff
    style S7 fill:#c5504b,color:#ffffff
    style S9 fill:#c5504b,color:#ffffff
    style S8 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Load-Use Hazard Stall Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cycle T: LW R1, 0R2] --> B[Cycle T+1: ADD R3, R1, R4]
    B --> C{Hazard Detection: R1 Needed in EX}
    C -->|Yes| D[Insert 1 Stall Cycle: NOP]
    D --> E[Cycle T+2: Forward R1 from MEM to EX]
    E --> F[Cycle T+3: ADD Executes Correctly]
    C -->|No forwarding available| G[Insert 2 Stall Cycles]
    
    style A fill:#9bc2e6
    style B fill:#9bc2e6
    style C fill:#c5504b,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#c5504b,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Forwarding Datapath

```mermaid
graph LR
    RF[Register File] --> MUX1[MUX A]
    MUX1 --> ALU[ALU]
    EXMEM[EX/MEM Pipeline Reg] --> MUXA_F[Forwarding MUX A]
    MEMWB[MEM/WB Pipeline Reg] --> MUXA_F
    EXMEM --> MUXB_F[Forwarding MUX B]
    MEMWB --> MUXB_F
    MUXA_F --> MUX2[MUX B Input]
    MUXB_F --> MUX2
    RF --> MUX2
    MUX2 --> ALU
    ALU --> EXMEM
    
    style RF fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style ALU fill:#c5504b,color:#ffffff
    style MUXA_F fill:#bf8f00
    style MUXB_F fill:#bf8f00
    style MUX1 fill:#9bc2e6
    style MUX2 fill:#9bc2e6
    style EXMEM fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style MEMWB fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The two forwarding MUXes (MUXA_F and MUXB_F) select between the **register file output**, the **EX/MEM latch**, and the **MEM/WB latch** to supply the ALU operands. This eliminates most RAW data hazards without any stall cycles.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Pipeline Hazard. Classify the three types of pipeline hazards with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A pipeline hazard is a condition in a pipelined processor that prevents the next instruction from executing in its designated clock cycle, causing stalls or flushes. The three types are:

1. **Structural Hazard**: Hardware resource conflict (e.g., single memory port serving both IF and MEM stages).
2. **Data Hazard**: Data dependency between instructions (e.g., ADD R1, R2, R3 followed by SUB R4, R1, R5 — RAW dependency).
3. **Control Hazard**: Pipeline uncertainty due to branch instructions (e.g., BEQ R1, R2, LABEL — pipeline must wait for branch resolution).

---

### Question 2: What is data forwarding? Why is it ineffective for load-use hazards? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Data forwarding (bypassing)** is a hardware technique that routes the result of an instruction's EX, MEM, or WB stage directly to the ALU input of a dependent instruction, eliminating the need to wait for the register file write-back.

It is **ineffective for load-use hazards** because a load instruction's data is only available **after the MEM stage**. Even with MEM-to-EX forwarding, the consumer instruction's EX stage occurs in the same cycle as the load's MEM stage, so **1 stall cycle is mandatory** to allow the load to complete its memory access before the dependent ALU operation can use the forwarded value.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Explain the three techniques to handle data hazards in pipelined processors with neat diagrams. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

The three techniques to handle data hazards are:

**1. Pipeline Stalling (Hardware Interlock):**
- The **Hazard Detection Unit (HDU)** in the ID stage monitors the destination register of the instruction in EX/MEM and MEM/WB pipeline registers.
- If the source register of the current instruction matches the destination of a preceding instruction that hasn't yet written back, the HDU asserts a **Stall** signal.
- The PC and IF/ID register are frozen, and a **NOP (bubble)** is inserted into the ID/EX register.
- **Cost**: Up to 3 stall cycles for ALU-ALU dependencies without forwarding.

**2. Data Forwarding (Bypassing):**
- Forwarding paths are added from:
  - **EX/MEM register → ALU input** (MEM-to-EX forwarding)
  - **MEM/WB register → ALU input** (WB-to-EX forwarding)
  - **ALU output → ALU input** (EX-to-EX forwarding, for same-cycle dependencies)
- **Multiplexers** at the ALU input select between register file value, MEM/WB value, and EX/MEM value.
- Controlled by the **Forwarding Unit**, which compares the source registers of the current instruction with the destination register of instructions in EX and MEM stages.
- **Cost**: Reduces RAW stalls to **zero** for register-register ALU operations, but adds hardware complexity.

**3. Compiler Scheduling (Software Interlock):**
- The compiler analyzes the instruction stream and **rearranges instructions** to insert independent instructions between a producer and a dependent consumer.
- Example transformation:
  - Original: `LW R1, 0(R2) ; ADD R3, R1, R4`
  - Scheduled: `LW R1, 0(R2) ; SUB R5, R6, R7 ; ADD R3, R1, R4`
- **Cost**: Requires intelligent compilers; effectiveness depends on available independent instructions.

---

#### Part (b): A 5-stage MIPS pipeline with full forwarding executes 1,000 instructions where 25% are loads, 15% are branches (resolved in EX stage with 2-cycle flush penalty), and 30% of loads are immediately followed by an ALU instruction using the loaded register. Calculate: (i) Total stall cycles, (ii) Effective CPI, (iii) Speedup vs. non-pipelined processor (τ = 1 ns). (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total instructions: $I = 1000$
- $f_{LW} = 0.25$, $f_{branch} = 0.15$
- Branch penalty = 2 cycles
- Load-use fraction = 30% → stall = 1 cycle
- $\tau = 1$ ns, $k = 5$ stages

**Step (i) — Total Stall Cycles:**

Branch stalls:
$$\text{Branch stalls} = f_{branch} \times I \times \text{Penalty}_{branch}$$
$$\text{Branch stalls} = 0.15 \times 1000 \times 2 = 300 \text{ cycles}$$

Load-use stalls:
$$\text{Load-use stalls} = f_{LW} \times f_{load-use} \times I \times \text{Stall}_{load-use}$$
$$\text{Load-use stalls} = 0.25 \times 0.30 \times 1000 \times 1 = 75 \text{ cycles}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total stalls} = 300 + 75 = 375 \text{ cycles}}$$

**Step (ii) — Effective CPI:**

$$CPI_{actual} = CPI_{base} + \text{Avg stalls per instruction}$$
$$CPI_{actual} = 1 + \dfrac{375}{1000} = 1.375$$

$$\boxed{CPI_{actual} = 1.375}$$

**Step (iii) — Speedup vs. Non-Pipelined:**

Non-pipelined time:
$$T_{non-pipe} = 1000 \times 5 \times 1 = 5000 \text{ ns}$$

Pipelined time:
$$T_{pipe} = (1000 + 5 - 1 + 375) \times 1 = 1379 \text{ ns}$$

$$S = \dfrac{5000}{1379} \approx 3.625$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 3.63}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

#### Part (a): With a neat diagram, explain the concept of branch prediction and branch target buffer (BTB) for handling control hazards. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Branch Prediction:**

Branch prediction is a technique to guess the outcome of a branch instruction before it is resolved, allowing the pipeline to continue fetching instructions speculatively.

**Static Prediction Schemes:**
- **Predict Not Taken**: Fetch the next sequential instruction; if branch is taken, flush the pipeline.
- **Predict Taken**: Fetch the instruction at the target address; if branch is not taken, flush.
- **Backward Taken, Forward Not Taken (BTFNT)**: Backward branches (loops) are predicted taken; forward branches (if-else) are predicted not taken.

**Dynamic Prediction Schemes:**
- **1-bit predictor**: Stores the last outcome; predicts the same next time.
- **2-bit saturating counter**: Requires two consecutive mispredictions to change the prediction (more stable).
- **Correlating predictor**: Uses the history of recent branches (global history) to predict the next.

**Branch Target Buffer (BTB):**

The BTB is a small, fast cache that stores:
- The **PC (address)** of recently executed branch instructions.
- The **predicted target address**.
- The **prediction bits** (1-bit or 2-bit counter).

**Operation:**
1. During IF stage, the PC is used to look up the BTB.
2. **Hit**: Use the stored target address and prediction to fetch the next instruction.
3. **Miss**: Use default prediction (e.g., not taken) and proceed sequentially.
4. When the branch is resolved in EX stage, the BTB is **updated** with the actual outcome and target.

**BTB Hit Rate Impact:**
If BTB hit rate is $h$, then effective branch penalty is:
$$\text{Penalty}_{eff} = (1 - h) \times \text{Stall}_{miss}$$

---

#### Part (b): Consider a 6-stage pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB, COMMIT) with $\tau = 0.5$ ns latch overhead. The unpipelined critical path is 8 ns. A program of 500 instructions has 20% branches with 80% prediction accuracy, 30% loads, and 20% of loads cause a 1-cycle stall. Compute: (i) Pipelined clock cycle time, (ii) Total execution time, (iii) Speedup over unpipelined execution. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $k = 6$ stages
- $T_{critical} = 8$ ns (unpipelined)
- $\tau_{latch} = 0.5$ ns latch delay (approximated as part of stage)
- $I = 500$ instructions
- $f_{branch} = 0.20$, prediction accuracy = 80%
- $f_{LW} = 0.30$, $f_{load-use} = 0.20$
- Misprediction penalty = 1 cycle (assuming branches resolved in EX → 1-cycle flush for our 6-stage design)

**Step (i) — Pipelined Clock Cycle Time:**

Base stage delay:
$$\tau_{stage} = \dfrac{T_{critical}}{k} = \dfrac{8}{6} \approx 1.333 \text{ ns}$$

Including latch:
$$\tau_{pipe} = 1.333 + 0.5 = 1.833 \text{ ns}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{pipe} \approx 1.833 \text{ ns}}$$

**Step (ii) — Total Execution Time:**

Branch mispredictions:
$$\text{Misprediction rate} = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20$$
$$\text{Stall}_{branch} = f_{branch} \times \text{MispredRate} \times \text{Penalty}$$
$$\text{Stall}_{branch} = 0.20 \times 0.20 \times 1 \times 500 = 20 \text{ cycles}$$

Load-use stalls:
$$\text{Stall}_{load-use} = f_{LW} \times f_{load-use} \times I \times 1$$
$$\text{Stall}_{load-use} = 0.30 \times 0.20 \times 500 \times 1 = 30 \text{ cycles}$$

Total stall cycles = $20 + 30 = 50$ cycles.

Total cycles:
$$C_{total} = I + (k - 1) + \text{Total stalls}$$
$$C_{total} = 500 + 5 + 50 = 555 \text{ cycles}$$

Total time:
$$\boxed{T_{pipe} = 555 \times 1.833 \approx 1017.5 \text{ ns}}$$

**Step (iii) — Speedup:**

Unpipelined time:
$$T_{non-pipe} = I \times T_{critical} = 500 \times 8 = 4000 \text{ ns}$$

Speedup:
$$S = \dfrac{4000}{1017.5} \approx 3.93$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 3.93}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(k-1)$ pipeline fill term**: Many students write $T_{pipe} = n + \text{stalls}$ instead of $(n + k - 1 + \text{stalls})$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Confusing CPI with clock cycle time**: $CPI_{actual}$ and $\tau$ are multiplied, not added. Misplacing units is a frequent error.
> 3. **Ignoring load-use hazard exception**: Forwarding does NOT eliminate all stalls. **Load-use hazard ALWAYS needs 1 stall** even with full forwarding. Forgetting this leads to wrong answers in numerical problems.
> 4. **Misprediction penalty calculation**: Many students use prediction accuracy as the penalty, but the correct formula is $(1 - \text{accuracy}) \times \text{flush\_cycles}$.
> 5. **Using percentage values directly**: KTU expects decimal fractions (0.20, not 20%) in CPI formulas. Mixing them is a calculation error.
> 6. **Branch hazard resolution stage**: KTU's 5-stage MIPS resolves branches in **EX stage** (not MEM), so the penalty is **2 cycles**, not 3.
> 7. **Forwarding vs. Stalling comparison**: Forwarding reduces stalls to 0 for ALU-ALU, but **does not affect branches**. Students often mistakenly claim forwarding solves control hazards.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pipeline hazard = any event causing a stall or flush** in the pipeline.
- **Three types**: Structural (resource), Data (dependency), Control (branch).
- **RAW, WAR, WAW**: RAW is the only natural hazard in in-order pipelines; WAR and WAW appear in out-of-order execution.
- **Forwarding** eliminates RAW stalls for ALU-to-ALU dependencies but **fails for load-use** (1 stall remains).
- **Structural hazard fix**: Duplicate resources (e.g., separate I-cache and D-cache).
- **Control hazard fix**: Branch prediction, delay slots, BTB, early branch resolution.
- **CPI formula**: $CPI = CPI_{base} + \sum (Stalls \times Frequency)$.
- **Speedup formula**: $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$; max speedup $\to k$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **Efficiency formula**: $\eta = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$.
- **Total execution time**: $T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1 + \text{total stalls}) \times \tau$.
- **Branch misprediction penalty**: $(1 - \text{accuracy}) \times \text{flush cycles}$.
- **Amdahl's law**: $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + F/S}$.
- **KTU 5-stage MIPS** resolves branches in **EX stage** → **2-cycle penalty** without prediction.
- **Modern processors** combine **speculative execution + branch prediction + forwarding + out-of-order execution** to approach ideal CPI ≈ 1.
- **Pipeline depth trade-off**: Deeper pipelines = higher clock rate but more hazards and latch overhead.
- **KTU numerical problems** always require showing $(k - 1)$ pipeline fill cycles in execution time calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Module 3: Introduction to Memory Organization

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PBCST404 – Computer Organization and Architecture*, **Module 3** introduces the student to the foundational concept of **Memory Organization** within a computer system. It lays the groundwork for understanding how a computer stores, retrieves, and manages data and instructions during program execution.

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Organization** is the architectural discipline concerned with the hierarchical structuring, interconnection, and management of various memory units (Registers, Cache, Main Memory, and Secondary Storage) in a computer system so as to provide the processor with the **fastest possible access to data and instructions** at the **lowest possible cost per bit**.

It encompasses the structural design of the memory subsystem, including:
- **Memory Hierarchy**
- **Main Memory (RAM / ROM organization)**
- **Associative Memory (Content Addressable Memory – CAM)**
- **Cache Memory (Mapping techniques)**
- **Virtual Memory (Paging, Segmentation, TLB)**

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Student's Study Desk"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a student preparing for the KTU University Exam.
> - **Registers** = The student's *active hand* holding the pen and the line being read right now. Extremely fast, but can hold only a few things.
> - **Cache Memory** = The *open notebook* on the desk. Quick to reach, holds a small collection of frequently needed formulas.
> - **Main Memory (RAM)** = The *bookshelf* in the room. Holds all chapters of the syllabus, but requires walking to fetch.
> - **Secondary Storage (Hard Disk / SSD)** = The *library down the street*. Has unlimited capacity but is very slow to access.
>
> Just as the student arranges study material by *speed of access vs. size*, the computer architect arranges memory in a **Memory Hierarchy** to balance speed, cost, and capacity.

### 1.1.3 Why Study Memory Organization? (KTU 2024 Context)

The **Von Neumann bottleneck** arises because the CPU is orders of magnitude faster than main memory. Without a cleverly designed memory subsystem, the CPU would sit idle waiting for data, wasting enormous computational potential. Module 3 equips the B.Tech student with the tools to resolve this bottleneck through:

- **Principle of Locality** (Temporal & Spatial)
- **Hierarchical design** to exploit the **Cost–Performance trade-off**
- **Cache mapping policies** to minimize *miss rate*
- **Virtual memory** to give programs the illusion of unlimited RAM

### 1.1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard metrics** govern all memory design decisions and will appear repeatedly in KTU numerical problems:

- **Access Time (tₐ)** — Time (in **ns** or **µs**) to read/write a memory location.
- **Cycle Time (t_c)** — Minimum time between two successive memory operations.
- **Bandwidth** — Amount of data transferred per unit time (in **MB/s** or **GB/s**).
- **Hit Ratio (H)** — Fraction of memory accesses found in the faster memory. **0 ≤ H ≤ 1**
- **Miss Ratio (1 − H)** — Fraction of accesses not found in the faster memory.
- **Capacity (C)** — Total storage in **bits, bytes, KB, MB, GB, or TB**.
- **Cost per bit** — Measured in **₹/bit** or **$/bit** in industrial design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Whenever a numerical problem is asked, the units of *time, capacity, and cost* must be consistent. Convert everything to a common base (e.g., nanoseconds and bytes) before substitution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Memory Hierarchy — The Heart of Module 3

The **Memory Hierarchy** is a structural arrangement of storage devices ordered by **decreasing access speed** and **increasing capacity/cost-effectiveness**. The student must internalize the trade-off: *smaller + faster = expensive; larger + slower = cheap*.

### 2.1.1 Layered Structure (Top → Bottom)

1. **CPU Registers** — Inside the processor. Capacity: **bytes (32–64 registers)**. Access time: **~1 ns**.
2. **Level 1 Cache (L1)** — On-chip SRAM. Capacity: **16 KB – 128 KB**. Access time: **1–3 ns**.
3. **Level 2 Cache (L2)** — On-chip or off-chip SRAM. Capacity: **256 KB – 1 MB**. Access time: **3–10 ns**.
4. **Level 3 Cache (L3)** — Shared across cores. Capacity: **2 MB – 64 MB**. Access time: **10–20 ns**.
5. **Main Memory (RAM)** — DRAM. Capacity: **4 GB – 64 GB**. Access time: **50–100 ns**.
6. **Solid State Drive (SSD)** — Flash. Capacity: **256 GB – 4 TB**. Access time: **~100 µs**.
7. **Hard Disk Drive (HDD)** — Magnetic. Capacity: **1 TB – 20 TB**. Access time: **~5–10 ms**.
8. **Optical / Tape Backup** — Off-line archive. Capacity: **TBs**. Access time: **seconds**.

### 2.1.2 The Three Defining Properties of the Hierarchy

| Property | Direction in Hierarchy | Real-World Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed (Access Time)** | Decreases as we go down | Physics of cell technology (SRAM vs. DRAM vs. magnetic) |
| **Capacity** | Increases as we go down | Larger cells cost less per bit |
| **Cost per bit** | Decreases as we go down | Economy of scale and storage physics |

### 2.1.3 The Principle of Locality

> [!NOTE]
> Programs do not access memory randomly — they exhibit predictable access patterns. This is the *theoretical foundation* on which the entire memory hierarchy is built.

- **Temporal Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, it is *likely to be referenced again soon* (e.g., loop variables, counters).
- **Spatial Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, *nearby locations* are likely to be referenced soon (e.g., sequential array traversal, instruction fetch).

### 2.1.4 Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) — Core Formula

For a **two-level memory system** (Cache + Main Memory), the KTU gold-standard formula is:

$$
\text{AMAT} = H \cdot t_{\text{cache}} + (1 - H) \cdot t_{\text{main}}
$$

Where:
- **H** = Cache Hit Ratio
- **t_cache** = Cache access time
- **t_main** = Main memory access time
- **(1 − H)** = Cache Miss Ratio

For a **three-level hierarchy** (L1 → L2 → Main), the formula **extends naturally**:

$$
\text{AMAT} = H_1 t_1 + (1 - H_1) H_2 t_2 + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) t_m
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board examiner's favourite "cheat-sheet" trick is to extend the AMAT formula layer by layer. **Always reduce the problem to a two-level case first**, then generalize.

### 2.1.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Average Access Time (2-level) | $T_A = H \cdot t_c + (1-H) \cdot t_m$ | seconds / ns | $0 \leq H \leq 1$ |
| Hit Ratio | $H = \dfrac{N_{\text{hits}}}{N_{\text{hits}} + N_{\text{misses}}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq H \leq 1$ |
| Miss Penalty | $t_{\text{mp}} = t_m - t_c$ | seconds / ns | $t_m \gt t_c$ |
| Memory Capacity | $C = N \times W$ | bits / bytes | $N, W \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| Addressable Units | $N = 2^{a}$ where $a$ = address bits | locations | $N = 2^a$ |
| Effective Access Time w/ TLB | $EAT = h \cdot (t_{TLB} + t_m) + (1-h)(t_{TLB} + 2t_m)$ | seconds / ns | $0 \leq h \leq 1$ |
| Speedup Ratio | $S = \dfrac{t_m}{T_A}$ | dimensionless | $S \gt 1$ if cache helps |
| Cost per bit | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Total Price}}{\text{Total Capacity}}$ | ₹/bit | always positive |

### 2.1.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

In modern engineering practice, memory hierarchy design directly impacts:
- **Processor Performance (IPC)** — A higher cache hit ratio means more *Instructions Per Cycle*.
- **Datacenter TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** — DRAM is **~100× more expensive per GB** than SSD.
- **Embedded Systems** — In IoT edge devices, the cache hierarchy is tuned for *power efficiency* (nJ per access), not raw speed.
- **AI/ML Accelerators (GPUs, TPUs)** — Tensor caches and HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) are direct applications of hierarchical design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Modern Relevance:** The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that students must appreciate *why* hierarchical memory is used, not just memorize its layers. The answer is always: **to balance the trade-off between speed, cost, and capacity.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds up the **AMAT derivation from first principles** and demonstrates the typical KTU numerical method step-by-step, so the student can reproduce the logic in the exam hall.

## 3.1 Derivation of Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)

### Step 1: Define the probabilistic model

Suppose the processor issues a stream of memory references. Let the total number of references be **N**. Let the number of references that find the data in **Cache** be **N_h**, and references that must go to **Main Memory** be **N_m**.

$$
N = N_h + N_m
$$

### Step 2: Define the Hit Ratio

By definition, the **hit ratio H** is the fraction of total references satisfied by the cache:

$$
H = \dfrac{N_h}{N} = \dfrac{N_h}{N_h + N_m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a probability — it represents the *likelihood* that a random memory access is a cache hit.

### Step 3: Express Miss Ratio

The miss ratio is simply the complement:

$$
1 - H = \dfrac{N_m}{N_h + N_m}
$$

### Step 4: Compute total memory access time

Each hit consumes **t_c** (cache access time), and each miss consumes **t_c + t_m** (the cache check followed by a main-memory fetch — note the *miss penalty* includes checking the cache first).

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N_h \cdot t_c + N_m \cdot (t_c + t_m)
$$

### Step 5: Average by dividing by N

$$
T_A = \dfrac{T_{\text{total}}}{N} = \dfrac{N_h \cdot t_c + N_m \cdot (t_c + t_m)}{N}
$$

### Step 6: Substitute the ratios

Divide numerator and denominator by **N**:

$$
T_A = \left(\dfrac{N_h}{N}\right) t_c + \left(\dfrac{N_m}{N}\right) (t_c + t_m)
$$

$$
T_A = H \cdot t_c + (1 - H)(t_c + t_m)
$$

### Step 7: Expand the second term

$$
T_A = H \cdot t_c + (1 - H) \cdot t_c + (1 - H) \cdot t_m
$$

$$
T_A = t_c \cdot [H + (1 - H)] + (1 - H) \cdot t_m
$$

### Step 8: Recognize the probability identity

Since $H + (1 - H) = 1$ (a fundamental axiom of probability), we obtain the **canonical KTU formula**:

$$
T_A = t_c + (1 - H) \cdot t_m
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most common form** asked in KTU numericals. The student must memorize both forms:
> 1. $T_A = H \cdot t_c + (1 - H)(t_c + t_m)$
> 2. $T_A = t_c + (1 - H) \cdot t_m$

## 3.2 Worked-Out KTU Numerical Example

> **Problem (Modeled on KTU pattern):** A computer system has a cache with access time of **10 ns** and a main memory with access time of **100 ns**. The cache hit ratio is **0.9**.
> **Find:**
> (a) The Average Memory Access Time (AMAT).
> (b) The Speedup achieved by adding the cache.
> (c) The new AMAT if hit ratio improves to 0.95.

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $t_c = 10 \text{ ns}$
- $t_m = 100 \text{ ns}$
- $H_1 = 0.9$

### Part (a): AMAT at H = 0.9

Using the formula $T_A = t_c + (1 - H) \cdot t_m$:

$$
T_A = 10 + (1 - 0.9) \cdot 100
$$

$$
T_A = 10 + 0.1 \cdot 100
$$

$$
T_A = 10 + 10
$$

$$
\boxed{T_A = 20 \text{ ns}}
$$

### Part (b): Speedup Ratio

Without cache, every access takes **t_m = 100 ns**. With cache, the average is **T_A = 20 ns**. Speedup **S** is:

$$
S = \dfrac{t_m}{T_A} = \dfrac{100}{20}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 5}
$$

> The system runs **5 times faster** on average with the cache.

### Part (c): AMAT at H = 0.95

$$
T_A = 10 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 100
$$

$$
T_A = 10 + 0.05 \cdot 100
$$

$$
T_A = 10 + 5
$$

$$
\boxed{T_A = 15 \text{ ns}}
$$

A **5% increase in hit ratio** reduces AMAT by **5 ns** (from 20 → 15 ns), which is a **25% performance gain**. This is why the KTU examiner loves asking about marginal improvements in H.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Multi-Level AMAT

For the curious student, the **multi-level extension** is obtained by **induction** on the two-level formula. Let:

- $H_1, t_1$ = Hit ratio and access time of L1
- $H_2, t_2$ = Hit ratio and access time of L2
- $t_m$ = Main memory access time

$$
T_A = H_1 t_1 + (1 - H_1) H_2 t_2 + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) t_m
$$

This is read as: "If L1 hits, pay $t_1$. If L1 misses but L2 hits, pay $t_1 + t_2$. If both miss, pay $t_1 + t_2 + t_m$." The KTU examiner will typically give **two levels** only, but the formula is worth knowing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

The following diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters**, with **clean uppercase labels inside double quotes** (no bold, italics, or HTML inside node labels).

## 4.2 The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["CPU REGISTERS - 1 ns - Few Bytes"] --> A2["LEVEL 1 CACHE L1 - 2 ns - 64 KB"]
    A2 --> A3["LEVEL 2 CACHE L2 - 5 ns - 512 KB"]
    A3 --> A4["LEVEL 3 CACHE L3 - 15 ns - 8 MB"]
    A4 --> A5["MAIN MEMORY RAM - 80 ns - 16 GB"]
    A5 --> A6["SOLID STATE DRIVE SSD - 100 us - 1 TB"]
    A6 --> A7["HARD DISK DRIVE HDD - 8 ms - 4 TB"]
    A7 --> A8["OPTICAL AND TAPE BACKUP - Seconds - 10 TB+"]
```

> **Reading the Pyramid:** From top (A1) to bottom (A8), **speed decreases** while **capacity increases**. This is the central visual that the KTU examiner expects the student to draw or describe.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Level Memory System

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU1["CPU ISSUES MEMORY REFERENCE"]
    CPU1 --> CACHE1["STEP 1 CHECK CACHE L1"]
    CACHE1 --> DEC1{"STEP 2 HIT OR MISS"}
    DEC1 -->|"YES HIT"| RET1["STEP 3A RETURN DATA TO CPU - TC ns"]
    DEC1 -->|"NO MISS"| MAIN1["STEP 3B FETCH FROM MAIN MEMORY - TM ns"]
    MAIN1 --> RET1
    RET1 --> AVG1["STEP 4 COMPUTE AVERAGE ACCESS TIME AMAT"]
    AVG1 --> PERF1["STEP 5 EVALUATE SPEEDUP S = TM divided by AMAT"]
```

> This flow diagram captures the **operational logic** of cache lookups and is the standard KTU "draw and explain" answer.

## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

The following diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters**, with **clean uppercase labels inside double quotes** (no bold, italics, or HTML inside node labels).

## 4.5 Information Flow in a Memory Hierarchy Request

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["PROCESSOR CORE"] --> R1["REGISTER FILE"]
    R1 --> C1["L1 CACHE 32 KB"]
    C1 --> C2["L2 CACHE 256 KB"]
    C2 --> C3["L3 CACHE 8 MB"]
    C3 --> M1["MAIN RAM 16 GB DRAM"]
    M1 --> S1["SSD STORAGE 1 TB"]
    S1 --> H1["HDD ARCHIVE 4 TB"]
    H1 --> T1["TAPE CLOUD BACKUP"]
```

> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow:** Each downward arrow represents a **miss path** (data not found in current level, fetched from the next slower level). The hierarchy is engineered so that ~95% of accesses are satisfied within the **top three levels**.

## 4.6 Mapping the AMAT Formula Visually

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1["INPUT H tcache tmain"] --> CALC1["CALCULATE AMAT tcache plus one minus H times tmain"]
    CALC1 --> OUT1["OUTPUT AMAT in ns"]
    CALC1 --> OUT2["OUTPUT SPEEDUP tmain divided by AMAT"]
```

This compact representation is useful in the KTU exam to **visually anchor the formula derivation** to the physical memory access flow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the Principle of Locality. Mention its types.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Principle of Locality states that a program tends to access a small, localized region of memory repeatedly over a short interval. It has two types:
1. **Temporal Locality:** Recently accessed items are likely to be accessed again (e.g., loop counters, accumulator variables).
2. **Spatial Locality:** Items near a recently accessed address are likely to be accessed soon (e.g., sequential array traversal, instruction stream).

This principle justifies the use of cache memory and is fundamental to the design of the memory hierarchy. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. What is meant by "Hit Ratio" in a memory hierarchy? Why is it important?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Hit Ratio (H)** is defined as the ratio of memory accesses found in the faster memory (cache) to the total number of memory accesses made by the CPU. Mathematically, $H = \dfrac{N_{\text{hits}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$, where $0 \leq H \leq 1$. A higher hit ratio reduces the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT), thereby increasing the system's overall performance and speedup. Hence, designing caches to maximize H is a primary goal in memory organization. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Modified Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **Memory Hierarchy** in a computer system. List its various levels with their typical access times and capacities. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Memory Hierarchy is a structural arrangement of memory devices in a computer system, organized by decreasing access speed and increasing capacity and cost-effectiveness. The various levels, from fastest/smallest to slowest/largest, are:

1. **Registers** – Inside the CPU, access time ~1 ns, capacity ~32–64 registers (bytes).
2. **L1 Cache (SRAM)** – On-chip, ~1–3 ns, 16–128 KB.
3. **L2 Cache (SRAM)** – ~3–10 ns, 256 KB – 1 MB.
4. **L3 Cache (SRAM)** – ~10–20 ns, 2 MB – 64 MB.
5. **Main Memory (DRAM)** – ~50–100 ns, 4 GB – 64 GB.
6. **Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD)** – 100 µs – 10 ms, 256 GB – 20 TB.
7. **Tertiary Storage (Tape/Cloud)** – Seconds, TBs and above.

The hierarchy is justified by the **Principle of Locality** and the **Cost–Performance trade-off**, ensuring the CPU gets fast access at low average cost. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q. (b)** A two-level memory system has a cache access time of **20 ns** and main memory access time of **200 ns**. If the cache hit ratio is **0.85**, calculate:
- (i) The Average Memory Access Time (AMAT).
- (ii) The Speedup achieved over a system without cache.
- (iii) The percentage reduction in access time if hit ratio improves to 0.95. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $t_c = 20 \text{ ns}$, $t_m = 200 \text{ ns}$, $H_1 = 0.85$.

**(i) AMAT at H = 0.85:**

$$
T_A = t_c + (1 - H) \cdot t_m
$$

$$
T_A = 20 + (1 - 0.85) \cdot 200 = 20 + 0.15 \cdot 200 = 20 + 30
$$

$$
\boxed{T_A = 50 \text{ ns}}
$$

**(ii) Speedup S:**

$$
S = \dfrac{t_m}{T_A} = \dfrac{200}{50} = \boxed{4}
$$

The system runs **4 times faster** with the cache.

**(iii) AMAT at H = 0.95 and percentage reduction:**

$$
T_A' = 20 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 200 = 20 + 0.05 \cdot 200 = 20 + 10 = 30 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{T_A - T_A'}{T_A} \times 100\% = \dfrac{50 - 30}{50} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Reduction} = 40\%}
$$

A **10% improvement in hit ratio** yields a **40% reduction in AMAT**, demonstrating the disproportionate impact of cache efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Alternative Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) computation** for a two-level memory hierarchy. Derive the AMAT formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
(See **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation, and **Section 4.3** for the block diagram. The student should reproduce both the probability derivation and the visual flow.) The final canonical result is:

$$
T_A = t_c + (1 - H) \cdot t_m
$$

This formula emerges from the fact that *every access must check the cache (cost $t_c$)*, and a miss adds the penalty $t_m$. The derivation involves expressing total access time as $N_h t_c + N_m (t_c + t_m)$, dividing by N, and using the identity $H + (1 - H) = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q. (b)** Compare **Temporal Locality** and **Spatial Locality** with one real-world example each. Explain how each is exploited by cache and main memory design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Temporal Locality | Spatial Locality |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Recently accessed data is likely to be accessed again | Data near a recently accessed location is likely to be accessed |
| **Mechanism in Hardware** | Cache **retains** recently used blocks (LRU/FIFO replacement) | Cache fetches **entire block** (multiple words) on a miss |
| **Block Size Effect** | Largely independent of block size | **Larger blocks improve** spatial locality exploitation |
| **Example in Code** | `for(int i=0; i<n; i++) sum += a[i];` — the variable `sum` and `i` are re-read | `arr[0], arr[1], arr[2], ...` — sequential array traversal |
| **Hardware Example** | L1 cache holding a loop's hot variables | L1 cache line loading 64 bytes at a time |

**Conclusion:** Both principles are essential. Temporal locality is exploited by **keeping recently-used data in cache**, while spatial locality is exploited by **loading a contiguous block of data** on each miss. Modern caches are designed around block sizes of 32–128 bytes to balance both forms of locality. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Module 3:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing µs and ns (1 µs = 1000 ns). KTU examiners explicitly deduct **½ mark** for this. Always convert to a common unit (ns) **before** substitution.
> 2. **Wrong Miss Penalty:** Many students write $T_A = H \cdot t_c + (1-H) \cdot t_m$ **without** the cache-check term. The correct form is $T_A = t_c + (1-H) \cdot t_m$ **or** $T_A = H \cdot t_c + (1-H)(t_c + t_m)$. Forgetting the cache check loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Hit Ratio as Percentage:** Sometimes data is given as a *percentage* (e.g., "hit rate = 85%"). Always convert to a fraction (**0.85**) before using the formula.
> 4. **Skipping the Derivation:** KTU values *derivation steps* (see Section 3.1). A student who writes only the final formula loses up to **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Ignoring Multi-Level Cases:** If the problem says "L1 and L2 cache", you **must** use the three-level formula. Stopping at two levels loses **2 marks**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 Introduction:**
>
> ✅ **Memory Hierarchy:** Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → RAM → SSD → HDD → Tape. Speed decreases, capacity increases as we go down.
>
> ✅ **Three Pillars of Hierarchy:** Speed, Cost per bit, Capacity — all trade off against each other.
>
> ✅ **Principle of Locality:** Temporal (re-use) and Spatial (proximity). Foundation of all caching.
>
> ✅ **Von Neumann Bottleneck:** CPU–Memory speed gap. Solved by the memory hierarchy.
>
> ✅ **AMAT Formula (2-level):** $T_A = t_c + (1 - H) \cdot t_m$
>
> ✅ **AMAT Formula (3-level):** $T_A = H_1 t_1 + (1 - H_1) H_2 t_2 + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) t_m$
>
> ✅ **Hit Ratio:** $H = N_{\text{hits}} / N_{\text{total}}$, $0 \leq H \leq 1$.
>
> ✅ **Speedup:** $S = t_m / T_A$. Always greater than 1 if cache helps.
>
> ✅ **Units Convention:** ns for time, bytes for capacity, dimensionless for H and S.
>
> ✅ **Cache Miss Penalty:** $t_{\text{mp}} = t_m - t_c$.
>
> ✅ **Hierarchical Trade-off:** Faster memory = smaller + more expensive per bit. This is the *engineering reason* for the hierarchy.
>
> ✅ **Typical Sizes to Memorize for KTU:** Registers (bytes), L1 (KB), L2 (hundreds of KB), L3 (MB), RAM (GB), SSD (TB), HDD (TB).
>
> ✅ **Typical Times to Memorize for KTU:** Registers (1 ns), L1 (2 ns), L2 (5 ns), L3 (15 ns), RAM (80 ns), SSD (100 µs), HDD (8 ms).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Performance Analysis?

**Performance Analysis** in Computer Organization and Architecture is the **quantitative evaluation of a computer system's efficiency, speed, and reliability** using standardized metrics, equations, and benchmarking techniques. It is the science of measuring *how fast*, *how efficiently*, and *how reliably* a computing system executes a given workload.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Performance analysis is the systematic study of the relationship between a computer's **clock speed**, **instruction count**, **cycles per instruction (CPI)**, and the resulting **execution time**, combined with the use of **Amdahl's Law** to predict the impact of architectural enhancements.

### The Three Pillars of Performance

| Pillar | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| **Speed** | How fast instructions complete | Seconds, Clock Cycles |
| **Efficiency** | Throughput per unit resource | MIPS, MFLOPS, FLOPS |
| **Reliability** | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | Hours |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Delivery Truck**
> 
> Imagine a **delivery truck company** trying to measure performance:
> - **Truck Speed (Clock Rate)** = How fast the truck moves (km/h)
> - **Number of Packages (Instruction Count)** = Total workload
> - **Stops per Package (CPI)** = How many times the truck stops to drop one package
> - **Total Delivery Time (CPU Execution Time)** = Final performance metric
> 
> A faster truck (higher clock rate) with fewer stops (lower CPI) delivering fewer packages (lower instruction count) will finish first. This is precisely the **CPU Performance Equation**!

## 1.3 Why Performance Analysis Matters in KTU

The KTU 2024 scheme places heavy emphasis on **outcome-based education (OBE)**. Performance analysis enables engineers to:
- **Compare** two competing CPU designs objectively
- **Predict** the impact of an enhancement before manufacturing
- **Identify bottlenecks** in pipeline, memory, or I/O subsystems
- **Justify architectural trade-offs** (cost vs. speed)

## 1.4 Fundamental Performance Metrics (Preview)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Golden Metrics to Remember:**
> 1. **CPU Execution Time** — The *real* performance metric
> 2. **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — Architectural efficiency
> 3. **Clock Cycle Time** — Hardware technology
> 4. **Instruction Count** — Compiler/ISA efficiency
> 
> **Memory Aid:** *E x C = I / P* → **E**xecution **C**ycle Time = **I**nstructions / **P**rograms

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CPU Performance Equation (The Heart of the Topic)

The fundamental performance equation is derived by decomposing execution time into its three independent factors.

### 2.1.1 Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Logic Flow)

- **Step 1:** Performance is inversely proportional to execution time. *Lower time = Higher performance.*
- **Step 2:** Execution time equals the number of clock cycles multiplied by the clock cycle time.
- **Step 3:** Number of clock cycles equals the instruction count multiplied by the average CPI.
- **Step 4:** Clock cycle time is the reciprocal of the clock rate.
- **Step 5:** Substitute all to get the master equation.

### 2.1.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference Table)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{c}$ | Master CPU Performance Equation | Seconds |
| 2 | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clock}}$ | Alternative form using clock frequency | Seconds |
| 3 | $CPI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)}{IC}$ | Weighted average CPI for n instruction types | Cycles/Instr |
| 4 | $MIPS = \dfrac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | $10^{6}$ Instr/s |
| 5 | $MFLOPS = \dfrac{\text{FLOP Count}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Floating-Point Ops per Second | $10^{6}$ FLOP/s |
| 6 | $Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{Performance_{new}}{Performance_{old}}$ | Ratio of improvement | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{enhanced}}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** (most critical for KTU) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System availability/reliability | Percentage |
| 9 | $Throughput = \dfrac{\text{Number of Tasks}}{T_{CPU}}$ | Work done per unit time | Tasks/sec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Guide for KTU:**
> - $T_{CPU}$ = CPU Execution Time (seconds)
> - $IC$ = **Instruction Count** (number of instructions executed)
> - $CPI$ = **Cycles Per Instruction** (average)
> - $T_c$ = **Clock Cycle Time** (seconds/cycle)
> - $f_{clock}$ = **Clock Frequency** (Hz)
> - $f$ = **Fraction enhanced** (in Amdahl's Law)
> - $S_{enhanced}$ = **Speedup of the enhanced portion**

### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This equation is used by:
- **Intel/AMD engineers** to compare Core i7 vs. Ryzen designs
- **Compiler designers** (GCC, LLVM) to minimize instruction count
- **Cloud providers (AWS, Azure)** to price virtual machines based on performance
- **Embedded systems designers** for IoT power-performance tradeoffs

## 2.2 MIPS — Million Instructions Per Second

**MIPS** measures instruction throughput but has a critical limitation: it ignores instruction complexity. A RISC instruction doing 1 simple task is counted the same as a CISC instruction doing 5 tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** MIPS is *not* a reliable metric for comparing systems with different **ISAs** (e.g., ARM vs. x86). Use it only for comparing machines with the *same* instruction set.

## 2.3 MFLOPS — Million Floating-Point Operations Per Second

Used primarily in **scientific computing** (weather prediction, CFD simulations, AI training). Normalized MFLOPS (per unit benchmark workload) is more reliable than peak MFLOPS.

## 2.4 Amdahl's Law (The Most Tested KTU Concept)

**Gene Amdahl (1967)** formulated the law that defines the **theoretical maximum speedup** when only a *portion* of a system is improved.

### 2.4.1 Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The One-Lane Bridge:**
> Imagine a highway where 90% is a 5-lane expressway (very fast) and 10% is a one-lane bridge (very slow). Even if you make the expressway 100x faster with a rocket booster, the overall trip time is dominated by the bridge. **The slow part dictates the overall speed.**

### 2.4.2 Boundary Conditions Table

| Condition | Resulting Speedup | Interpretation |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| $f = 0$ (nothing enhanced) | $S = 1$ | No improvement |
| $f = 1, S_{enhanced} \to \infty$ | $S = 1$ | Even infinite local speedup gives only 1x overall |
| $S_{enhanced} \to \infty$ | $S = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ | Maximum possible speedup |
| $f = 1$ (everything enhanced) | $S = S_{enhanced}$ | Perfect improvement |

### 2.4.3 Engineering Application

Used to evaluate:
- **Parallelization** limits in multi-core CPUs
- **GPU acceleration** in deep learning
- **Cache memory** impact on overall performance
- **Pipeline depth** limits in superscalar processors

## 2.5 System Reliability Metrics

| Metric | Full Form | Meaning |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | Non-repairable systems (e.g., satellites) |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | Average repair duration |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | MTTF + MTTR (repairable systems) |
| **Availability** | Uptime fraction | Critical for servers (e.g., 99.999% "five nines") |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the CPU Performance Equation

We begin with the most fundamental definition: **Performance is the inverse of execution time.**

### Derivation Step 1: Start with CPU Execution Time

The total time the CPU spends executing a program is the product of the number of clock cycles and the duration of each clock cycle.

$$T_{CPU} = \text{Number of Clock Cycles} \times T_{clock}$$

This is because if each clock cycle takes $T_{clock}$ seconds, and we need $N$ cycles total, the total time is simply $N \times T_{clock}$. There is no overlap; cycles execute sequentially.

### Derivation Step 2: Decompose Clock Cycles into Instruction Components

The total number of clock cycles is the sum over all instruction types. For a program with $n$ different instruction classes, where the $i$-th class has $IC_i$ instructions each taking $CPI_i$ cycles:

$$\text{Number of Clock Cycles} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)$$

The reason we use a sum is that different instruction types (loads, stores, ALU ops, branches) consume different numbers of cycles. Loads might take 5 cycles, ALU might take 1 cycle, etc.

### Derivation Step 3: Introduce the Weighted Average CPI

To simplify, we define an average CPI by dividing the total cycles by the total instruction count.

$$\text{Number of Clock Cycles} = IC \times \overline{CPI}$$

where $\overline{CPI} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i)}{IC}$ and $IC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i$.

This weighted average weighs each CPI by how frequently that instruction type appears in the program, giving a single number that represents the "average cost" of executing one instruction.

### Derivation Step 4: Final Master Equation

Substituting Step 3 into Step 1:

$$T_{CPU} = IC \times \overline{CPI} \times T_{clock}$$

This is the **CPU Performance Equation** — the single most important formula in KTU's Module 3.

### Derivation Step 5: Express Using Clock Frequency

Since $T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}$, we get the equivalent form:

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{IC \times \overline{CPI}}{f_{clock}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation reveals that *all three* factors (IC, CPI, clock rate) are **independent design variables** controlled by *different* teams: **compiler** (IC), **architect** (CPI), and **circuit designer** (clock rate). Performance optimization is a **team sport**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Amdahl's Law

We derive the law by partitioning execution time into the *enhanced fraction* and the *un-enhanced fraction*.

### Step 1: Partition Execution Time

Let $T_{old}$ be the original execution time. Partition it as:

$$T_{old} = T_{enhanced} + T_{unenhanced}$$

The enhanced part takes fraction $f$ of the time, so $T_{enhanced} = f \cdot T_{old}$ and $T_{unenhanced} = (1-f) \cdot T_{old}$.

### Step 2: Apply Improvement Factor

The enhanced portion is sped up by a factor of $S_{enhanced}$. Its new time is:

$$T'_{enhanced} = \dfrac{f \cdot T_{old}}{S_{enhanced}}$$

The un-enhanced portion is unchanged:

$$T'_{unenhanced} = (1-f) \cdot T_{old}$$

### Step 3: Sum the New Total Time

$$T_{new} = \dfrac{f \cdot T_{old}}{S_{enhanced}} + (1-f) \cdot T_{old}$$

Factor out $T_{old}$:

$$T_{new} = T_{old} \left[ \dfrac{f}{S_{enhanced}} + (1-f) \right]$$

### Step 4: Compute Speedup

Since $Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$:

$$Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{old}\left[(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{enhanced}}\right]} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{enhanced}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Amdahl's Law Formula (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> $$S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_e}}$$
> where $S$ = overall speedup, $f$ = fraction enhanced, $S_e$ = speedup of enhanced part.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem Walkthrough (KTU-Style)

> **Worked Example 1:** A program has $2 \times 10^9$ instructions. 40% are ALU (CPI = 1), 30% are load/store (CPI = 5), 20% are branch (CPI = 2), 10% are multiply (CPI = 10). The clock rate is 3 GHz. Find CPU time, MIPS, and average CPI.

**Step 1 — Compute average CPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{CPI} &= \dfrac{\sum (IC_i \times CPI_i)}{IC} \\
&= \dfrac{(0.4 \times 1) + (0.3 \times 5) + (0.2 \times 2) + (0.1 \times 10)}{1} \\
&= 0.4 + 1.5 + 0.4 + 1.0 \\
&= 3.3 \text{ cycles/instruction}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CPU Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{IC \times \overline{CPI}}{f_{clock}} \\
&= \dfrac{2 \times 10^9 \times 3.3}{3 \times 10^9} \\
&= \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^9} \\
&= 2.2 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute MIPS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MIPS &= \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6} \\
&= \dfrac{3 \times 10^9}{3.3 \times 10^6} \\
&\approx 909.09 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Average CPI = **3.3**, CPU Time = **2.2 s**, MIPS ≈ **909**.

---

## 3.4 Amdahl's Law Numerical Example

> **Worked Example 2:** A web server spends 70% of its time in network I/O. A new network card speeds up I/O by a factor of 5. What is the overall speedup?

**Solution using Amdahl's Law:**

- Enhanced fraction: $f = 0.7$
- Local speedup: $S_e = 5$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{overall} &= \dfrac{1}{(1-0.7) + \dfrac{0.7}{5}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.3 + 0.14} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.44} \\
&\approx 2.27
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Even though the network card is **5x faster**, the server is only **2.27x faster overall** because 30% of the time is spent in non-I/O work (CPU, disk).

---

## 3.5 Comparative Performance Analysis Table

| Scenario | Clock | CPI | IC | CPU Time (s) | Performance vs. Baseline |
|----------|-------|-----|----|--------------|--------------------------|
| Baseline | 2 GHz | 2.0 | $10^9$ | 1.000 | 1.00x |
| Faster Clock | 4 GHz | 2.0 | $10^9$ | 0.500 | 2.00x |
| Lower CPI (pipeline) | 2 GHz | 1.0 | $10^9$ | 0.500 | 2.00x |
| Compiler Opt (-25% IC) | 2 GHz | 2.0 | $0.75 \times 10^9$ | 0.750 | 1.33x |
| All Three Combined | 4 GHz | 1.0 | $0.75 \times 10^9$ | 0.1875 | 5.33x |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPU Performance Equation Block Diagram (Mermaid)

This Mermaid diagram shows the relationship between the four key performance variables and how they combine to produce execution time.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Count IC] --> B[Total Clock Cycles]
    C[Average CPI] --> B
    B --> D[CPU Execution Time]
    E[Clock Cycle Time Tc] --> D
    F[Clock Frequency f] --> G[Inverse Relation]
    G --> E
    D --> H[System Performance Output]
    H --> I[Lower Time = Higher Performance]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **CPU Execution Time** depends on three independent inputs: **IC**, **CPI**, and **Clock Cycle Time**.
- Clock frequency and clock cycle time have an **inverse relationship** ($T_c = 1/f$).
- The final output is system performance, where *lower execution time = higher performance*.

---

## 4.2 Amdahl's Law Performance Ceiling Diagram

This block diagram illustrates the fundamental limitation captured by Amdahl's Law.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Original System Execution Time] --> Q{Partition by Fraction f}
    Q --> R[Enhanced Portion f x Told]
    Q --> S[Unenhanced Portion 1-f x Told]
    R --> T[Apply Local Speedup Se]
    T --> U[New Enhanced Time f x Told / Se]
    S --> V[New Unenhanced Time 1-f x Told]
    U --> W[Sum: New Total Time]
    V --> W
    W --> X[Overall Speedup = Told / Tnew]
    X --> Y[Max Speedup = 1 / 1-f when Se is infinite]
```

**Key insight:** The unenhanced portion acts as a *floor* under the system. No matter how fast we make the enhanced part, the unenhanced portion keeps the overall time from dropping below a certain threshold.

---

## 4.3 MIPS vs. MFLOPS Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Performance Metrics] --> B[Time-based Metrics]
    A --> C[Throughput-based Metrics]
    B --> D[CPU Execution Time in seconds]
    B --> E[Clock Cycles per second]
    C --> F[MIPS - integer instructions]
    C --> G[MFLOPS - floating point ops]
    F --> H[Limitation: Ignores ISA complexity]
    G --> I[Limitation: Ignores integer workload]
    D --> J[Most Reliable Metric]
    E --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

---

## 4.4 Benchmarking Hierarchy Flow (KTU Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Performance Measurement Methods] --> B[Synthetic Benchmarks]
    A --> C[Application Benchmarks]
    A --> D[Kernel Benchmarks]
    B --> E[SPEC CPU Dhrystone Linpack]
    C --> F[Real Programs Compilers Databases]
    D --> G[Livermore Loops Matrix Mult]
    E --> H[Standardized Easy to Compare]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Final Performance Score]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Note:** Modern benchmarking (like SPEC 2017) uses a *geometric mean* across many workloads to avoid any single benchmark biasing the score. This is why a single MIPS number is **never** quoted in modern processor reviews.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
> 
> **Q1.** Define *CPU Execution Time*. How is it different from *Response Time* and *Throughput*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **CPU Execution Time (1 mark):** The total time the CPU spends actively executing a program, given by $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$. It does not include time spent waiting for I/O or other processes.
- **Response Time (1 mark):** The wall-clock time from submission of a job to its completion, including I/O wait, OS overhead, and CPU time. It is the *user-perceived* time.
- **Throughput (1 mark):** The total amount of work done per unit time, e.g., transactions per second or jobs per hour. It is a measure of *system* performance, not *individual* job performance.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
> 
> **Q2.** State **Amdahl's Law**. What is the maximum speedup achievable if 80% of a program can be parallelized?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Amdahl's Law Statement (1 mark):** Amdahl's Law states that the overall speedup from improving one portion of a system is limited by the fraction of time that portion is used. Mathematically: $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_e}}$.
- **Substitution (1 mark):** With $f = 0.8$ and assuming infinite parallel speedup ($S_e \to \infty$), we get $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.8} = \dfrac{1}{0.2} = 5$.
- **Conclusion (1 mark):** The **maximum achievable speedup is 5x**, regardless of how many processors are used. This is the fundamental bottleneck of parallelization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3, Question Pattern]**

> **Q3 (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **CPU Performance Equation** in detail. A processor has a clock rate of 2.5 GHz, an average CPI of 1.5, and executes a program with $5 \times 10^8$ instructions. Calculate the **CPU execution time** and the **MIPS rating**. What happens to performance if we double the clock rate but CPI increases by 20%?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part 1 — Explanation of CPU Performance Equation (3 marks):**

The CPU performance equation expresses execution time as a product of three independent design parameters:
- **IC (Instruction Count)** — determined by the algorithm, programming language, compiler, and ISA.
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — determined by the CPU microarchitecture and memory system.
- **$T_{clock}$ (Clock Cycle Time)** — determined by the hardware technology and pipelining depth.

The equation $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ allows architects to identify performance bottlenecks. To improve performance, we can reduce any one or more of the three factors.
