The three factors are **independent**, allowing separate optimization by compiler teams, architects, and circuit designers.

**Part 2 — Calculation of CPU Time and MIPS (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clock}} \\
&= \dfrac{5 \times 10^8 \times 1.5}{2.5 \times 10^9} \\
&= \dfrac{7.5 \times 10^8}{2.5 \times 10^9} \\
&= 0.3 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MIPS &= \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6} \\
&= \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^9}{1.5 \times 10^6} \\
&\approx 1666.67 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 3 — Doubled Clock Rate with 20% Higher CPI (1 mark):**

New CPI = $1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.8$; new $f = 5$ GHz.

$$
T_{new} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^8 \times 1.8}{5 \times 10^9} = 0.18 \text{ seconds}
$$

Speedup = $0.3 / 0.18 \approx 1.67$x. **The performance improves by 67%** because the clock rate doubling outweighs the CPI increase.

---

> **Q3 (b)** [7 Marks] With the help of **Amdahl's Law**, discuss why improving only the CPU's floating-point unit does not dramatically improve overall system performance when most programs are integer-intensive. A particular enhancement provides a speedup of 20 for a fraction $f = 0.25$ of the computation. What is the overall speedup?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part 1 — Amdahl's Law Explanation (3 marks):**

Amdahl's Law, formulated by Gene Amdahl in 1967, defines the maximum improvement achievable by enhancing only a portion of a system. The formula is:

$$S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_e}}$$

The law reveals a **diminishing returns** phenomenon. When $f$ is small (a small fraction of execution time is enhanced), the maximum possible speedup is $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$, which is bounded even for infinite local speedup. Therefore, a CPU enhancement targeted at floating-point operations (e.g., adding vector instructions) yields little benefit for integer-intensive programs because the fraction $f$ of time spent in floating-point is small.

**Part 2 — Integer vs. Floating-Point Argument (2 marks):**

In integer-intensive programs, the floating-point unit is mostly idle. Enhancing it by even 10x improves overall performance by only $\dfrac{1}{0.7 + 0.3/10} \approx 1.30$x. Designers must **profile real workloads** to identify hot spots before investing in architectural enhancements.

**Part 3 — Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given $f = 0.25$, $S_e = 20$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{overall} &= \dfrac{1}{(1-0.25) + \dfrac{0.25}{20}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.75 + 0.0125} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.7625} \\
&\approx 1.31
\end{aligned}
$$

Even with a 20x local speedup on a quarter of the work, the overall system is only **1.31x faster**.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3, Question Pattern]**

> **Q4 (a)** [7 Marks] Define **MIPS** and **MFLOPS**. A computer system has a clock rate of 4 GHz and an average CPI of 2.0. Calculate MIPS. If the system executes $1.2 \times 10^9$ instructions, what is the CPU time? Mention the limitations of MIPS as a performance metric.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part 1 — Definitions (2 marks):**

- **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second):** The number of millions of instructions executed per second. It measures instruction throughput but ignores instruction complexity.
- **MFLOPS (Million Floating-Point Operations Per Second):** The number of millions of floating-point arithmetic operations per second. Used to benchmark scientific workloads.

**Part 2 — Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MIPS &= \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6} \\
&= \dfrac{4 \times 10^9}{2.0 \times 10^6} \\
&= 2000 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clock}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^9 \times 2.0}{4 \times 10^9} \\
&= 0.6 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 3 — Limitations of MIPS (2 marks):**
1. MIPS **does not account for instruction complexity** — a CISC instruction may do the work of multiple RISC instructions, making direct comparison unfair.
2. MIPS **varies between programs** on the same machine, so a single MIPS number is meaningless without specifying the benchmark.
3. MIPS **can be inversely correlated with performance** in some cases (e.g., adding floating-point instructions lowers MIPS but increases performance).

---

> **Q4 (b)** [7 Marks] Explain **system reliability** in computer systems. Define **MTTF**, **MTTR**, **MTBF**, and **Availability**. A disk drive has MTTF = 200,000 hours and MTTR = 20 hours. Compute its **availability** and **MTBF**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part 1 — Reliability Concepts (3 marks):**

System reliability quantifies how consistently a system performs its intended function over time. The key metrics are:
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** The average expected lifetime of a *non-repairable* component (e.g., a satellite, an LED bulb).
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time taken to repair a failed *repairable* component.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average time between consecutive failures of a repairable system. $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$.
- **Availability:** The fraction of time the system is operational. $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.

**Part 2 — Disk Drive Calculation (3 marks):**

Given MTTF = 200,000 hours, MTTR = 20 hours:

$$
MTBF = MTTF + MTTR = 200{,}000 + 20 = 200{,}020 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Availability &= \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
&= \dfrac{200{,}020}{200{,}020 + 20} \\
&= \dfrac{200{,}020}{200{,}040} \\
&\approx 0.999900 \\
&\approx 99.99\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 3 — Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):**

The disk drive is "four nines" available, which is acceptable for most enterprise servers. Cloud providers like Google aim for "five nines" (99.999%) by using redundancy and hot spares.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Performance Analysis Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing CPU Time with Response Time (–2 marks):** CPU time excludes I/O wait. Writing $T_{CPU}$ when the question asks for response time is a common blunder.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert MHz/GHz in MIPS formula (–2 marks):** MIPS uses $10^6$ as the multiplier. Writing $MIPS = f_{clock} / CPI$ without the $10^6$ scaling yields a wrong answer.
> 
> 3. **Misapplying Amdahl's Law (–3 marks):** Students often forget that $f$ is the *fraction of time*, not the *fraction of code*. A function taking 40% of execution time has $f = 0.4$, even if it occupies 70% of lines of code.
> 
> 4. **Averaging CPI Incorrectly (–2 marks):** CPI is a *weighted average* by instruction count, not a simple arithmetic mean of instruction types.
> 
> 5. **Not Specifying the Benchmark when quoting MIPS (–1 mark):** Always mention the program/workload used to compute MIPS.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring Units in Final Answer (–1 mark):** Always state units explicitly: seconds for time, MIPS for millions/sec, etc.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Performance Analysis**
> 
> - **Master Equation:** $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clock} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f_{clock}}$
> - **Performance Rule:** *Smaller execution time = Better performance*. Speedup is a *ratio*, not a difference.
> - **MIPS Formula:** $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ — applicable only when CPI is known and ISA is fixed.
> - **MFLOPS Formula:** $MFLOPS = \dfrac{\text{FLOP Count}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}}$ — used for scientific computing.
> - **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_e}}$; maximum speedup is $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$.
> - **Reliability Quartet:** MTTF (non-repairable), MTTR (repair duration), MTBF = MTTF + MTTR, Availability = $\dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
> - **Three Independent Variables:** IC (compiler), CPI (architect), $T_{clock}$ (hardware).
> - **Speedup = $T_{old}/T_{new}$**; values greater than 1 indicate improvement.
> - **Golden Rule:** Never compare MIPS across different ISAs. Always use a *standardized benchmark* (SPEC, Linpack).
> - **Diminishing Returns:** Amdahl's Law implies that the law of diminishing returns applies to *every* architectural enhancement. The unenhanced portion is the *fundamental bottleneck*.
> - **Benchmarks Hierarchy:** Synthetic (easy, biased) → Kernel (specific ops) → Application (realistic) → SPEC (industry standard).
> - **Five-Nines Availability:** 99.999% uptime = ~5.26 minutes downtime per year. This is the gold standard for telecom and cloud systems.
> - **Common Pitfall:** Amdahl's Law uses *time* fractions, not *code* fractions — always profile before applying.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Caches: Basic Concepts, Cache Mapping, Cache Replacement, Multiple-Level Caches, Reducing Miss Rate, Write Policy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Caches: Basic Concepts, Cache Mapping, Cache Replacement, Multiple-Level Caches, Reducing Miss Rate, Write Policy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cache Memory?

A **Cache Memory** is a small, high-speed volatile memory unit positioned between the Central Processing Unit (CPU) and the Main Memory (RAM). Its fundamental purpose is to temporarily store a subset of the data and instructions from main memory so that future requests for those items are served from the cache, thereby dramatically reducing the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Hamacher & Vranesic Standard):**
> "A cache is a high-speed storage mechanism that stores a subset of a larger, slower main memory. The fundamental principle governing its operation is the principle of **Locality of Reference**, which exploits the temporal and spatial locality inherent in program execution."

### 1.2 The Principle of Locality

> [!NOTE]
> **Locality of Reference** is the empirical observation that programs do not access memory locations uniformly or randomly. They exhibit two distinct patterns:

**1. Temporal Locality (Time Locality):**
- If a memory location is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again in the near future.
- *Example:* Loop counters, variables updated repeatedly in a tight loop, the program counter (PC) of instructions in a loop.

**2. Spatial Locality (Space Locality):**
- If a memory location is referenced, nearby memory locations are likely to be referenced in the near future.
- *Example:* Sequential instruction execution, array traversals, sequential data structures.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Personal Study Desk" (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> Imagine you are a student studying for an exam in a large library. The library is your **Main Memory (RAM)** — it is vast but located far away, and getting a book takes a long time (high latency). Your **personal study desk** is the **Cache** — it is small, located right next to you, and accessing a book on it is nearly instantaneous (low latency).
>
> - When you first need a chapter, you walk to the library and bring it to your desk (**Cache Miss**). This is slow.
> - As long as you keep referring to that chapter for your current section, it remains on your desk — you don't have to walk back (**Cache Hit**). This is the principle of **Temporal Locality**.
> - When you bring a chapter to the desk, you also bring a few adjacent chapters from the same textbook because you know you'll likely need them soon (**Spatial Locality** / Cache Lines / Blocks).
> - When the desk gets full and you need a new chapter, you must remove an existing one. The strategy you use (e.g., "remove the one I haven't used for the longest time" = **LRU Replacement**) is your **Replacement Policy**.

### 1.4 Cache Hit vs. Cache Miss

| Event | Definition | CPU Action | Performance Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cache Hit** | The data requested by the CPU is **found** in the cache. | CPU reads/writes the data directly from/to the cache. | Fast — accesses in a few clock cycles. |
| **Cache Miss** | The data requested by the CPU is **NOT found** in the cache. | CPU stalls; the cache controller fetches the required block from the next level (L2 or RAM). | Slow — incurs the **miss penalty** (many clock cycles). |
| **Compulsory Miss** | First-time access to a block; the block has never been in cache. | Block is brought in for the first time. | Unavoidable. |
| **Capacity Miss** | The cache is too small to hold all blocks needed during program execution. | Useful blocks get evicted before reuse. | Reduced by increasing cache size. |
| **Conflict Miss** | Multiple blocks compete for the same cache slot in a set-associative/direct-mapped cache. | Useful block is evicted even though cache has free space elsewhere. | Reduced by higher associativity. |

### 1.5 Cache Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Metrics)

- **Hit Rate (h):** Fraction of memory accesses found in cache. Expressed as a percentage.
- **Miss Rate (m):** Fraction of memory accesses NOT found in cache. $m = 1 - h$.
- **Hit Time:** Time required to access the cache on a hit (includes tag comparison).
- **Miss Penalty:** Additional time required to fetch the block from the next memory level on a miss.
- **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT):** The single most important cache performance metric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU 2024 Physical Constants for the Course:**
> - **Main Memory Access Time:** typically **100 ns** (or **100 clock cycles** for KTU exam problems).
> - **Cache Access Time:** typically **5 ns** (or **1-2 clock cycles**).
> - The 3 C's Model of Misses: **Compulsory, Capacity, Conflict**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cache Operation: Step-by-Step Flow

When the CPU issues a memory read request for a given **Main Memory Address**, the following sequence occurs inside the cache controller:

1. **Address Decomposition:** The CPU's main memory address is partitioned into three logical fields:
   - **Tag** — the high-order bits used for *uniqueness identification*.
   - **Index** — the middle bits used to *locate the cache set/line*.
   - **Block Offset (or Word Offset)** — the low-order bits used to *select the byte/word inside the block*.

2. **Indexing:** The *Index* field is used to directly locate the candidate cache line (or cache set). For a direct-mapped cache, this points to exactly one line. For an n-way set-associative cache, this points to a set containing n lines.

3. **Tag Comparison (Lookup):** The *Tag* field of the incoming address is compared **in parallel** against the stored tags of the candidate lines (set entries).
   - If a stored tag matches the incoming tag **AND** the corresponding *Valid Bit* = 1, it is a **Cache Hit**. The block-offset selects the desired data.
   - If no tag matches (or valid bit = 0), it is a **Cache Miss**.

4. **Miss Handling:** The cache controller initiates a fetch from the next memory level (L2, L3, or RAM). Once the block arrives, a placement decision is made based on the **Mapping Scheme**, and a possible **Replacement** is triggered.

5. **Write Operations (if applicable):** The chosen **Write Policy** (Write-Back or Write-Through) is invoked to keep main memory consistent.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize compilation** for KTU 2024 ESE and Continuous Evaluation. Every numeric cache problem in KTU is solved using one of these formulas.

### A. Address Partitioning Formulas

Let:
- $\text{Physical Address} = A$ bits
- $\text{Block Size} = 2^B$ words (i.e., $B$ offset bits)
- $\text{Number of Cache Lines (or Sets)} = 2^I$ (i.e., $I$ index bits)
- $\text{Associativity} = N$ ways
- $\text{Number of Blocks in Main Memory} = 2^M$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block Offset Bits } (B) &= \log_2(\text{Block Size in bytes}) \\
\text{Index Bits } (I) &= \log_2\left(\frac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Block Size} \times \text{Associativity}}\right) \\
\text{Tag Bits } (T) &= A - I - B
\end{aligned}
$$

### B. Total Number of Bits Stored in the Cache (KTU Favorite Question)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cache Bits} &= \text{Data Bits} + \text{Overhead Bits} \\
\text{Data Bits} &= \text{Cache Size (in bits)} \\
\text{Overhead Bits per Line} &= \text{Tag Bits} + \text{Valid Bit} + \text{Dirty Bit (if write-back)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Total Overhead} = \text{Number of Lines} \times \text{Overhead per Line}
$$

### C. AMAT Formulas (Average Memory Access Time)

For a **Single-Level Cache**:
$$
\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} + m \times T_{\text{miss penalty}}
$$

For a **Two-Level Cache** (L1 and L2):
$$
\text{AMAT}_{L1} = T_{h1} + m_1 \times \left( T_{h2} + m_2 \times T_{m2} \right)
$$

Where $T_{h1}$ = L1 hit time, $m_1$ = L1 miss rate, $T_{h2}$ = L2 hit time, $m_2$ = L2 miss rate, $T_{m2}$ = L2 miss penalty (main memory access time).

For an **n-Level Cache** (Recursive Form):
$$
\text{AMAT}_i = T_{hi} + m_i \times \text{AMAT}_{i+1}
$$

### D. CPU Execution Time with Cache

$$
\text{CPU Time} = \text{(CPU Execution Clock Cycles + Memory Stall Cycles)} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}
$$

$$
\text{Memory Stall Cycles} = \text{IC} \times \frac{\text{Memory References}}{\text{Instruction}} \times \text{Miss Rate} \times \text{Miss Penalty}
$$

For a system with base CPI (CPI_execution) and memory stall cycles:
$$
\text{Total CPI} = \text{CPI}_{\text{execution}} + \text{Memory Stall Cycles per Instruction}
$$

### E. Cache Size, Associativity, and Line Count Relationships

$$
\text{Number of Sets} = \frac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Block Size} \times \text{Associativity}}
$$

$$
\text{Number of Lines} = \text{Number of Sets} \times \text{Associativity}
$$

### F. KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hit Rate | $h$ | 0.90 to 0.99 (dimensionless) | Fraction of accesses served by cache |
| Miss Rate | $m$ | $1 - h$ | Fraction requiring main memory access |
| Hit Time | $T_h$ | 1-2 clock cycles (L1) | Time for cache hit |
| Miss Penalty | $T_{mp}$ | 100-200 cycles | Time to fetch block from RAM |
| Tag Bits | $T$ | bits | Uniqueness identifier |
| Index Bits | $I$ | bits | Set/line locator |
| Block Offset | $B$ | bits | Byte selector inside block |
| Valid Bit | V | 1 bit/line | Indicates whether line holds valid data |
| Dirty Bit | D | 1 bit/line | Indicates line modified (write-back) |

## 2.3 Why and How — The Real-World Engineering Utility

**Why do we need caches in modern CPUs?**
The speed gap between the CPU and main memory (RAM) is enormous — a phenomenon known as the **"Memory Wall."** A modern processor can execute an instruction in less than **1 nanosecond**, but accessing DRAM takes **50-100 ns**. Without caches, the CPU would be idle for ~99% of the time waiting for memory. Caches bridge this gap by exploiting locality.

**How do caches work in real systems?**
In a modern multi-core processor (e.g., Intel Core i9, AMD Ryzen 9, Apple M-series), the memory hierarchy is multi-leveled:
- **L1 Cache:** Split into L1i (instruction) and L1d (data). ~32-64 KB per core. Hit time ~1 ns.
- **L2 Cache:** Unified (instructions + data). ~256 KB - 1 MB per core. Hit time ~3-10 ns.
- **L3 Cache (Last Level Cache, LLC):** Shared across all cores. ~8-64 MB. Hit time ~10-20 ns.
- **Main Memory (DRAM):** 8-64 GB. Access time ~50-100 ns.
- **Storage (SSD/NVMe):** ~100-500 µs.
- **Magnetic Disk (HDD):** ~5-10 ms.

This multi-level hierarchy applies the same caching principle recursively: each level acts as a cache for the level below it.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Address Decomposition and AMAT Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU ESE Typical Style):**
> Consider a computer system with the following specifications:
> - Main Memory size: **16 MB** (byte-addressable)
> - Cache size: **64 KB**
> - Block size: **32 bytes**
> - Associativity: **4-way set-associative**
> - Cache hit time: **1 clock cycle**
> - Miss penalty: **20 clock cycles**
>
> **Compute:**
> (a) Number of address bits, tag bits, index bits, and block offset bits.
> (b) Total number of bits stored in the cache (data + overhead).
> (c) AMAT for a program with 80% hit rate.

### 3.1(a) Solution — Address Decomposition

**Step 1: Compute the Total Number of Main Memory Address Bits (A).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Main Memory Size} &= 16 \text{ MB} = 16 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = 2^4 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = 2^{24} \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Number of Address Bits } (A) &= 24 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Block Offset Bits (B).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block Size} &= 32 \text{ bytes} = 2^5 \text{ bytes} \\
B &= \log_2(32) = 5 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Number of Sets, then Index Bits (I).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Cache Lines} &= \frac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Block Size}} = \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{32 \text{ B}} = \frac{2^{16}}{2^5} = 2^{11} \text{ lines} \\
\text{Number of Sets} &= \frac{\text{Number of Lines}}{\text{Associativity}} = \frac{2^{11}}{4} = \frac{2^{11}}{2^2} = 2^9 \text{ sets} \\
I &= \log_2(\text{Number of Sets}) = 9 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Tag Bits (T).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A - I - B = 24 - 9 - 5 = 10 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Verification (Mandatory KTU Check).**

$$
T + I + B = 10 + 9 + 5 = 24 = A \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Partition:** `[ Tag: 10 bits | Index: 9 bits | Block Offset: 5 bits ]`

### 3.1(b) Solution — Total Cache Bits

**Step 1: Data Bits (D).**

$$
D = \text{Cache Size in bits} = 64 \text{ KB} \times 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2: Overhead Bits per Line.**

Assume a **Write-Back** policy (so a Dirty bit is needed) and a Valid bit is always present.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead per line} &= \text{Tag bits} + \text{Valid bit} + \text{Dirty bit} \\
&= 10 + 1 + 1 = 12 \text{ bits per line}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total Overhead.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Lines} &= 2^{11} = 2048 \text{ lines} \\
\text{Total Overhead} &= 2048 \times 12 = 24{,}576 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Cache Storage Required.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cache Bits} &= D + \text{Overhead} \\
&= 524{,}288 + 24{,}576 = 558{,}864 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Express as a Percentage Overhead.**

$$
\text{Overhead \%} = \frac{24{,}576}{558{,}864} \times 100\% \approx 4.40\%
$$

### 3.1(c) Solution — AMAT Calculation

Given: $T_h = 1$ cycle, Miss Penalty = 20 cycles, Hit Rate $h = 0.80$, Miss Rate $m = 0.20$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= T_h + m \times T_{\text{miss penalty}} \\
\text{AMAT} &= 1 + (0.20) \times 20 \\
\text{AMAT} &= 1 + 4 \\
\text{AMAT} &= 5 \text{ clock cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, every memory access takes 5 clock cycles. Without the cache, every access would take 20 clock cycles — a **4x speedup**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Two-Level Cache AMAT

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU ESE Typical Style):**
> A system has L1 and L2 caches. The L1 cache has a hit time of 1 cycle, miss rate of 5%. The L2 cache has a hit time of 10 cycles, miss rate of 3%. The main memory access time is 100 cycles. Compute the AMAT seen by the processor.

**Step 1: Compute the effective miss penalty as seen by L1.**

The L1 miss penalty is the AMAT of L2 (i.e., the time it takes to satisfy an L1 miss, which goes through L2).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{L2} &= T_{h2} + m_2 \times T_{m2} \\
\text{AMAT}_{L2} &= 10 + (0.03) \times 100 \\
\text{AMAT}_{L2} &= 10 + 3 = 13 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the AMAT seen by the processor (i.e., AMAT for L1).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{L1} &= T_{h1} + m_1 \times \text{AMAT}_{L2} \\
\text{AMAT}_{L1} &= 1 + (0.05) \times 13 \\
\text{AMAT}_{L1} &= 1 + 0.65 = 1.65 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

Without the L2 cache, the L1 miss penalty would have been 100 cycles:
$$
\text{AMAT}_{\text{no L2}} = 1 + (0.05) \times 100 = 6 \text{ cycles}
$$
The L2 cache reduces the AMAT from 6 cycles to 1.65 cycles — a **3.6x improvement** in memory access performance.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cache Mapping Techniques Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is a KTU ESE high-frequency question. Memorize the relative advantages.**

| Parameter | Direct-Mapped | Fully Associative | n-way Set-Associative |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mapping Rule** | Each MM block maps to **exactly one** cache line: $\text{Line} = (\text{Block Number}) \bmod (\text{Number of Lines})$ | A block can be placed in **any** cache line. | A block maps to a specific **set**, but can occupy **any of n lines** within that set. |
| **Number of Sets** | Equals number of lines | Only 1 set | $\text{Cache Lines} / n$ |
| **Hardware Cost** | **Lowest** (1 comparator) | **Highest** (N comparators) | **Moderate** (n comparators per set) |
| **Lookup Speed** | **Fastest** (1 comparison) | **Slowest** (N parallel comparisons) | **Moderate** (n comparisons) |
| **Conflict Misses** | **Highest** | **Lowest** (zero) | **Reduced** (proportional to n) |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Simplest | Most complex | Moderate |
| **Use Case** | Small L1 caches, embedded systems | TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) | Modern L1/L2/L3 caches |

### 3.4 Detailed Mapping Mechanism Walkthrough

**Direct-Mapped Cache — Placement of MM Block Number B:**

$$
\text{Cache Line Number} = B \bmod (\text{Number of Lines})
$$

*Example:* A 16-line direct-mapped cache. Block 5 maps to line $5 \bmod 16 = 5$. Block 21 maps to line $21 \bmod 16 = 5$. This shows the **conflict problem** — blocks 5 and 21 fight for the same line.

**Fully Associative Cache — Placement of MM Block B:**

A block B can be placed in **any** of the N lines. The entire Tag must be stored and compared against B's tag on every access. Cost = N comparators.

**n-way Set-Associative Cache — Placement of MM Block B:**

$$
\text{Set Number} = B \bmod (\text{Number of Sets})
$$

Within the selected set, the block can occupy any of the n ways. The tag is compared against the n tags in the set. Cost = n comparators per set.

## 3.5 Replacement Algorithms (Detailed)

When a miss occurs and all candidate lines in a set are occupied, the cache controller must **evict** one line. KTU 2024 expects deep understanding of these four policies:

### 3.5.1 LRU — Least Recently Used

- **Rule:** Evict the line that has not been accessed for the longest time.
- **Rationale:** Exploits temporal locality.
- **Pros:** Near-optimal hit rate.
- **Cons:** Expensive to implement perfectly for high associativity (e.g., 16-way LRU requires hardware to track 16! orderings). For 4-way or 8-way, a simple matrix or tree-based pseudo-LRU is used.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Mention "uses age bits / stack" as the implementation mechanism.

### 3.5.2 FIFO — First In, First Out

- **Rule:** Evict the line that has been in the cache the longest, regardless of usage.
- **Rationale:** Treats cache like a circular queue.
- **Pros:** Simple to implement (just a counter per set).
- **Cons:** Can evict a frequently-used block (no concept of "recently used").

### 3.5.3 LFU — Least Frequently Used

- **Rule:** Evict the line with the lowest access count.
- **Pros:** Good for stable access patterns.
- **Cons:** A frequently-used block from the past may never be re-referenced, yet occupies space (cache pollution).

### 3.5.4 Random

- **Rule:** Pick any line at random.
- **Pros:** Trivially implemented; surprisingly competitive in practice.
- **Cons:** Non-deterministic, harder to analyze.

## 3.6 Write Policies — Detailed Analysis

The cache and main memory must be kept consistent. There are two primary write policies:

### 3.6.1 Write-Through Policy

- **Mechanism:** On every CPU write, data is written to **both the cache and main memory simultaneously**.
- **Pros:** Main memory is always up-to-date. Simple to implement. No need for a Dirty bit.
- **Cons:** Generates significant memory traffic; write bandwidth becomes a bottleneck.
- **Optimization:** Use a **Write Buffer** — a small FIFO queue between the cache and main memory. The CPU can proceed with the next operation while the write buffer drains to RAM.

### 3.6.2 Write-Back Policy

- **Mechanism:** On a CPU write, data is written to the cache line only. Main memory is updated **only when the line is evicted** from the cache.
- **Pros:** Minimizes memory traffic; multiple writes to the same line result in only one RAM write.
- **Cons:** Requires a **Dirty bit (Modified bit)** per line to track modifications. Main memory may be stale — other devices (e.g., DMA, I/O) may read stale data. Cache coherency in multi-core systems becomes a major issue.
- **Marking:** When a line is modified, its Dirty bit is set to 1. On eviction, if Dirty bit = 1, the cache controller performs a "write-back" to RAM.

### 3.6.3 Write-Miss Policies (Allocate on Write Miss)

When a write misses in the cache, two sub-policies apply:

| Policy | Behavior | Best With |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Write Allocate (Fetch on Write Miss)** | On a write miss, **fetch the block** from main memory into the cache, then perform the write to the cache. | Write-Back (clusters writes) |
| **No-Write Allocate (Write Around)** | On a write miss, **bypass the cache** entirely and write directly to main memory. | Write-Through (avoids cache pollution from one-time writes) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Standard Combination:** Most modern processors use **Write-Back + Write-Allocate** (e.g., Intel, AMD, ARM Cortex-A series).

## 3.7 Techniques to Reduce Cache Miss Rate

KTU syllabus requires the following six techniques (mentioned explicitly in the module):

1. **Larger Block Size:**
   - Exploits spatial locality.
   - Caveat: Increases miss penalty (larger block takes longer to fetch) and may increase conflict misses (the "cache pollution" effect). Typical optimum: 32-128 bytes.

2. **Higher Associativity:**
   - Reduces conflict misses.
   - Caveat: Increases hit time and hardware cost.
   - Trade-off curve flattens after 8-way associativity (diminishing returns).

3. **Victim Cache:**
   - A small fully-associative cache (typically 4-16 entries) placed between L1 and L2.
   - Stores recently-evicted blocks from L1. On an L1 miss, victim cache is checked first.
   - Rescues conflict-missed blocks.

4. **Pseudo-Associative (Column-Associative) Cache:**
   - Implemented as direct-mapped, but on a miss, the *other* possible location is checked before going to L2.
   - Offers most of the benefit of 2-way set-associative with direct-mapped speed.

5. **Hardware Prefetching:**
   - The cache controller anticipates future accesses and prefetches blocks before the CPU requests them.
   - E.g., **Stream Buffers** — prefetch sequential blocks ahead of the current access pattern.

6. **Compiler Optimizations (Software-controlled):**
   - **Loop Interchange:** Reorder nested loops to access memory in row-major order.
   - **Loop Fusion / Blocking (Tiling):** Reorganize loops to work on small data "tiles" that fit in cache, e.g., matrix multiplication blocked for L1 cache size.
   - **Data Layout Alignment:** Align arrays to cache line boundaries to avoid split-line accesses.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

## 4.4 Write Policy Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1["CPU Issues Memory Write to Address A"] --> W2["Check if A is in Cache HIT or MISS"]
    W2 -->|HIT| W3{"Which WRITE POLICY"}
    W3 -->|WRITE THROUGH| W4["Write Data to Cache Line AND to Main Memory<br/>Update Both Simultaneously"]
    W3 -->|WRITE BACK| W5["Write Data to Cache Line ONLY<br/>Set Dirty Bit EQUAL TO 1"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
