# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **"What is cache memory? Explain the principle of locality of reference."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Cache memory is a small, high-speed volatile memory that resides between the CPU and main memory. It stores a subset of the data and instructions from main memory to reduce the average memory access time, exploiting the fact that programs exhibit **locality of reference**.

**Principle of Locality of Reference** has two forms:
- **Temporal Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again soon (e.g., loop variables, instructions in a loop).
- **Spatial Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, nearby locations are likely to be referenced soon (e.g., sequential instruction execution, array traversal).

The cache exploits these properties to predict and prefetch data that the CPU will need, achieving a high hit rate and dramatically reducing AMAT.

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **"Differentiate between write-through and write-back cache policies."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Write-Through | Write-Back |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Update on Write | Cache + Main Memory (simultaneous) | Cache only |
| Main Memory State | Always up-to-date | May be stale |
| Dirty Bit Required | No | Yes (1 bit per line) |
| Memory Traffic | High (every write goes to RAM) | Low (writes batched on eviction) |
| Risk of Data Loss | Low | High on power failure before eviction |
| Performance | Slower for write-heavy workloads | Faster for write-heavy workloads |
| Common Use | Simple embedded systems | Modern high-performance CPUs |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the three cache mapping techniques with neat diagrams. Compare them in terms of **hardware complexity, hit rate, and access time**. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A 32 KB direct-mapped cache has a block size of 16 bytes. The main memory is 16 MB and is byte-addressable. Compute the **number of tag bits, index bits, and block offset bits**. What is the total number of bits stored in the cache (assuming a 1-bit valid field per line)? (7 Marks)

#### Solution to Question A(a)

**Three Cache Mapping Techniques:**

**1. Direct-Mapped Cache:**
In a direct-mapped cache, each block of main memory maps to **exactly one** specific cache line, determined by the formula:

$$
\text{Cache Line} = (\text{Main Memory Block Number}) \bmod (\text{Number of Cache Lines})
$$

*Diagram (text representation):*
```
Main Memory:    Cache:
+-------+      +-------+
|Block 0|----->|Line 0 |
|Block 1|----->|Line 1 |
|Block 2|----->|Line 2 |
|Block 3|----->|Line 3 |
| ...   |      | ...   |
|Block N|---+->|Line 0 |  (conflict)
+-------+   |  +-------+
            +--|Line 1 |  (block N+1)
```

**2. Fully Associative Cache:**
A block of main memory can be placed in **any** cache line. The full tag must be compared with the incoming address tag simultaneously against all stored tags. This requires N comparators for an N-line cache.

*Diagram (text representation):*
```
Main Memory:         Cache:
+-------+           +-------+
|Block 0|---------->|Line 0 |
|Block 0|---------->|Line 1 |  (any line)
|Block 0|---------->|Line 2 |
| ...   |           |Line 3 |
|Block N|---------->| ...   |
+-------+           |Line N-1|
                    +-------+
```

**3. n-way Set-Associative Cache:**
A compromise between the two extremes. Main memory is divided into a number of sets; each block maps to a unique set, but can occupy any of the n lines within that set. Set selection uses modular arithmetic, but within a set, the block can be in any of the n ways.

$$
\text{Set Number} = (\text{Main Memory Block Number}) \bmod (\text{Number of Sets})
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Direct-Mapped | Fully Associative | n-way Set-Associative |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hardware Complexity | **Lowest** (1 comparator) | **Highest** (N comparators) | **Moderate** (n comparators) |
| Hit Rate | Lowest (highest conflict) | Highest (zero conflict) | High (reduced conflict) |
| Access Time | Fastest | Slowest (parallel compare) | Moderate |
| Power Consumption | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| Typical Use | L1 caches, simple cores | TLBs, victim caches | L1/L2/L3 in modern CPUs |

#### Solution to Question A(b)

**Given Data:**
- Cache Size = 32 KB = $2^{15}$ bytes
- Block Size = 16 B = $2^4$ bytes
- Main Memory Size = 16 MB = $2^{24}$ bytes
- Mapping: Direct-Mapped (1-way)
- Valid Bit = 1 bit per line

**Step 1: Compute the Block Offset Bits (B).**

$$
B = \log_2(16) = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Number of Cache Lines and Index Bits (I).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Cache Lines} &= \frac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Block Size}} = \frac{32 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ B}} = \frac{2^{15}}{2^4} = 2^{11} \text{ lines} \\
I &= \log_2(2^{11}) = 11 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Address Bits (A) and Tag Bits (T).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \log_2(16 \text{ MB}) = \log_2(2^{24}) = 24 \text{ bits} \\
T &= A - I - B = 24 - 11 - 4 = 9 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Cache Storage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data Bits} &= 32 \text{ KB} \times 8 = 262{,}144 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Overhead per Line} &= \text{Tag bits} + \text{Valid bit} = 9 + 1 = 10 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Number of Lines} &= 2^{11} = 2048 \\
\text{Total Overhead} &= 2048 \times 10 = 20{,}480 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Total Cache Storage} &= 262{,}144 + 20{,}480 = 282{,}624 \text{ bits} \\
&= \frac{282{,}624}{8} = 35{,}328 \text{ bytes} \approx 34.5 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Tag = **9 bits**, Index = **11 bits**, Block Offset = **4 bits**, Total Cache Storage = **282,624 bits ≈ 34.5 KB**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Valid Bit** in total bits calculation — KTU students frequently miss this 1-bit overhead, leading to a 2048-bit undercount.
> 2. **Confusing "Number of Sets" with "Number of Lines"** in set-associative problems. The index bits correspond to the **number of sets**, not lines.
> 3. **Using cache size in bytes but dividing by block size in words** — always ensure consistent units.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** A system uses a two-level cache. L1 has a hit time of 1 cycle, miss rate of 8%. L2 has a hit time of 6 cycles, miss rate of 5%. Main memory access time is 80 cycles. Calculate the **AMAT seen by the processor**. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain the **various cache replacement policies** (LRU, FIFO, LFU, Random) and discuss their **advantages and disadvantages**. (7 Marks)

#### Solution to Question B(a)

**Given:**
- L1: $T_{h1} = 1$ cycle, $m_1 = 0.08$
- L2: $T_{h2} = 6$ cycles, $m_2 = 0.05$
- Main Memory: $T_{m2} = 80$ cycles

**Step 1: Compute the AMAT for L2 (L2 acts as the "next level" for L1 misses).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{L2} &= T_{h2} + m_2 \times T_{m2} \\
\text{AMAT}_{L2} &= 6 + (0.05) \times 80 \\
\text{AMAT}_{L2} &= 6 + 4 = 10 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the AMAT for L1 (this is the AMAT seen by the processor).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{L1} &= T_{h1} + m_1 \times \text{AMAT}_{L2} \\
\text{AMAT}_{L1} &= 1 + (0.08) \times 10 \\
\text{AMAT}_{L1} &= 1 + 0.8 = 1.8 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verification and Interpretation.**

To verify, the **global miss rate** (probability of accessing main memory) is:

$$
m_{\text{global}} = m_1 \times m_2 = 0.08 \times 0.05 = 0.004 = 0.4\%
$$

This means only 0.4% of all memory accesses actually reach DRAM — an excellent outcome achieved by the two-level hierarchy.

**Final Answer:** AMAT = **1.8 clock cycles** per memory access.

**Comparison (what-if analysis):**
- If L2 did not exist, miss penalty for L1 = 80 cycles: $\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.08 \times 80 = 7.4$ cycles.
- With L2: AMAT = 1.8 cycles. **Speedup factor = 7.4 / 1.8 ≈ 4.1x**.

#### Solution to Question B(b)

When a miss occurs in a set-associative or fully-associative cache and all candidate lines are occupied (valid), the cache controller must select one line for **eviction** using a **replacement policy**.

**1. Least Recently Used (LRU):**
- **Rule:** Evict the line that has not been accessed for the longest time.
- **Rationale:** Exploits temporal locality — a line not used recently is unlikely to be used soon.
- **Advantages:** Near-optimal hit rate; performs well across most workloads.
- **Disadvantages:** Costly to implement for high associativity (e.g., 16-way LRU is impractical due to combinatorial state); a tree-based pseudo-LRU is used in practice.
- **Implementation:** Age bits, stack, or matrix-based tracking.

**2. First-In-First-Out (FIFO):**
- **Rule:** Evict the line that has been in the cache the longest, regardless of recent usage.
- **Rationale:** Treats the cache as a circular queue; oldest arrival is evicted.
- **Advantages:** Very simple to implement (one pointer/counter per set); low hardware cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Can evict a frequently-used "old" block; ignores access patterns; lower hit rate than LRU.
- **Implementation:** A circular counter increments on each replacement.

**3. Least Frequently Used (LFU):**
- **Rule:** Evict the line with the lowest access count.
- **Rationale:** A line accessed often is "popular" and should be retained.
- **Advantages:** Good for stable access patterns with strong frequency bias.
- **Disadvantages:** A block accessed heavily in the past but no longer needed will continue to occupy cache space (**cache pollution**); requires saturating counters per line.
- **Implementation:** Access counter per line; requires aging/reset mechanisms.

**4. Random Replacement:**
- **Rule:** Pick any candidate line at random for eviction.
- **Rationale:** Surprisingly effective in practice; avoids the worst-case pathological behavior of LRU.
- **Advantages:** Trivially implemented (a pseudo-random number generator); no tracking state required.
- **Disadvantages:** Non-deterministic performance; can occasionally evict a hot line by bad luck; harder for designers to reason about worst-case.

**Summary Comparison Table:**

| Policy | Implementation Cost | Hit Rate | Worst-Case Behavior | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| LRU | High | Highest (near-optimal) | Reasonable | L1/L2 in modern CPUs |
| FIFO | Very Low | Moderate | Can evict hot blocks | Simple embedded systems |
| LFU | Moderate | High for skewed access | Cache pollution | Specialized accelerators |
| Random | Trivial | Surprisingly good | Pathological rare cases | Low-power/embedded cores |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(b):**
> 1. **Confusing LRU with FIFO:** LRU considers *recency of access*; FIFO considers *arrival order*. Mark this distinction explicitly in your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to discuss implementation cost:** KTU values the trade-off discussion. Always mention hardware overhead.
> 3. **For multi-level problems, the L2 AMAT must be computed FIRST** as the effective miss penalty for L1. Skipping this step yields the wrong answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this section for last-minute revision before the KTU ESE exam. Each bullet is a high-frequency test point.**

### A. Definitions You Must Know Word-for-Word
- **Cache Hit:** Data found in cache.
- **Cache Miss:** Data not found in cache; must be fetched from lower level.
- **AMAT:** Average Memory Access Time.
- **Locality of Reference:** Program property of clustered memory access.
- **Temporal Locality:** Same location accessed repeatedly.
- **Spatial Locality:** Adjacent locations accessed in sequence.
- **3 C's of Misses:** Compulsory, Capacity, Conflict.

### B. Key Formulas (Memorize)
- $\text{AMAT} = T_h + m \times T_{mp}$
- $\text{AMAT}_{L1} = T_{h1} + m_1 \times (T_{h2} + m_2 \times T_{m2})$
- $T = A - I - B$
- $\text{Total Cache Bits} = \text{Data} + \text{Number of Lines} \times (\text{Tag} + \text{Valid} + \text{Dirty})$
- $\text{Number of Sets} = \frac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Block Size} \times \text{Associativity}}$

### C. Address Partitioning Recipe
1. **Block Offset** = $\log_2(\text{Block Size})$
2. **Index** = $\log_2(\text{Number of Sets})$
3. **Tag** = Total Address Bits − Index − Block Offset
4. Always verify: $T + I + B = A$.

### D. Mapping Techniques
- **Direct-Mapped:** $B \bmod N_{\text{lines}}$ — one location only.
- **Fully Associative:** Anywhere — most flexible, most expensive.
- **Set-Associative:** $B \bmod N_{\text{sets}}$, then any way within set — the modern standard.

### E. Replacement Policies
- **LRU** = evict the line not used for the **longest time**.
- **FIFO** = evict the line that **arrived first**.
- **LFU** = evict the line used the **least number of times**.
- **Random** = evict a line **at random**.

### F. Write Policies
- **Write-Through:** Cache + Main Memory updated together (always consistent).
- **Write-Back:** Cache only, main memory updated on eviction (uses **Dirty bit**).
- **Write-Allocate:** On write miss, fetch block into cache first.
- **No-Write-Allocate (Write-Around):** On write miss, write to main memory, bypass cache.
- **Modern Standard Pairing:** Write-Back + Write-Allocate.

### G. Multi-Level Cache AMAT Computation (Critical Procedure)
1. Compute AMAT of deepest level first (innermost).
2. Substitute upward — each level's AMAT becomes the next level's miss penalty.
3. Final AMAT is the AMAT of L1.
4. Always report the **global miss rate** $= m_1 \times m_2 \times \ldots \times m_n$ for completeness.

### H. Six Techniques to Reduce Miss Rate
1. Larger block size (caveat: larger miss penalty).
2. Higher associativity (caveat: longer hit time).
3. Victim cache (small FA between L1 and L2).
4. Pseudo-associative (column-associative) cache.
5. Hardware prefetching / stream buffers.
6. Compiler optimizations (loop interchange, blocking/tiling, alignment).

### I. Examiner's Top 5 Loss-of-Marks Mistakes
1. Forgetting the **valid bit** in total cache size calculation.
2. Forgetting to include **dirty bit** when the policy is **write-back**.
3. Confusing **number of sets** vs. **number of lines** in set-associative addressing.
4. Mixing **byte-offset** and **word-offset** in block offset computation.
5. For the AMAT, using the wrong recursion (e.g., not nesting L2 hit time inside L1 miss penalty).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Final Exam Strategy for this Module:**
> - Part A (3 marks): Expect direct definition questions on **locality**, **3 C's of misses**, **write policies**, or **replacement policies**.
> - Part B (14 marks): Expect a **numerical on address decomposition** (always 7 marks) paired with a **theory question on mapping techniques, replacement, or write policy** (7 marks).
> - **Diagrams are scoring gold:** Always draw the mapping diagram for direct-mapped, fully-associative, and set-associative. Marks are reserved for visuals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Memory: Address Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual Memory: Address Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 – Virtual Memory: Address Translation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Virtual Memory** is a storage allocation technique in which a computer's main memory is augmented with a secondary storage (typically a hard disk or SSD) acting as a logical extension of physical RAM. **Address Translation** is the hardware-supported process of dynamically mapping every **Virtual Address (VA)** issued by the CPU into a unique **Physical Address (PA)** in main memory, executed transparently by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**.

The CPU executes every instruction using only **virtual addresses** belonging to a process-specific logical address space. The MMU, in coordination with the **Page Table** and the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)**, converts these virtual addresses into physical addresses at run-time, without any software intervention on the critical path.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Library Card Catalogue)

Imagine a vast university library containing **10 million** physical books (this is the **physical memory**). Each book has a unique shelf number (the **physical address**), e.g., `A-23, Shelf-4`. 

Now, every student is given a **logical card catalogue** (this is the **virtual address space**), where books are listed by topic and abstract number, e.g., `Computer Architecture – Book #4521`. 

When a student requests `Book #4521`, the **librarian (MMU)** flips open the **central index register (page table)**, looks up the abstract number, and walks to the real shelf. The student never learns the shelf number — only the abstract one. This decouples how the user thinks about data from where the data physically lives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The **virtual address space** is a CPU-illusion; it is *not* a real hardware storage.
> - The **physical address space** corresponds to the actual silicon cells of main memory.
> - The **page table** is the bridge — it is held in main memory itself and is consulted by hardware on every memory access.

### 1.3 Why is Address Translation Needed?

| Reason | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Process Isolation** | One program cannot corrupt another's memory |
| **Memory Over-commitment** | More virtual space than physical RAM is exposed |
| **Efficient Disk Paging** | Inactive pages are swapped out to free physical frames |
| **Simplified Linking/Loading** | Programs can be linked at *any* virtual base address |
| **Sharing & Protection** | Read/Write/Execute bits enforce access control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Virtual Memory Systems:**
> - **Page size (P):** Typically **4 KB** (2¹² bytes) on x86, or **4 MB / 2 MB** on some RISC architectures.
> - **TLB size:** Usually **64 – 1024 entries**, fully associative, with lookup latency of **1 – 2 clock cycles**.
> - **TLB hit ratio:** Practically observed between **0.80 and 0.99** for well-tuned workloads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Virtual Address

A virtual address of size $(p + d)$ bits is logically split into two fields:

$$\text{VA} = \underbrace{VPN}_{\text{Virtual Page Number (p bits)}} \;\Big\vert\; \underbrace{d}_{\text{Page Offset (d bits)}}$$

- **VPN (Virtual Page Number):** Identifies which *virtual page* the CPU is requesting. Used as an **index into the Page Table**.
- **Page Offset (d bits):** Identifies the *byte within* the page. The offset bits are *not translated*; they are passed through unchanged because pages and frames are the **same size**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The page offset passes through the MMU **unchanged**. Only the VPN portion is translated into a PPN. This is a recurring KTU question.

A physical address of size $(q + d)$ bits has the form:

$$\text{PA} = \underbrace{PPN}_{\text{Physical Page Number (q bits)}} \;\Big\vert\; \underbrace{d}_{\text{Page Offset (d bits)}}$$

## 2.2 The Address Translation Pipeline — Step-by-Step

The hardware performs translation in this canonical order:

1. **CPU emits a virtual address** on the address bus.
2. The MMU **splits the VA** into `VPN` and `offset`.
3. The MMU **queries the TLB** in parallel with possible cache lookup:
   - **TLB Hit:** PPN is extracted in `1 – 2 cycles`; physical address is formed.
   - **TLB Miss:** The MMU performs a **page-table walk** in main memory, fetching the required **Page Table Entry (PTE)**.
4. The physical address `PA = PPN \mid d` is sent to the cache / main memory.
5. Data is returned to the CPU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **PTE (Page Table Entry)** typically contains: **Valid bit, Frame number (PPN), Protection bits (R/W/X), Dirty bit, Reference bit, Caching-disabled bit**. The KTU examiner frequently asks for a PTE layout diagram.

## 2.3 Role of the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)

The TLB is a small, **fully-associative**, **content-addressable** hardware cache that stores *recent* VPN → PPN mappings. Without the TLB, **every** memory access would require at least one extra main-memory access to consult the page table — effectively **halving CPU throughput**.

> [!NOTE]
> **TLB properties to memorise for KTU:**
> - Associativity: **Fully associative** (for maximum flexibility).
> - Replacement policy: typically **LRU** or **random**.
> - Lookup mechanism: **parallel** content-addressable match.
> - On miss, a **page-table walk** is initiated by the MMU hardware (x86: hardware page walker; some RISC: software-loaded TLB).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Page size (bytes) | $P = 2^{d}$ | Determined by offset bits $d$ |
| 2 | Number of virtual pages | $N_{v} = 2^{p}$ | Determined by VPN bits $p$ |
| 3 | Number of physical frames | $N_{f} = 2^{q}$ | Determined by PPN bits $q$ |
| 4 | Virtual address space size | $VAS = 2^{p+d}$ bytes | Total logical space |
| 5 | Physical address space size | $PAS = 2^{q+d}$ bytes | Real RAM size |
| 6 | Page Table size (bytes) | $\vert PT \vert = N_{v} \times \vert PTE\vert$ | $N_{v}$ entries, each $\vert PTE\vert$ wide |
| 7 | **EAT (1-level paging, TLB)** | $EAT = t + m(2 - h)$ | $h$ = hit ratio, $t$ = TLB time, $m$ = memory time |
| 8 | **EAT (2-level paging, TLB)** | $EAT = t + m(3 - 2h)$ | Miss requires 2 page-table accesses + data |
| 9 | **EAT (3-level paging, TLB)** | $EAT = t + m(4 - 3h)$ | Miss requires 3 page-table accesses + data |
| 10 | Memory overhead ratio | $r = \dfrac{\vert PT \vert}{PAS}$ | Useful for sizing analyses |
| 11 | Hit ratio from EAT | $h = \dfrac{t + km - EAT}{m(k-1)}$ | $k$ = total memory accesses on a miss |
| 12 | TLB reach (bytes) | $R = \vert TLB \vert \times P$ | Largest contiguous VA covered by TLB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the use of `\vert` for absolute-value / set-size notation. KTU examiners often give marks for the correct **closed-form** EAT expression in the final line, so always simplify.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The address-translation mechanism is the **backbone of process isolation** in every modern OS — from **Windows** and **Linux** to **RTOS** systems in embedded automotive ECUs. Concepts such as **kernel virtual address space**, **ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization)**, **demand paging**, and **container memory cgroups** in **Docker / Kubernetes** are all direct descendants of the address translation pipeline studied here.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Effective Access Time (EAT) for Single-Level Paging

### 3.1.1 Setup & Symbol Definitions

Let the following symbols hold for the entire derivation:

- $t$ = **TLB lookup time** (time to compare a VPN against all TLB entries)
- $m$ = **Main memory access time** (time to read one word from physical RAM)
- $h$ = **TLB hit ratio** (fraction of memory references served by TLB)
- $(1 - h)$ = **TLB miss ratio**

The CPU issues one virtual address per memory reference. The translation system must resolve the address *before* the data access can complete. The total time per memory reference is therefore:

$$T_{ref} = T_{translation} + T_{data}$$

### 3.1.2 Case Analysis

**Case A — TLB Hit (probability = h):**

Only two steps occur:
1. TLB lookup (cost $= t$)
2. Single main-memory access to read the data (cost $= m$)

$$T_{hit} = t + m$$

**Case B — TLB Miss (probability = $1 - h$):**

Three steps occur:
1. TLB lookup (cost $= t$) — yields *no match*
2. Page table access in main memory (cost $= m$) — fetches the PTE
3. Data access in main memory (cost $= m$) — fetches the requested word

$$T_{miss} = t + m + m = t + 2m$$

### 3.1.3 Weighted Average (EAT)

The Effective Access Time is the expected value of $T_{ref}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= h \cdot T_{hit} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{miss} \\
&= h(t + m) + (1 - h)(t + 2m) \quad \text{— substitute case times} \\
&= ht + hm + (t + 2m - ht - 2hm) \quad \text{— distribute $(1-h)$} \\
&= ht + hm + t + 2m - ht - 2hm \quad \text{— open the bracket} \\
&= (ht - ht) + (hm - 2hm) + t + 2m \quad \text{— group like terms} \\
&= 0 - hm + t + 2m \quad \text{— cancel and combine} \\
&= t + 2m - hm \quad \text{— final simplified form} \\
&= \boxed{\,t + m(2 - h)\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The closed-form result $EAT = t + m(2 - h)$ is the single most important formula in this module.** Memorise it — almost every Part-B question in KTU on this topic uses it.

### 3.1.4 Numerical Verification (Worked Example)

Suppose a paging system has:
- $t = 20\,\text{ns}$
- $m = 100\,\text{ns}$
- $h = 0.80$ (80%)

Plug in:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 20 + 100 \times (2 - 0.80) \\
&= 20 + 100 \times 1.20 \\
&= 20 + 120 \\
&= 140\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-check by direct weighted sum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 0.80 \times (20 + 100) + 0.20 \times (20 + 100 + 100) \\
&= 0.80 \times 120 + 0.20 \times 220 \\
&= 96 + 44 = 140\,\text{ns} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Both paths agree. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — EAT for Two-Level Paging

### 3.2.1 Modified Miss Cost

In a **two-level page table**, the virtual page number is split into two sub-indices: $P_1$ (page directory index) and $P_2$ (page-of-PT index). On a TLB miss, the MMU walks **two** page tables before reaching the data:

1. TLB lookup $\Rightarrow t$
2. Access **outer page table** (PDE) $\Rightarrow m$
3. Access **inner page table** (PTE) $\Rightarrow m$
4. Access the **data** itself $\Rightarrow m$

$$T_{miss}^{(2\text{-level})} = t + 3m$$

### 3.2.2 EAT Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= h(t + m) + (1 - h)(t + 3m) \quad \text{— hit/miss times} \\
&= ht + hm + t + 3m - ht - 3hm \quad \text{— distribute $(1-h)$} \\
&= (ht - ht) + (hm - 3hm) + t + 3m \quad \text{— group} \\
&= 0 - 2hm + t + 3m \quad \text{— combine} \\
&= \boxed{\,t + m(3 - 2h)\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Generalised n-Level Paging Formula

By induction, for an $n$-level page table:

$$EAT_{(n)} = t + m\bigl((n+1) - n \cdot h\bigr)$$

This neatly generalises:
- $n = 1$: $EAT = t + m(2 - h)$ ✓
- $n = 2$: $EAT = t + m(3 - 2h)$ ✓
- $n = 3$: $EAT = t + m(4 - 3h)$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Computing Page Table Size

Given a virtual address of $(p + d)$ bits, the page table has $N_v = 2^p$ entries. If each PTE is $w$ bytes wide:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert PT \vert &= N_v \times w \quad \text{bytes} \\
&= 2^{p} \times w \quad \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** A system has 32-bit virtual addresses, 4 KB pages, and 4-byte PTEs. Then $d = 12$, $p = 20$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_v &= 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ entries} \\
\vert PT \vert &= 2^{20} \times 4\,\text{B} = 4\,\text{MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows why **multi-level page tables** are essential on 32-bit and 64-bit systems — a single-level page table would consume megabytes or even gigabytes of contiguous memory.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Hit Ratio and Hierarchy

| $h$ (hit ratio) | $EAT$ (1-level, ns, $t$=20, $m$=100) | $EAT$ (2-level, ns) | Comment |
|---:|---:|---:|---|
| 0.50 | $20 + 100(1.5) = 170$ | $20 + 100(2) = 220$ | TLB of little use |
| 0.70 | $20 + 100(1.3) = 150$ | $20 + 100(1.6) = 180$ | Moderate |
| 0.80 | $20 + 100(1.2) = 140$ | $20 + 100(1.4) = 160$ | Typical |
| 0.90 | $20 + 100(1.1) = 130$ | $20 + 100(1.2) = 140$ | Good |
| 0.99 | $20 + 100(1.01) = 121$ | $20 + 100(1.02) = 122$ | Near-optimal |
| 1.00 | $20 + 100(1) = 120$ | $20 + 100(1) = 120$ | TLB always hits |

> [!NOTE]
> Observe how rapidly EAT converges to $t + m$ as $h \to 1$. This is precisely why operating systems devote so much engineering effort to **page colouring**, **huge-page support**, and **TLB shootdown minimisation**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Address Translation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[CPU generates Virtual Address VA] --> SPLIT[MMU splits VA into VPN and Offset]
    SPLIT --> TLBCheck{TLB Lookup for VPN}
    TLBCheck -->|TLB Hit| GetPPN1[Read PPN directly from TLB]
    TLBCheck -->|TLB Miss| WalkOuter[Access Outer Page Table in RAM]
    WalkOuter --> WalkInner[Access Inner Page Table in RAM]
    WalkInner --> GetPPN2[Read PPN from Page Table Entry]
    GetPPN2 --> UpdateTLB[Update TLB with new VPN to PPN mapping]
    GetPPN1 --> FormPA[Form Physical Address by concatenating PPN and Offset]
    UpdateTLB --> FormPA
    FormPA --> MemAccess[Issue Physical Address to Main Memory]
    MemAccess --> ReadData[Read Data Word from RAM]
    ReadData --> ReturnData[Return Data Word to CPU over Data Bus]
```

**How to read this diagram (left-to-right, top-to-bottom):**
1. The CPU emits a virtual address.
2. The MMU splits the address and queries the TLB.
3. **Hit path** (top branch): translation completes in $\approx t$ time.
4. **Miss path** (bottom branch): the page-table walker fetches two levels of PTEs, updates the TLB, and proceeds.
5. The combined PPN + offset is sent to main memory; data is returned.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — PTE Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    PTE[Page Table Entry - 32 bits] --> ValidBit[Valid Bit - 1 bit]
    PTE --> ProtBits[Protection R W X - 3 bits]
    PTE --> RefBit[Reference Bit - 1 bit]
    PTE --> DirtyBit[Dirty Bit - 1 bit]
    PTE --> Caching[Caching Disabled Bit - 1 bit]
    PTE --> FrameNum[Frame Number PPN - 25 bits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The PTE bit-layout above is one common example. KTU problems usually specify a custom layout (e.g., 1 V-bit, 4 protection bits, 20 PPN bits, etc.) — always use the layout given in the question.

## 4.3 Mermaid — Two-Level Paging Address Decomposition

```mermaid
graph LR
    VA[32 bit Virtual Address] --> P1[Bits 31 to 22 - P1 - Page Directory Index - 10 bits]
    VA --> P2[Bits 21 to 12 - P2 - Page Table Index - 10 bits]
    VA --> OFF[Bits 11 to 0 - Offset within page - 12 bits]
    P1 --> PT1[Outer Page Directory at CR3 base]
    PT1 --> PT2[Inner Page Table located at PDE address]
    P2 --> PT2
    PT2 --> PA[32 bit Physical Address - PPN 20 bits plus Offset 12 bits]
    OFF --> PA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a two-level scheme the **offset width = page size exponent** and the **PPN width = physical address width − offset width**. The VPN is partitioned into $P_1$ and $P_2$ whose sum of bit-widths equals $p$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define virtual memory. With a neat block diagram, explain how a virtual address is translated to a physical address.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Virtual memory is a memory management technique that uses secondary storage as a logical extension of main memory, presenting a large, contiguous, private address space to every process. Address translation converts a Virtual Address (VA) issued by the CPU into a Physical Address (PA) in main RAM, with the help of a Page Table and (typically) a TLB.

**Diagram & Explanation (2 marks):**

```
  +--------+      +---------+      +---------+      +--------+
  |  CPU   | ---> |   MMU   | ---> |   TLB   | ---> |  RAM   |
  |  VA    | VA   | split   |      | lookup  | PPN  |  Data  |
  +--------+      +---------+      +---------+      +--------+
                       | miss
                       v
                  +-----------+
                  |Page Table |
                  |  in RAM   |
                  +-----------+
```

The MMU extracts the VPN from the VA, queries the TLB, and on a miss walks the page table to fetch the PTE. The PPN and the page offset are concatenated to form the physical address.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? Why is it essential for performance in a paged system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A TLB is a small, fully-associative, hardware cache that stores recently used **VPN → PPN** translations. It is consulted on every memory reference before the page table in main memory is accessed.

**Why essential (1.5 marks):** Without the TLB, every memory access would require an extra main-memory access to read the page table, effectively **doubling** the access time and halving CPU throughput. The TLB exploits the principle of **locality** (both temporal and spatial), serving the vast majority of translations in 1–2 clock cycles and bringing EAT close to the ideal $t + m$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to **add the data-access time** after TLB miss cost — students often compute miss time as $t + m$ and skip the final $m$. Cost: **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 2. Confusing the hit and miss branches when expanding EAT — losing signs in the algebra. **Always** show the substitution step.
> 3. Writing the offset bits as needing translation — they are **passed through unchanged**.
> 4. In multi-level EAT problems, using the wrong number of memory accesses on miss. **Memorise**: $n$-level paging ⇒ $n$ page-table accesses on miss.
> 5. Not simplifying to a closed form — full marks require the final $EAT = t + m(2-h)$ form.

---

### Question A (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### (a) **Explain the address translation mechanism in a paged virtual memory system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of TLB, page table, and MMU.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Components of a virtual address (1 mark):** A virtual address has two fields: **Virtual Page Number (VPN)** and **Page Offset (d bits)**. The offset bits are passed through the MMU unchanged.

**Step 2 — Role of the MMU (1 mark):** The Memory Management Unit is a dedicated hardware block between the CPU and main memory. It intercepts every virtual address, performs the translation, and emits a physical address.

**Step 3 — Role of the Page Table (2 marks):** The page table is an array of **Page Table Entries (PTEs)** stored in main memory. The VPN is used as an index. Each PTE contains the **valid bit, PPN, protection bits, dirty bit, and reference bit**. A page-table walk fetches the PTE on a TLB miss.

**Step 4 — Role of the TLB (2 marks):** The TLB is a small, fully-associative cache of recent translations. On a hit, the PPN is obtained in 1–2 cycles; on a miss, the MMU walks the page table and refills the TLB.

**Step 5 — Block diagram (1 mark):** *(See Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic.)*

The complete translation process: CPU → MMU (split VA) → TLB (lookup) → (on miss) Page Table walk → form PA → Memory → Data → CPU.

---

#### (b) **A paging system has a TLB with 90% hit ratio. TLB lookup time is 10 ns and main memory access time is 100 ns. Compute the Effective Access Time (EAT).** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $t = 10\,\text{ns}$
- $m = 100\,\text{ns}$
- $h = 0.90$
- Single-level page table (1 PT access on miss)

**Case analysis (2 marks):**
- TLB hit: $T_{hit} = t + m = 10 + 100 = 110\,\text{ns}$
- TLB miss: $T_{miss} = t + 2m = 10 + 200 = 210\,\text{ns}$

**EAT formula (1 mark):**
$$EAT = h \cdot T_{hit} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{miss}$$

**Substitution & computation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 0.90 \times 110 + 0.10 \times 210 \\
&= 99 + 21 \\
&= 120\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using closed-form (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= t + m(2 - h) \\
&= 10 + 100(2 - 0.90) \\
&= 10 + 100 \times 1.10 \\
&= 10 + 110 = 120\,\text{ns} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{EAT = 120\,\text{ns}}$

---

### Question B (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

#### (a) **With a neat diagram, explain the working of a two-level paging system. How does it reduce the size of the page table compared to single-level paging?** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):** A single-level page table for a 32-bit system with 4 KB pages contains $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ entries, occupying 4 MB of contiguous memory per process — even if most pages are unused.

**Step 2 — Structure of the 2-level scheme (2 marks):** The VPN is split into $P_1$ and $P_2$. The **outer page directory** (indexed by $P_1$) holds pointers to **inner page tables** (each indexed by $P_2$). Only inner page tables for *populated* virtual regions are allocated.

**Step 3 — Address translation flow (2 marks):**
1. Read **PDE** from outer page directory at fixed base (CR3 in x86).
2. Read **PTE** from inner page table located at PDE address.
3. Combine PPN with offset to form PA.

**Step 4 — Size reduction (2 marks):** Memory consumed is proportional to the *used* address space, not the entire virtual address space. A typical sparse process with 1 MB actually in use may consume only a handful of inner page tables + one outer page directory — a 99%+ reduction. The trade-off is **3 memory accesses** on a TLB miss (one per level + data) instead of 2.

*(Refer to the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.3 for the address decomposition.)*

---

#### (b) **In a two-level paging system, TLB access time = 20 ns, main memory access time = 80 ns, and TLB hit ratio = 0.85. Calculate the Effective Access Time.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $t = 20\,\text{ns}$
- $m = 80\,\text{ns}$
- $h = 0.85$
- Two-level page table: **3 memory accesses on TLB miss** (2 for page tables + 1 for data)

**Case analysis (2 marks):**
- TLB hit: $T_{hit} = t + m = 20 + 80 = 100\,\text{ns}$
- TLB miss: $T_{miss} = t + 3m = 20 + 240 = 260\,\text{ns}$

**EAT formula (1 mark):**
$$EAT = h \cdot T_{hit} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{miss}$$

**Substitution & computation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 0.85 \times 100 + 0.15 \times 260 \\
&= 85 + 39 \\
&= 124\,\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using closed-form (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= t + m(3 - 2h) \\
&= 20 + 80(3 - 2 \times 0.85) \\
&= 20 + 80(3 - 1.70) \\
&= 20 + 80 \times 1.30 \\
&= 20 + 104 = 124\,\text{ns} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{EAT = 124\,\text{ns}}$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Virtual address = VPN + Offset**; **Physical address = PPN + Offset**; the offset is never translated.
- [x] **Page size** is fixed by the offset width: $P = 2^{d}$ bytes.
- [x] **Virtual pages** = $2^{p}$; **Physical frames** = $2^{q}$.
- [x] **Page Table size** = $2^{p} \times \vert PTE \vert$ bytes.
- [x] The **MMU** is the hardware unit performing translation; it is *transparent* to the running process.
- [x] The **TLB** is a fully-associative hardware cache of recent translations; *misses* trigger a **page-table walk** in main memory.
- [x] **Single-level EAT:** $EAT = t + m(2 - h)$.
- [x] **Two-level EAT:** $EAT = t + m(3 - 2h)$.
- [x] **n-level generalisation:** $EAT = t + m\bigl((n+1) - n \cdot h\bigr)$.
- [x] On TLB miss, count **all** memory accesses: $n$ for page tables $+ 1$ for data = $n+1$ total.
- [x] **PTE contents** to memorise: V-bit, PPN, R/W/X, Dirty, Reference, Cache-disable.
- [x] **Multi-level page tables** shrink the resident page-table size by allocating only the inner tables that are actually populated.
- [x] **TLB reach** $= \vert TLB \vert \times \text{page size}$ — governs how much virtual memory can be accessed without a TLB miss.
- [x] **Address translation is on the critical path of every memory access** — without the TLB, performance halves. This is why modern CPUs devote silicon area and complexity to TLB hierarchy (L1 TLB, L2 TLB, separate I-TLB and D-TLB).
- [x] **Always simplify EAT to closed-form** in the answer — the KTU examiner awards the final mark only for the simplified expression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Page Table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Page Table ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **paging** and **virtual memory management** within the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** architecture, a **Page Table** is defined as a fundamental kernel-maintained, per-process **data structure** that resides in **physical memory (RAM)** and is used by the operating system and hardware to perform the dynamic, run-time translation of **virtual (logical) addresses** generated by the CPU into their corresponding **physical (real) addresses** in main memory. Formally, a Page Table is an **array of Page Table Entries (PTEs)**, indexed by the **virtual page number (VPN)**, where each entry stores the **physical frame number (PFN)** along with critical control bits such as the **Valid/Invalid bit**, **Dirty bit**, **Reference bit**, and **Protection/Access bits**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Page Table is the cornerstone of **Demand Paging** and is mandatory for implementing the **Virtual Address → Physical Address mapping** process. Every B.Tech student must master its structure because it is a high-weightage topic linking Module 2 (Memory Hierarchy) with Module 3 (Paging & Virtual Memory). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are in a **massive library** (physical memory) with **billions of books** (frames), but as a reader, you don't know where each book is physically kept. Instead, you interact with a **digital catalog** (the Page Table) that lives at the librarian's desk. When you ask for "Book #5,427" (your **virtual address**), the librarian does not give you the shelf position directly; he looks up his catalog, sees that Book #5,427 is actually located on Shelf B-12, Row 4, Slot 9 (the **physical frame**), and then fetches it for you. The **catalog** is the **Page Table**. The **book number you request** is the **Virtual Page Number (VPN)**. The **shelf position** is the **Physical Frame Number (PFN)**. The **librarian** is the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**. The **catalogue index → shelf mapping** is a **Page Table Entry (PTE)**. This decoupling is what allows every program to behave as if it owns the *entire* memory from address `0x00000000` upwards, even though it physically shares RAM with dozens of other programs. ### 1.3 Core Standard Metrics The following standard parameters govern the design and sizing of any Page Table in a KTU-level computer architecture problem: - **Page Size (P)** = Typically **4 KB (2¹² bytes)**, though 8 KB, 16 KB, and 4 MB (Huge Pages) are also common. - **Virtual Address Space Size (V)** = Process address space, e.g., **2³² bytes (4 GB)** for a 32-bit system. - **Physical Address Space Size (R)** = Real RAM, e.g., **2³² bytes (4 GB)**. - **Number of Virtual Pages (N_v)** = V / P - **Number of Physical Frames (N_p)** = R / P - **Page Table Entry size (E)** = Typically **4 bytes (32 bits)**. > [!NOTE] > The total memory consumed by a single-level page table for a process is given by the standard KTU formula: **Page Table Size = (V / P) × E bytes**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown: How a Page Table Translates an Address The MMU performs a strictly sequential, hardware-accelerated operation whenever the CPU issues a memory access. The logic is as follows: 1. **Address Splitting:** The CPU generates a logical address of width `b` bits. The MMU splits this into two fields: - **Virtual Page Number (VPN)** occupying the higher-order bits. - **Page Offset (d)** occupying the lower-order bits, where the offset remains **unchanged** during translation (because the unit of mapping is a page, not a byte). 2. **Indexing:** The VPN is used as an **index** into the Page Table array. 3. **Lookup:** The corresponding **Page Table Entry (PTE)** is fetched from physical memory. 4. **Validity Check:** The **Valid (V) bit** of the PTE is examined. - If `V = 1` → the page is in RAM; proceed to step 5. - If `V = 0` → **Page Fault** is raised; the OS swaps the page in from disk and re-issues the instruction. 5. **Frame Extraction:** The **Physical Frame Number (PFN)** is extracted from the PTE and concatenated with the original page offset to form the final physical address. 6. **Protection Check:** Hardware compares the requested access type (Read/Write/Execute) against the **Protection Bits** stored in the PTE. A mismatch causes a **Segmentation Fault** (or "Access Violation"). ### 2.2 Standard Page Table Entry (PTE) Layout A 32-bit PTE typically contains the following fields, which are **examined verbatim** in KTU exam questions: | PTE Field | Typical Width (bits) | Purpose (KTU Standard) | |-------------------|----------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------| | Frame Number | 20 | Physical Frame Number where the page resides in RAM. | | Valid/Present Bit | 1 | `1` if page is in memory, `0` if a Page Fault is needed. | | Reference Bit | 1 | Set by hardware on any access; used by LRU/Clock algorithms. | | Dirty Bit | 1 | Set by hardware on any write; tells OS whether to write-back. | | Protection Bits | 2 or 3 | Encodes R, W, X permissions (e.g., 00=RW, 01=R, 10=RX, 11=RWX). | | Caching Bit | 1 | Disables cache for memory-mapped I/O pages. | | Global Bit | 1 | Page is not flushed from TLB on context switch. | | Available/Reserved| 5 or 6 | Used by OS for its own bookkeeping (e.g., PID, age). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Total PTE size** must equal a power of 2. The classic 32-bit PTE sums up to exactly **32 bits = 4 bytes**, which is why PTEs fit one-per-word on almost every KTU reference architecture. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table consolidates every high-yield equation for KTU 2024 Scheme exam problems. **The vertical bar symbol `\vert` is used instead of `\|` to keep the table parser-safe.** | Quantity | Formula (KTU Standard) | Unit | |-------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|---------------| | Page Offset bits (`d`) | $d = \log_2(P)$ | bits | | Virtual Page Number bits (`p`) | $p = \log_2(V \div P)$ | bits | | Physical Frame Number bits (`f`) | $f = \log_2(R \div P)$ | bits | | Total Virtual Address bits (`b`) | $b = p + d$ | bits | | Number of Virtual Pages (`N_v`) | $N_v = 2^{p}$ | pages | | Number of Physical Frames (`N_p`) | $N_p = 2^{f}$ | frames | | Page Table Size (Single-Level) | $S_{PT} = N_v \times E$ | bytes | | Effective Access Time (with TLB) | $EAT = (1 - p) \times T_m + p \times (T_m + T_{TLB})$ | nanoseconds | | Effective Access Time (no TLB) | $EAT = 2 \times T_m$ | nanoseconds | | Memory Overhead Ratio | $\text{Overhead} = S_{PT} \div V$ | dimensionless | | Page Fault Service Time (PFST) | $PFST = T_{swap} + T_{restart}$ | time units | Where: - $T_m$ = Memory access time (e.g., **100 ns**). - $T_{TLB}$ = TLB access time (e.g., **20 ns**). - $p$ = TLB hit ratio. - $T_{swap}$ = Disk I/O latency. - $T_{restart}$ = Instruction restart overhead. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Page Table is not just a textbook concept. It is the **silent enabler** of every modern computing paradigm: - **Cloud Computing & Server Virtualization:** Hypervisors (VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V) use **nested/extended Page Tables (EPT/NPT)** to let multiple guest OSes run efficiently on one host. Without the page table, **memory isolation** between tenants in AWS EC2 or Azure VMs would be impossible. - **Mobile Operating Systems:** Android and iOS use paging to enforce **per-app sandboxing**, which is mandated by their security models. - **Databases (PostgreSQL, Oracle):** Database engines carefully design PTE access patterns to ensure that hash joins and B-Tree traversals cause **minimal TLB misses**, because a TLB miss is more expensive than an L1 cache miss. - **Embedded Systems & IoT:** Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) like FreeRTOS and VxWorks use **Inverted Page Tables** to keep memory overhead bounded, which is critical on devices with only **256 KB to 2 MB of RAM**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Page Table Size and Memory Overhead **Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical):** A system uses a 32-bit virtual address space, a page size of 4 KB, and 4 bytes per Page Table Entry. Compute (a) the number of virtual pages, (b) the total page table size, and (c) the memory overhead ratio if the physical RAM is 64 MB. **Step 1: Identify the standard parameters.** - Virtual Address Space: $V = 2^{32}$ bytes - Page Size: $P = 4 \text{ KB} = 2^{12}$ bytes - PTE Size: $E = 4$ bytes $= 2^{2}$ bytes - Physical Address Space: $R = 64 \text{ MB} = 2^{26}$ bytes **Step 2: Compute the Page Offset bits (`d`).** The offset must be able to address every byte *within* a single page. So: $$d = \log_2(P) = \log_2(2^{12}) = 12 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3: Compute the Virtual Page Number bits (`p`).** The VPN must index every distinct page in the virtual space: $$p = \log_2\left(\frac{V}{P}\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{2^{32}}{2^{12}}\right) = \log_2(2^{20}) = 20 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 4: Compute the Number of Virtual Pages (`N_v`).** This is simply $2^p$, which equals the total count of PTEs needed: $$N_v = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pages}$$ **Step 5: Compute the Total Page Table Size (`S_PT`).** Multiply the number of pages by the size of one PTE: $$S_{PT} = N_v \times E = 2^{20} \times 2^{2} = 2^{22} \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}$$ **Step 6: Compute the Memory Overhead Ratio.** We compare the page table size against the entire virtual address space (which is what the OS must "support" via the page table): $$\text{Overhead} = \frac{S_{PT}}{V} = \frac{2^{22}}{2^{32}} = 2^{-10} = \frac{1}{1024} \approx 0.0977\%$$ **Step 7: Physical Frame Number bits (`f`) for completeness.** $$f = \log_2\left(\frac{R}{P}\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{2^{26}}{2^{12}}\right) = 14 \text{ bits}$$ **Final Analytical Result:** - Number of virtual pages: **1,048,576** - Page Table Size: **4 MB** - Memory Overhead: **≈ 0.0977%** - This is the **exact reason** why a 4 MB page table is acceptable on a 4 GB virtual space — the overhead is negligible compared to disk-backed virtual memory capacity. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Access Time (EAT) with and without a TLB **Problem Statement:** Consider a system with main memory access time $T_m = 200$ ns, TLB access time $T_{TLB} = 20$ ns, and a TLB hit ratio of 80%. Compute the Effective Access Time. Repeat the calculation for a hit ratio of 99%, and explain why the difference is significant. **Step 1: Define the EAT formula for TLB-enabled paging.** $$EAT = (1 - p) \times (T_m + T_{TLB}) + p \times (T_{TLB} + T_m)$$ This simplifies algebraically to the standard KTU form: $$EAT = T_{TLB} + (1 - p) \times T_m + p \times T_m = T_{TLB} + T_m$$ Wait — careful! That collapse only holds when *every* access uses the TLB. The standard KTU formulation is more precise: $$EAT = p \times (T_{TLB} + T_m) + (1 - p) \times (T_{TLB} + 2T_m)$$ **Why the second term?** - On a **TLB hit**: We do 1 TLB lookup + 1 memory access = $T_{TLB} + T_m$. - On a **TLB miss**: We do 1 TLB lookup + 1 memory access to *read the PTE* + 1 memory access to *read the actual data* = $T_{TLB} + 2T_m$. **Step 2: Substitute the values for `p = 0.80`.** $$EAT = 0.80 \times (20 + 200) + 0.20 \times (20 + 400)$$ $$EAT = 0.80 \times 220 + 0.20 \times 420$$ $$EAT = 176 + 84 = 260 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 3: Substitute the values for `p = 0.99`.** $$EAT = 0.99 \times 220 + 0.01 \times 420$$ $$EAT = 217.8 + 4.2 = 222 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 4: Interpretation.** A 19% absolute improvement in TLB hit ratio (from 80% to 99%) reduces the EAT by **38 ns**, or about **14.6%**. This empirically demonstrates the textbook statement: *"Even a 1% drop in TLB hit ratio can cost a modern CPU roughly 5–10% of its user-perceived performance."* --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Single-Level vs. Multi-Level vs. Inverted Page Tables | Property | Single-Level PT | Two-Level (Hierarchical) PT | Inverted Page Table (IPT) | |-----------------------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------| | **Total Memory Footprint** | Very High (4 MB for 4 GB VAS, 4 KB pages) | Sparse; only allocated for used regions | Bounded by physical RAM, not VAS | | **Lookup Speed** | 1 memory access (with TLB) | 2 memory accesses (with TLB) | Hash or associative search (slow) | | **Best Suited For** | Small/early OS | Modern 32-bit / 64-bit desktop OS | Large VAS on small RAM (e.g., PowerPC) | | **Scalability (64-bit)** | Catastrophic | Good (4-level / 5-level for x86-64) | Excellent | | **Sharing Support** | Natural | Natural (PTE per page, not per process)| Difficult; needs chained PTEs | | **Example System** | Early UNIX, DOS | x86 (32-bit), ARMv7 | IBM PowerPC, IA-64 (Itanium) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Virtual-to-Physical Address Translation Flow The diagram below shows the **end-to-end data path** of how a CPU's virtual address is decomposed, looked up, validated, and translated by the MMU using the Page Table. This is the most important Mermaid figure for any KTU memory-management question. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CPU Issues Virtual Address
32 bits"]:::entryNode B["MMU Splits Address
VPN 20 bits Offset 12 bits"]:::splitNode C["TLB Lookup
Using VPN as Tag"]:::tlbNode D{"TLB HIT"}:::decisionNode E["Extract PFN from TLB Entry
Bypass Page Table in RAM"]:::fastPath F{"Valid Bit = 1"}:::decisionNode G["Page Table Lookup in RAM
Use VPN to index PT"]:::slowPath H{"Valid Bit = 1"}:::decisionNode I["Raise Page Fault Trap
OS Swaps Page from Disk"]:::faultNode J["Load PFN from PTE
Update TLB if Policy Allows"]:::loadNode K["Protection Check
Read Write Execute Match"]:::protectNode L{"Permissions OK"}:::decisionNode M["Raise Segmentation Fault
Access Violation Trap"]:::segfaultNode N["Concatenate PFN and Offset
Form Final Physical Address"]:::finalNode O["Memory Bus Accesses RAM
Data Returned to CPU"]:::memoryNode A --> B B --> C C --> D D -- YES --> E D -- NO --> G G --> H H -- YES --> J H -- NO --> I E --> K J --> K K --> L L -- YES --> N L -- NO --> M I -.->|After Swap| G
