The I/O Module is the *intermediary* between the CPU/Memory subsystem and the external device. It performs **six core functions** (per KTU 2024 Module 4 specification):

1. **Control & Timing** — Coordinates data flow between CPU and device (handshaking).
2. **CPU Communication** — Receives commands, returns status; communicates via registers.
3. **Device Communication** — Issues device-specific control signals.
4. **Data Buffering** — Stores data temporarily to absorb the speed mismatch between CPU (nanosecond) and device (millisecond–microsecond).
5. **Error Detection** — Detects and reports mechanical or transmission errors.
6. **Data Format Conversion** — e.g., parallel-to-serial, byte-streaming, packetization.

### 2.2 I/O Device Connection Structure

The I/O module exposes **three types of internal registers** to the CPU:
- **Data Register (DR)** — Holds data being transferred.
- **Status Register (SR)** — Reports the current state (BUSY, READY, ERROR).
- **Control Register (CR)** — Accepts commands from CPU (READ, WRITE, SEEK, RESET).

The CPU interacts with these registers using two principal addressing schemes:

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory-Mapped I/O:** I/O registers share the same address space as memory. The CPU uses standard `LOAD`/`STORE` instructions. The address range is unified. **Advantage:** Rich instruction set, no special I/O instructions. **Disadvantage:** Reduces effective memory address space.
>
> **Isolated (Port-Mapped) I/O:** I/O registers have a *separate* address space. Special `IN` and `OUT` instructions are used. **Advantage:** Full memory address space preserved, faster I/O. **Disadvantage:** Smaller instruction set for I/O.

### 2.3 Block-Level Operation of an I/O Module

The operational sequence for a typical **I/O Read Operation** is as follows:

- **Step 1:** CPU checks the device status by reading the Status Register (SR).
- **Step 2:** CPU waits until SR indicates `READY`.
- **Step 3:** CPU writes the device address & `READ` command into the Control Register (CR).
- **Step 4:** I/O module identifies the target device, raises the select line.
- **Step 5:** Device reads data from its internal buffer and places it into the Data Register (DR).
- **Step 6:** I/O module signals an **interrupt** (in interrupt-driven mode) or the CPU **polls** DR (in programmed I/O).
- **Step 7:** CPU reads the Data Register and transfers the byte/word into a memory buffer.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit |
|---|-------------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{n \times W}{R_{bus}}$ | Time to transfer *n* words of width *W* over bus of rate $R_{bus}$ | seconds |
| 2 | $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rotational} + T_{transfer}$ | Total disk access time | ms |
| 3 | $T_{rotational} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{60}{RPM}$ | Average rotational latency (half rotation) | ms |
| 4 | $R_{effective} = \dfrac{B_{sector}}{T_{access}}$ | Effective transfer rate of a disk | B/s |
| 5 | $B_{bus} = W_{bus} \times f_{bus}$ | Bus bandwidth = width × frequency | B/s |
| 6 | $T_{interrupt\_latency} = T_{save} + T_{ack} + T_{vector}$ | Time from interrupt assertion to ISR entry | cycles |
| 7 | $N_{busy\_wait} = f_{polling} \times T_{device}$ | CPU cycles wasted in polling | cycles |
| 8 | $T_{DMA} = T_{setup} + \dfrac{N \times B}{R_{DMA}}$ | Total DMA transfer time | s |
| 9 | $Speedup_{DMA} = 1 + \dfrac{T_{CPU\_free}}{T_{CPU\_engaged}}$ | Speedup over programmed I/O | dimensionless |
| 10 | $U_{bus} = \dfrac{T_{data}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Bus utilization (fraction of time data is on bus) | ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **rotational latency formula** assumes a uniform distribution of the read-head position relative to the desired sector, justifying the **½ factor** (statistical mean of a full rotation period).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The I/O subsystem is the **bottleneck determinant** of modern computing systems. **Amdahl's Law** applied to I/O dictates that overall system speedup is limited by the slowest component — and I/O is frequently the slowest. The 2024 KTU syllabus emphasises this link between I/O architecture and **high-performance computing (HPC)**, **cloud data centres**, and **embedded system design** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M I/O subsystems).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Effective Transfer Rate of a Magnetic Disk

**Given:**
- Rotational speed: $N = 7200 \text{ RPM}$ (revolutions per minute)
- Average seek time: $T_{seek} = 8 \text{ ms}$
- Sector size: $B_{sector} = 512 \text{ bytes}$
- Sectors per track: $S = 600$
- Controller overhead: $T_{ctrl} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$

**Required:** Effective transfer rate $R_{effective}$.

---

**Step 1: Convert rotational speed to time per revolution.**

The disk completes $N$ revolutions per minute, hence time per revolution is:

$$
T_{rev} = \frac{60 \text{ s}}{N \text{ rev/min}} = \frac{60}{7200} \text{ s/rev}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the average rotational latency.**

The read head is, on average, halfway through the required sector, so:

$$
T_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} \times T_{rev} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{7200} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{rot} = \frac{30}{7200} \text{ s} = 4.1667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.167 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the data transfer time for one sector.**

Since the disk rotates at $T_{rev}$ per revolution and contains $S$ sectors per track, the time to read one sector is:

$$
T_{xfer\_sector} = \frac{T_{rev}}{S} = \frac{60 / 7200}{600} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{xfer\_sector} = \frac{8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{600} \text{ s} = 1.389 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 13.89 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 4: Total access time for one sector.**

$$
T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{xfer\_sector} + T_{ctrl}
$$

$$
T_{access} = 8.0 + 4.167 + 0.0139 + 0.5 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{access} = 12.681 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5: Effective transfer rate.**

$$
R_{effective} = \frac{B_{sector}}{T_{access}} = \frac{512 \text{ bytes}}{12.681 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}
$$

$$
R_{effective} = 40{,}376.2 \text{ B/s} \approx 40.38 \text{ KB/s}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — CPU Wastage in Programmed (Polled) I/O

**Given:**
- CPU clock frequency: $f_{CPU} = 1 \text{ GHz} = 10^9 \text{ Hz}$
- Device data-ready rate: 1 byte every $T_{device} = 1 \text{ ms}$
- Polling loop: 4 instructions × 1 cycle/instruction = 4 cycles per poll

**Step 1: Polls required per byte.**

$$
N_{poll} = \frac{T_{device}}{T_{poll\_interval}}
$$

Assuming the CPU polls every 10 μs (typical), then:

$$
N_{poll} = \frac{1000 \text{ μs}}{10 \text{ μs}} = 100 \text{ polls}
$$

**Step 2: Total cycles wasted.**

$$
C_{wasted} = N_{poll} \times 4 \text{ cycles} = 100 \times 4 = 400 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3: Time wasted.**

$$
T_{wasted} = \frac{C_{wasted}}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{400}{10^9} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.4 \text{ μs per byte}
$$

**Step 4: CPU utilisation in polling (per 1 ms window).**

$$
U_{poll} = \frac{T_{wasted}}{T_{device}} = \frac{0.4 \text{ μs}}{1000 \text{ μs}} = 0.0004 = 0.04\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The polling overhead appears small for a *slow* device, but for a device producing **1 byte per μs** (1 MB/s), the CPU would be **100% busy** polling — leaving **zero cycles** for actual computation. This is precisely why **interrupt-driven I/O** and **DMA** were invented.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — I/O Techniques

| Parameter | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|-----|
| **CPU Overhead** | Very High (100% polling) | Low (only on interrupt) | Minimal (setup + final) |
| **Suitable Device Speed** | Very slow (< 1 KB/s) | Moderate (≤ 1 MB/s) | High (> 1 MB/s) |
| **Data Path** | CPU ↔ I/O Module ↔ Device | CPU ↔ I/O Module ↔ Device | I/O Module ↔ Memory (direct) |
| **Transfer Granularity** | Word/Byte at a time | Word/Byte at a time | Block of N bytes |
| **CPU Cycles/Byte** | High | Moderate | Near zero |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Lowest | Medium | High (DMA controller) |
| **Latency** | Deterministic (polling cycle) | Bounded by ISR entry | Best for bulk transfer |
| **Concurrency** | None (CPU blocked) | Partial (CPU free between interrupts) | Full (CPU fully free) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the I/O Subsystem

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **canonical KTU 2024 Module 4 I/O architecture**, showing the interaction of the CPU, Memory, System Bus, I/O Module, and multiple external devices.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuA[CPU] -->|Address Bus| sysbusA[SYSTEM BUS INTERFACE]
    cpuA -->|Data Bus| sysbusA
    cpuA -->|Control Bus| sysbusA
    sysbusA --> iomodA[I/O MODULE CONTROLLER]
    iomodA --> devA1[KEYBOARD CONTROLLER]
    iomodA --> devA2[DISK CONTROLLER HDD SSD]
    iomodA --> devA3[DISPLAY CONTROLLER VGA HDMI]
    iomodA --> devA4[NETWORK INTERFACE CARD NIC]
    iomodA --> devA5[PRINTER PARALLEL PORT]
    memA[MAIN MEMORY RAM] -->|Direct Bus Access| sysbusA
    iomodA -->|Status Register| regA1[STATUS REGISTER SR]
    iomodA -->|Data Register| regA2[DATA REGISTER DR]
    iomodA -->|Control Register| regA3[CONTROL REGISTER CR]
    regA1 --> sysbusA
    regA2 --> sysbusA
    regA3 --> sysbusA
```

### 4.2 I/O Data Transfer Flow (Read Operation — Interrupt-Driven Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[CPU Issues READ Command to CR] --> step2[I/O Module Selects Target Device]
    step2 --> step3[Device Loads Data into DR]
    step3 --> step4[Device Asserts Interrupt Signal]
    step4 --> step5[CPU Saves PC and PSW on Stack]
    step5 --> step6[CPU Jumps to Interrupt Service Routine ISR]
    step6 --> step7[ISR Reads Data from DR]
    step7 --> step8[Data Transferred to Memory Buffer via BUS]
    step8 --> step9[CPU Restores PC and PSW]
    step9 --> step10[CPU Resumes Original Program Execution]
```

### 4.3 DMA Block Transfer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuB[CPU] -->|Sets up DMA: Source, Dest, Count| dmaA[DMA CONTROLLER]
    dmaA -->|Bus Request BR| arbB[SYSTEM BUS ARBITER]
    arbB -->|Bus Grant BG| dmaA
    dmaA -->|Direct Read/Write| memB[MAIN MEMORY]
    dmaA -->|Data Transfer| devB[EXTERNAL DEVICE DISK NIC]
    dmaA -->|Interrupt on Completion| cpuB
    cpuB -.->|Continues Other Computation| otherB[OTHER TASKS RUNNING]
```

### 4.4 External Device Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    extA[EXTERNAL I O DEVICES] --> catA1[HUMAN READABLE]
    extA --> catA2[MACHINE READABLE]
    extA --> catA3[COMMUNICATION DEVICES]
    catA1 --> subA1[Input Keyboard Mouse Scanner]
    catA1 --> subA2[Output Monitor Printer Speaker]
    catA2 --> subA3[Storage Magnetic Disk SSD Tape Optical]
    catA2 --> subA4[Block vs Character Access]
    catA3 --> subA5[Network Modem NIC Router]
    catA3 --> subA6[Serial vs Parallel Links]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The architecture diagrams above emphasise the **centrality of the I/O module** — every external device, regardless of category, must traverse the I/O module to communicate with the CPU/memory. This is a *core KTU 2024 examination point*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between memory-mapped I/O and isolated I/O. List one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Address Space** | Unified with memory | Separate I/O address space |
| **Instructions** | Standard `LOAD`/`STORE` | Special `IN`/`OUT` instructions |
| **Memory Size** | Reduced | Fully preserved |
| **Decoding** | Single decoder | Two decoders (memory + I/O) |
| **Advantage** | Larger instruction set, flexible addressing | Faster I/O, full memory available |
| **Disadvantage** | Reduces usable memory | Limited I/O instruction set |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Tabular comparison (1.5) + Advantage of each (0.5).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series 1]**
**What is an I/O module? List any four of its functions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An I/O module is an intermediary hardware interface between the CPU/memory and external peripheral devices. It performs:

1. **Control & Timing** — coordinates data transfer sequencing.
2. **Data Buffering** — absorbs speed mismatch between CPU and device.
3. **Error Detection** — identifies and reports transmission errors.
4. **Device Selection** — activates the correct peripheral via select lines.
5. **Command Interpretation** — translates CPU commands into device-specific signals.
6. **Data Format Conversion** — e.g., parallel-to-serial.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + 4 functions × 0.5 each (2).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the three I/O techniques: Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, and Direct Memory Access (DMA). Compare their CPU overhead and data transfer path. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A magnetic disk rotates at **10,000 RPM**, has an average seek time of **5 ms**, and contains **1024 sectors per track** of **4096 bytes** each. Compute the average rotational latency, the time to read one sector, and the effective data transfer rate. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Three I/O Techniques (7 Marks)**

**1. Programmed I/O (2 Marks)**
- The CPU is **actively involved** in every byte transfer.
- The CPU executes a **polling loop** that repeatedly reads the Status Register until the device signals `READY`.
- Once ready, the CPU transfers the byte between the device and an accumulator/register.
- **Data path:** CPU ↔ I/O Module ↔ Device (CPU must be the intermediary for *every* byte).
- **CPU overhead:** **Maximum**. CPU cannot perform any other task while polling.
- **Best for:** Very slow devices (e.g., keyboard, switches).

**2. Interrupt-Driven I/O (2.5 Marks)**
- The CPU issues the I/O command and **continues executing** other instructions.
- When the device is ready, it raises an **interrupt request (IRQ)** line.
- The CPU completes the current instruction, **saves its state** (PC, PSW), and jumps to the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**.
- The ISR reads/writes the data register, then returns via the `IRET` instruction.
- **Data path:** CPU ↔ I/O Module ↔ Device (still byte-by-byte via CPU).
- **CPU overhead:** Moderate. CPU is free between interrupts.
- **Best for:** Moderate-speed devices (e.g., serial port, line printer).

**3. Direct Memory Access — DMA (2.5 Marks)**
- A dedicated **DMA controller** takes over the system bus from the CPU.
- The CPU sets up the DMA with **source address, destination address, and byte count**, then returns to other work.
- The DMA controller handles the entire block transfer **directly between device and memory**, asserting the bus via `HOLD`/`HLDA` signals.
- Upon completion, the DMA raises an interrupt to notify the CPU.
- **Data path:** Device ↔ DMA ↔ Memory (**CPU is bypassed**).
- **CPU overhead:** Minimum. CPU sets up DMA once per block.
- **Best for:** High-speed, block-oriented devices (e.g., disk, NIC, SSD).

**Comparison Summary Table (built within answer):**
| Feature | Programmed I/O | Interrupt I/O | DMA |
|---------|---------------|---------------|-----|
| CPU Overhead | High | Moderate | Low |
| Data Path | Via CPU | Via CPU | Direct |
| Suitable Device Speed | < 1 KB/s | 1 KB/s – 1 MB/s | > 1 MB/s |

---

**(b) — Disk Performance Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Average Rotational Latency**

$$
T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} = \frac{60}{10{,}000} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} \times T_{rev} = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \text{ ms} = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Time to Read One Sector**

$$
T_{sector} = \frac{T_{rev}}{1024} = \frac{6 \text{ ms}}{1024} = 5.86 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ms} = 5.86 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3: Total Access Time per Sector**

$$
T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{sector} = 5 + 3 + 0.00586 = 8.006 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Effective Transfer Rate**

$$
R_{effective} = \frac{B_{sector}}{T_{access}} = \frac{4096 \text{ bytes}}{8.006 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}
$$

$$
R_{effective} = 511{,}616 \text{ B/s} \approx 499.6 \text{ KB/s} \approx 0.49 \text{ MB/s}
$$

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice, 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal structure of an I/O module. List its major registers and their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An interrupt-driven I/O system has the following parameters: device data rate = **50 KB/s**, CPU instruction execution rate = **100 MIPS**, and the ISR takes **40 instructions** to service each interrupt. Compute the fraction of CPU time consumed by I/O handling. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — I/O Module Internal Structure (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):**
The I/O module consists of:
- An **interface to the system bus** (data, address, control lines).
- **Internal registers** — Data Register (DR), Status Register (SR), Control Register (CR).
- A **device interface logic** unit that drives the external peripheral.
- A **data buffer** for temporary storage.
- **Interrupt and arbitration logic** to coordinate with the CPU.

**Register Functions (4 Marks):**

| Register | Function |
|----------|----------|
| **Data Register (DR)** | Temporarily holds the data byte/word being transferred between CPU and device. |
| **Status Register (SR)** | Indicates device state: READY, BUSY, ERROR, COMPLETE. CPU reads this to check progress. |
| **Control Register (CR)** | Receives commands from CPU: READ, WRITE, SEEK, RESET, START, STOP. |

Additionally, many I/O modules have a **buffer memory** for block transfers, especially in DMA-based controllers.

---

**(b) — CPU Utilisation by Interrupt Handling (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Data rate: $R_{data} = 50 \text{ KB/s} = 50{,}000 \text{ B/s}$
- CPU speed: $f_{CPU} = 100 \text{ MIPS} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ instructions/s}$
- ISR instruction count: $N_{ISR} = 40 \text{ instructions}$

**Step 1: Interrupts per second (assuming 1 byte per interrupt):**

$$
f_{IRQ} = R_{data} = 50{,}000 \text{ interrupts/second}
$$

**Step 2: Total instructions consumed by ISR per second:**

$$
I_{ISR} = f_{IRQ} \times N_{ISR} = 50{,}000 \times 40 = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ instructions/second}
$$

**Step 3: Fraction of CPU time consumed:**

$$
U_{CPU} = \frac{I_{ISR}}{f_{CPU}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.02 = 2\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Only **2%** of CPU time is consumed by I/O interrupts, leaving **98%** for actual computation — a major improvement over **programmed I/O**, which would consume 100% of CPU cycles.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing rotational latency with full rotation time.** Always apply the **½ factor** unless the problem explicitly states the head is *already* positioned at the start of the sector.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units.** RPM → seconds/revolution is the most common error. Show the conversion step explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing up *transfer time* and *access time*.** Transfer time excludes seek and rotational latency. Access time *includes* all three.
> 4. **In comparison questions,** students often describe features without using a **table format**. KTU examiners award **0.5 extra marks** for a well-structured comparative table.
> 5. **In DMA questions,** students forget to mention the **HOLD/HLDA bus arbitration** sequence — this is a 2-mark deduction item.
> 6. **Interrupt-driven I/O** requires the mention of **state saving** (PC, PSW on stack) and the **IRET** return instruction for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — External I/O Devices (Module 4)**

- [x] **External devices** are classified into **Human-Readable**, **Machine-Readable**, and **Communication** categories.
- [x] The **I/O Module** is the mandatory intermediary between CPU/Memory and every external device.
- [x] **Three internal registers** of the I/O module: **Data Register (DR)**, **Status Register (SR)**, **Control Register (CR)**.
- [x] **Memory-Mapped I/O** = unified address space; **Isolated I/O** = separate address space with `IN`/`OUT` instructions.
- [x] **Three I/O techniques:** Programmed I/O (high CPU overhead, low complexity), Interrupt-Driven I/O (moderate overhead, ISR-based), DMA (minimal CPU involvement, direct device↔memory).
- [x] **Disk access time formula:** $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{xfer}$.
- [x] **Rotational latency** = ½ × (60/RPM) seconds.
- [x] **DMA requires bus arbitration** via `HOLD` (CPU) and `HLDA` (DMA controller) signals.
- [x] **Block devices** (disk, SSD) use fixed-size sectors; **Character devices** (keyboard, mouse) use streaming I/O.
- [x] **Effective transfer rate** of a disk: $R_{eff} = B_{sector} / T_{access}$.
- [x] **Interrupt latency** = time from IRQ assertion to first ISR instruction execution.
- [x] **Polling inefficiency** grows linearly with device data rate — DMA becomes mandatory above ~1 MB/s.
- [x] **Firmware-level I/O scheduling** is beyond KTU Module 4 scope but is referenced for continuity in Module 5 (Memory Hierarchy).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** I/O Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
An **I/O Module** (Input/Output Module) is a hardware interface unit situated between the **Central Processing Unit (CPU) / Main Memory** and one or more **external peripheral devices** (disks, keyboards, displays, sensors, etc.). It is responsible for mediating, synchronising, buffering, and error-checking all data exchanges so that the high-speed digital core is insulated from the low-speed, electromechanical, and asynchronous nature of real-world I/O devices.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the I/O Module is the **functional and physical subsystem that implements the I/O interface**, providing a well-defined set of registers and control signals that allow the CPU to issue I/O commands without needing to know the internal operating details of the peripheral.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCST404 – Module 4):** The I/O Module is the *cornerstone concept* on which the three data transfer techniques (Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, DMA) are subsequently built. Mastery of its functions is mandatory before proceeding to DMA controller design.

### 1.2 The Four Key Operations an I/O Module Supports
| Operation | Direction | Purpose |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **Read** | Device $\rightarrow$ CPU/Memory | Acquire data from peripheral |
| **Write** | CPU/Memory $\rightarrow$ Device | Send data to peripheral |
| **Control** | CPU $\rightarrow$ Module | Issue command (e.g., rewind, seek) |
| **Status** | Module $\rightarrow$ CPU | Report state (busy / error / ready) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Receptionist"
Imagine the **CPU** is a busy business traveller, and the **external devices** (printer, disk, scanner) are various service counters spread across a large hotel.

- The CPU cannot leave its desk and walk to every counter — it would waste precious time.
- Instead, it sends a message to the **Receptionist (I/O Module)**.
- The Receptionist **queues requests**, **translates the order** into the right counter's language, **stores parcels (buffers)**, **notifies the traveller when ready**, and **reports any problems (errors)**.

The Receptionist never does the traveller's job; it just **manages the interface** so both sides can operate at their natural speeds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not connect the CPU directly to devices?** Because peripheral speeds range from **$\approx$ 1 byte/sec (keyboard)** to **$\approx$ 1 GB/sec (NVMe SSD)** — a span of nine orders of magnitude. A mismatch of this scale would paralyse the CPU. The I/O Module absorbs this speed disparity through buffering and asynchronous handshaking.

### 1.4 Generic I/O Module Block Diagram (Textual)
The classical structure comprises:
1. **CPU/Memory Side Interface** — Address bus, data bus, control bus lines.
2. **Device Side Interface** — Data, status, and control lines specific to the peripheral.
3. **Internal Registers** — Data-in, Data-out, Status, Control.
4. **Buffer (temporary storage)** — Typically a small high-speed register or FIFO.
5. **Control & Timing Logic** — Implements the bus protocol and handshaking.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** The I/O Module = *Interface + Buffer + Logic + Registers*. Memorise these four pillars — every KTU question on this topic pivots on at least one of them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Six Core Functions of an I/O Module
1. **Control & Timing** — Coordinates the flow of traffic between internal (CPU/Memory) and external resources. Synchronises speed differences.
2. **CPU Communication** — Decodes commands, transfers data, provides status information, recognises its own address on the bus.
3. **Device Communication** — Sends control signals, receives status signals, transfers data using the device's native protocol.
4. **Data Buffering** — Temporary storage (typically a few bytes to a few KB) to bridge speed gaps. Essential because bus speeds $\gg$ device speeds in most cases.
5. **Error Detection & Reporting** — Detects mechanical/electrical faults (paper jam, disk CRC error) and reports them to the CPU via status flags.
6. **Data Formatting & Code Conversion** — Translates between parallel internal data and serial/parallel external formats (e.g., USB serial $\rightarrow$ parallel bus).

### 2.2 Why the I/O Module is Necessary (Engineering Justification)
- **Speed Mismatch:** CPU cycle time $\approx$ **1 ns**; printer throughput $\approx$ **100 KB/s**; the I/O module absorbs the **$10^6 \times$ difference**.
- **Data Format Mismatch:** Internal bus is parallel; many devices are serial.
- **Electrical Mismatch:** Internal logic is **3.3 V / 5 V CMOS**; devices may use **24 V industrial signalling**, optical isolation, or wireless RF.
- **Logical Mismatch:** CPU uses memory-mapped addresses; devices use protocols like SPI, I²C, UART, USB.

### 2.3 Three I/O Techniques (Module Overview)
| Technique | CPU Involvement | Speed | Hardware Cost | Use Case |
|-----------|-----------------|-------|---------------|----------|
| **Programmed I/O** | Busy-Wait (100%) | Slowest | Lowest | Simple embedded, rare transfers |
| **Interrupt-Driven I/O** | Interrupt Service Routine | Medium | Medium | Keyboards, network cards |
| **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** | Only at start/end | Fastest | Highest | Disk, bulk data transfer |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **board-exam favourites**. Memorise the *form*, the *variables*, and the *units*.

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|-------------------|---------|---------------|
| $T_{PIO} = n \times t_{cycle}$ | Time for Programmed I/O of $n$ bytes | seconds; $t_{cycle}$ = per-byte CPU loop time |
| $T_{DMA} = T_{setup} + \frac{n}{R_{DMA}}$ | Total DMA transfer time | seconds; $R_{DMA}$ = DMA transfer rate (bytes/s) |
| $B_{eff} = \frac{n}{T_{total}}$ | Effective throughput | bytes/s |
| $\eta_{DMA} = \frac{T_{transfer}}{T_{transfer} + T_{setup}}$ | DMA efficiency | dimensionless; $\eta \in [0,1]$ |
| $N_{break-even} = \frac{T_{setup}}{t_{cycle} - \frac{1}{R_{DMA}}}$ | Min. bytes for DMA to beat PIO | bytes |
| $A_{MMIO} = 2^{k} - A_{mem}$ | Memory-mapped I/O address budget | addressable units |
| $f_{bus} = \frac{1}{T_{bus}}$ | Bus operating frequency | Hz |
| $T_{cycle-steal} = n \times t_{1byte}$ | DMA cycle-stealing per byte | seconds |

> **NOTATION SAFEGUARD:** Absolute-value bars are rendered as $\vert$ or $\mid$ so they do **not** break markdown table structure.

### 2.5 I/O Addressing Schemes — Conceptual Comparison
1. **Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O):**
   - Separate address space for I/O devices.
   - Special bus signals: $\overline{IOR}$ and $\overline{IOW}$.
   - Uses dedicated instructions: `IN`, `OUT` (x86), `INP`, `OUTP` (8085/8086).
   - Memory address space remains **fully available** for RAM.

2. **Memory-Mapped I/O:**
   - I/O devices occupy addresses in the main memory address space.
   - No special instructions — uses ordinary `LOAD` / `STORE`.
   - Address space is **shared** with memory.

3. **Hybrid (e.g., 8086):**
   - Both schemes coexist; decoded by the bus control signals.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Smartphones:** The I/O Module is the SoC's *bus fabric* (ARM NIC-301, AMBA AXI).
- **Industrial PLCs:** Galvanically-isolated I/O modules protect the controller from 230 V AC lines.
- **Aerospace:** ARINC 429 / MIL-STD-1553 modules are ruggedised I/O interfaces.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** I/O modules handle sensor ADC, PWM, and digital I/O at ultra-low power.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Break-Even Point Between Programmed I/O and DMA
**Problem Setup (typical KTU numerical):**
A CPU runs at a clock period of $t_{cycle} = 1\ \mu\text{s}$ per byte during Programmed I/O. A DMA controller has a setup overhead of $T_{setup} = 50\ \mu\text{s}$ and a transfer rate of $R_{DMA} = 5\ \text{MB/s}$. Find the **minimum number of bytes $N$** for which DMA outperforms Programmed I/O.

**Step 1 — Write the two time expressions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{PIO}(n) &= n \cdot t_{cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{DMA}(n) &= T_{setup} + \frac{n}{R_{DMA}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate to find the break-even point.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{DMA}(N) &= T_{PIO}(N) \\
T_{setup} + \frac{N}{R_{DMA}} &= N \cdot t_{cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $N$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{setup} &= N \cdot t_{cycle} - \frac{N}{R_{DMA}} \\
T_{setup} &= N \left( t_{cycle} - \frac{1}{R_{DMA}} \right) \\
N &= \frac{T_{setup}}{t_{cycle} - \frac{1}{R_{DMA}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cycle} &= 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s/byte} \\
\frac{1}{R_{DMA}} &= \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6}}\ \text{s/byte} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s/byte} \\
t_{cycle} - \frac{1}{R_{DMA}} &= (1 - 0.2) \times 10^{-6} = 0.8 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s/byte} \\
N &= \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 62.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &\approx 63\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

For any transfer of **63 or more bytes**, DMA is faster than Programmed I/O. For smaller bursts, Programmed I/O wins because the setup cost dominates.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If the question says "interrupt-driven I/O", insert a *fixed interrupt service time* $T_{ISR}$ per byte and re-derive. The principle is identical.

### 3.2 Derivation — DMA Throughput with Cycle Stealing
In *cycle-stealing* mode, the DMA controller transfers **one byte per bus cycle**, then returns control to the CPU. Each steal freezes the CPU for one bus cycle.

**Step 1 — Per-byte steal time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{steal} &= \frac{1}{f_{bus}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total CPU freeze time for $n$ bytes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{freeze} &= n \cdot t_{steal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total wall-clock transfer time (including CPU activity).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wall} &= T_{setup} + n \cdot t_{steal} + \frac{n}{R_{device}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective DMA efficiency $\eta$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{DMA} = \frac{T_{data\,transfer}}{T_{wall}} = \frac{n / R_{device}}{T_{setup} + n \cdot t_{steal} + n / R_{device}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** As $n \rightarrow \infty$, the setup term becomes negligible and the cycle-steal overhead $n \cdot t_{steal}$ dominates, so $\eta \rightarrow 0$ unless $t_{steal} \ll 1/R_{device}$. This is why **block transfer** mode (single large burst) is preferred for bulk transfers.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — I/O Technique Selection Guide

| Criterion | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|-----|
| Predictable latency | High | Low (interrupt jitter) | Medium |
| CPU utilisation for bulk data | Very Poor | Poor | Excellent |
| Hardware complexity | 1 status bit | Interrupt controller | DMA controller + bus arbiter |
| Best for | Single-byte, time-critical | Sporadic, low-rate | High-throughput, block |
| Power consumption | CPU always active | CPU sleeps between events | CPU sleeps during burst |
| KTU exam frequency | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Memory-Mapped I/O Address Space Calculation
A system uses a **16-bit address bus** with **48 KB of RAM** and **4 KB of I/O**. The RAM occupies the lowest addresses, I/O the highest.

**Step 1 — Addressable space.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536\ \text{addresses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Memory addresses.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{RAM} = 48 \times 1024 = 49{,}152
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — I/O addresses (memory-mapped, contiguous with RAM).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{IO} = 4 \times 1024 = 4{,}096
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the address map.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{RAM} + A_{IO} &= 49{,}152 + 4{,}096 = 53{,}248 \\
A_{unused} &= 65{,}536 - 53{,}248 = 12{,}288\ \text{addresses remain free}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Memory-mapped I/O *costs* address space. In a 16-bit system, 4 KB I/O = 6.25% of the total address map. In a 32-bit system this is negligible, which is why **all modern 32/64-bit CPUs (ARM, RISC-V, x86) use MMIO extensively** for graphics, network, and storage controllers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic I/O Module — Block-Level Architecture
The diagram below shows the internal organisation of a generic I/O module, its connection to the system bus on one side, and the peripheral on the other.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU and Main Memory] -->|Address Bus| AddrDec
    CPU -->|Data Bus| DataBuf[(Data Buffer FIFO)]
    CPU -->|Control Bus| CtrlLogic{Control and Timing Logic}
    AddrDec[Address Decoder] -->|Chip Select| CtrlLogic
    CtrlLogic -->|Read Write| DataBuf
    DataBuf -->|Device Data Lines| Dev[External Peripheral Device]
    Dev -->|Status Lines| StatusReg[Status Register]
    StatusReg -->|Bus Reporting| CPU
    CtrlLogic -->|Error Flags| CPU
    DataBuf -->|Buffered Output| Dev
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Address Decoder** recognises the module's unique address range.
- The **Control & Timing Logic** arbitrates bus ownership and sequences handshaking.
- The **Data Buffer** holds one or more words to bridge speed differences.
- **Status** and **Error** lines are read by the CPU via dedicated status registers.

### 4.2 DMA Controller — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPUside[CPU Bus Interface] --> AR[Address Register]
    CPUside --> WC[Word Count Register]
    CPUside --> CR[Control Register]
    CPUside --> ST[Status Register]
    AR -->|Address Bus Lines| MemBus((System Bus))
    WC -->|Decrement and Zero Detect| DMAE{DMA Engine FSM}
    CR -->|Mode Select| DMAE
    ST -->|Interrupt to CPU| CPURq
    DMAE -->|Bus Request BR| Arb[Bus Arbiter]
    Arb -->|Bus Grant BG| DMAE
    DMAE -->|Memory Read Memory Write| MemBus
    DevIO[Device Handshake Lines DREQ and DACK] <--> DMAE
```

**Key signals to memorise:**
- `HRQ` / `HOLD` — CPU bus request.
- `HLDA` — CPU bus acknowledge.
- `DREQ` / `DACK` — Device request / acknowledge pair.
- `MEMR` / `MEMW` — Memory read / memory write strobes.
- `IOR` / `IOW` — I/O read / I/O write strobes.

### 4.3 Comparison Flow — Three I/O Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([CPU needs to transfer data]) --> Q1{Transfer size?}
    Q1 -->|Single byte or short| Q2{Latency-critical?}
    Q1 -->|Large block high throughput| DMA
    Q2 -->|Yes| PIO[Programmed I/O Busy Wait]
    Q2 -->|No| IntIO[Interrupt Driven I/O]
    PIO --> Done([Transfer Complete])
    IntIO --> Done
    DMA[Direct Memory Access] --> Done
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading strategy:** Trace this flow in any KTU question that asks *which I/O technique should be used for scenario X*. Identify (i) the data size, (ii) the latency constraint, and (iii) the hardware budget. The flow above directly yields the correct technique.

### 4.4 Interrupt-Driven I/O — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU
    participant IO as I/O Module
    participant DEV as Peripheral Device
    CPU->>IO: Issue READ command
    IO->>DEV: Forward command
    DEV-->>IO: Data ready
    IO-->>CPU: Interrupt request
    CPU-->>IO: Acknowledge interrupt
    CPU->>IO: Read data word
    IO-->>CPU: Data on bus
    CPU->>CPU: Service routine executes
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice the CPU is **not** busy-waiting between *Forward command* and *Interrupt request*. This is the principal advantage of interrupt-driven I/O over programmed I/O.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List and briefly explain the major functions of an I/O module.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute 1 mark per salient point):**

An I/O module performs the following major functions:

1. **Control & Timing:** Coordinates the data flow between the CPU/memory and external devices, handling the speed mismatch and bus arbitration.
2. **CPU Communication:** Decodes commands, transfers data, and provides status/error information to the processor.
3. **Device Communication:** Sends control signals to the device and receives status signals, including data, control, and handshaking lines.
4. **Data Buffering:** Acts as a temporary storage area to bridge the speed gap between the high-speed CPU/memory and the slower external devices.
5. **Error Detection:** Detects and reports mechanical or data errors (parity, CRC, device faults) to the CPU.

*(Award full 3 marks for any 3 of the above, explained in 1–2 lines each.)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Compare Isolated I/O and Memory-Mapped I/O.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — table format acceptable):**

| Parameter | Isolated I/O | Memory-Mapped I/O |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|
| Address Space | Separate from memory | Shared with memory |
| Instructions | Special `IN`, `OUT` | Normal `LOAD`, `STORE` |
| Memory Space | Fully available for RAM | Reduced by I/O size |
| Flexibility | Less flexible | Highly flexible |
| Cost | Extra control lines needed | No extra hardware |
| Example | 8085 `IN 08H` | ARM MMIO for GPIO |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of an I/O module. List its key functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the exact block diagram.)

The I/O module consists of:
- **CPU-side interface:** Address, Data, Control bus lines.
- **Device-side interface:** Data, Status, Control lines specific to the peripheral.
- **Internal components:**
  - Data buffer (FIFO/Register).
  - Control & Timing logic.
  - Status register.
  - Control register.
  - Address decoder.

**Explanation (2 marks):** The CPU places the device address on the address bus; the address decoder activates the module. The CPU issues a command (`READ`/`WRITE`/`CONTROL`) on the control bus. Data is transferred via the data bus, buffered temporarily, and forwarded to/from the peripheral. The status register continuously reports device readiness and errors back to the CPU.

**Key Functions (1 mark):** Control & Timing, CPU Communication, Device Communication, Data Buffering, Error Detection.

---

**b)** A system uses a 16-bit address bus with 32 KB of RAM and 8 KB of I/O devices implemented as memory-mapped I/O. Compute the address space occupied by RAM, the I/O, and the unused region. Justify why modern 32/64-bit systems prefer memory-mapped I/O. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total addressable space.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Memory space.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{RAM} = 32 \times 1024 = 32{,}768\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — I/O space (memory-mapped).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{IO} = 8 \times 1024 = 8{,}192\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Unused space.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{unused} = 65{,}536 - 32{,}768 - 8{,}192 = 24{,}576\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Justification for MMIO in 32/64-bit systems (3 marks):**

- **Vast address space:** $2^{32} = 4\ \text{GB}$ and $2^{64} = 16\ \text{EB}$ — the small I/O address budget is *negligible*.
- **Uniform instructions:** MMIO uses the same `LOAD`/`STORE` instructions, simplifying compilers, linkers, and OS kernels.
- **No extra pins:** No dedicated $\overline{IOR}$ / $\overline{IOW}$ signals needed.
- **Memory protection:** MMIO regions can be guarded by the MMU, supporting virtualisation and secure boot.
- **Examples:** All ARM Cortex-A, RISC-V, and modern x86 peripherals are memory-mapped.

---

#### **Question B** (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**a)** Explain the operation of a DMA controller. With a neat block diagram, describe the data transfer modes of DMA. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**DMA Operation (3 marks):**
The DMA (Direct Memory Access) controller is a dedicated hardware unit that transfers blocks of data between I/O devices and main memory **without CPU intervention**. The CPU initially loads the DMA registers with the source address, destination address, and word count, then continues with other tasks. The DMA controller requests the bus from the CPU via `HOLD`, receives `HLDA`, and performs the transfer autonomously, byte-by-byte or in bursts. Upon completion, it raises an interrupt to inform the CPU.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)

**Three DMA Transfer Modes (2 marks):**

1. **Burst / Block Transfer Mode:** The DMA controller takes exclusive control of the bus and transfers the **entire block** in one continuous operation. Highest throughput, but freezes the CPU for the entire duration. Suitable for disk-to-memory transfers.
2. **Cycle Stealing Mode:** The DMA controller transfers **one byte per bus cycle**, then returns the bus to the CPU. The CPU is *interleaved* with the transfer. Lower throughput but no long CPU freeze. Suitable for moderate-speed devices.
3. **Transparent Mode:** The DMA controller performs a transfer **only when the CPU is not using the bus**. The CPU is never slowed down. Most complex to implement; rarely used.

---

**b)** A CPU uses programmed I/O at $t_{cycle} = 0.5\ \mu\text{s/byte}$ to transfer a 1 MB file. A DMA controller has $T_{setup} = 100\ \mu\text{s}$ and a transfer rate of $R_{DMA} = 20\ \text{MB/s}$. Calculate the time taken by each method and the speedup achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Programmed I/O time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{PIO} &= n \times t_{cycle} \\
n &= 1\ \text{MB} = 1 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bytes} \\
T_{PIO} &= 1 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} \\
T_{PIO} &= 0.5\ \text{s} = 500\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — DMA time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{DMA} &= T_{setup} + \frac{n}{R_{DMA}} \\
T_{DMA} &= 100 \times 10^{-6} + \frac{1 \times 10^{6}}{20 \times 10^{6}} \\
T_{DMA} &= 100 \times 10^{-6} + 0.05\ \text{s} \\
T_{DMA} &\approx 0.0501\ \text{s} = 50.1\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{PIO}}{T_{DMA}} = \frac{500\ \text{ms}}{50.1\ \text{ms}} \approx 9.98
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with break-even formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{BE} &= \frac{T_{setup}}{t_{cycle} - 1/R_{DMA}} = \frac{100\ \mu\text{s}}{0.5\ \mu\text{s} - 0.05\ \mu\text{s}} = \frac{100}{0.45} \approx 223\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $n = 1\ \text{MB} \gg 223\ \text{bytes}$, DMA is overwhelmingly preferred. The result $S \approx 10\times$ is realistic for bulk transfers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner Observations):**
> 1. **Confusing *cycle stealing* with *burst mode*.** Cycle stealing transfers *one word* per bus grant; burst mode transfers the *entire block*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting to add $T_{setup}$ in DMA calculations.** A bare $\frac{n}{R_{DMA}}$ answer loses 1 mark. Always add the setup overhead.
> 3. **Stating "DMA has no CPU involvement" — wrong!** The CPU *must* initialise the DMA controller and is *interrupted* on completion. Use the precise phrase: *"DMA minimises CPU involvement."*
> 4. **Drawing memory-mapped I/O as occupying its own address space.** It is *part of* the memory address space. Examiners are strict on this.
> 5. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** in answer sheets — although this is a digital artifact, in physical answer sheets ensure your diagrams are clearly labelled; an unlabelled block diagram scores 0–1 out of 4.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **I/O Module = Interface + Buffer + Logic + Registers** (the four pillars).
- [x] **Six core functions:** Control & Timing, CPU Comm, Device Comm, Buffering, Error Detection, Code/Format Conversion.
- [x] **Two addressing schemes:** Isolated I/O (port-mapped, separate address space) vs. Memory-Mapped I/O (shared address space, ordinary instructions).
- [x] **Three transfer techniques:** Programmed I/O (busy-wait), Interrupt-Driven I/O (event-driven), DMA (autonomous block transfer).
- [x] **DMA modes:** Burst, Cycle Stealing, Transparent.
- [x] **Key DMA signals:** `HRQ`, `HLDA`, `DREQ`, `DACK`, `MEMR`, `MEMW`.
- [x] **Break-even formula:** $N = \frac{T_{setup}}{t_{cycle} - 1/R_{DMA}}$ — must appear in *every* DMA-vs-PIO numerical.
- [x] **MMIO advantage grows with address bus width:** 16-bit systems are constrained; 32/64-bit systems make MMIO the default.
- [x] **Always include $T_{setup}$** in DMA timing calculations.
- [x] **CPU is never fully off-loaded** — it sets up DMA and gets an end-of-transfer interrupt.
- [x] **Examiner's mantra:** "Define → Draw → Derive → Discuss." Every 14-mark answer must hit all four.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmed I/O, Interrupt Driven I/O
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmed I/O, Interrupt Driven I/O

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Input/Output (I/O) Interfacing** is the mechanism by which the Central Processing Unit (CPU) communicates with peripheral devices (keyboard, disk, printer, sensors) through dedicated I/O controllers. The two fundamental CPU-driven data transfer strategies prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programmed I/O (Polled I/O / Busy-Wait I/O):** A synchronous I/O technique in which the **CPU** is entirely responsible for managing data transfer. The CPU executes a continuous loop, repeatedly *reading the status register* of the I/O device until it signals *READY*, then performs the actual data transfer one word/byte at a time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** An asynchronous I/O technique in which the CPU *initiates* the I/O operation, then proceeds with other useful computation. The I/O controller, upon completion (or readiness), sends an **interrupt request (IRQ)** signal to the CPU, which suspends the current process, services the I/O device via an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and then resumes its prior task.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Programmed I/O (The "Knocking on the Door" Scenario):**
Imagine you are a student waiting for a delivery at your hostel gate. Instead of going back to study, you stand at the gate and **continuously peek through the window** every 5 seconds to check if the delivery boy has arrived. You are completely **idle and unproductive** while waiting — this is exactly how Programmed I/O wastes the CPU's cycles in a *polling loop*.

**Analogy 2 — Interrupt-Driven I/O (The "Doorbell" Scenario):**
Now imagine the same scenario, but the delivery boy simply **rings the doorbell** when he arrives. You remain in your room **studying productively**, and only when you hear the bell do you pause, go to the gate, receive the parcel, and return to your work. The *doorbell* is the **interrupt signal**, your walk to the gate is the **context switch to the ISR**, and your return is **resumption of the main program**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Programmed I/O = *CPU is slave to the device*. Interrupt-Driven I/O = *Device notifies CPU only when needed*. This single distinction governs throughput, latency, and CPU efficiency in the entire I/O subsystem.

### 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally benchmarked in I/O systems:
- **CPU Clock Cycle Time (T):** Base unit, typically **1 ns** in modern processors.
- **Instruction Execution Rate:** 100 MIPS to 5,000 MIPS.
- **I/O Device Data Rate:** 1 B/s (keyboard) to 6 Gb/s (NVMe SSD).
- **Interrupt Latency:** Time from interrupt assertion to ISR execution start — typically **50–200 cycles**.
- **Context Switch Overhead:** Time to save/restore CPU state — typically **200–1000 cycles**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of the I/O Subsystem

Every I/O device is mediated by an **I/O Controller** (a hardware interface chip like Intel 8255, 8259, or modern southbridge/Platform Controller Hub). The controller exposes three fundamental registers mapped either to **isolated I/O space** (using IN/OUT instructions) or **memory-mapped I/O** (using normal load/store instructions):

| Register | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Register (DR)** | Read/Write | Holds the actual byte/word being transferred |
| **Status Register (SR)** | Read-only | Flags like READY (1 = ready, 0 = busy), ERROR |
| **Control Register (CR)** | Write-only | Commands like START, RESET, INTERRUPT-ENABLE |

### 2.2 Programmed I/O — Operational Logic

The CPU executes a tight, deterministic sequence. Below is the **step-by-step bulleted logic** that examiners expect:

1. **Device Address Setup:** CPU places the *device address* on the address bus and asserts `IOR` or `IOW` control signal.
2. **Command Issuance:** CPU writes a *START* bit into the device's Control Register to begin the I/O operation.
3. **Polling Loop Entry:** CPU enters a `while(status != READY)` loop, repeatedly reading the Status Register.
4. **Status Check:** Each iteration consumes CPU clock cycles — *no useful work* is done.
5. **Data Transfer:** Once `READY = 1`, the CPU performs a single `READ` or `WRITE` on the Data Register.
6. **Block Transfer / Termination:** For multi-byte transfers, steps 3–5 repeat for every word. For the *last word*, the CPU clears the *START* bit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Programmed I/O is Inefficient:** During the polling phase, the CPU executes *only* comparison-and-branch instructions (no productive computation). On a 3 GHz CPU, even a 1 ms device response wastes **3 million clock cycles** that could have executed real work.

### 2.3 Interrupt-Driven I/O — Operational Logic

1. **Initialization:** CPU writes to Control Register with `INTERRUPT-ENABLE = 1`.
2. **Command Issuance:** CPU writes *START* bit; I/O controller begins operation autonomously.
3. **Productive Computation:** CPU **returns immediately** to executing the user program.
4. **Device Completion:** When the device finishes, the controller asserts the **INTR (Interrupt Request)** line.
5. **CPU Acknowledgment:** At the end of the current instruction, the CPU checks the INTR line. If asserted, it:
   - Completes the current instruction.
   - Saves **PC**, **PSW**, and other registers onto the **stack** (hardware-controlled).
   - Loads the **Interrupt Vector Address** (obtained via hardware or interrupt acknowledge cycle) into the PC.
6. **ISR Execution:** The Interrupt Service Routine transfers one word/buffer, clears the interrupt, and executes `IRET` (Interrupt Return).
7. **Resumption:** Saved state is restored; main program continues seamlessly.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maximum CPU Utilization (Programmed I/O)** | $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu} + T_{io}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of time CPU is *not* blocked |
| **I/O Wait Fraction (p)** | $p = \dfrac{T_{io}}{T_{cpu}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of I/O time to CPU compute time |
| **CPU Utilization (general)** | $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{1}{1 + p}$ | dimensionless | Amdahl-style bound on system efficiency |
| **Average Time per Character (Programmed I/O)** | $T_{avg} = T_{poll} + T_{transfer}$ | seconds | Throughput for serial character devices |
| **Interrupt Service Time (T_isr)** | $T_{isr} = T_{save} + T_{ack} + T_{handler} + T_{restore}$ | seconds | Overhead per interrupt event |
| **Interrupt Overhead Ratio** | $\rho = \dfrac{T_{isr}}{T_{between\_interrupts}}$ | dimensionless | Measures ISR cost vs. useful work |
| **Effective CPU Time per Transfer** | $T_{eff} = T_{isr} + T_{transfer}$ | seconds | Total CPU time spent in interrupt mode |
| **Block Transfer Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot T_{data}}{N \cdot T_{data} + T_{isr}}$ | dimensionless | Improvement gained by transferring buffers |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR EXAMS:** When writing $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{1}{1+p}$ in your answer script, **never use the pipe symbol `|`** for "divided by" — use the proper fraction or "per". KTU digital valuation scripts often mis-render `|` as the I-character.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Programmed I/O** is still used today in **microcontroller firmware** (8051, ARM Cortex-M bare-metal) for simple, deterministic devices like GPIO, UART status polling, and EEPROM readiness checks.
- **Interrupt-Driven I/O** is the **backbone of every modern OS** — Linux, Windows, and RTOS kernels like FreeRTOS rely on interrupts for keyboard, network, disk, and timer events. Without it, multi-tasking would be impossible.
- The **trade-off matrix** evaluated in industry: *high-frequency, low-latency events* → interrupts; *slow, predictable peripheral polling* → programmed I/O (saves interrupt-controller bandwidth).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: CPU Utilization Under Programmed I/O

Consider a system where the CPU alternates between *useful computation* of duration $T_{cpu}$ and an *I/O operation* of duration $T_{io}$. Under Programmed I/O, the CPU cannot do anything during $T_{io}$ because it is *busy-waiting* in the polling loop.

**Step 1 — Define the total cycle time.**
The total time for one full CPU-I/O cycle is:

$$T_{cycle} = T_{cpu} + T_{io}$$

**Step 2 — Define CPU utilization.**
CPU utilization is the fraction of total time spent doing *useful work*:

$$U_{cpu} = \dfrac{\text{Useful time}}{\text{Total time}} = \dfrac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu} + T_{io}}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the I/O wait fraction $p$.**
Let $p$ represent the ratio of I/O time to CPU compute time:

$$p = \dfrac{T_{io}}{T_{cpu}}$$

**Step 4 — Express $T_{io}$ in terms of $T_{cpu}$ and $p$.**

$$T_{io} = p \cdot T_{cpu}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the utilization equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{cpu} &= \dfrac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu} + p \cdot T_{cpu}} \\
&= \dfrac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu}(1 + p)} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1 + p}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
When $p \to 0$ (negligible I/O), $U_{cpu} \to 1$ (100% utilized). When $p \to \infty$ (heavy I/O), $U_{cpu} \to 0$ (CPU is fully blocked). This is the **fundamental scalability limit of Programmed I/O**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Performance Comparison — Programmed vs. Interrupt-Driven I/O

Suppose a printer requires $T_{io} = 5$ ms per character and the CPU executes one poll instruction in $T_{poll} = 100$ ns.

**Step 1 — Calculate number of poll iterations.**

$$N = \dfrac{T_{io}}{T_{poll}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-9}} = 50{,}000 \text{ iterations}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate CPU time wasted in polling (Programmed I/O).**

$$T_{wasted} = N \cdot T_{poll} = 50{,}000 \times 100 \text{ ns} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

The CPU is **100% busy for 5 ms doing nothing useful**.

**Step 3 — For Interrupt-Driven I/O.**
Assume the ISR takes $T_{isr} = 5 \, \mu s$ per interrupt (save state + transfer data + restore).

**Step 4 — Calculate interrupt-driven CPU time spent.**

$$T_{cpu}^{int} = T_{isr} = 5 \, \mu s = 0.005 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the speedup factor.**

$$S = \dfrac{T_{wasted}^{prog}}{T_{cpu}^{int}} = \dfrac{5 \text{ ms}}{0.005 \text{ ms}} = 1000 \times$$

The CPU is **1000× more efficient** under interrupt-driven I/O for this scenario.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Block Transfer Efficiency

When transferring a block of $N$ words via interrupt-driven I/O, the total CPU time is:

$$T_{total} = T_{isr} + N \cdot T_{data}$$

Efficiency (fraction spent on actual data movement):

$$\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot T_{data}}{N \cdot T_{data} + T_{isr}}$$

Let $r = \dfrac{T_{data}}{T_{isr}}$ (ratio). Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{N \cdot T_{data}}{N \cdot T_{data} + T_{isr}} \\
&= \dfrac{N \cdot r \cdot T_{isr}}{N \cdot r \cdot T_{isr} + T_{isr}} \\
&= \dfrac{N \cdot r}{N \cdot r + 1}
\end{aligned}$$

As $N \to \infty$ or $r \to \infty$, $\eta \to 1$. This **mathematically justifies buffer-based block transfers** in OS kernels (DMA-style design philosophy even without DMA hardware).

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | Very low (busy-wait) | High (overhead only) | Interrupt wins |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Minimal (no interrupt lines) | Moderate (controller + vector logic) | Programmed wins |
| **Determinism / Latency** | Predictable polling interval | Variable (depends on masking) | Programmed wins |
| **Suitability for High-Speed Devices** | Poor (CPU cannot keep up) | Good | Interrupt wins |
| **Power Consumption** | High (CPU always active) | Low (CPU sleeps between events) | Interrupt wins |
| **Software Complexity** | Simple linear code | ISR design, re-entrancy, debouncing | Programmed wins |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Programmed I/O — Block & Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Starts Program] --> B[Issue I/O Command via Control Register]
    B --> C[Write START bit to Device CR]
    C --> D{Read Status Register}
    D -- Status 0 = BUSY --> D
    D -- Status 1 = READY --> E[Read/Write Data Register]
    E --> F{Last Word?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Clear START bit]
    G --> H[CPU Resumes Next Instruction]
```

### 4.2 Interrupt-Driven I/O — Block & Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Starts Program] --> B[Enable Interrupt in Control Register]
    B --> C[Write START bit to Device CR]
    C --> D[CPU Resumes Useful Computation]
    D --> E{Interrupt INTR Asserted?}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Complete Current Instruction]
    F --> G[Hardware Saves PC and PSW on Stack]
    G --> H[Load ISR Vector Address into PC]
    H --> I[Execute Interrupt Service Routine]
    I --> J[Transfer Data Word]
    J --> K[Clear Interrupt Flag in Device]
    K --> L[IRET Instruction Restores State]
    L --> D
```

### 4.3 CPU–Controller–Device Hardware Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Registers] -- Address Bus --> BUS[System Bus]
    BUS -- Control Signals --> CTRL[I/O Controller Chip]
    CTRL -- Status Register SR --> CPU
    CTRL -- Data Register DR --> CPU
    CTRL -- Control Register CR --> CPU
    CTRL -- Device Handshake --> DEV[Peripheral Device]
    CTRL -. Interrupt Line INTR .-> CPU
```

### 4.4 Interrupt Acknowledgement Sequence (Timing Diagram as Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant CTRL as I/O Controller
    participant DEV as Device

    CPU->>CTRL: Write START = 1 (initiate I/O)
    Note over CPU: Executes user program
    CTRL->>DEV: Begin physical operation
    DEV-->>CTRL: Operation complete
    CTRL->>CPU: Assert INTR line
    Note over CPU: Finishes current instruction
    CPU->>CTRL: Interrupt Acknowledge INTA
    CTRL->>CPU: Vector address on data bus
    CPU->>CPU: Push PC and PSW onto stack
    CPU->>CPU: Jump to ISR
    CPU->>CTRL: Read/Write Data Register
    CPU->>CPU: IRET - Restore state
    Note over CPU: Resumes user program
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Programmed I/O. Mention its major disadvantage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Programmed I/O is a data transfer technique in which the CPU directly controls the I/O device by repeatedly reading its **status register** in a tight polling loop until the device becomes *ready*, and then performs the data transfer.

**Major Disadvantage (1 mark):** The CPU remains *busy-waiting* in the polling loop, wasting valuable clock cycles that could be used for productive computation, leading to poor **CPU utilization**, especially for slow I/O devices.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the role of an Interrupt Service Routine ISR in Interrupt-Driven I/O?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is a special piece of software that the CPU executes in response to an interrupt request from an I/O device. Its roles are:
1. **Save context** of the interrupted program (PC, PSW, registers).
2. **Perform the data transfer** by reading/writing the device's data register.
3. **Clear the interrupt flag** in the device controller.
4. **Restore context** and return to the interrupted program via the `IRET` instruction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule (Module Internal Choice):** *From each module, the question paper will contain TWO full 14-mark questions, of which the student must answer ONE. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Programmed I/O Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the operation of **Programmed I/O** data transfer. Compare it with **Interrupt-Driven I/O** in terms of CPU utilization. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CPU runs at 100 MHz and executes one poll instruction in 4 clock cycles. An I/O device has a data rate of 1 byte every 200 microseconds. Calculate (i) the number of polls the CPU performs, and (ii) the fraction of CPU time wasted in polling. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flowchart and Comparison (7 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.1** (Programmed I/O flowchart). Description:
1. CPU issues START command (1 mark)
2. CPU enters polling loop, reading Status Register (1 mark)
3. If NOT READY, loop continues; if READY, transfer data (1 mark)
4. Comparison table (4 marks):

| Parameter | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
|---|---|---|
| CPU activity during I/O | Continuously polling | Executing other tasks |
| CPU utilization | Low (wasteful) | High |
| Complexity | Simple | Requires ISR design |
| Best for | Simple, fast devices | Slow or bursty devices |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate clock period (0.5 mark).**

$$T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate poll instruction execution time (0.5 mark).**

$$T_{poll} = 4 \times T_{clk} = 4 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 40 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate number of polls performed (2 marks).**

$$N = \dfrac{T_{io}}{T_{poll}} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{40 \times 10^{-9}} = 5000 \text{ polls}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate total time wasted in polling (2 marks).**

$$T_{wasted} = N \times T_{poll} = 5000 \times 40 \text{ ns} = 200 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Calculate fraction of CPU time wasted (2 marks).**

$$\text{Fraction wasted} = \dfrac{T_{wasted}}{T_{io}} = \dfrac{200 \, \mu s}{200 \, \mu s} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

**Conclusion:** The CPU spends **100% of its time** in the polling loop, completely unable to perform any useful computation during the I/O operation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Interrupt-Driven I/O Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the sequence of operations that occur in **Interrupt-Driven I/O**, from the moment the CPU issues the START command to the resumption of the interrupted program. Include a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An Interrupt Service Routine takes 600 ns to execute (including context save and restore). The interrupt occurs every 50 microseconds from a high-speed device. Calculate (i) the **CPU utilization** lost to interrupt handling, and (ii) the **maximum sustainable interrupt rate** for the CPU if no more than 5% of CPU time may be spent in ISRs. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Interrupt-Driven I/O Sequence (7 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.2** (Interrupt-Driven I/O flowchart).
1. CPU enables interrupts in the Control Register (0.5 mark)
2. CPU writes START bit to the I/O device (0.5 mark)
3. CPU resumes executing the main program (1 mark)
4. Device asserts INTR line when ready (0.5 mark)
5. CPU completes current instruction, saves PC + PSW on stack (1 mark)
6. CPU fetches ISR vector address, jumps to ISR (1 mark)
7. ISR transfers data, clears interrupt, executes IRET, main program resumes (2 marks — with diagram)
8. Diagram in main answer (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the interrupt overhead ratio (1 mark).**

$$\rho = \dfrac{T_{isr}}{T_{interrupt\_period}} = \dfrac{600 \text{ ns}}{50 \, \mu s} = \dfrac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.012$$

**Step 2 — CPU utilization lost to ISR (1 mark).**

$$U_{lost} = 0.012 = 1.2\%$$

The CPU loses only **1.2% of its time** to interrupt handling — vastly better than the 100% lost in programmed I/O.

**Step 3 — Set up the 5% constraint equation (1 mark).**

$$0.05 = \dfrac{T_{isr}}{T_{min}} \Rightarrow T_{min} = \dfrac{T_{isr}}{0.05}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for minimum period (2 marks).**

$$T_{min} = \dfrac{600 \text{ ns}}{0.05} = 12{,}000 \text{ ns} = 12 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Convert to interrupt rate (2 marks).**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{min}} = \dfrac{1}{12 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 83{,}333 \text{ interrupts/second}$$

**Conclusion:** The CPU can sustainably handle up to **~83,333 interrupts per second** while keeping ISR overhead below 5%.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Status Register —** Many students describe Programmed I/O without mentioning that the CPU must *read the status register* in a loop. Marks are lost for skipping this. Always mention "polling of status register."
> 2. **Confusing Polling with Interrupt —** Writing "the CPU checks periodically" is NOT interrupt-driven I/O. Polling is **Programmed I/O**. Interrupt-driven I/O requires an **explicit hardware INTR line** assertion.
> 3. **Missing Stack Operations —** ISR-based answers must mention *saving PC, PSW on stack* and *IRET for restoration*. Examiners deduct 2 marks if this is omitted.
> 4. **Calculation Errors with Units —** Mixing up microseconds (µs) and nanoseconds (ns) in numerical problems is the **#1 cause of zero in Part B numericals**. Always write units at every step.
> 5. **No Diagram = -2 Marks —** A neat flowchart is *mandatory* for full marks in any I/O question worth 7+ marks. Practice drawing it cleanly before the exam.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Programmed I/O** = CPU polls status register in a tight loop; CPU utilization is **severely degraded** for slow devices.
- **Interrupt-Driven I/O** = CPU does other work, device signals via **INTR line**, ISR handles transfer.
- **Three key registers** in any I/O controller: *Data Register, Status Register, Control Register*.
- **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** = the heart of interrupt-driven I/O; must save context, transfer data, clear flag, restore context.
- **CPU Utilization formula:** $U_{cpu} = \dfrac{1}{1 + p}$ where $p$ is the I/O-to-CPU time ratio.
- **Block transfer efficiency** improves with larger N: $\eta = \dfrac{Nr}{Nr + 1}$.
- **Interrupt-driven I/O is ~1000× more efficient** than programmed I/O for typical slow peripherals.
- **Trade-off:** Programmed I/O is simpler but CPU-bound; Interrupt-driven I/O is efficient but requires careful ISR design (re-entrancy, masking, priority).
- **Real-world application:** All modern OSes (Linux, Windows, RTOS) are built on interrupt-driven I/O at the hardware-abstraction layer.
- **Exam mantra:** "Status register polling = Programmed I/O; INTR line assertion = Interrupt-Driven I/O." Memorize this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Memory Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct Memory Access

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a high-performance data transfer mechanism in computer architecture that allows peripheral devices to transfer data to or from the main system memory **without the continuous intervention of the Central Processing Unit (CPU)**. The CPU initiates the transfer by providing the DMA controller with source address, destination address, and the byte count, after which the DMA controller assumes control of the system bus to perform the data movement autonomously.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, DMA is classified as a **bus mastering technique** wherein a dedicated hardware subsystem (the **DMA Controller / DMAC**) takes temporary ownership of the address bus, data bus, and control bus to orchestrate I/O-to-Memory, Memory-to-I/O, or Memory-to-Memory transfers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCST404 - Module 4)**
> The module explicitly requires the student to understand:
> 1. The conceptual need for DMA over Programmed I/O and Interrupt-driven I/O.
> 2. The block-level architecture of a generic DMA controller.
> 3. The handshaking protocol between the CPU, DMAC, and I/O device.
> 4. The three standard transfer modes: **Burst Mode**, **Cycle Stealing Mode**, and **Transparent Mode**.
> 5. The two standard transfer types: **Fly-by Transfer** and **Fetch-Store (Indirect) Transfer**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large office where a senior manager (the **CPU**) has 100 boxes that need to be moved from the warehouse (the **I/O device**) to the main storage room (the **RAM**).

- **Without DMA (Programmed I/O)**: The manager personally picks up each box, walks it to the storage room, places it on the shelf, and repeats this 100 times. While doing this, the manager is **completely blocked** from doing any other work (calculations, attending calls, etc.).
- **With DMA**: The manager calls a junior assistant (the **DMA Controller**), hands over a checklist (source address, destination address, count), and goes back to his desk. The junior assistant does all 100 trips autonomously. The manager is only interrupted briefly to sign the final delivery slip — or, with sophisticated scheduling, is interrupted only at idle moments.

The "junior assistant" is the DMA controller, and the "delivery slip" is the **interrupt** the DMAC sends back to the CPU once the transfer is complete.

### 1.3 Why DMA Exists: The I/O Bottleneck Problem

In Programmed I/O, the CPU spends a significant portion of its clock cycles in a tight polling loop:

$$
\text{CPU\_Cycles\_Wasted} = N \times T_{\text{bus\_access}}
$$

where $N$ is the number of data words and $T_{\text{bus\_access}}$ is the time per word. For high-bandwidth devices (e.g., disk controllers, network cards, audio/video streaming), $N$ can be in the millions, effectively **halting the CPU**.

DMA solves this by amortizing the CPU's setup cost (a single initialization) across thousands of bus cycles, achieving what is often called **"Zero-Copy" I/O**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember**
> - Typical DMA channel width: **8, 16, or 32 bits**.
> - Classic industry-standard DMAC chip: **Intel 8237A** (used in the original IBM PC/AT, frequency of **5 MHz** with 8-bit data bus and 16-bit address bus).
> - Modern equivalent: **Intel 82574L / ICH chipsets**, supporting **64-bit** bus mastering.
> - Maximum transferable block in classic 8237: $2^{16} = \mathbf{65{,}536}$ bytes (64 KB) per channel.

### 1.4 The Three I/O Strategies — A Bird's-Eye View

| Strategy | CPU Involvement | Speed | Hardware Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Programmed I/O** | Continuous (polling loop) | **Slowest** | Lowest |
| **Interrupt-Driven I/O** | Per-byte/per-block interrupt | **Moderate** | Low |
| **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** | Only at start and end of block | **Fastest** | **Highest** (needs DMAC) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a DMA System

The DMA system consists of three principal actors that arbitrate for control of the shared system bus:

1. **The CPU** — the default bus master. Owns the bus unless it explicitly relinquishes it.
2. **The DMA Controller (DMAC)** — the secondary bus master. Requests the bus via a **Hold Request (HRQ)** signal to the CPU.
3. **The I/O Device (Slave)** — connected to the DMAC via dedicated DMA Request (**DREQ**) and DMA Acknowledge (**DACK**) lines.

### 2.2 The DMA Handshaking Protocol — Step-by-Step

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires understanding the signal-level handshake. Here is the rigorous sequence:

1. **I/O Device Issues DREQ**: The peripheral asserts its DMA Request line (e.g., `DREQ0` for channel 0) to inform the DMAC that it has data to transfer.
2. **DMAC Issues HRQ to CPU**: The DMAC translates the DREQ into a Hold Request (`HRQ`) signal sent to the CPU.
3. **CPU Tri-States Its Buses**: Upon receiving HRQ, the CPU completes its current instruction, then asserts **HLDA (Hold Acknowledge)** and electrically disconnects itself from the address, data, and control buses (puts them in **high-impedance / tri-state**).
4. **DMAC Asserts DACK**: The DMAC sends a DMA Acknowledge (`DACK`) signal back to the I/O device, telling it that the bus has been granted.
5. **DMAC Performs Transfer**: The DMAC now drives the address bus, places the source/destination address, asserts `MEMR`/`MEMW` (for memory) or `IOR`/`IOW` (for I/O), and moves one word.
6. **DMAC Increments/Decrements Address and Decrements Count**: Internal registers (Base Address, Current Address, Base Word Count, Current Word Count) are updated.
7. **DMAC Releases HRQ**: When the word count reaches zero, the DMAC de-asserts `HRQ` and asserts an **interrupt** to the CPU.
8. **CPU Regains Control**: The CPU de-asserts `HLDA` and resumes execution from the next instruction.

### 2.3 DMA Transfer Modes

The KTU syllabus mandates coverage of three transfer modes. They differ only in *when* the DMAC is allowed to steal a bus cycle from the CPU.

| Mode | Behaviour | CPU Impact | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Burst Mode (Block Transfer)** | DMAC takes the bus and holds it until the entire block is transferred. | CPU is **completely frozen** for the duration. | Disk-to-memory backups, memory-to-memory copies. |
| **Cycle Stealing Mode** | DMAC transfers **one word**, then releases the bus back to the CPU for one cycle, then steals again. | CPU slows down by ~50% during the DMA window. | Real-time streams (audio, serial ports, keyboards). |
| **Transparent Mode** | DMAC only transfers data when the CPU is **executing an instruction that does not use the bus** (e.g., internal ALU operation). | CPU **runs at full speed**; zero performance penalty. | Slow devices, mainframe-class mainframes. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**
> In **Cycle Stealing Mode**, the DMAC is effectively executing one bus cycle for every CPU bus cycle, making the effective throughput of the system halve. This is why burst mode is preferred for bulk transfers and cycle stealing is preferred for latency-sensitive peripherals.

### 2.4 DMA Transfer Types (Data Path Architectures)

This is a **high-weight KTU question area** (often asked for 7 marks).

#### Type 1: Fly-By Transfer (Direct / Single-Bus Transfer)

- The I/O device and the Memory are connected to the **same data bus**.
- A single bus read/write operation handles both: the DMAC asserts both `MEMR` and `IOW` (or `MEMW` and `IOR`) simultaneously.
- **One address cycle, one data cycle.** Extremely fast.
- **Limitation**: Cannot perform Memory-to-Memory or I/O-to-I/O transfers.

#### Type 2: Fetch-Store Transfer (Indirect / Dual-Address Transfer)

- The DMAC reads the data from the source into an **internal holding register**, then writes it to the destination on a *separate* bus cycle.
- **Two address cycles, two data cycles.** Slower than Fly-By.
- **Advantage**: Can do Memory-to-Memory transfers and supports the chaining of multiple buffers (called **Cascade Mode** in the 8237).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Boundary Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you are likely to need for solving a KTU 2024 ESE numerical on DMA. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Boundary | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum transfer block size (8237) | $N_{\max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535$ | words, per single DMA operation |
| 2 | Effective CPU slowdown (Cycle Stealing) | $\eta_{\text{CPU}} \approx 50\%$ | One stolen cycle per CPU cycle |
| 3 | Bus utilization by DMAC (Burst Mode) | $U_{\text{bus}} = 100\%$ | CPU has 0% bus access during burst |
| 4 | Total transfer time (Burst Mode) | $T_{\text{burst}} = \dfrac{N}{f_{\text{bus}}}$ | seconds; $N$ = words, $f_{\text{bus}}$ in Hz |
| 5 | Total transfer time (Cycle Stealing) | $T_{\text{cs}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{N}{f_{\text{bus}}}$ | seconds; factor of 2 because of interleaving |
| 6 | Transfer rate (Fly-By) | $R_{\text{fly}} = \dfrac{W \cdot f_{\text{bus}}}{1}$ | bytes/sec; $W$ = bus width |
| 7 | Transfer rate (Fetch-Store) | $R_{\text{fs}} = \dfrac{W \cdot f_{\text{bus}}}{2}$ | bytes/sec; factor of 2 due to 2 cycles per word |
| 8 | Number of DMA channels (8237) | 4 (or 8 cascaded) | channels per controller chip |
| 9 | Address space per channel | 16-bit addressing | 64 KB (8-bit mode) or 128 KB (16-bit mode) |
| 10 | Auto-initialization boundary | $C_{\text{word}} = 0$ triggers termination | TC (Terminal Count) signal asserted |
