> [!WARNING]
> The vertical bar in the table above has been rendered using `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser happy. If you copy-paste this into a notes application, be aware that **absolute value symbols** must NEVER be raw `|` inside a table.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in the Real World

DMA is the unsung hero of every modern device:

- **Hard Disk SSDs/HDDs**: The SATA/NVMe controller uses DMA to dump megabytes per second directly into the page cache.
- **Network Interface Cards (NICs)**: A 1 Gbps Ethernet NIC generates ~1.4 million packets/second at minimum size; only DMA can keep up.
- **Audio/Video**: Sound cards use DMA to stream PCM samples into the DAC without CPU intervention.
- **GPUs**: Modern GPUs are essentially monstrous DMA-capable bus masters that pull texture data directly from VRAM using **Direct Memory Access over PCIe** (often called **bus mastering DMA**).
- **Embedded Systems**: ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers use the **MDMA (Memory DMA)** and **DMA1/DMA2 controllers** to offload ADC, SPI, and UART transfers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Time in Burst vs. Cycle Stealing Mode

This is a classic KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problem. The examiner loves to ask: "Given a 16-bit DMA controller operating at 5 MHz transferring 4096 bytes from a hard disk to memory, compute the time taken in (a) Burst mode and (b) Cycle Stealing mode."

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters from the problem statement.**

$$
N = 4096 \text{ words}, \quad W = 16 \text{ bits}, \quad f_{\text{bus}} = 5 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bus cycle period.**

$$
T_{\text{bus}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{bus}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 0.2 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Burst Mode transfer time.**

In Burst Mode, the DMAC acquires the bus and holds it for the entire transfer. Each word requires exactly one bus cycle.

$$
T_{\text{burst}} = N \times T_{\text{bus}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{burst}} &= 4096 \times 0.2 \, \mu s \\
&= 819.2 \, \mu s \\
&= 0.8192 \, \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the Cycle Stealing transfer time.**

In Cycle Stealing, the DMAC transfers one word, then yields the bus for one CPU cycle, then steals again. Thus, each word effectively consumes **2 bus cycles**.

$$
T_{\text{cs}} = 2 \cdot N \cdot T_{\text{bus}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cs}} &= 2 \times 4096 \times 0.2 \, \mu s \\
&= 1638.4 \, \mu s \\
&= 1.6384 \, \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

The Cycle Stealing mode takes **exactly twice as long** as the Burst mode. The CPU, however, gets to execute instructions at 50% of its normal speed during cycle stealing, which is acceptable for real-time peripherals.

### 3.2 Derivation: Memory Bandwidth Saved by DMA

**Step 1 — Define the I/O-bound CPU waste without DMA.**

Assume a CPU clock of $f_{\text{CPU}}$ and an I/O data rate of $R_{\text{IO}}$ bytes/sec. In Programmed I/O, the CPU spends $K$ clock cycles per byte.

$$
T_{\text{wasted}} = K \times R_{\text{IO}} \times \frac{1}{f_{\text{CPU}}} \text{ seconds of CPU time per second}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the savings with DMA.**

With DMA, the CPU only spends $S$ cycles for the **entire block setup** (where $S \ll K \cdot N$).

$$
S_{\text{DMA}} = \frac{S}{f_{\text{CPU}}} \text{ seconds per transfer}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the percentage of CPU time saved.**

$$
\text{Saving} \% = \left(1 - \frac{S}{K \cdot N}\right) \times 100
$$

For a typical hard-disk transfer of $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ bytes with $K = 50$ CPU cycles/byte and $S = 200$ cycles of setup:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Saving} \% &= \left(1 - \frac{200}{50 \times 1{,}000{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{200}{50{,}000{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= (1 - 0.000004) \times 100 \\
&= 99.9996 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that DMA is essential for high-bandwidth I/O.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The Three I/O Methods Side-by-Side

| Parameter | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU cycles per byte | $K$ (high) | Moderate (interrupt overhead) | $S/N$ (negligible) |
| Hardware complexity | Trivial (IN/OUT) | Medium (interrupt controller) | High (full DMAC chip) |
| Best for | Very slow, simple devices (switches) | Moderate-speed (keyboards, mice) | High-speed (disk, NIC, audio) |
| Latency | Predictable but blocks CPU | Low latency, jitter from interrupt handler | Predictable, zero CPU intervention |
| Power consumption | High (CPU always active) | Moderate | **Lowest** (CPU can sleep) |
| Data path | CPU register ↔ I/O | CPU register ↔ I/O | DMAC ↔ Memory directly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of a DMA Architecture

This is the **most important diagram** for a KTU 2024 Module 4 exam. It shows the tri-bus architecture with the DMAC sitting between the CPU, Memory, and I/O devices.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu[CPU] --Address Bus--> busarb[System Bus Arbiter]
    cpu --Data Bus--> busarb
    cpu --Control Bus--> busarb
    busarb --HRQ and HLDA--> dmac[DMA Controller DMAC]
    dmac --DREQ Channel 0--> devA[Hard Disk Controller]
    dmac --DREQ Channel 1--> devB[Network Card]
    dmac --DREQ Channel 2--> devC[Sound Card]
    dmac --DREQ Channel 3--> devD[Floppy Disk]
    dmac --Address and Data Buses--> memory[Main Memory RAM]
    devA -.DACK0.-> dmac
    devB -.DACK1.-> dmac
    devC -.DACK2.-> dmac
    devD -.DACK3.-> dmac
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam**: The CPU is the default master. The DMAC requests the bus from the System Bus Arbiter via `HRQ`. The arbiter responds with `HLDA` and tri-states the CPU's buses. The DMAC then drives the address/data buses directly to memory, while the I/O device provides/receives data on a separate channel.

### 4.2 Internal Block Diagram of a Generic DMA Controller

The internal registers are the focus of **7-mark** KTU questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ctrl[Control Logic and Timing Unit]
    baseAddr[Base Address Register]
    currAddr[Current Address Register]
    baseCount[Base Word Count Register]
    currCount[Current Word Count Register]
    mode[Mode Register]
    mask[Mask Register]
    status[Status Register]
    ctrl --- mode
    ctrl --- mask
    ctrl --- status
    baseAddr --> ctrl
    currAddr --> ctrl
    baseCount --> ctrl
    currCount --> ctrl
```

**Description of internal registers:**

- **Base Address Register**: Holds the **starting address** of the memory block. Auto-loaded on auto-initialization.
- **Current Address Register**: Holds the **current address** being accessed; incremented/decremented after each transfer.
- **Base Word Count Register**: Stores the **initial number of words** to transfer.
- **Current Word Count Register**: Stores the **remaining word count**; decremented after each transfer; when it hits zero, the **TC (Terminal Count)** signal is asserted.
- **Mode Register**: Selects **Read/Write/Verify**, **Auto-Init On/Off**, **Address Increment/Decrement**, and the transfer **mode** (Demand, Single, Block, Cascade).
- **Mask Register**: Used to disable a DMA channel (e.g., DREQ0 = 0 means channel 0 is masked and cannot request).
- **Status Register**: Indicates which channels have reached TC.

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram: Burst Mode DMA Handshake

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant IODev as I/O Device
    participant DMAC as DMA Controller
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant Mem as Main Memory

    IODev->>DMAC: DREQ asserted (data ready)
    DMAC->>CPU: HRQ asserted (bus request)
    CPU->>DMAC: HLDA asserted (bus granted)
    Note over CPU: CPU tri-states its buses
    DMAC->>IODev: DACK asserted (acknowledged)
    loop Until word count is zero
        DMAC->>Mem: Place address on bus, assert MEMR or MEMW
        Mem-->>IODev: Data transferred directly
        DMAC->>DMAC: Increment address, decrement count
    end
    DMAC->>CPU: HRQ de-asserted, INT asserted
    CPU->>DMAC: HLDA de-asserted, CPU resumes
```

### 4.4 Comparison of Fly-By vs. Fetch-Store Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FlyBy[Fly-By Direct Transfer]
        io1[I/O Device] --Single Bus Cycle--> mem1[Memory]
    end
    subgraph FetchStore[Fetch-Store Indirect Transfer]
        io2[I/O Device] --> hr[Internal Holding Register in DMAC]
        hr --> mem2[Memory]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**: In Fly-By, only **one** address is put on the address bus per transfer (either source or destination). In Fetch-Store, the DMAC must put **two** addresses per word — one for the source read, one for the destination write. This is why Fly-By is twice as fast but only works for I/O↔Memory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Direct Memory Access (DMA). Mention any two advantages of using DMA over programmed I/O.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Direct Memory Access is a technique that allows I/O devices to transfer data to or from the main memory directly, without the continuous involvement of the CPU. A dedicated hardware called the DMA Controller (DMAC) takes temporary control of the system bus to perform the transfer.

**Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **CPU offloading**: The CPU is freed from the burden of byte-by-byte data movement, allowing it to execute useful instructions in parallel. This drastically improves system throughput.
2. **Higher data transfer rate**: For high-speed peripherals like disks and network cards, DMA can sustain transfer rates of tens or hundreds of MB/s, which is impossible with programmed I/O.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Burst Mode DMA and Cycle Stealing Mode DMA.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per row, 3 differences):**

| Parameter | Burst Mode DMA | Cycle Stealing DMA |
|---|---|---|
| Bus holding | DMAC holds the bus for the **entire** block. | DMAC releases the bus after **each word**. |
| CPU activity | CPU is **completely halted** during the transfer. | CPU is interleaved; **slows down by ~50%** but still functional. |
| Use case | Bulk transfers (disk backups, memory copies). | Real-time streams (audio, serial I/O). |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme follows the ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern where each module allows an **internal choice**. Below are two alternative questions (A and B), each split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Replicated for 2024 Scheme]**

**A (a)** Explain the block diagram of a DMA controller and the function of its internal registers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

A DMA controller is a peripheral device that manages multiple DMA channels. Its internal architecture consists of the following registers, each serving a distinct purpose:

1. **Base Address Register (BAR)** — Stores the **starting memory address** of the transfer. This register is **read-only** during the transfer; it is reloaded only during auto-initialization. **(1 mark)**

2. **Current Address Register (CAR)** — Holds the **active address** being read from or written to. After every successful bus cycle, the DMAC automatically **increments or decrements** this register (depending on the mode register setting). When CAR is read, the user can see the current transfer progress. **(1 mark)**

3. **Base Word Count Register (BWCR)** — Stores the **initial number of words** to be transferred. Loaded by the CPU before the transfer starts. **(1 mark)**

4. **Current Word Count Register (CWCR)** — Holds the **remaining word count**. Decremented after each transfer. When CWCR reaches **zero**, the DMAC asserts the **Terminal Count (TC)** signal, marking the end of the DMA operation. **(1 mark)**

5. **Mode Register** — Configures the channel's behavior: read/write direction, auto-initialization on/off, address increment/decrement, and the transfer mode (Single, Block, Demand, Cascade). **(1 mark)**

6. **Mask Register** — Each channel has a mask bit. When set, the corresponding `DREQ` line is **inhibited**, preventing the channel from requesting the bus. Useful for temporarily disabling a DMA channel. **(1 mark)**

7. **Control & Status Registers** — The control register is written by the CPU to start/stop a DMA. The status register indicates which channels have completed (TC reached). **(1 mark)**

---

**A (b)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the DMA transfer in **Burst Mode** for an I/O-to-Memory transfer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Initialization by the CPU (1 mark)**
The CPU programs the DMAC by writing the source I/O address, the destination memory address, and the byte count into the DMAC's internal registers via normal I/O instructions. The CPU then enables the DMA channel by writing to the mask register.

**Step 2: I/O Device Raises DREQ (1 mark)**
When the I/O device has data ready (e.g., a hard disk has a sector in its buffer), it asserts its `DREQ` line connected to the DMAC.

**Step 3: DMAC Requests the Bus (1 mark)**
The DMAC combines the `DREQ` with its internal logic and asserts the **Hold Request (`HRQ`)** line to the CPU.

**Step 4: CPU Grants the Bus (1 mark)**
After completing its current instruction, the CPU asserts **Hold Acknowledge (`HLDA`)** and **tri-states** (electrically disconnects) its address, data, and control lines. The CPU then enters a **hold state** — it does not fetch or execute new instructions.

**Step 5: DMAC Performs the Transfer (1.5 marks)**
The DMAC places the destination memory address on the system address bus. It simultaneously asserts `IOR` (to read from the I/O device) and `MEMW` (to write to memory). In a single bus cycle, the data flows directly from the I/O device to memory. This is the **Fly-By** style. The DMAC increments CAR and decrements CWCR.

**Step 6: Repetition until TC (1 mark)**
Steps 4–5 repeat in **consecutive bus cycles** (this is why it is called "burst") until CWCR reaches zero, at which point the DMAC asserts the **TC (Terminal Count)** signal.

**Step 7: Bus Release and Interrupt (0.5 marks)**
The DMAC de-asserts `HRQ` and raises an interrupt (`INT`) to the CPU. The CPU de-asserts `HLDA`, regains control, and processes the interrupt handler.

**Timing Diagram (in words for the written exam, or as Mermaid for digital submission):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant DMAC as DMAC
    participant IODev as I/O Device
    participant Mem as Memory

    Note over CPU: CPU programs DMAC registers
    IODev->>DMAC: DREQ goes HIGH
    DMAC->>CPU: HRQ goes HIGH
    CPU->>DMAC: HLDA goes HIGH (CPU tri-states)
    Note over CPU: CPU is in HOLD state
    loop Burst (repeated N times)
        DMAC->>Mem: Address on bus
        DMAC->>IODev: IOR asserted
        DMAC->>Mem: MEMW asserted
        IODev-->>Mem: Data flows I/O to Memory
        DMAC->>DMAC: CAR++, CWCR--
    end
    DMAC->>CPU: TC signal, HRQ de-asserted
    DMAC->>CPU: INT asserted
    CPU->>DMAC: HLDA de-asserted
    Note over CPU: CPU resumes execution
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Replicated for 2024 Scheme]**

**B (a)** Explain the three transfer modes of DMA: **Burst Mode, Cycle Stealing Mode, and Transparent Mode**. Compare their relative impact on CPU performance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**1. Burst Mode (Block Transfer) — 2 marks**
In burst mode, the DMAC obtains control of the bus from the CPU and **retains it for the entire duration** of the data transfer, moving all bytes in one continuous operation. The CPU remains in a **hold state** and is unable to execute any instructions until the DMAC releases the bus. This mode is the **fastest** for DMA itself but causes the **worst CPU latency**.

**2. Cycle Stealing Mode — 2 marks**
In cycle stealing, the DMAC transfers **only one byte (or word)** at a time, after which it releases the bus back to the CPU for one cycle. It then re-requests the bus for the next byte. The DMAC effectively "steals" cycles from the CPU in an interleaved fashion. The CPU is **not completely halted** — it can execute instructions between stolen cycles — but its effective speed drops by approximately **50%**. This mode is widely used in **audio cards and serial ports** where the CPU still needs to process other tasks.

**3. Transparent Mode — 2 marks**
In transparent mode, the DMAC is designed to transfer data **only when the CPU is performing an operation that does not require the system bus** (e.g., an internal ALU computation, a register-to-register operation, or a multiplication that uses the internal hardware multiplier). This means the DMA transfer is "transparent" to the CPU — the CPU runs at **full speed** with **zero performance penalty**. The trade-off is that the transfer is slower overall (it must wait for the CPU's idle moments), so it is best suited for **slow, predictable peripheral devices** or mainframes.

**Comparison Table — 1 mark:**

| Aspect | Burst Mode | Cycle Stealing | Transparent Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU speed during DMA | 0% (halted) | ~50% | 100% (unaffected) |
| DMA transfer speed | Fastest | Moderate | Slowest |
| Hardware complexity | Low | Moderate | Highest |

---

**B (b)** Differentiate between **Fly-By DMA** and **Fetch-Store DMA**. Explain with a diagram how each handles an I/O-to-Memory transfer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**1. Fly-By DMA (Direct Transfer) — 3 marks**

In Fly-By DMA, the I/O device and the memory are connected to the **same data bus**. The DMAC puts the target memory address on the address bus and asserts **both** `IOR` (I/O Read) and `MEMW` (Memory Write) **simultaneously**. The data travels directly from the I/O device to memory in a **single bus cycle**. There is no intermediate register inside the DMAC.

- **Pros:** Very fast (one bus cycle per word).
- **Cons:** The source and destination cannot both be memory (no memory-to-memory support). Cannot support I/O-to-I/O either. Only I/O ↔ Memory.

**2. Fetch-Store DMA (Indirect Transfer) — 3 marks**

In Fetch-Store DMA, the DMAC first reads the data from the source into its **internal holding register** (fetch), and then writes that data to the destination on a **separate bus cycle** (store). This requires **two address cycles per word**: one for the source read, one for the destination write.

- **Pros:** Supports memory-to-memory transfers, I/O-to-I/O transfers, and buffer chaining.
- **Cons:** Twice as slow as Fly-By because of the extra bus cycle per word.

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FlyByBlock[Fly-By Direct Path]
        io1[I/O Device] --Address A on bus, IOR + MEMW asserted--> mem1[Memory at Address A]
    end
    subgraph FetchStoreBlock[Fetch-Store Indirect Path]
        io2[I/O Device at Address A] --Cycle 1 Read--> hr[DMA Internal Holding Register]
        hr --Cycle 2 Write--> mem2[Memory at Address B]
    end
```

In the diagram, note the **two distinct address cycles** in the Fetch-Store path versus the **single combined cycle** in Fly-By.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing DREQ and HRQ**: DREQ is from I/O Device **to** DMAC. HRQ is from DMAC **to** CPU. Many students draw the arrow backwards.
> 2. **Forgetting that the CPU does not "stop" in Cycle Stealing**: The CPU is slowed but not halted. Saying "CPU is halted" in cycle stealing mode will cost you a mark.
> 3. **Mixing Fly-By with Cycle Stealing**: They are orthogonal concepts. Fly-By describes the **data path** (one cycle vs. two cycles per word). Cycle Stealing describes the **bus arbitration policy** (continuous vs. interleaved). Students often confuse them.
> 4. **Wrong Terminal Count (TC) trigger**: TC is triggered when CWCR reaches zero, **not** when CAR equals the base address. The count register is the trigger, not the address register.
> 5. **Omitting the 8237's 64 KB limit**: Many answers fail to mention the 16-bit address limitation of the classic 8237 DMAC, which is a favorite KTU 2024 Scheme question.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DMA = offloading bulk I/O from the CPU to a dedicated DMAC chip**.
- The DMAC uses the **same system bus** as the CPU, so arbitration is required via `HRQ`/`HLDA`.
- **Three transfer modes**: Burst (fast, CPU halted), Cycle Stealing (50% CPU slowdown), Transparent (zero CPU impact).
- **Two transfer types**: Fly-By (1 cycle/word, I/O↔Memory only) and Fetch-Store (2 cycles/word, supports M↔M).
- **The 8237 DMAC** has 4 channels, 16-bit addresses, and a max transfer size of 64 KB. Modern systems use 32/64-bit DMACs.
- **TC (Terminal Count)** = end of DMA, triggered by CWCR reaching zero.
- **Mask register** is used to disable a channel without modifying its setup.
- **Auto-initialization** allows the DMAC to automatically reload the Base Address and Base Word Count from the Base registers, useful for circular buffers (e.g., audio).
- **DMA cycle stealing factor of 2** in transfer time is a frequent KTU numerical.
- **Real-world examples**: HDDs, NICs, GPUs, sound cards, and high-speed serial peripherals all rely on DMA.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme focus**: Expect at least one 7-mark question on transfer modes or on the 8237 register architecture in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Embedded I/O System?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> An **Embedded I/O System** refers to the specialized collection of input/output hardware, registers, and control logic integrated within a microcontroller or System-on-Chip (SoC) that enables the processor core to interact with the external physical world. Unlike general-purpose computer I/O (PCIe, USB, SATA), embedded I/O is **tightly coupled to the CPU**, often sharing the same address/data bus, and is optimized for **low power, deterministic response, and real-time operation**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠
Think of the CPU as the **brain** of an embedded system (e.g., a washing machine controller or a car's ABS unit). The **Embedded I/O System** is the **nervous system + hands** of the brain. The CPU cannot directly touch a motor or read a temperature sensor — it needs specialized pathways (registers, pins, controllers) to send electrical signals out (to actuators) and receive electrical signals in (from sensors). These pathways must be **fast, predictable, and energy-efficient** because embedded systems often run on batteries and must respond within microseconds to brake a car or open a valve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Characteristic — Determinism**
> Unlike desktop I/O (where a millisecond delay is acceptable), embedded I/O must guarantee a worst-case response time. This is why embedded systems often use **memory-mapped I/O** with dedicated registers rather than bus-mastering DMA for simple tasks.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Embedded I/O

| Pillar | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **General Purpose I/O (GPIO)** | Discrete digital bit-level input/output | LED control, push-button reading |
| **Serial I/O** | Sequential bit-stream communication with peripherals | UART to PC, SPI to SD card, I²C to sensor |
| **Other Peripherals** | Specialized analog/timing/control hardware | Timers, ADC, DAC, Watchdog, PWM |

---

## 1.3 General Purpose I/O (GPIO)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> A **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) pin** is a software-controllable digital signal pin on a microcontroller that has no predefined purpose. At any given moment, the firmware can configure the pin to act as either a **digital input** (reading 0 V or V_DD) or a **digital output** (driving 0 V or V_DD). Each GPIO pin is typically controlled by a set of **memory-mapped configuration registers**.

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠
Imagine a wall full of identical **light switches** in a building. Each switch is not permanently wired to a specific light — instead, an **electrician (configuration register)** can re-wire each switch on the fly to control *any* light. Some switches can also be flipped into "sensor mode" to *read* whether a door is open. **GPIO is this re-programmability** for microcontrollers.

### Key GPIO Configuration Parameters
- **Direction:** Input or Output (configured via Direction Register — DDR)
- **Output Value:** Logic 0 or Logic 1 (via Port Register)
- **Input Read:** Sampled logic level (via Pin Register)
- **Pull-up / Pull-down Resistors:** Internal resistors to default the pin to a known state when floating
- **Drive Strength:** Current sourcing/sinking capability
- **Slew Rate Control:** Speed of voltage transition (for EMI reduction)
- **Interrupt Capability:** Ability to wake the CPU on a pin state change

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Most modern MCUs (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC) provide **8-bit GPIO ports** (e.g., PORT A, PORT B). A 32-pin MCU typically has 4 ports × 8 bits = 32 GPIO lines.

---

## 1.4 Serial I/O

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Serial I/O** is a method of data transmission where bits are sent **one after another over a single wire (or differential pair)**, synchronized by a clock. In embedded systems, three protocols dominate: **UART (asynchronous)**, **SPI (synchronous, full-duplex)**, and **I²C (synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master)**.

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠
Picture a **conveyor belt** in a factory:
- **Parallel I/O** is 8 conveyor belts side by side — 8 items (a byte) arrive at once. Fast, but needs 8 wires.
- **Serial I/O (UART)** is 1 conveyor belt where a **foreman blows a whistle (start bit)** to announce the first item, items are placed one at a time, and a **whistle (stop bit)** marks the end. No shared clock — both sides must agree on speed beforehand.
- **Serial I/O (SPI)** is 1 conveyor belt with a **visible clock signal** ticking beside it — both sender and receiver march to the same drummer.
- **Serial I/O (I²C)** is a **shared conveyor belt** between many workers, where only the worker whose **name is called (address)** is allowed to listen or speak.

---

## 1.5 Other Peripherals (Special-Purpose I/O)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Other peripherals** are dedicated hardware blocks within the MCU/SoC that perform specialized I/O functions beyond simple digital I/O. These include **Timers/Counters, ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter), DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter), PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) controllers, Watchdog Timers, and Interrupt Controllers**.

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠
If GPIO is the **hands** of the CPU, peripherals are the **specialized tools**:
- **Timer/Counter** → a stopwatch for measuring time intervals
- **ADC** → a translator that converts human voice (analog) into written text (digital)
- **DAC** → the reverse translator (text to voice)
- **PWM** → a dimmer switch that flickers an LED so fast your eyes see it as half-brightness
- **Watchdog Timer** → a strict supervisor that reboots the system if the CPU "falls asleep" too long

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 GPIO Operational Model — Register Architecture

Every GPIO pin is typically controlled by **three core registers** (with modern MCUs adding more):

1. **Direction Register (DDR / TRIS):** Configures pin as INPUT (0) or OUTPUT (1)
2. **Output Register (PORT / LAT):** The value to drive on the pin when configured as output
3. **Input Register (PIN / PORT-in):** The actual logic level read from the pin

### Why three separate registers?
The **decoupling of read and write** prevents the classic "read-modify-write" hazard. If a single register did both, doing `PORT = PORT | 0x01` could cause a glitch if the pin's physical state differs from the register state (e.g., a short circuit forces the pin LOW, but the register "remembers" HIGH).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Enhancement: Write-Only LAT Register**
> PIC and ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers separate the read path (PORT) and write path (LAT/Latch) into two distinct registers. Writes go to LAT, reads come from PORT — eliminating the read-modify-write problem entirely.

### GPIO Input Modes Explained
| Mode | Behavior | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Floating (High-Z)** | Pin has no defined state when not driven | External pull-up/down required |
| **Pull-up** | Internal resistor to V_DD | Default HIGH when button open |
| **Pull-down** | Internal resistor to GND | Default LOW when button open |
| **Analog** | Pin disconnected from digital input, routed to ADC | Sensor reading |
| **Open-Drain** | Can only pull LOW; needs external pull-up | I²C bus, wired-OR logic |

### GPIO Output Modes Explained
| Mode | Behavior | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Push-Pull** | Drives both HIGH and LOW actively | LEDs, digital logic |
| **Open-Drain** | Only pulls LOW; external pull-up sets HIGH | I²C, level shifting |

---

## 2.2 Serial I/O Protocol Deep-Dive

### 2.2.1 UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** UART is an **asynchronous, full-duplex** serial protocol. No shared clock line — both ends must pre-agree on a **baud rate** (bits per second). Each frame consists of: **Start bit (0), 5–9 data bits, optional Parity bit, 1–2 Stop bits (1)**.

#### The Baud Rate Formula (CRITICAL for KTU)

$$f_{baud} = \frac{f_{clock}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$$

Or equivalently, the **UBRR (USART Baud Rate Register)** value is:

$$UBRR = \frac{f_{clock}}{16 \times f_{baud}} - 1$$

Where:
- $f_{clock}$ = peripheral clock frequency (Hz)
- $f_{baud}$ = desired baud rate (bits/second)
- $UBRR$ = integer register value (must be $\geq 0$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Baud Rates:** **9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200** bps. The 16× factor exists because the UART hardware **oversamples** the incoming bit at 16× the baud rate, then samples each bit at the middle (bit 8 of 16 samples) for maximum noise margin.

#### Frame Timing for One UART Byte (10 bits: 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop at 9600 baud)

$$T_{frame} = \frac{10 \text{ bits}}{9600 \text{ bps}} = 1.0417 \text{ ms}$$

#### Error Rate Calculation

$$\text{Error \%} = \left| \frac{f_{baud,\_actual} - f_{baud,\_ideal}}{f_{baud,\_ideal}} \right| \times 100$$

Where:

$$f_{baud,\_actual} = \frac{f_{clock}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)}$$

> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** UART error must be **< 2%** for reliable communication. Standard crystal frequencies (8 MHz, 16 MHz) are chosen specifically to give clean divisions for popular baud rates.

---

### 2.2.2 SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** SPI is a **synchronous, full-duplex, master-slave** protocol using **4 wires**: SCLK (clock), MOSI (Master Out Slave In), MISO (Master In Slave Out), SS/CS (Slave Select). Speed: typically 1 MHz to 50+ MHz.

#### SPI Configuration Parameters
- **Clock Polarity (CPOL):** Idle state of SCLK (0 = LOW, 1 = HIGH)
- **Clock Phase (CPHA):** Which edge of SCLK latches data (0 = leading, 1 = trailing)
- **Bit Order:** MSB-first or LSB-first

> Combined, CPOL × CPHA create **4 SPI "modes" (0, 1, 2, 3)**. Master and slave MUST use the same mode.

#### SPI Throughput

$$\text{Throughput}_{SPI} = f_{SCLK} \times \text{bits per frame}$$

For an 8-bit frame at 10 MHz: $\text{Throughput} = 10 \text{ MHz} \times 8 = 80 \text{ Mbit/s}$

---

### 2.2.3 I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** I²C is a **synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master** protocol using **2 wires**: SDA (data) and SCL (clock). Both lines are **open-drain** with pull-up resistors. Addressing is **7-bit or 10-bit**, allowing up to **127 (or 1023)** devices on one bus.

#### Standard I²C Speeds
- **Standard Mode:** 100 kHz
- **Fast Mode:** 400 kHz
- **Fast Mode Plus:** 1 MHz
- **High Speed Mode:** 3.4 MHz

#### Bit Timing (per bit period)

$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{f_{SCL}}$$

For 100 kHz: $T_{bit} = 10 \mu s$

#### Pull-up Resistor Selection

$$R_{min} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}, \quad R_{max} = \frac{t_{r}}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}}$$

Where $C_{bus}$ = total bus capacitance (typical max **400 pF**), $t_r$ = rise time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wired-AND Logic:** Because all I²C devices use open-drain outputs, the bus is pulled HIGH by the pull-ups and any device can pull it LOW. This naturally implements the I²C arbitration protocol when multiple masters attempt to transmit simultaneously — whichever master first outputs a HIGH while the other outputs LOW loses arbitration.

---

## 2.3 Other Peripherals — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Timers / Counters

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A timer/counter is a hardware register that **increments (or decrements) on every clock tick** (timer mode) or **external event** (counter mode). When the register rolls over (or matches a compare value), it can generate an interrupt or trigger a hardware action.

#### Timer Frequency / Period Formulas

$$f_{timer} = \frac{f_{clock}}{Prescaler \times (Reload + 1)}$$

$$T_{overflow} = \frac{1}{f_{timer}} = \frac{Prescaler \times (Reload + 1)}{f_{clock}}$$

For an 8-bit timer (max count = 255) with prescaler 64 and $f_{clock} = 8 \text{ MHz}$:

$$T_{overflow} = \frac{64 \times 256}{8 \times 10^6} = 2.048 \text{ ms}$$

#### PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) Duty Cycle

$$\text{Duty Cycle \%} = \frac{T_{HIGH}}{T_{period}} \times 100 = \frac{Compare\ Value}{Reload + 1} \times 100$$

> **Real-world use:** Motor speed control, LED dimming, servo motor positioning, audio generation.

---

### 2.3.2 ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An ADC converts a continuous analog voltage into a discrete digital number. Resolution is specified in **bits** (typically 8, 10, 12, 16, 24).

#### Resolution (Step Size / LSB Voltage)

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref+} - V_{ref-}}{2^n}$$

Where $n$ = ADC resolution in bits.

For a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$:

$$\Delta V = \frac{3.3}{2^{10}} = \frac{3.3}{1024} = 3.2227 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

#### Quantization Error (Worst Case)

$$E_{q} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}$$

#### Digital Output for a Given Input Voltage

$$D_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} - V_{ref-}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \times 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor$$

#### Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times n + 1.76$$

For a 12-bit ADC: $\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$

---

### 2.3.3 DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)

$$V_{out} = \frac{D_{in}}{2^n} \times V_{ref}$$

For $D_{in} = 2048$, 12-bit DAC, $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$:

$$V_{out} = \frac{2048}{4096} \times 3.3 = 1.65 \text{ V}$$

---

### 2.3.4 Watchdog Timer (WDT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Watchdog Timer** is a hardware timer that **resets the MCU** if the firmware fails to "pet" (refresh) it within a programmed interval. It is the last line of defense against software lockups.

$$T_{WDT} = \frac{Prescaler \times Reload}{f_{WDT\_clock}}$$

> **Real-world use:** Automotive ECUs, medical devices, industrial PLCs — anywhere a software hang could cause safety hazards.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is exam-critical. Memorize all equations, units, and boundary conditions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | UART Baud Rate | $UBRR = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{16 \times f_{baud}} - 1$ | $f$ in Hz, $UBRR \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | UART Frame Time | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{1 + D + P + S}{f_{baud}}$ | D=data bits, P=parity, S=stop bits |
| 3 | SPI Throughput | $R_{SPI} = f_{SCLK} \times N_{bits}$ | Bits per second |
| 4 | I²C Bit Time | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{f_{SCL}}$ | Standard mode: 10 μs |
| 5 | Timer Overflow | $T_{ovf} = \dfrac{Prescaler \times (Reload+1)}{f_{clock}}$ | Seconds |
| 6 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D\% = \dfrac{Compare}{Reload+1} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 7 | ADC Resolution (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Volts per LSB |
| 8 | ADC Digital Output | $D_{out} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n \right\rfloor$ | Integer 0 to $2^n - 1$ |
| 9 | ADC Quantization Error | $E_q = \pm \dfrac{1}{2}$ LSB | Worst case |
| 10 | ADC SQNR | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Decibels |
| 11 | DAC Output | $V_{out} = \dfrac{D_{in}}{2^n} \times V_{ref}$ | Volts |
| 12 | WDT Interval | $T_{WDT} = \dfrac{Prescaler \times Reload}{f_{WDT}}$ | Seconds |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are **exam-grade worked examples** that demonstrate how to apply the formulas in Section 2. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: UART UBRR Calculation for 115200 Baud

**Problem:** An ATmega328P MCU has $f_{clock} = 16 \text{ MHz}$. Calculate the UBRR value for 115200 baud, and find the actual baud rate and percentage error.

### Step 1 — Write the master formula
$$UBRR = \frac{f_{clock}}{16 \times f_{baud}} - 1$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the known values
$$UBRR = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 115200} - 1$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the denominator
$$16 \times 115200 = 1{,}843{,}200$$

### Step 4 — Perform the division
$$UBRR = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{1{,}843{,}200} - 1 = 8.681 - 1 = 7.681$$

### Step 5 — Round to nearest integer (UBRR must be an integer)
$$UBRR = 8 \text{ (rounded)}$$

> A more accurate UBRR value of **8.681** is sometimes achievable via the **UBRRnL/UBRRnH double-speed mode** (U2X = 1), which uses an 8× multiplier instead of 16×.

### Step 6 — Compute the actual baud rate (using UBRR = 8)
$$f_{baud,\_actual} = \frac{f_{clock}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 9} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{144}$$

$$f_{baud,\_actual} = 111{,}111.11 \text{ bps}$$

### Step 7 — Compute percentage error
$$\text{Error \%} = \left| \frac{111{,}111.11 - 115{,}200}{115{,}200} \right| \times 100$$

$$= \left| \frac{-4{,}088.89}{115{,}200} \right| \times 100 = 0.0355 \times 100 = 3.55\%$$

### Step 8 — Engineering Conclusion
> [!WARNING]
> The error (3.55%) **exceeds the 2% reliability threshold**. This is why at 16 MHz, **115200 baud has noticeable errors** on ATmega328P. For higher reliability at 16 MHz, the recommended baud is **38400** (error ≈ 0.16%).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: 10-bit ADC Conversion of a Thermistor Voltage

**Problem:** An NTC thermistor outputs $V_{in} = 1.234 \text{ V}$. The ADC has $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ and is configured for 10-bit resolution. Compute the digital output, the LSB voltage, and the quantization error.

### Step 1 — Calculate LSB (Step Size)
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{2^{10}} = \frac{3.3}{1024}$$

$$\Delta V = 0.003223 \text{ V} = 3.2227 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the ideal (fractional) digital count
$$D_{ideal} = \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} = \frac{1.234}{0.003223} = 382.86$$

### Step 3 — Apply the floor function (ADC is an integer quantizer)
$$D_{out} = \lfloor 382.86 \rfloor = 382$$

### Step 4 — Compute the quantization error
$$E_q = V_{in} - (D_{out} \times \Delta V) = 1.234 - (382 \times 0.003223)$$

$$E_q = 1.234 - 1.2312 = 0.0028 \text{ V} = 2.8 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 5 — Verify against the worst-case bound
$$E_{q,\_worst} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{3.2227}{2} = \pm 1.611 \text{ mV}$$

> Our 2.8 mV error is within $\pm 1.611$ mV? **NO!** Wait — let us recompute:
>
> $$E_q = 1.234 - 1.2312 = 0.0028 \text{ V} = 2.8 \text{ mV}$$
>
> The worst-case bound is $\pm 1.61$ mV. The error $2.8$ mV **exceeds** the bound because we are using the *floor* of $382.86$ but the ADC's internal comparator rounds to the nearest. Most ADCs use **mid-rise quantization** (round to nearest), so the actual digital output is $383$ and the error is $-1.17$ mV, well within $\pm 1.61$ mV.

### Step 6 — Re-compute with mid-rise (round-to-nearest) quantization
$$D_{out} = \text{round}(382.86) = 383$$

$$E_q = 1.234 - (383 \times 0.003223) = 1.234 - 1.2344 = -0.0004 \text{ V} = -0.4 \text{ mV}$$

> Within $\pm 1.61$ mV ✓

### Step 7 — Compute SQNR
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Timer Overflow & Software Debounce

**Problem:** A push-button is connected to a GPIO pin. The mechanical bounce lasts at most **10 ms**. Configure an 8-bit timer on a 16 MHz MCU to generate an **exact 10 ms overflow** interrupt, and use it to debounce the button.

### Step 1 — Choose a prescaler
The maximum count of an 8-bit timer is $2^8 = 256$. The maximum reload time is therefore:
$$T_{max} = \frac{Prescaler \times 256}{f_{clock}}$$

For $T_{max} \geq 10 \text{ ms}$:
$$Prescaler \geq \frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \times 16 \times 10^6}{256} = 625$$

Choose the next valid prescaler. AVR prescalers are 1, 8, 64, 256, 1024. The smallest prescaler $\geq 625$ is **1024**.

### Step 2 — Solve for the reload value
$$T_{overflow} = \frac{Prescaler \times (Reload + 1)}{f_{clock}}$$

$$0.010 = \frac{1024 \times (Reload + 1)}{16 \times 10^6}$$

$$Reload + 1 = \frac{0.010 \times 16 \times 10^6}{1024} = \frac{160{,}000}{1024} = 156.25$$

Since 156.25 is not an integer, we **cannot achieve exactly 10 ms** with prescaler 1024. We round to $Reload + 1 = 156$:

$$T_{actual} = \frac{1024 \times 156}{16 \times 10^6} = 9.984 \text{ ms}$$

Error: $\frac{9.984 - 10}{10} \times 100 = -0.16\%$ (well within tolerance).

So $Reload = 155$ (we load 155, the timer counts 155, 156, ..., 255, then overflows on the next count, giving 256 - 155 = 101 counts? No — for 8-bit timer, we load the **initial value**, and overflow occurs when it rolls from 255 to 0).

> **Reload value = 256 - 156 = 100**. Verify: timer counts 100, 101, ..., 255, then 0 (overflow). That's 256 - 100 = **156 counts** ✓

$$T_{overflow} = \frac{1024 \times 156}{16 \times 10^6} = 9.984 \text{ ms} \approx 10 \text{ ms} ✓$$

### Step 3 — Debounce Logic
```text
ISR(Timer0_OVF_vect) {
    if (PIN_read == PRESSED) {
        button_state = PRESSED;     // accept the press only after 10 ms
    }
}
```

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Embedded I/O Comparison

| Parameter | GPIO | UART | SPI | I²C | Timer | ADC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Width** | 1 bit (parallel port) | 5–9 bits/frame | 8 or 16 bits | 8-bit bytes | 8/16/32 bit count | 8/10/12/16 bits |
| **Wires** | 1 per pin (or 8 per port) | 2 (TX, RX) | 4 (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 1 (output compare) | 1 (analog input) |
| **Clock** | N/A (async) | Async (pre-agreed) | Sync (SCLK) | Sync (SCL) | Internal (clock or event) | Internal (sample clock) |
| **Speed** | f_cpu / 2 (toggle) | Up to ~1 Mbaud | Up to 50+ MHz | 100k / 400k / 1M / 3.4M Hz | Depends on prescaler | Up to MS/s |
| **Duplex** | I or O only | Full | Full | Half | N/A | N/A |
| **Devices** | 1 per pin | Point-to-point | 1 master, N slaves (via SS) | Up to 127 nodes | N/A | Multiple channels |
| **Use Case** | LEDs, buttons, relays | Debug console, GPS, BT module | SD card, display, flash | Sensors, RTC, EEPROM | PWM, debounce, scheduling | Sensor reading, audio |
| **Power** | Very low | Low | Medium | Low | Very low | Medium (sample hold) |

---

## 3.5 Edge-Case Analytical Problems

### Problem A: Maximum ADC Sampling Rate
**Given:** A 12-bit ADC has 16 input channels and a total throughput of **1 MS/s** (1 million samples per second). What is the per-channel sample rate?

**Solution:**

$$f_{ch} = \frac{f_{total}}{N_{ch}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{16} = 62{,}500 \text{ samples/second/channel}$$

> Engineering insight: This is why **analog multiplexers** are used — one ADC is time-shared across many channels.

---

### Problem B: I²C Maximum Bus Length
**Given:** I²C standard mode (100 kHz), bus capacitance max = 400 pF, rise time $t_r$ ≤ 1000 ns. Find the maximum pull-up resistor.

**Solution:**

$$R_{max} = \frac{t_r}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-9}}{0.8473 \times 400 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$R_{max} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-9}}{338.9 \times 10^{-12}} = 2950 \text{ Ω} \approx 3 \text{ kΩ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, designers use **4.7 kΩ or 10 kΩ** pull-ups for short buses (capacitance < 100 pF).

---

### Problem C: PWM Audio via Timer
**Given:** Generate a **440 Hz square wave** (musical note A4) on an ATmega328P (16 MHz) using Timer1 (16-bit), with prescaler = 1.

**Solution:**

$$Reload + 1 = \frac{f_{clock}}{f_{out} \times Prescaler} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{440 \times 1} = 36{,}363.6$$

$$Reload = 36{,}362$$

$$f_{actual} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{36{,}363} = 440.0 \text{ Hz (with rounding error < 0.001\%)}$$

> To set 50% duty cycle: set Compare = $\frac{Reload}{2} = 18{,}181$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall Embedded I/O Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU Core<br>ALU + Registers"]
    BUS["System Bus<br>Address + Data"]
    MMU["Memory Map<br>Memory-Mapped I/O"]
    GPIO["GPIO Controller<br>Port A, B, C, D"]
    UART["UART Module<br>TX + RX + Baud Gen"]
    SPI["SPI Controller<br>SCLK MOSI MISO SS"]
    I2C["I2C Controller<br>SDA SCL"]
    TIM["Timers Counters<br>Timer0 1 2"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between memory-mapped I/O and port-mapped I/O. Which is more commonly used in embedded systems, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Memory-Mapped I/O | Port-Mapped I/O |
|---|---|---|
| Address space | I/O devices share the same address space as memory | I/O devices have a separate address space |
| Instructions | Any memory instruction (LDR, STR) can access I/O | Special IN/OUT instructions needed |
| Address space size | Reduced (memory loses some addresses) | Full memory available for data |
| Speed | Generally faster (no extra bus cycles) | May require extra wait states |
| Example | ARM Cortex-M, most RISC MCUs | Intel x86 (IN/OUT on port space) |

**Embedded systems predominantly use memory-mapped I/O** because RISC architectures (ARM, RISC-V, AVR) lack special I/O instructions, and the unified address space allows the same load/store instructions to be used for both memory and peripherals — reducing code size and improving compiler optimization.

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, state the definition, give 2 differences in a table, and conclude with the reason. KTU examiners award the third mark for the "why" justification.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of open-drain output in GPIO. Why is it mandatory in the I²C bus?**

**Model Answer:**

**Open-Drain Output:** A GPIO configuration where the output transistor can only **pull the line LOW** (to GND) but cannot drive it HIGH. An **external pull-up resistor** is required to bring the line HIGH when the transistor is off.

**Two Key Reasons it is Mandatory in I²C:**

1. **Wired-AND bus arbitration:** Multiple masters and slaves can connect to the same SDA/SCL lines. Open-drain ensures no device ever "fights" another device by driving HIGH while another drives LOW. The bus is LOW if *any* device pulls it LOW.

2. **Level shifting between mixed-voltage devices:** A 3.3 V MCU and a 5 V sensor can communicate on the same I²C bus by simply using a pull-up resistor to 3.3 V or 5 V, depending on the slave's voltage tolerance.

> [!TIP]
> Mention "wired-AND" — it is a KTU-favorite keyword. Always pair open-drain with the term "wired-AND logic" to score full marks.

---

## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **General Purpose I/O (GPIO) pin** in a modern microcontroller. List any **four** configuration modes. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** An ATmega328P microcontroller is clocked at **16 MHz**. Configure its **UART module** to communicate with a PC at **57600 baud** with **8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit** (8-N-1). Compute the **UBRR value**, the **actual baud rate achieved**, and the **percentage error**. State whether the communication is reliable. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a) — GPIO Architecture (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PAD["External Pin"]
    ESD["ESD Protection"]
    SCH["Schmitt Trigger"]
    DRV["Output Driver"]
    PUPD["Pull-up Pull-down"]
    DDR["Direction Reg DDR"]
    PORT["Output Latch PORT"]
    PIN["Input Read PIN"]
    ALT["Alt Function Mux"]
    INT["Interrupt Logic"]

    PAD --> ESD --> SCH --> PIN
    SCH --> INT
    PUPD --> PAD
    DRV --> PAD
    DDR --> DRV
    DDR --> SCH
    PORT --> DRV
    ALT --> DRV
    ALT --> SCH
```

**Working Principle:**

1. **Configuration Phase:** The CPU writes to the **DDR (Data Direction Register)**. Writing `1` configures the pin as OUTPUT; writing `0` configures it as INPUT.

2. **Output Mode:** When configured as output, the value in the **PORT register** is routed through the **output driver** to the pad. In **push-pull mode**, the driver can actively drive both HIGH (P-MOS) and LOW (N-MOS). In **open-drain mode**, only the N-MOS is used; an external pull-up is needed for HIGH.

3. **Input Mode:** When configured as input, the pad's voltage is sampled by the **Schmitt trigger** (which adds hysteresis to prevent false triggering on noisy edges). The result is read via the **PIN register**.

4. **Alternate Function:** The pin can be **multiplexed** to internal peripherals (UART TX, SPI MOSI, ADC channel, etc.) via the alternate function mux.

5. **Interrupts:** Independent of the direction, the pin can be configured to generate an **interrupt** on rising, falling, or both edges — useful for waking the CPU from sleep.

**Four Configuration Modes:**

1. **Input Floating** — high-impedance; pin state undefined when not driven externally.
2. **Input Pull-up** — internal resistor to V_DD; default HIGH.
3. **Input Pull-down** — internal resistor to GND; default LOW.
4. **Output Push-Pull** — actively drives both HIGH and LOW.
5. **Output Open-Drain** — only pulls LOW.
6. **Analog Mode** — digital input buffer disabled; pin routed to ADC.

*(Any four of the above suffice for full marks.)*

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b) — UART UBRR Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $f_{clock} = 16 \text{ MHz} = 16 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- $f_{baud} = 57600 \text{ bps}$
- 8-N-1 frame

**Step 1:** Apply the UBRR formula:

$$UBRR = \frac{f_{clock}}{16 \times f_{baud}} - 1$$

$$UBRR = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 57600} - 1 = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{921600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = 17.361 - 1 = 16.361$$

**Step 2:** Round to nearest integer:

$$UBRR = 16$$

**Step 3:** Compute the actual baud rate with UBRR = 16:

$$f_{baud,\_actual} = \frac{f_{clock}}{16 \times (UBRR + 1)} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 17}$$

$$f_{baud,\_actual} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{272} = 58{,}823.53 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 4:** Compute percentage error:

$$\text{Error \%} = \left| \frac{58{,}823.53 - 57{,}600}{57{,}600} \right| \times 100$$

$$= \left| \frac{1{,}223.53}{57{,}600} \right| \times 100 = 2.124\%$$

**Step 5:** Engineering Conclusion:

> [!WARNING]
> The error **2.124% slightly exceeds the 2% reliability threshold**. Communication **may work but is at the edge of reliability**, and timing-sensitive data transfer could occasionally fail. For robust 16 MHz operation, **38400 baud** is preferred (error ≈ 0.16%).

**Frame Time Calculation (Bonus):**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{1 + 8 + 0 + 1}{57600} = \frac{10}{57600} = 173.6 \text{ μs per byte}$$

> **Effective throughput** = $\frac{8 \text{ data bits}}{173.6 \text{ μs}} = 46.08 \text{ kB/s}$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **UART, SPI, and I²C** serial communication protocols. Compare them on the basis of **wires required, speed, duplex mode, and number of devices supported**. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** A **12-bit ADC** is used in an embedded temperature monitoring system with $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$. The analog input voltage varies from **0.5 V to 4.5 V**. Calculate:
  - (i) The **resolution (LSB voltage)**
  - (ii) The **digital output codes** for 0.5 V and 4.5 V inputs
  - (iii) The **SQNR in dB**
  - (iv) The **worst-case quantization error** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a) — Serial Protocol Comparison (7 marks)**

**UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter):**
- **Wires:** 2 (TX, RX) — point-to-point only
- **Speed:** Up to ~1 Mbaud (typically 9600–115200)
- **Duplex:** Full-duplex
- **Synchronization:** Asynchronous; both ends must pre-agree on baud rate
- **Frame:** Start bit + 5–9 data bits + optional parity + 1–2 stop bits
- **Use Case:** Debug console, GPS module, Bluetooth (HC-05)

**SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface):**
- **Wires:** 4 (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS)
- **Speed:** Up to 50+ MHz
- **Duplex:** Full-duplex
- **Synchronization:** Synchronous (shared clock SCLK)
- **Devices:** 1 master, multiple slaves selected via individual SS lines
- **Use Case:** SD card, TFT display, high-speed ADC

**I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit):**
- **Wires:** 2 (SDA, SCL) — both open-drain with pull-ups
- **Speed:** 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz / 3.4 MHz
- **Duplex:** Half-duplex
- **Synchronization:** Synchronous
- **Devices:** Up to **127** (7-bit addressing) on the same bus
- **Use Case:** Sensors, RTC, EEPROM, low-speed peripherals

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | UART | SPI | I²C |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wires** | 2 | 4 + N (SS lines) | 2 |
| **Max Speed** | ~1 Mbaud | 50+ MHz | 3.4 MHz |
| **Duplex** | Full | Full | Half |
| **Devices** | 1-to-1 | 1 master + N slaves | Up to 127 |
| **Clock** | None (async) | SCLK | SCL |
| **Pin Count** | Low | High (SS per slave) | Lowest |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium-High |

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b) — 12-bit ADC Calculations (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $n = 12$ bits
- $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$
- $V_{in,\_min} = 0.5 \text{ V}$, $V_{in,\_max} = 4.5 \text{ V}$

---

**(i) Resolution (LSB Voltage) — (2 marks):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{2^{12}} = \frac{5.0}{4096}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = 1.2207 \text{ mV per LSB}}$$

---

**(ii) Digital Output Codes — (2 marks):**

For $V_{in} = 0.5 \text{ V}$:

$$D_{0.5V} = \left\lfloor \frac{0.5}{1.2207 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 409.6 \right\rfloor = 409$$

For $V_{in} = 4.5 \text{ V}$:

$$D_{4.5V} = \left\lfloor \frac{4.5}{1.2207 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3686.4 \right\rfloor = 3686$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The actual ADC hardware will round to the nearest code. The 12-bit output range is **0 to 4095**, so both codes (409 and 3686) are well within the valid range.

---

**(iii) SQNR in dB — (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76$$

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR} = 74.0 \text{ dB}}$$

---

**(iv) Worst-Case Quantization Error — (1.5 marks):**

$$E_{q,\_worst} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{1.2207 \text{ mV}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{E_{q,\_worst} = \pm 0.6104 \text{ mV}}$$

> **Engineering note:** This 0.61 mV worst-case error corresponds to ±0.5 LSB. The system can resolve temperature changes that produce voltage changes greater than 1.22 mV. If a thermistor outputs 10 mV/°C, the system resolution is $\frac{1.22 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} = 0.122 \text{ °C}$ — sufficient for most HVAC and weather-station applications.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **UBRR Rounding Error:** Students often forget to **subtract 1** in $UBRR = \frac{f_{clock}}{16 \cdot f_{baud}} - 1$. This produces a baud rate ~6% off, exceeding the reliability threshold. Always show the -1 step explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 16× Oversampling Factor:** UART baud rate formula has 16 in the denominator. Students sometimes use $\frac{f_{clock}}{f_{baud}}$ and get a wrong UBRR. The 16× is because the UART samples each incoming bit 16 times to find the center.
>
> 3. **ADC LSB Confusion:** The LSB formula is $\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$, **NOT** $\frac{V_{ref}}{n}$. Mixing these up gives a wrong answer by a factor of ~300 for a 12-bit ADC. Memorize: $2^n$ goes in the denominator.
>
> 4. **PWM Duty Cycle Formula:** Some students write $D\% = \frac{Compare}{Reload}$. The correct formula is $D\% = \frac{Compare}{Reload + 1} \times 100$. The +1 matters because a counter of value `Reload` still takes **one full overflow cycle** to come back.
>
> 5. **I²C vs SPI Wire Count:** I²C = 2 wires, SPI = 4 wires (minimum). Students sometimes write "SPI uses 3 wires" — forgetting that **SS/CS** is mandatory unless using daisy-chained slaves.
>
> 6. **Open-Drain Confusion:** GPIO push-pull CAN drive both HIGH and LOW; GPIO open-drain CANNOT drive HIGH without a pull-up. Mixing this up loses the "wired-AND" concept mark.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This!)**

### 🎯 GPIO
- ✅ Three core registers: **DDR** (direction), **PORT** (output), **PIN** (input read)
- ✅ Modern MCUs use **LAT** (write-only) to avoid read-modify-write hazards
- ✅ **Push-pull** = drives both HIGH and LOW; **Open-drain** = pulls only LOW
- ✅ **Pull-up / pull-down** resistors default the pin to a known state when floating
- ✅ Pin mux allows **alternate functions** (UART TX, SPI MOSI, ADC, PWM)

### 🎯 UART
- ✅ Formula: $UBRR = \frac{f_{clock}}{16 \cdot f_{baud}} - 1$ — **don't forget the −1**
- ✅ Error must be **< 2%** for reliable communication
- ✅ Standard baud rates: **9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200**
- ✅ Frame: **Start (0) + Data (5–9) + Parity (optional) + Stop (1 or 2)**
- ✅ Async — no clock line; both ends pre-agree on baud

### 🎯 SPI
- ✅ **4 wires:** SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS
- ✅ **Synchronous**, **full-duplex**, **master-slave**
- ✅ **4 modes** based on CPOL × CPHA — must match master and slave
- ✅ Speed up to 50+ MHz; throughput = $f_{SCLK} \times N_{bits}$

### 🎯 I²C
- ✅ **2 wires** (SDA, SCL) — both **open-drain** with pull-ups
- ✅ **Synchronous**, **half-duplex**, **multi-master**
- ✅ **7-bit addressing** → up to **127 devices**
- ✅ Speeds: **100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz / 3.4 MHz**
- ✅ "Wired-AND" arbitration — if any device pulls LOW, bus is LOW

### 🎯 Timers / Counters
- ✅ $T_{overflow} = \frac{Prescaler \times (Reload + 1)}{f_{clock}}$
- ✅ **8-bit** timer max count = 256; **16-bit** = 65536
- ✅ PWM duty cycle: $D\% = \frac{Compare}{Reload + 1} \times 100$

### 🎯 ADC
- ✅ Resolution: $\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$
- ✅ Digital output: $D_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n \right\rfloor$
- ✅ Worst-case quantization error: $\pm 0.5 \text{ LSB} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2}$
- ✅ SQNR: $6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ (8-bit → 49.9 dB, 10-bit → 62.0 dB, 12-bit → 74.0 dB)

### 🎯 DAC
- ✅ $V_{out} = \frac{D_{in}}{2^n} \times V_{ref}$

### 🎯 Watchdog Timer
- ✅ Independent hardware timer that **resets the MCU** if firmware fails to "pet" it
- ✅ Critical for safety systems (automotive, medical)

### 🎯 Universal Rule
- ✅ In **all** timer/counter formulas: the count is **(Reload + 1)**, never just Reload. This is the single most common KTU mistake.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggestion on Project Topics: Use Simulators Such as Ripes (https://github.com/mortbopet/Ripes) / GEM5 (https://www.gem5.org/) to Implement Components of Computer Systems Such as Various Cache Organization and Study the Effect, Solutions to Hazards, TLBs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST404 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestion on Project Topics: Use Simulators Such as Ripes (https://github.com/mortbopet/Ripes) / GEM5 (https://www.gem5.org/) to Implement Components of Computer Systems Such as Various Cache Organization and Study the Effect, Solutions to Hazards, TLBs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Computer Architecture Simulators?

A **computer architecture simulator** is a software tool that emulates the behavior of a processor, memory subsystem, and I/O devices at varying levels of abstraction — without requiring physical hardware. For KTU 2024 Scheme project work in PBCST404, two specific simulators are officially suggested:

> [!NOTE]
> **Ripes** — A graphical, educational **RISC-V** processor simulator and assembly editor (https://github.com/mortbopet/Ripes). It is a *functional + visualization* simulator aimed at students.
>
> **gem5** — A modular platform for **computer architecture research** (https://www.gem5.org/). It is *cycle-accurate*, supports multiple ISAs (RISC-V, ARM, x86, MIPS, SPARC, POWER), and is the industry/research-standard architectural simulator.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Ripes** is an open-source, cross-platform (Linux/Windows/macOS) Qt-based graphical simulator that loads RISC-V (RV32I/RV64I) assembly programs, executes them cycle-by-cycle, and visualizes register state, pipeline stages, cache hits/misses, and memory access patterns in real time. It supports **single-cycle, multi-cycle, and 5-stage pipelined** processor models out of the box.

**gem5** is a C++-based, event-driven, modular discrete-event simulator that models the full system (CPU cores, caches, interconnect, memory controllers, devices) at configurable levels — **Atomic Simple, Timing Simple, In-Order, and Out-of-Order (O3)** — and provides detailed performance statistics (CPI, miss rates, queue occupancy, etc.).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Flight Simulator for Pilots:**
>
> - **Ripes** is like a *cockpit trainer for a small Cessna* — easy to use, gives visual feedback (altimeter, airspeed, attitude), lets you try a few flight maneuvers safely. It teaches the *fundamentals* of how a RISC-V plane flies.
> - **gem5** is like a *full-motion Boeing 747 simulator* — used by airline researchers to study fuel consumption under turbulence, engine failure, new wing designs, etc. It produces *publishable numbers* but is far more complex to set up.
>
> In the same way, **Ripes** lets a student observe a pipeline stall or a cache miss visually in 5 minutes, while **gem5** lets an advanced student produce a publishable graph of "miss rate vs. cache size" for a SPEC CPU benchmark.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics You Will Use in Projects

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cycles Per Instruction | **CPI** | cycles/instr | Average clock cycles to retire one instruction |
| Hit Rate | **H** | % (0–1) | Fraction of accesses that find data in cache |
| Miss Rate | **MR** = 1 − H | % (0–1) | Fraction of accesses that miss |
| Miss Penalty | **MP** | cycles | Extra cycles to fetch from lower level on a miss |
| Average Memory Access Time | **AMAT** | cycles | Effective time per memory reference |
| Speedup | **S** | unitless (×) | Ratio of execution times |
| Translation Lookaside Buffer Hit Rate | **TLB-H** | % | Fraction of virtual-to-physical translations cached |
| Page Fault Rate | **PFR** | exceptions/1000 instr | Frequency of TLB misses that reach page walker |

### 1.5 Project Topics Officially Suggested by KTU (Module 4)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly suggests the following simulation-based project themes. Each of these is examinable in Module 4 of PBCST404:
> 1. **Various Cache Organizations** — Direct-mapped, fully-associative, set-associative; write-back vs. write-through; L1 vs. L2 vs. L3; inclusive vs. exclusive; replacement policies (LRU, FIFO, Random).
> 2. **Study the Effect** — Vary cache size, block size, associativity, or replacement policy and plot miss-rate curves.
> 3. **Solutions to Hazards** — Data forwarding, pipeline stalling, branch prediction (static: always-taken, BTFN; dynamic: 1-bit, 2-bit saturating counters).
> 4. **TLBs** — Fully-associative, set-associative, multi-level TLBs; interaction with paging.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Simulator Stack — What Each Tool Does Internally

Both simulators implement a **fetch → decode → execute → memory → write-back** loop, but at very different fidelities.

**Ripes pipeline path (RV32I, 5-stage, default):**
1. Load `.asm` or `.c` (compiled via built-in RISC-V GCC).
2. Lexer/parser converts to internal intermediate representation.
3. Each cycle, the simulator steps through IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB.
4. On a load-use hazard, Ripes **automatically inserts a bubble** (or, with forwarding enabled, routes the EX/MEM result back to the ALU input).
5. Cache hits/misses are displayed in the **Cache View** pane with annotated timeline.

**gem5 simulation path (modular):**
1. User writes a Python configuration script (e.g., `system.py`) selecting CPU model, cache hierarchy, memory type.
2. gem5 compiles the simulated binary for the target ISA (RISC-V/Linux in `fs` mode; bare-metal in `se` mode).
3. The **simulate()** call runs until a chosen event (e.g., 100 M instructions) then dumps statistics.
4. Stats are output in `.txt` and `.json`; users plot with Python/matplotlib.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (All Marks-Locked Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these formulas — they are the foundation of every simulation project the examiner may test. Each one has been picked from the KTU 2024 syllabus outcomes **CO4** (memory hierarchy) and **CO5** (pipelining).

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Average Memory Access Time (single-level cache)** | $\text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1-H) \cdot (T_{\text{hit}} + MP)$ | $T_{\text{hit}}$ in cycles, MP in cycles |
| 2 | **AMAT simplified** | $\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \cdot \text{MP}$ | Always true; MR = 1 − H |
| 3 | **AMAT for 2-level cache** | $\text{AMAT} = T_{1} + \text{MR}_{1} \cdot \bigl(T_{2} + \text{MR}_{2} \cdot \text{MP}_{\text{main}}\bigr)$ | Cascade — L1 miss rate multiplied by L2 cost |
| 4 | **AMAT with TLB** | $\text{AMAT}_{\text{eff}} = \text{AMAT} + \text{PFR} \cdot \text{DiskPenalty}$ | DiskPenalty ≈ 1,000,000 cycles |
| 5 | **Speedup (Amdahl)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | f = fraction enhanced, n = speedup of that fraction |
| 6 | **CPU Time** | $T_{\text{CPU}} = \text{Instr} \cdot \text{CPI} \cdot T_{\text{clock}}$ | $T_{\text{CPU}}$ in seconds |
| 7 | **CPI with stalls** | $\text{CPI} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \sum_i \text{MR}_i \cdot \text{Penalty}_i$ | One term per stall source |
| 8 | **CPI for pipelined processor (no hazards)** | $\text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} = 1$ (with forwarding) | Otherwise = 1 + stall cycles per instr |
| 9 | **Branch prediction accuracy gain** | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{BP}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - \text{branch\_freq} \cdot \text{MP}_{\text{branch}} \cdot (1 - \text{acc})}$ | acc = prediction accuracy |
| 10 | **Cache capacity** | $\text{Capacity} = \text{Sets} \times \text{Associativity} \times \text{BlockSize (bytes)}$ | All in bytes or words |
| 11 | **Number of sets** | $\text{Sets} = \dfrac{\text{CacheSize}}{\text{Associativity} \times \text{BlockSize}}$ | Result is a power of 2 |
| 12 | **Tag bits** | $\text{Tag} = \text{AddrBits} - \log_2(\text{Sets}) - \log_2(\text{BlockSize})$ | For 32-bit address = 32 |
| 13 | **TLB reach** | $\text{Size} \times \text{PageSize}$ | Must exceed working set, else thrashing |
| 14 | **Multilevel TLB miss check** | $\text{AccessTime} = T_{\text{L1TLB}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1TLB}} \cdot (T_{\text{L2TLB}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2TLB}} \cdot \text{WalkTime})$ | Page-walk time ≈ 10–30 cycles |
| 15 | **Miss rate 3 C's** | $\text{MR} = \text{MR}_{\text{compulsory}} + \text{MR}_{\text{capacity}} + \text{MR}_{\text{conflict}}$ | Each component reducible by a different technique |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These simulators are the **de-facto industry tools** used by:
> - **Intel/AMD** — pre-silicon validation of cache replacement policies in gem5 before taping out a chip.
> - **Apple/Qualcomm** — evaluating branch predictor accuracy for the next mobile SoC.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section gives **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of the most-tested formulas on this topic. Every transition is written out, in the style of a KTU board examiner's model solution.

### 3.1 Derivation of the AMAT Formula for a Single-Level Cache

**Problem statement:** A level-1 cache has a hit time of 1 cycle, a miss rate of 5%, and a miss penalty of 100 cycles to main memory. Compute the AMAT.

**Step 1 — Define the two possible outcomes for a memory access.**

A memory access can result in exactly one of two outcomes:
- A **cache hit** with probability $H$, costing $T_{\text{hit}}$ cycles.
- A **cache miss** with probability $1-H$, costing $T_{\text{hit}} + \text{MP}$ cycles (we always pay the hit time, *plus* the miss penalty to fetch from the next level).

**Step 2 — Express AMAT as a probability-weighted expectation.**

$$
\text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1-H) \cdot (T_{\text{hit}} + \text{MP})
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the right-hand side.**

$$
\text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1-H) \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1-H) \cdot \text{MP}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $T_{\text{hit}}$.**

$$
\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} \cdot \bigl[H + (1-H)\bigr] + (1-H) \cdot \text{MP}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the identity $H + (1-H) = 1$.**

$$
\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} \cdot 1 + (1-H) \cdot \text{MP}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize $1-H = \text{MR}$ and rewrite.**

$$
\boxed{\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \cdot \text{MP}}
$$

**Step 7 — Plug in the numerical values for the problem.**

Given $T_{\text{hit}} = 1$ cycle, $\text{MR} = 0.05$, $\text{MP} = 100$ cycles:

$$
\text{AMAT} = 1 + (0.05)(100) = 1 + 5 = 6 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 8 — Interpret.** A memory access that *appears* to take 1 cycle (the hit time) actually takes an average of 6 cycles once misses are accounted for. The **5% miss rate adds 5 extra cycles per access** — a 6× slowdown in memory latency.

### 3.2 Derivation of 2-Level AMAT

**Problem statement:** L1 has $T_1 = 1$ cycle, $\text{MR}_1 = 5\%$. L2 has $T_2 = 10$ cycles, $\text{MR}_2 = 3\%$ (of accesses that reach L2). Main memory access costs $\text{MP}_{\text{main}} = 200$ cycles. Compute AMAT.

**Step 1 — Decompose outcomes.**

An access to L1 either hits (probability $H_1$, cost $T_1$) or misses (probability $\text{MR}_1$, cost $T_1$ + cost of L2 access).

**Step 2 — L2 access cost.**

The L2 access itself either hits (prob $H_2 = 1 - \text{MR}_2$, cost $T_2$) or misses (prob $\text{MR}_2$, cost $T_2 + \text{MP}_{\text{main}}$).

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{AMAT} = T_1 + \text{MR}_1 \cdot \bigl[T_2 + \text{MR}_2 \cdot \text{MP}_{\text{main}}\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in values.**

$$
\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.05 \cdot \bigl[10 + 0.03 \cdot 200\bigr]
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the inner bracket first.**

$$
10 + 0.03 \cdot 200 = 10 + 6 = 16 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by L1 miss rate.**

$$
0.05 \cdot 16 = 0.8 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 7 — Add L1 hit time.**

$$
\boxed{\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.8 = 1.8 \text{ cycles}}
$$

**Step 8 — Insight.** Without L2 the AMAT would be **6 cycles**; with L2 it is **1.8 cycles** — a **3.33× reduction in memory stall time**. This is why modern CPUs have L2/L3 caches.

### 3.3 Derivation of CPI with Pipeline Hazards

**Problem statement:** A pipelined RISC-V processor has a base CPI of 1 (perfect pipelining, no stalls). The instruction mix is: 40% ALU, 25% load, 10% store, 15% branch, 10% jump. Load-use hazards cause 1 stall cycle per load instruction. Branches are resolved in EX stage; with **always-not-taken** prediction, 30% of branches are mispredicted, each costing 2 cycles.

**Step 1 — Identify each stall source and its frequency.**

| Hazard | Frequency | Stall per occurrence | Total stall cycles per instr |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load-use | 25% of all instr | 1 cycle | $0.25 \cdot 1 = 0.25$ |
| Branch mispredict | 15% × 30% = 4.5% of all instr | 2 cycles | $0.045 \cdot 2 = 0.09$ |

**Step 2 — Sum all stall-cycle contributions.**

$$
\text{Stall CPI} = 0.25 + 0.09 = 0.34
$$

**Step 3 — Add base CPI.**

$$
\boxed{\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = 1 + 0.34 = 1.34}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute CPU time for 1 billion instructions on a 2 GHz clock.**

$$
T_{\text{clock}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.5 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = N \cdot \text{CPI} \cdot T_{\text{clock}} = (10^9)(1.34)(0.5 \cdot 10^{-9}) = 0.67 \text{ seconds}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of TLB Reach and Miss Penalty

**Problem statement:** A 32-bit system has 4 KB pages, a 64-entry fully-associative TLB, and a page-walk cost of 30 cycles. Compute TLB reach and the AMAT contribution.

**Step 1 — Compute TLB reach.**

$$
\text{Reach} = \text{Entries} \times \text{PageSize} = 64 \times 4096 \text{ bytes} = 262\,144 \text{ bytes} = 256 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the working set.**

If a program references a working set $> 256$ KB, the TLB will thrash (constant misses). For example, a 1 MB array of `int` (4 bytes each → 256 K elements) just barely fits; a 1.5 MB array thrashes.

**Step 3 — TLB contribution to AMAT.**

$$
\text{AMAT}_{\text{TLB}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + \text{MR}_{\text{TLB}} \cdot \text{WalkPenalty}
$$

Assume TLB hit time is 1 cycle, miss rate is 1%:

$$
\text{AMAT}_{\text{TLB}} = 1 + 0.01 \cdot 30 = 1.3 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine with cache AMAT.**

If the cache AMAT is 6 cycles and the TLB check is on the critical path, the effective access time is:

$$
\text{AMAT}_{\text{eff}} = 1.3 + \text{MR}_{\text{TLB}} \cdot \text{AMAT}_{\text{cache}} \quad \text{(sequential TLB-then-cache access)}
$$

**Step 5 — Plug in numbers.**

$$
\text{AMAT}_{\text{eff}} = 1.3 + 0.01 \cdot 6 = 1.36 \text{ cycles}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation of Amdahl's Speedup for Branch Predictor

**Problem statement:** A 2-bit saturating counter branch predictor improves branch prediction accuracy from 80% to 95%. Branches occur 15% of the time. The misprediction penalty is 4 cycles. Compute the speedup.

**Step 1 — Compute old and new stall cycles per branch.**

Old: mispredict rate $= 1 - 0.80 = 0.20$
$$
\text{Stall}_{\text{old}} = 0.20 \cdot 4 = 0.8 \text{ cycles per branch}
$$
New: mispredict rate $= 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$
$$
\text{Stall}_{\text{new}} = 0.05 \cdot 4 = 0.2 \text{ cycles per branch}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to CPI contribution (multiply by branch frequency).**

Old:
$$
\Delta\text{CPI}_{\text{old}} = 0.15 \cdot 0.8 = 0.12
$$
New:
$$
\Delta\text{CPI}_{\text{new}} = 0.15 \cdot 0.2 = 0.03
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total CPI in each case.**

Assume base CPI = 1.0:
$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{old}} = 1.0 + 0.12 = 1.12
$$
$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{new}} = 1.0 + 0.03 = 1.03
$$

**Step 4 — Speedup (CPU time inversely proportional to CPI).**

$$
\boxed{S = \frac{\text{CPI}_{\text{old}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{new}}} = \frac{1.12}{1.03} \approx 1.087}
$$

A modest **8.7% speedup** — a typical real-world result for branch-predictor upgrades on SPEC INT benchmarks.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Project Outcomes vs. Simulator Choice

| Project Question | Key Metric | How to Measure in Ripes | How to Measure in gem5 | Expected Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Does increasing associativity reduce miss rate? | MR vs. assoc | Cache View → toggle 1/2/4/8-way | `MyL1Cache.assoc` parameter sweep | MR falls, then plateaus (3 C's) |
| Does larger block size always help? | MR vs. block | Cache View → block size 16/32/64 | `MyL1Cache.block_size` sweep | MR first drops (spatial), then rises (conflict/pollution) |
| How does write-back vs. write-through affect performance? | AMAT, write count | Toggle in Settings → Cache | `MyL1Cache.writeback` | Write-back: fewer memory writes, more complex |
| Effect of branch predictor on CPI? | CPI vs. predictor | Enable BPU → static/dynamic | `MyBP` class selection | CPI drops with better predictor |
| TLB thrashing on large arrays? | TLB miss rate | Not directly visualized | `MyL1TLB.size`, page walker config | Miss rate jumps at working set > reach |
| Does pipelining help? | Speedup vs. cycle count | Toggle single-cycle vs. 5-stage | `TimingSimpleCPU` vs. `O3CPU` | Speedup approaches # of stages, then plateaus |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ripes Simulation Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User writes RISC-V assembly .asm file] --> B[Ripes GUI / CLI loads file]
    B --> C[Lexer parses into IR intermediate representation]
    C --> D[Processor model selection: Single-cycle / Multi-cycle / 5-stage Pipeline]
    D --> E[Cycle step loop begins]
    E --> F[IF: Instruction Fetch from I-cache]
    F --> G[ID: Decode, read register file]
    G --> H{Load-Use or Branch Hazard?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Insert NOP bubble or stall]
    H -- No --> J[EX: ALU / branch resolution]
    I --> J
    J --> K[MEM: D-cache access hit or miss]
    K --> L{Miss in D-cache?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Stall MP cycles, fetch from L2 or RAM]
    L -- No --> N[WB: Write back to register file]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Update Cache View pane visual annotation]
    N --> P[Update Register View pane]
    O --> Q[Performance metrics: CPI, hit rate, miss rate displayed]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R{More instructions to execute?}
    R -- Yes --> E
    R -- No --> S[Generate statistics report and exit]
```

### 4.2 gem5 Modular Simulation Configuration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Python config script: system.py] --> B[Define System class with clock, voltage]
    B --> C[Select CPU model: AtomicSimple / TimingSimple / MinorCPU / O3CPU]
    C --> D[Build Cache hierarchy: L1I, L1D, L2, L3]
    D --> E[Add Bus interconnect and CoherenceXBar]
    E --> F[Define Memory: DDR3 / DDR4 / SimpleMemory]
    F --> G[Add TLB hierarchy: L1 I-TLB, D-TLB, L2 TLB page walker]
    G --> H[Set branch predictor class: MyLocalBP / MyTournamentBP]
    H --> I[Compile target binary: gem5.opt se / fs binary]
    I --> J[Run simulate call with exit_event]
    J --> K[gem5 dumps stats.txt and stats.json]
    K --> L[User plots with Python matplotlib or pandas]
    L --> M[Final report: CPI, MR, speedup graphs]
```

### 4.3 Two-Level Cache Hierarchy with TLB

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU[CPU Core: issues virtual address VA] --> TLB{L1 TLB}
    TLB -- Hit --> LA[Translate VA to PA physical address]
    TLB -- Miss --> WLK[Page Table Walker 30 cycles]
    WLK --> LA
    LA --> L1{L1 Cache hit?}
    L1 -- Hit --> R1[Return data in 1 cycle]
    L1 -- Miss --> L2{L2 Cache hit?}
    L2 -- Hit --> R2[Return data in 10 cycles, fill L1]
    L2 -- Miss --> RAM[Main memory 200 cycles, fill L1 and L2]
    RAM --> R2
    R2 --> R1
```

### 4.4 5-Stage Pipeline with Hazard Detection Unit (HDU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IF[IF: Instruction Fetch] --> ID[ID: Instruction Decode]
    ID --> EX[EX: Execute ALU]
    EX --> MEM[MEM: Data memory access]
    MEM --> WB[WB: Write back to register file]

    HDU[Hazard Detection Unit] -.-> ID
    HDU -.-> EX
    HDU -.-> IF

    FWD[Forwarding Unit EX to EX] -.-> EX
    FWD -.-> ID
```
